All these vital components were unified and harmonized in the recently issued BSP Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap, sstrategically anchored on three critical pillars: 1. digitalizing the payment streams; 2. enhancing the robustness and resilience of the digital finance infrastructures; and 3. strengthening governance by having an enabling policy and regulatory environment. The Roadmap is envisioned to successfully transform the Philippines from a cash-heavy into a cash-lite economy. Seen from a broad perspective, the QR Ph P2M will help empower our small economic actors to take part in the digital payments ecosystem, and more importantly, in the formal financial system. Rest assured that the BSP remains committed in ensuring our nation’s smooth transition into the New Economy where every Filipino stand to benefit from a universal access to safe, convenient, affordable and inclusive digital payments. Truly, this era has proved that digital transformation has become an imperative. While the road to digital transformation may seem challenging, we are optimistic that it is achievable with our proactive regulatory stance and our openness to leverage on technological innovations. Through the support of our industry partners, the BSP is optimistic that we can build pathways towards a cash-lite Philippine economy, and enable more Filipinos to reap the benefits of a growing digital economy. Thank you. ​

Title: Inspirational Message of BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno during the Turnover of PiTaKa (Pinansyal na Talino at Kaalaman) Learning Videos and Trainers’ Manual to OWWA
Occasion: Turnover of PiTaKa (Pinansyal na Talino at Kaalaman) Learning Videos and Trainers’ Manual to OWWA
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ OWWA Administrator Hans Leo J. Cacdac, BDO Foundation President Mario A. Deriquito, BDO Foundation Trustee and BDO Unibank President Nestor V. Tan, sa ating mga kababayan sa ibat-ibang bahagi ng mundo, magandang araw po sa inyong lahat. ​ Milyon-milyong Pilipino ang umaalis taun-taon upang magtrabaho sa ibang bansa dala ang pag-asang mabigyan ng magandang buhay ang kanilang pamilya. Subalit kasabay ng pag-angat ng antas ng pamumuhay na dulot ng mataas na kita sa pag-a-abroad, madalas na nakakalimutan ang naunang layunin na makapag-ipon ng sapat na pera para matupad ang kanilang mga pangarap para sa kanilang mga mahal sa buhay. Dahil dito, napatagal o hindi tuluyang makauwi ang ating mga OFW dahil nauubos agad ang kanilang padala, at walang naiipon o nai-pupundar na kabuhayan sa Pilipinas. Isa sa mga pangunahing dahilan nito ay maling pamamahala ng sweldo o remittance, at kakulangan sa kaalaman tungkol sa tamang pag-iipon at pag-iinvest. Ayon sa isinagawang survey sa mga OFWs at kanilang pamilya noong 2020, halos pitumpong porsiyento (70%) ng OFWs ang hindi nakapag-iipon. Nasa anim na porsyento lamang (6%) ang nag-iinvest. Halos dalawampong porsyento (20%) naman ang gumagastos ng remittances para sa appliances at iba pang kagamitan, na hindi naman nagagamit sa kabuhayan. Ang kwentong napanood natin sa unang video ay isa lamang halimbawa. Pagkatapos ng anim na taon sa pag-aabroad, wala siyang naipon, nabaon sa utang, at na-scam pa. Nakalulungkot isipin na ang pagod, hirap, at sakripisyo sa pag-aabroad ay nauwi sa trahedya at naglahong pangarap. Iwasan po natin na maging ganito ang kwento ng ating mga OFW at ng kanilang pamilya. Napakahalaga na maging wais sa pera (financially prudent) ang ating mga OFW, pati ang kanilang buong pamilya. Tandaan po natin na hindi panghabambuhay ang pag-a-abroad. Kaya habang may kinikita pa, tipirin, ipunin, ipundar sa hanapbuhay o negosyo. Dapat ay matuto silang maghanda sa mga hindi inaasahang pangyayari – katulad ng COVID-19, ang biglaang pagkawala ng trabaho, pagkakasakit, o pagkakaroon ng kalamidad. Upang mas maging financially literate ang ating mga kababayan, nagsama-sama ang BSP, OWWA, at BDO Foundation para ipatupad ang programang tinawag na “Pinansyal na Talino at Kaalaman” o “PiTaKa” para sa OFWs. Nais namin na bawat OFW, at lahat ng miyembro ng kanilang pamilya, ay magkaroon ng angkop na kaalaman upang sila ay makagawa ng financial plan na makatutulong sa pagtupad ng layuning mapa-unlad ang kanilang buhay. Sana ay sa pamamagitan ng programang ito, dadating ang panahon na ang bawat OFW ay makapag-pundar ng kabuhayan sa Pilipinas, para hindi na nila kailangang bumalik sa abroad para kumita. Sana ay magsilbing aral at inspirasyon ang mga videos na ipapakita natin sa araw na ito. Maaring i-share ang mga ito sa inyong mga kapamilya at kaibigan. Makakaasa kayo na patuloy na susuporta ang BSP sa OWWA, kaisa ang BDO Foundation, sa paggawa ng mga learning tools at financial literacy modules para magamit sa iba’t ibang training programs para sa ating mga OFWs. Ikinalulugod naming i-turn-over sa OWWA ang Financial Education Trainers Manual na magiging bahagi ng Pre-Departure Orientation Seminars para sa mga kababayan nating OFWs. Nagpupugay ang Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas, kaisa ang OWWA at BDO Foundation, sa bawat OFW na nagsusumikap mapabuti ang kanilang kalagayan sa buhay. Tunay kayong bagong bayani at dangal ng inyong pamilya at ng bansang Pilipinas. Maraming salamat at magandang araw.

Title: Philippines: Traversing the Path to Economic Recovery
Occasion: Eastern Communication E-Huddle Webinar Series: “Making Remote Work and Creating Opportunities”
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Good afternoon to all the participants and to my fellow speakers! It is an honor to be part of this E-Huddle virtual webinar series organized by Eastern Communications. The BSP always welcomes the opportunity to engage with industries and share its views on recent Philippine economic developments as well as the BSP’s policy thrust towards helping banks and corporates recover and adapt to the emerging new normal environment. Allow me to highlight the following points in my keynote presentation: First, I will start with a discussion on the state of the Philippine economy amid the pandemic. It has been a year since the onset of COVID-19 in the country, and like other economies across the globe, the country’s economy has been affected by the shocks from the health crisis. Second, I will highlight the BSP’s policy initiatives in helping the country weather the economic and financial headwinds from the pandemic. As you are aware, the BSP together with the national government, has put in place extraordinary measures to ensure stability and minimize the scarring effects of the pandemic on the financial system and the economy. I will also touch on some of the BSP’s digitalization efforts that support economic recovery. Third, I will discuss how banks are incorporating sustainable finance efforts to ensure business continuity, given the long-term social and economic effects of the pandemic and climate change. The year 2020 has been particularly challenging for the Philippine economy due to the unprecedented COVID-19 crisis. Yet, some of the key economic indicators in 2020 show that the country’s macroeconomic fundamentals remained broadly intact, even at the peak of the COVID-19 crisis as evidenced by: • the quarter-on-quarter improvements in GDP outturn towards year-end; • within-target inflation; • ample system liquidity; • sound and stable banking system; • robust external payments position; and • manageable fiscal deficit. Like many other countries, the Philippines was severely affected by the coronavirus pandemic in 2020. During the year, we saw the country’s GDP drop to a historic low of -9.6 percent. After a 16.9 percent contraction in Q2 2020 due to the imposition of lockdown and containment measures, the domestic economy quickly showed some signs of improvement as the contraction eased to -11.4 percent and -8.3 percent in Q3 and Q4 2020, respectively. Going forward, GDP is expected to rebound starting the second quarter 2021, as the domestic and the global economy gradually reopens. This resiliency can be attributed to two things. First, the country entered the crisis in a position of strength. This insulated the domestic economy to more adverse economic and financial shocks from the pandemic. Second, the timely and extraordinary monetary and fiscal policy measures that were put in place ensured financial stability and helped minimize scarring effects on the economy. In response to the COVID-19 pandemic, the BSP has implemented measures to ensure adequate domestic liquidity and sustain the flow of credit, restore financial market functioning, and shore up market confidence. These include a mix of decisive monetary policy adjustments and regulatory relief and forbearance measures. The BSP acted swiftly and decisively. It reduced the policy rate by a cumulative 200 basis points (bps) since February 2020. The cut in the policy rate is aimed at cushioning the country’s growth momentum and uplifting market confidence amid stronger headwinds brought about by the pandemic. The BSP has also reduced the reserve requirement ratios (RRR) by 200 basis points effective in April 2020. The lower reserve requirement is intended to calm the markets and support bank lending to both households and businesses. As part of the whole-of-government response to the COVID-19 pandemic, the BSP has extended provisional advances to the national government on a time-bound basis and within the limits prescribed by law. The BSP has also been purchasing government securities in the secondary market to help shore up domestic liquidity and restore market confidence to continue participating in the primary auctions for government securities. These measures have helped address temporary volatilities in the market. By maintaining market interests to continue to hold government securities, the national government was able to meet its funding needs for its COVID-19- related programs. In addition to liquidity enhancing measures, the BSP has also relaxed prudential and regulatory standards to soften the impact of the pandemic on the economy and improve the prospects of a faster recovery. These measures are summarized into four (4) categories: 1.Extension of financial relief to borrowers. Banks were given regulatory relief to enable them to grant equivalent financial relief to their borrowers in the form of more flexible and favorable lending terms, or to restructure loan accounts. 2. Incentivized lending. The BSP allowed new peso loans to micro, small and medium enterprises or MSMEs, and large enterprises that were critically affected by the pandemic, as alternative mode of compliance to reserve requirements. 3.Promotion of continued access to financial services. Policies were put in place to ensure access of deeply affected retail clients to formal financing channels. The use of information technology in carrying out financial transactions was highly encouraged during the lockdown. 4.Support for continued financial services delivery. The BSP granted operational relief measures to the BSP-supervised financial institutions. These measures aim to assist BSFIs in focusing their limited resources on the delivery of financial services and support their subsequent recovery efforts. The BSP also supports the passage of key structural reforms which will help propel the economy to a more solid path to recovery. RA No. 11519 or “An Act Extending the Availability of Appropriations of Bayanihan 2” was signed into law on December 29, 2020 and is valid until June 30, 2021to ensure the continuity and implementation of response measures in Bayanihan 1, which granted loans to micro, small and medium enterprises (MSMEs). Republic Act No. 11534 or the Corporate Recovery and Tax Incentives for Enterprises (CREATE). The law reduced the corporate income tax outright from 30 percent to 25 percent for big firms and 20 percent for small firms. The law also rationalized the tax incentives structure, making it performance-based, time-bound, targeted and transparent. This will be the biggest stimulus package for businesses. With the resulting tax savings, MSMEs can continue to fund their operations and retain employees. Republic Act No. 11523 or the Financial Institutions Strategic Transfer Act, popularly known as the FIST Act, aims to strengthen the financial sector by allowing banks and other financial institutions to efficiently dispose of their non-performing assets (NPAs) and non-performing loans (NPLs) to asset management firms, known as FIST corporations, thereby enabling banks to extend credit to more sectors. The FIST law can possibly free up 1.9 trillion worth of loans from the sale by banks of their non-performing assets. The FIST law is expected to improve the banking systems’ NPL ratio by 0.63 to 0.71 percentage points. Pending in Congress is House Bill NO. 7749 or the Government Financial Institutions Unified Initiative to Distressed Enterprises for Economic Recovery or GUIDE Bill. This seeks to expand the loan assistance, rediscounting, and other credit facilities of government financial institutions—Development Bank of the Philippines and Land Bank of the Philippines, to help MSMEs cope with the effects of COVID-19. Lastly, the Agri-Agra Bill,will strengthen rural development by providing for a holistic approach in addressing the financing needs of the broader agricultural financing ecosystem. Aside from these broad-based pandemic policy responses, the BSP has also been seizing new opportunities and ramping up its digitalization efforts to support economic recovery. The pandemic has been the catalyst for the exponential rise in the use of electronic payment channels. With the precautionary health measures associated with the pandemic, digital channels for conducting financial services have become imperative. Fortunately, the BSP has embraced digitalization even before the pandemic. But to further strengthen its digitalization efforts, the BSP launched the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap 2020 – 2023 last October. This roadmap is in line with the BSP’s goal to shift from a cash-heavy to a cash-lite economy. Targeted outcomes of this roadmap include shifting at least 50% of the total volume of retail payments into digital form and having at least 70% of Filipino adults onboarded to the financial system through the ownership and use of transaction account by 2023. The BSP believes that financial digitization will ensure the resurgence of businesses and boost economic growth as digitalization makes payments easier and quicker and helps improve working capital efficiency. Digitalization also accelerates financial inclusion to those unserved and underserved by traditional bank offices. The Philippine banking system remains sound and stable. The banking system consistently posted capital adequacy ratios (CAR) at about 15.0 percent in the past 10 years, which is well above the 10.0 percent minimum threshold set by the BSP, and 10 percent international threshold set by the Bank for International Settlements (BIS). As of end-September 2020, the risk-based capital adequacy ratios of the universal and commercial banking industry stood at 17.2 percent on consolidated basis. Banks also continue to have ample liquidity buffers, which allow them to provide adequate and stable funding in the medium term. Further, domestic banks’ assets continue to grow robustly. As of end-December 2020, the banking system assets expanded by 6.1 percent year-on-year to ₱19.4 trillion. Thi

Title: Welcome Message of BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno for the 18th Citi Microentrepreneurship Awards (CMA) National Selection Committee (NSC) Meeting
Occasion: 18th Citi Microentrepreneurship Awards (CMA) National Selection Committee (NSC) Meeting
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​Good morning to Citi Philippines CEO, Mr. Aftab Ahmed, and esteemed members of the National Selection Committee. I am pleased to welcome you to today’s deliberation of nominees for the Citi Microentrepreneurship Awards. Also joining us are Secretary Berna Romulo-Puyat, Miss Salve Duplito, and Mr. Lance Gokongwei, new additions to the Committee, whose insights and viewpoints will enrich our discussions. I would like to thank our partners Citi and Microfinance Council of the Philippines, and the Citi Microentrepreneurship Awards country team for making this 18th run of the Citi Microentrepreneurship Awards possible. We’re all aware that the COVID-19 pandemic has been harsh to our micro, small, and medium enterprises. They continue to weather the pandemic’s adverse effects. But true to the Filipino entrepreneurial spirit, many have stepped up by adapting, pivoting, and innovating. Based on this year’s nominees, we can say our microentrepreneurs remain tenacious, resilient, and optimistic amid the pandemic. To my fellow Committee members, we have a tough but exciting task before us. Let’s select winners who most embody the spirit and values of this awards program. Through this year’s Citi Microentrepreneurship Awards, we have a chance to convey a message of hope and courage to the microentrepreneurs hurt by the ongoing pandemic. In these extraordinary times, the nominees serve as a beacon of inspiration. Their stories show us that perseverance, determination, and commitment to uplift each other are far stronger than the virus. Finally, I hope that you are looking forward to the video presentations and deliberation as I am. We have quite a roster of nominees, so let’s get started. Again, good morning and thank you everyone.​

Title: Acceptance Speech of BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno for the Central Banking Awards
Occasion: Central Banking Awards
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​On behalf of the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas, I would like to convey my utmost gratitude to Central Banking Publications for recognizing our efforts to upgrade our website, bsp.gov.ph. This “Website of the Year” award only confirms that we are on the right track in revamping and converting the BSP website into a potent tool for central bank communication—helping us in our goal to bring the BSP closer to the Filipino people. Again, this recognition inspires and motivates us to move forward in reforming, innovating, and transforming the BSP’s operations and services in line with the dynamic and digital New Economy. Thank you very much!​

Title: Moving Ahead and Embracing Change under the New Normal
Occasion: 31st Bankers Institute of the Philippines, Inc. (BAIPHIL) Virtual Convention
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: Distinguished officials of the Bankers Institute of the Philippines or BAIPHIL, guests, friends and colleagues in the industry, ladies and gentlemen, good afternoon. I am privileged to be your keynote speaker on the third and final session of BAIPHIL’s 31st virtual convention. Let me take this opportunity to share some insights on the regulatory landscape of the Philippine banking sector under the “new normal,” or what I would rather refer to as the “new economy.” Today’s theme, “Moving Ahead and Embracing Change Under the New Economy,” underpins two notions that are indispensable in today’s challenging environment. First, embracing the changes stemming from the COVID-19 pandemic is paramount to keeping businesses afloat. Second, capitalizing on factors that enable our banks to face the recent headwinds will help boost resilience and allow them to move ahead of the game. With these in mind, my keynote address will cover five central topics, namely: (1) outlook on the Philippine banking sector, (2) leveraging on BASEL standards for a more resilient banking system, (3) forging a digital economy, (4) gearing towards a sustainable new economy, and (5) responding to the present workforce challenges. Let me start with updates on the financial condition and performance of the banking industry. The Philippine banking system is sound and stasble as shown by sustained growth in assets, strong capital position, adequate liquidity buffers, ample loan loss reserves, and profitable operations. Total assets of the banking system registered at Php19.3 trillion as of end-February 2021, posting a 6.5 percent year-on-year growth rate. As of end-December 2020, the risk-based capital adequacy ratio of the universal and commercial banking industry on solo and consolidated bases stood at 16.6 percent and 17.1 percent, respectively—well above the minimum thresholds set by the BSP and the Bank for International Settlements. As of end-December 2020, the universal and commercial banking industry’s liquidity coverage ratio and net stable funding ratio remained higher than the minimum 100 percent threshold which indicate banks’ ability to fund requirements during short- and long-term liquidity shocks. For smaller banks such as the stand-alone thrift banks, rural banks and cooperative banks, the minimum liquidity ratios surpassed the 20 percent minimum. As to asset quality, indicators weakened but remained manageable. The banking system’s non-performing loan ratio rose to 4 percent as of end-February 2021 from 2.2 percent in the same period last year. Meanwhile, banks’ non-performing loan coverage ratio decreased to 87.3 percent as of end-February 2021, from 91.2 percent a year ago. On profitability, banks recorded positive bottom line in spite of higher provisioning. Net income of the banking industry dropped by 32.8 percent year-on-year to Php155.0 billion for the year-ended December 2020. Nonetheless, the positive bottom line was mainly driven by earnings from core business activities, complemented by improved cost-to-income ratio from streamlined operations. The BSP expects the banking system to remain stable with adequate capital and liquidity buffers to support the financing requirements of their clients. The impact of the pandemic on the overall condition and performance of the banking system has been manageable. This was largely due to the calibrated fiscal and prudential initiatives to address the impact of the pandemic as reinforced by the BSP’s timely implementation of time-bound and crucial regulatory relief measures in the first half of 2020. The BSP has conducted stress test exercises and ad hoc simulations to assess the banking system’s resiliency amid the COVID-19 crisis. Overall, the results reinforce that the banking system is reasonably well-capitalized and could continue lending to support the economy. However, the current challenges in the financial performance of the hardest-hit sectors and highly vulnerable banks warrant closer coordination and monitoring to ensure the banking system’s financial stability and continuing resilience. The BSP estimates that the non-performing loan ratio of the banking sector will remain manageable and will settle at slightly above 6.0 percent by end-December 2021. This view is also consistent with the feedback gathered from the industry through the Banking Sector Outlook Survey conducted in the second semester of 2020. With the operationalization of the Financial Institutions Strategic Transfer or FIST Act, the level of non-performing loans of banks will be managed through the transfer or sale of banks’ non-performing assets to FIST Corporations, Special Purpose Vehicles and eligible individuals. The stable outlook of the banking system is echoed by the banking industry, based on the results of the Banking Sector Outlook Survey. In sum, we are optimistic that the Philippine financial system is capable of withstanding the risks and challenges of the COVID-19 pandemic. Yet, as considerable economic uncertainty remains, the BSP continues to monitor relevant risks and vulnerabilities arising from banks’ activities through enhanced surveillance mechanism. The Philippines has already made significant strides in adopting the Basel reform package. The pace and sequencing of the reforms have taken into account local market conditions, as well as the risk profiles of banks by applying the principle of proportionality. The Basel reform agenda has reinforced local processes by enjoining banks to increase their holdings of core equity capital and to favor more stable sources of funding. Domestic banks likewise recognize the benefits of the Basel reforms as they become more risk-sensitive, prudent in managing their risk-taking activities, and improved their governance and internal controls. Currently, regional forums in ASEAN such as the Executives’ Meeting of East Asia Pacific Central Banks Working Group on Banking Supervision serve as venue to discuss the progress of adoption of the Basel III standards and COVID-19 developments and the related policy responses. Recently, the policy direction of standard-setting bodies is geared towards supporting the COVID-19 responses of national authorities. In fact, the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision or the BCBS has officially announced the deferral of the implementation date of Basel standards by one year to 1 January 2023. Such deferral provided both banks and regulators with some space and flexibility to devote their available resources for essential operations in responding to the impact of the global pandemic. With regard to the implementation of Basel standards in the Philippines during the pandemic, the BSP has given leeway to covered banks and quasi-banks by allowing them to utilize the built-up Capital Conservation Buffer and Liquidity Coverage Ratio buffer during the COVID-19 crisis. This move is consistent with the BCBS’ pronouncement that a measured drawdown of the buffers in order to absorb losses and liquidity-related shocks, as well as to support the real economy should be deemed appropriate during periods of stress. Meanwhile, for smaller banks (such as stand-alone thrift banks, rural banks and cooperative banks), the required implementation of the revised risk-based capital framework or Basel III was deferred to 1 January 2023. The minimum liquidity ratio was temporarily reduced to enable these banks to continue to support their rural community-based operations and to respond to the crisis. The BSP also conducts onsite and offsite supervision, data analytics, and thematic reviews to identify and gauge the risks faced by supervised institutions and the banking system as a whole. Rest assured, the BSP remains steadfast in rolling out the essential policy reforms aimed at aligning its policies, rules and regulations with the Basel and other international standards. The BSP recognizes the catalytic role of digital payments in accelerating financial inclusion and in spurring economic growth and recovery from the COVID-19 crisis. To sustain the impetus for wider adoption of digital payments, we launched a three-year Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap last October outlining the priority initiatives and strategy in advancing an efficient, inclusive, safe and secure digital finance ecosystem. Under this Roadmap, we envision to achieve the goals of shifting at least 50 percent of the total volume of retail payments into digital form and having at least 70 percent of Filipino adults onboarded to the financial system through ownership and use of a transaction account by 2023. To attain the strategic outcomes, the Roadmap is anchored on three key pillars: First, the digital payment streams, which focus on exploring digital payment use cases that will fuel wider acceptance and use of digital payments services; Second, the digital finance infrastructure, which supports the boost in digital payments adoption to ensure security, efficiency and interoperability within the ecosystem; and Third, the digital governance standards, which will ensure that the expansion of use cases is bound by sound standards that safeguard the integrity and privacy of consumer data. The BSP endeavors to provide an enabling policy and regulatory environment that allows these innovations to flourish while at the same time, preserving financial stability and upholding consumer welfare. As such, the BSP, in the near term, is pursuing key enabling policy initiatives to maintain the momentum towards digital transformation of the financial sector. In fact, the BSP recently introduced digital banks as a distinct bank classification. In the post-COVID era, digital banks will play a pivotal role in the growth and development of the economy. We expect Filipinos to benefit from new digital banks offering more affordable financial products and customized financial solutions that are responsive to the diverse and changing needs of the market. The BSP is also developing a framework for

Title: Unlocking Opportunities at the Local Level
Occasion: Investment Forum on Energy Transition
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​His Excellency Daniel Robert Pruce, British Ambassador to the Philippines, Department of Finance (DOF) Secretary Carlos G. Dominguez, Department of Energy (DOE) Secretary Alfonso G. Cusi, Governor Dakila Carlo Cua, National President of the Union of Local Authorities of the Philippines (ULAP), distinguished speakers, expert panelists, and participants, good afternoon!​ I would like to take this opportunity to thank the British Embassy Manila, the DOF, DOE, ULAP and Securities and Exchange Commission, for co-organizing this dialogue among our key stakeholders and leading the discussion on how the country can address the challenges in its energy transition and accelerate the action moving forward. This dialogue comes at an opportune time as the country has recently submitted its Nationally Determined Contributions (NDC)[1] to the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change, vowing to reduce its projected carbon emissions by 75 percent by the year 2030. Indeed, this development provides us a clearer direction and a greater impetus to work together in combatting climate change and ensuring sustainable development for the Filipino people. In the same light, the BSP joined the “Green Force" to support the creation of a harmonized and cohesive plan in mainstreaming green and sustainable finance in the country. Last March 15, 2021, the Philippine financial sector regulators and authorities led by the DOF signed a Memorandum of Understanding with the British Embassy to collaborate on the implementation of proposed interventions under the United Kingdom Prosperity Fund's ASEAN Economic Reform Programme and the ASEAN Low Carbon Energy Programme. Respectively, these programs aim to promote economic and sustainable development as well as improve energy security and access to clean energy in the country. The program likewise supports the Green Force's initiatives in building capacity of relevant government agencies and industry players with regards to sustainable finance implementation and in crafting a Sustainable Finance Roadmap and principles-based taxonomy. The development of the taxonomy is a key initiative aimed at providing guidance on identifying economic activities that contribute to sustainable development, with special focus on addressing climate change impact. Central to the climate change mitigation goal is the need to reduce greenhouse gas concentrations by shifting fossil fuels to renewable sources. We should note, however, that a successful transition is not just a matter of isolated changes in the energy sector. We must also consider the potential risks associated with this transition given the interplay among economic activities. According to some experts, “transitions are systemic in nature, characterized by co-evolution between different actors, institutions, supply and distribution chains," among others. A low-carbon transition could trigger a chain reaction that might affect the performance and viability of various loan and investment portfolios, which eventually could pose risks to the stability of the financial system and to the real economy. In this light, a progressive approach to transition must be considered without compromising environmental sustainability. The finalization of the principles-based taxonomy will play a critical part here, particularly in opening the door for the inflows of capital to relevant economic activities. The BSP's Sustainable Finance Framework which was released in April 2020 encourages the offering of green and sustainable finance instruments to support such economic activities. But at the same time, the Framework safeguards the stability of the financial system against potentially significant and protracted impact of climate change and other environment related risks. Leveraging on good governance and effective risk management systems, the Framework also expects banks to embrace sustainability principles and take strategic, concrete, and progressive actions in response to the climate call. The BSP will be issuing its second-phase regulation to enable the banking industry to make safe and sound response to risks arising from the transition to a low-carbon economy. This supplementary issuance will provide granular expectations on the integration of climate change and other environmental and social risks in banks' credit and operational risk management frameworks. Banks may gradually consider the future implications of “stranded asset risk" in their credit portfolio. No doubt, the energy transition is a complex issue and comes with both risks and opportunities. We are, however, fortunate to have a remarkable set of expert panelists who are at the frontlines of energy transition to provide us with insights on the opportunities, challenges, and potential solutions to accelerate renewable energy and energy efficiency in the country, including financing thereof. On this note, allow me to end this remark by wishing you a successful and meaningful conversation. Thank you and stay safe. [1] https://www4.unfccc.int/sites/ndcstaging/PublishedDocuments/Philippines%20First/Philippines%20-%20NDC.pdf

Title: Message of BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno for the Groundbreaking Ceremony of the UnionBank Innovation Campus
Occasion: Groundbreaking Ceremony of the UnionBank Innovation Campus
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: Good morning, everyone! It is a pleasure to take part in the groundbreaking ceremony of the UnionBank Innovation Campus. We congratulate UnionBank for establishing this banking and fintech innovation and co-creation hub dedicated to developing game-changing solutions from empowered Filipino talents. Indeed, this initiative is both timely and remarkable. Timely because we need to harness our talent and skills to build back better and rise from adversity; and remarkable because of the challenges brought upon by the COVID-19 pandemic. Although the pandemic has seriously affected the country’s economy, we were able to fast-track our digital transformation. While we must follow health protocols to keep us safe from exposure to the virus, we still found ways to work around our current limitations. We have been able to move forward because we chose to innovate and embrace digitalization. The BSP has noted a steady rise in digital transactions in the country since the pandemic begun. Combined transactions by electronic fund transfer facilities PESONet and InstaPay were higher by 348 percent in volume and 170 percent in value in February 2021 respectively, compared to figures recorded during the same month in 2020. Last month, the BSP and the payment services industry launched the QR Ph Person-to-Merchant, or QR PH P2M, digital payment stream which is expected to bring broad and wide-ranging benefits to both consumers and businesses. This endeavor will provide Filipinos with a convenient and safe alternative to traditional payment methods that use cash and coins. I am happy to report that the volume and value of QR Ph person-to-person (P2P) payments in February this year also increased by 22 percent and 26 percent respectively, compared to the figure posted a month earlier. Let me take this opportunity to thank UnionBank for participating in this program as one of its pilot participants. QR Ph is among the identified payment use cases under the digital payments streams, one of the pillars of the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap or DPTR. With digital finance infrastructures and digital governance and standards as its two other pillars, this roadmap is expected to build an environment that is conducive to the digital transformation of the country’s payments system. We invite the UnionBank Innovation Campus to work on these critical areas and help the country achieve further digital transformation. With your support, I am certain that we will be able to further reform, innovate, and transform our payments ecosystem into one that is safe, efficient, and reliable. Again, I congratulate the UnionBank Innovation Campus for this pioneering initiative. On behalf of the BSP, I wish you the very best. Padayon, UnionBank Innovation Campus!​

Title: Statement of the BSP Governor and FSCC Chairman on the State of Financial Stability
Occasion: Meeting of the Financial Stability Coordinating Council (FSCC)
Speaker: BSP Governor and FSCC Chairman Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ The damage from COVID-19 continues to impair both the Filipino public as well as the general economy. Throughout the world, COVID-19 has created similar problems and the loss of incomes is the most common scar from this pandemic-cum-recession. Yet, there are continuing signs that the incremental effects are now significantly less than the sharp contraction in Q2 2020. But we are also mindful of COVID’s lingering ill-effects. These continuing effects are the nature of systemic risks. And not only do they define financial instability, but they can also amplify to create add-on risks against future socio-economic prospects. COVID-19 has curtailed expected incomes and this loss is permanent. This is the primary risk which feeds other risks. This is more so in sectors that depend on the traffic of consumers, such as retail trade, events, leisure and auxiliary activities, and on those in the less-formal sector which are heavily dependent on economic momentum to begin with. That “this crisis is different” speaks not only of the depth of the damage but also the breadth and variability of its impact across different stakeholders. While every jurisdiction has a scar from COVID-19, the scars differ from jurisdiction to jurisdiction. We welcome projections of positive global growth for 2021. I am sure that the world is looking forward to getting back on track. However, while all forecasts point to a better year in 2021, the world economy is recovering at multiple speeds. This itself is unfortunately creating spillover risks from the Advanced Economies (AEs) and into Emerging Markets and Developing Economies (EMDEs). These cross-border effects are disruptive to countries like the Philippines and create a layer of risks on top of the scars that COVID has already caused on the domestic economy. Regulators may look at many indicators, but we act with the public on top of our mind. To ensure that our stakeholders make well-informed decisions, it is incumbent on us regulators to continue our dialogue with our publics. This is where the Council steps in and offers a cross-cutting view of risks. We have a unique vantage point of seeing how all the elements interact and affect the welfare of the system. Everyone is eventually better off with this transparency in place. With a view towards the rest of the year, six risks (those in green) are assessed to be under control. They are, however, being continuously monitored and will be re-assessed as updated information becomes available. Two other risk facets are still work-in-progress (those in grey) while two other risks (those in yellow) are relatively more elevated as a result of the spillovers from other jurisdictions. The balance between income and debt is the key downside risk today. This has left borrowers vulnerable because their capacity to service their debts has been put at risk. Let me end by saying that what we’re facing is a different crisis. And the authorities will not hesitate to think out-of-the-box in order to sustain a well-functioning market. Read and access the Statement on the State of Financial Stability by the FSCC through this link: STATEMENT ON THE STATE OF FINANCIAL STABILITY

Title: Speech of BSP Governor E. Diokno for the Research Collaboration and Educational Exchanges and Training MOU Signing Event
Occasion: Research Collaboration and Educational Exchanges and Training MOU Signing Event
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Good afternoon. It is my pleasure to welcome our colleagues and partners from the Agricultural Credit Policy Council or ACPC and the Philippine-American Educational Foundation or PAEF to this event—Secretary William Dar, Executive Director Jocelyn Alma Badiola, Acting Honorary Chairman of the PAEF John Law and Chairperson of the PAEF Board Philip Roskamp. Good afternoon as well to the BSP officers and fellow BSP colleagues who are here physically or virtually in this event. I wish all of us good health and safety wherever we are today. Central banks have long benefitted from academic and scholarly contributions to monetary and finance-related policymaking and other aspects of central banking. Academics and independent researchers, and the institutions that they come from, have provided valuable guidance in theoretical and empirical foundations of central banking policies, as well as in the assessment of policies and structural reform measures that are critical to sustainable, long-term economic growth. Former President of the European Central Bank, Jean-Claude Trichet, expressed that the main goal of economic research in any central bank is to provide a strong conceptual and empirical basis for policymaking. He says that among its most important objectives are “…to increase knowledge of the functioning of [the] economy and to provide high-quality models, tools and analyses that are relevant for the conduct of monetary policy and the fulfilment of the other tasks and functions of the central bank.” At the same time, it has been pointed out that “The pertinent question is not whether to conduct research in central banks, but [rather], how to organize it, how much to invest in it and how to assure effective transmission of research outcomes into the policy decision-making process.” This underscores the need not only to promote the intellectual examination of relevant central banking issues, but also to disseminate the research to the wider community so that central banking policies can stand scrutiny. To make this happen, it is crucial to connect the research staff of central banks with other knowledgeable institutions, including the policy making institutions, and to undertake educational exchanges and trainings. Central banks have been supporting research in the academia and other research institutions in many ways. The BSP has always attached great value to interactions with the academe and with other research institutions. One example is the BSP Sterling Chairs established since 2006. This was subsequently upgraded in 2008 to become BSP-UP Centennial Professorial Chairs, with the aim of continuously supporting the research, teaching, training and other programs of the University of the Philippines System. It is envisioned to help maintain and promote a critical mass of productive research scholars in economics, statistics, business administration, finance, accounting, agriculture and other related fields at the UP system as part of the collaboration between the BSP and the academe. The BSP also conducts regular Environmental Scanning Exercises. These fora serve to foster open discussions between the BSP and other institutions on various critical and current issues, where experts from various disciplines, whether from the academe, from government or private research institutions, or independent resource persons with vast and practical experience in banking, finance and other areas pertinent to the issues being discussed are invited. In strengthening the research capacity of the BSP, we recently created the BSP Research Academy. It is our vision that BSP Academy researchers will foster the research culture in the BSP by promoting and conducting objective, rigorous, independent and policy-relevant research at the BSP. We hope that they will achieve the level of objectivity and rigor as academics. We expect the BSP researchers to be committed to examining issues independently and effectively, with the view to coming up with rigorous research that can inform policy formulation and decisions, as well as engage in blue skies or basic research that can help the BSP be future ready. And this is where the importance of collaboration comes into play. To date, the BSP has drawn up Memoranda of Understanding (MOU) on research collaboration with the Philippine Institute for Development Studies (PIDS) and the UP School of Economics - Philippine Center for Economic Development (UPSE-PCED). An MOU on research collaboration with the Agricultural Credit Policy Council is expected to enrich our understanding of the agricultural credit and rural finance-related issues and policies. A Memorandum of Understanding on educational exchanges and trainings with the Philippine - American Educational Foundation – Fulbright is envisioned to promote the BSP’s thrust to improve the legal, scientific and technical capabilities of its officials and staff working in the central banking through the grant of scholarships and training programs and for facilitating educational exchanges under the BSP’s capacity-building and research initiatives. I am happy to note that the BSP has benefited from PAEF scholarships. Three of the BSP-PAEF Fulbright alumni are joining us virtually - Managing Director Antonio Lambino, Atty. Tanya Recalde, and Mr. Jeremy de Jesus. Finally, let me express my gratitude to our two new partners: the Agricultural Credit Policy Council and the Philippine - American Educational Foundation, in agreeing to participate in this valuable and noteworthy endeavor. May this mark the beginning of a long and productive and collegial relationship towards building a body of high-quality research for our institutions and for the nation. Maraming Salamat at mabuhay tayong lahat!

Title: Message of Governor Benjamin E. Diokno for the COP26 Climate Change Defenders Roundtable Discussion
Occasion: COP26 Climate Change Defenders Roundtable Discussion
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: His Excellency Daniel Robert Pruce, British Ambassador to the Philippines, Deputy Speaker Loren Legarda of the House of Representatives, Department of Finance (DOF) Secretary Carlos Dominguez, fellow COP26 Climate Defenders, ladies and gentlemen, a pleasant morning. Thank you to the British Embassy Manila for organizing this gathering of the Climate Defenders and for inviting the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas to be one of them. On our end, the BSP and financial institutions play a crucial part in mobilizing funds to finance green and sustainable projects, and at the same time, in safeguarding financial stability from shocks coming from climate and other environment-related factors. In line with this, the BSP issued the Sustainable Finance Framework in April 2020 which expects banks to embed sustainability principles in their corporate and risk management systems, business strategies and operations. It likewise complements the Securities and Exchange Commission’s guidelines pertaining to Green, Social or Sustainability Bonds issuance. Earlier, the BSP has strengthened governance standards on treasury activities and streamlined processes to allow for more bond issuances of BSP supervised financial institutions and to contribute to the accelerated growth of the capital markets. In fact, a total of more than USD 1.0 billion and PHP 85.4 billion worth of green, social, and sustainability bonds were already issued by ‘first mover’ banks since 2017. We expect more banks to follow suit as enabling regulations had been laid down complemented by continued capacity building activities for the industry. To advocate sustainability in the financial system, the BSP has also adopted the Sustainable Central Banking Program as one of its strategic priorities. Bank-wide technical working groups are conducting vulnerability assessment on the impact of climate change to BSP offices and branches. A gap analysis is also ongoing covering key functions or operations of the BSP such as monetary policy, supervision, reserve management, and currency production. The results of which will be used in drawing our Sustainable Central Banking roadmap which we aim to release a snapshot by the third quarter of this year. Complementary to this program, we launched the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap last year to accelerate digitalization in the financial sector. All these initiatives will be more meaningful and effective as we strengthen collaboration with local financial sector regulators, key government agencies through the Green Force, and with our counterpart foreign regulators and supervisors at the Network for Greening the Financial System, the ASEAN, EMEAP and Alliance for Financial Inclusion. This is to maintain momentum in mainstreaming sustainable finance and ensuring “just transition” towards a resilient, inclusive, and low-carbon economy. While every Filipino has a part to play in our sustainability agenda, the Climate Defenders are at the forefront of this very important task. Every contribution, big or small, will not be left unnoticed. As I close, let me wish everyone a successful and worthwhile discussion today. Thank you.

Title: Policymaking in the Time of COVID-19: Insights from BSP’s Experience
Occasion: UBS OneASEAN Conference
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ To the officers and staff of UBS, and to all the participants of this virtual conference, a pleasant afternoon. As the Philippines continues the battle against COVID-19 at the tail-end of this crisis, the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) remains one with the government and the entire Filipino people in pushing the economy toward full recovery. My presentation will be divided into three parts: • First – the BSP’s policy interventions amid the pandemic; • Second – policy challenges faced by the BSP and our responses; and • Third – outlook for the Philippine economy On policy interventions… Ample monetary and fiscal space enabled the Philippines to aggressively respond to the crisis. On the fiscal side, the government debt-to-GDP ratio was only 39.6 percent back in 2019. The bulk or 68 percent of government debt was denominated in local currency. Moreover, with fiscal prudence and tax reforms, revenue effort and tax effort rose to 16.1 and 14.5 percent, respectively, the highest level since 1998. Amid this backdrop, the government had resources to implement Bayanihan I and II, the laws on wide-ranging COVID-response measures, including relief for the most vulnerable. On the monetary front, the BSP had ample policy space, which was in part a result of a tightening cycle in 2018 -- when it raised the policy rate by a total of 175 basis points to 4.75 percent -- to rein in inflation pressures and anchor the public’s inflation expectations. The monetary space allowed the BSP to implement a series of unprecedented easing and liquidity-enhancing measures to mitigate the economic impact of the pandemic. The BSP’s COVID interventions fall under three categories: First were measures to boost market confidence on availability of credit resources. The BSP reduced the policy rate by a total of 200 basis points (bps) to help bring down the cost of borrowing, shore up market confidence, and address liquidity concerns in the financial system. The BSP also cut the reserve requirement to increase the volume of loanable funds. The BSP’s COVID interventions fall under three categories: First were measures to boost market confidence on availability of credit resources. The BSP reduced the policy rate by a total of 200 basis points (bps) to help bring down the cost of borrowing, shore up market confidence, and address liquidity concerns in the financial system. The BSP also cut the reserve requirement to increase the volume of loanable funds. Third were regulatory and operational relief measures to maintain stability of the financial system and ensure public access to financial services. We counted loans to MSMEs as compliance to the reserve requirement, increased the single borrower’s limit, and raised the ceiling for real-estate loans. We also excluded some loans from the “past-due” and “non-performing” classification and allowed grace period for loan settlement and restructuring of rediscounted loans. Such moves provided relief to banks and their borrowers. Since the onset of the pandemic, the BSP has injected about ₱2 trillion, or US$45 billion, equivalent to 12.1 percent of GDP, to the financial system through its liquidity-enhancing measures. Moving on to policy challenges and responses of the BSP… Among the challenges we face are related to the communication aspects of our policy responses, particularly: (i) on achieving clarity on rationale of policy measures; (ii) underscoring the BSP’s complementary role in the National Government’s pandemic response; and (iii) exit strategy. Over the past year, the BSP has made it clear that measures such as the repo agreement with the National Government were intended to complement the latter’s broad-based public health and fiscal programs against COVID-19. Let me emphasize that BSP’ support measures are temporary, time-bound, and capped. These will not cause a permanent expansion of the monetary base nor of the BSP’s balance sheet. The BSP also has reiterated that because of the core nature of the shock, which is a health crisis, the primary response necessarily involved public health measures and fiscal-side initiatives, including relief assistance to the vulnerable. Monetary policy, therefore, plays mainly a complementary role. Moving forward, we anticipate the communication challenge related to the exit strategy from the accommodative monetary policy, as economic recovery gets underway. We want to be very clear about our approach to future disengagement from our stimulus measures. The BSP will aim to gradually reduce policy stimulus, careful not to unwind either too early or too late. The timing of the exit strategy will primarily depend on the evolution of various factors. A crucial consideration will be the strength of domestic demand, which in turn will depend on the sustained decline in virus transmission and sufficient deployment of vaccines. Successful efforts on the public health front combined with targeted fiscal support will help boost consumer and business sentiment and lead to faster and sustained economic recovery. The BSP is also on the lookout for emerging risks to financial stability, including the buildup of imbalances in asset markets. Various macroprudential measures and monitoring tools remain in place to help safeguard against excessive risk-taking behavior. We also look at external risk factors, including global economic growth and shifts in monetary policy in major economies. We assess how they can impact domestic financial and growth conditions. Another challenge is on the transmission of monetary policy. As monetary policy works with a lag, there is much room for transmission of accommodative financing conditions to credit demand. Preliminary data shows that domestic liquidity (M3) grew by 8.3 percent in March and 5.1 percent in April, thus settling below trend. However, having said this, the continued rollout of the vaccination program, together with the wide-ranging fiscal and monetary measures to spur recovery and growth, economic activities and, therefore, credit demand are expected to revitalize the economy moving forward. We also note that the full recovery of the economy as well as the prevention of permanent scarring effects require that the heavy lifting should come primarily from the fiscal side, as they are able to implement more targeted policy responses. Nonetheless, the BSP’s liquidity-enhancing and accommodative monetary policy measures continue to provide significant support to the financial system. The BSP will continue to support the National Government amid health crisis by maintaining an accommodative monetary policy for as long as necessary until economic recovery becomes fully sustainable, while staying vigilant against emerging risks to its price and financial stability objectives. Looking at market interest rates, the 7-day and 14-day TDF yields, as well as the 28-day BSP bill rate, continue to be near the floor of the corridor amid ample liquidity in the financial system. Market participants also continue to have strong demand for the BSP’s deposit facilities. Having said this, the inflation environment remains manageable. The recent uptick in inflation is driven by transitory supply-side factors. The latest BSP inflation forecasts show that inflation is likely to settle at 3.9 percent this year and 3.0 percent next year, both within the target of 2.0 to 4.0 percent. Nevertheless, the BSP remains vigilant against risks to the inflation outlook. Should there be signs of spillover to the demand side in the form of wage- and fare-hike petitions or elevated inflation expectations, the BSP will assess the need for appropriate monetary policy response. At the same time, the BSP continues to support various non-monetary interventions of the National Government that will further mitigate the impact of supply-side factors on inflation. Moving on to the outlook on the Philippine economy. The government projects that the economy will expand by 6.0 to 7.0 percent this year. Gross domestic product (GDP) is expected to grow by 7.0 to 9.0 percent next year, thus return to its pre-COVID level, and continue to grow by 6.0 to 7.0 percent in 2023 and 2024. Meanwhile, the recent positive trends in global trade should push Philippine goods exports and imports in a recovery path. Broad and convenient access to financial services, which is among the goals of the BSP, will spur growth and recovery. Last year, we launched the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap, under which the BSP envisions that half of financial transactions would be done digitally and at least 70 percent of Filipino adults will have financial accounts by 2023. With financial digitalization, access to financial products and services become accessible to more people, thereby accelerating financial inclusion and broad economic growth. The BSP is one with the national government in pursuing a future for the Philippines that is much better than its pre-COVID state—a stronger, more digitalized, and more inclusive economy. In closing, I would like to highlight three key take-aways: o First, our fundamentals remain solid. These—together with the various policy responses and the vaccinations—will carry the Philippines through toward recovery. o Second, fiscal policy remains the key response to providing targeted support during the pandemic and preventing long-term economic scarring. o And third, monetary policy will continue to support domestic demand until recovery gets fully underway. The BSP stands ready to do more, if necessary, consistent with its price and financial stability mandates. Thank you for your attention and I look forward to our Q&A session. ​

Title: Keynote Message of BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno for the 18th Citi Microentrepreneurship Awards Virtual Awarding Ceremony
Occasion: 18th Citi Microentrepreneurship Awards Virtual Awarding Ceremony
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: Ladies and gentlemen: good morning and welcome to the 18th Citi Microentrepreneur Awards ceremony. Today is the culmination of our search for outstanding Filipino microentrepreneurs. It also marks a milestone, as we launched the 2020 run of the Citi Microentrepreneurship Awards virtually – a first in the awards program’s history. We take this occasion to recognize our microentrepreneurs who, despite the odds, have shown great resourcefulness, courage, and resilience to flourish amid the COVID-19 pandemic. They bring a message of hope and perseverance in these challenging times. As you all know, vaccination has started in the country, and approximately 6 million Filipinos have been vaccinated. With more citizens getting jabbed, we move closer to the full re-opening of the economy. In this light, I would like to share with you exciting developments which can greatly help our microentrepreneurs and the microfinance industry not only now, but more so in in the years ahead. The rollout of Philsys is in full swing with more than eleven million (11,439,214) registrants completing the collection of both demographic and biometric data process. The national ID will transform how services are delivered and accessed in the Philippines. Its online authentication and e-KYC will enable a more seamless digital onboarding, making it easier for clients to open an account. Aside from supporting massive onboarding, PhilSys can also quicken innovation and effective digitalization of various financial services, including credit and insurance. This will reduce the costs of financial transactions for microentrepreneurs and microfinance institutions alike. I also am happy to share with you that the president just signed the Executive Order 127 last March 10, 2021. The EO 127 liberalizes access to satellite technology, allowing more players to offer satellite broadband services. It is envisioned to fast-track development of internet infrastructure in underserved rural areas. With improved internet connectivity, financial service providers will be able to better serve these areas with wider access points such as ATMs and cash agents, among others. Microentrepreneurs, in turn, will be able to use more financial services to grow their businesses. For its part, the BSP is ramping up efforts to promote digital payments and improve credit access of microentrepreneurs. We launched the QR Ph P2P in November 2019 to enable digital fund transfers between peers or individual end-users. Drawing on the successful launch and adoption of the QR Ph P2P facility, we launched last April QR Ph P2M. This stream extends the payment use case of QR Ph to enable digital payments between customers and merchants. Using QR technology, microentrepreneurs need not invest in costly equipment, such as point of sale terminals, to capture electronic data. Instead, merchants only need to print the QR code that their respective payment service providers will generate for them, display those codes, and obtain payments by simply asking their customers to scan the codes. Part of our institutionalized support to the microenterprises and their cooperatives is the credit surety fund or the CSF program. Our goal is to strengthen the role of the CSF as a catalyst for inclusive economic opportunities in the communities they serve. BSP is aware that a deeper understanding of the needs and profile of the country’s MSMEs can support well-informed policy interventions and financial product development. Thus, in partnership with the Asian Development Bank, we have launched a national demand-side MSME survey, which is expected be completed before the end of the year. We are also supporting the conduct of a supply chain finance market development study as part of our efforts to promote supply chain and value chain financing as innovative lending approaches that can benefit our MSMEs. We also understand that part of the difficulties faced by MSMEs in accessing bank loans is having to fill out long and intimidating application forms. Thus, with support from the various banking industry associations and the Department of Trade and Industry, the BSP is spearheading an initiative that aims to introduce a simpler and borrower-friendly standard loan application form that will be adopted by participating banks as part of their commitment to support the MSMEs. These are just a few of our initiatives that we hope will give our microentrepreneurs with enough footing not only to weather the pandemic, but also to take advantage of opportunities in the new economy. Clearly, these initiatives would be not be possible without our stakeholder collaboration. The Citi Microentrepreneurship Awards, our enduring partnership with Citi Foundation and Microfinance Council of the Philippines, attests to this. While the pandemic has posed difficulties, it introduced a new dimension to the awards program. We are glad to know that, through the program, past winners were able to receive much needed recovery assistance. We also recognize the dedicated service to clients and selfless work of our microfinance institutions in advancing inclusive development amid the pandemic. We owe much of the achievement of our advocacy to you. At sa ating mga microentrepreneurs, isang malugod na pagbati sa inyong pagpanalo. Ang inyong tiyaga, tapang, at taos-pusong pagtulong sa kapwa sa panahon ng pandemya ay tunay na kahangahanga. Kayo ay nagsisilbing simbolo ng pag-asa para sa mga Pilipino. Finally, we thank the National Selection Committee, microfinance institutions, and our partners for their continued commitment to this Citi Microcroentrepreneurship awards program. Let us continue working together to uplift the lives of our kababayan. Again, my warmest congratulations to our winners. Thank you and I wish you all a good day.

Title: Paving the Path to Recovery, Safeguarding Macroeconomic Stability
Occasion: EJAP’s Macroeconomic Forum
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ EJAP President, Mr. Gerard de la Pena; members of EJAP; my fellow government officials; and to everyone joining us online, good morning. It is my pleasure to be with you in today’s virtual forum. In line with the theme of this year’s macroeconomic forum, let me share some insights on the global and Philippine economic outlook for 2021, some updates on the Philippines’ economic performance and the BSP’s policy actions to support the national government’s whole-of-nation approach in addressing the current crisis. Let me begin with a quick overview of the global and regional growth prospects, which point to an improved but differentiated and diverging recoveries. The IMF’s global growth projections improved to 6.0 percent for 2021 and 4.4 percent for 2022. Policy support and vaccines are expected to lend support to this expected economic recovery. The IMF also expects greater economic scarring, particularly among emerging and developing economies due to the prevalence of small businesses and shallow capital markets. ASEAN-5 economies are seen to rebound in 2021, reflective of the projected broad-based recovery of Vietnam, Malaysia, the Philippines, Indonesia, and Thailand. Multilateral agencies expect the Philippine economy to expand by 4.5 percent to 6.9 percent this year. These are broadly in line with the national government’s projection of 6.0 to 7.0 percent for 2021. Despite the challenges caused by the pandemic, the country also has a favorable medium-term growth prospects based on the stable outlook affirmed by major credit rating agencies. The economy was able to register sustained improvements on a quarter-on-quarter basis. From the 17.0 percent contraction recorded in the second quarter of 2020, the peak of the lockdown, the Philippine economy managed to post smaller declines in the succeeding quarters, with only -4.2 percent in Q1 2021. Without seasonality, the country has been posting positive quarter-on-quarter economic growth since the third quarter of 2020. This improvement is expected to continue in the succeeding quarters of 2021 with more massive roll out of the COVID-19 vaccines and a more strategic implementation of community restrictions. Business sentiments are up. Based on the latest business expectations survey, business confidence for Q1 2021 improved—rising to 17.4 percent from 10.6 percent in Q4 2020. Business outlook on the economy is more optimistic for the next quarter at 42.8 percent and for the next 12 months, at 60.5 percent, both higher than previous quarter’s survey results. The survey on consumer confidence in Q4 2020 indicates that consumer confidence for Q1 2021 likewise improved to 4.3 percent from -4.1 percent in the previous survey. For the next 12 months, consumer sentiment remains optimistic. The inflation environment remains manageable. While inflation increased to 4.7 percent in February 2021, this slowed down to 4.5 percent in March, April, and May. The latest number is consistent with the BSP’s assessment that the uptick in inflation in the first half of 2021 is transitory. This is because of supply-side pressures related to the African Swine Fever, weather-related disturbances, higher global oil prices, and positive base effects. Nevertheless, our latest inflation forecasts indicate that average inflation is likely to settle at 3.9 percent in 2021 and 3.0 percent in 2022 – both within the 2 to 4 percent target range. The improvement in 2021 is due to expected lower price pressures on food commodities owing to improved weather conditions, the implementation of Executive Order Nos. 128 and 133, and other non-monetary interventions to alleviate supply constraints. Risks to the inflation outlook appear to be broadly balanced. But the timely implementation of approved non-monetary measures could mitigate further supply-side pressure. In any event, analysts expect inflation to remain elevated in 2021 but to fall within the government’s 2 to 4 percent target range in 2022. For this year, analysts expect that risks to the inflation outlook will remain tilted to the upside due to supply disruptions and rising global oil prices. The country’s external position also continued to hold up well despite the deficit in the overall balance of payments (BOP) in the first four months of the year. This was due largely to pick up in trade deficit as the economy recovers. The BOP position reflects an increase in the gross international reserves (GIR) to US$107.7 billion as of end-April 2021. This level is equivalent to a year’s worth of import cover; the received doctrine is that three months’ worth of import cover is sufficient. GIR is also 7.4 times the country’s short-term external debt on original maturity. We also have ample fiscal space to tap further international financing, as needed. The country’s external debt-to-GDP ratio increased to 27.2 percent in end-2020, mainly due to pandemic-related financing needs of the national government. However, this remains at prudent level. In addition, a large part of the country’s external debt has medium-and-long term maturity profile, which supports a manageable debt repayment schedule. Moreover, a majority of these foreign borrowings carry fixed interest rates, rendering them not susceptible to global interest rates volatilities or foreign exchange fluctuations. Cash remittances from overseas Filipinos (OFs) has been resilient and continues to be strong. For the first three months of 2021, cash remittances amounted to US$7.6 billion. This is an increase of 2.6 percent, faster than the 1.4 percent that was posted in the same period last year. Meanwhile, BPO services in 2020 remained strong despite the disruption to business activities all over the world. In 2020, BPO earnings totaled US$22.4 billion, only 1.3 percent lower than the US$22.7 billion in 2019. The Philippine peso remained one of the strongest currencies in the region. This reflects in part the adequate level of the country’s international reserves and improving market sentiment. Finally, the banking system is strong and sound. This is one of the reasons why the rating agencies have continued to affirm a stable outlook for the country. Notwithstanding the various operational and market challenges encountered during the pandemic, the banking industry remained strong and financially healthy. It continues to be an efficient and responsible intermediator of funds. The banks’ capital adequacy ratio of 16.6 percent on solo basis and 17.1 percent on consolidated basis, as of end-December 2020, are above both standards set by the BSP and the Bank for International Settlements. Meanwhile, the rise in bad debt also remains manageable. Non-performing loans (NPL) and non-performing assets (NPA) ratios were modest at 3.9 percent and 2.5 percent, respectively as of end-April 2021, compared to the double-digit levels recorded during the Asian Financial crisis. Thus, given these factors and barring yet-unforeseen growth speed bumps, the Philippines has the essential elements to rebound this year. However, I do believe there is still a high level of uncertainty, both domestic and abroad. The government has to be more strategic in addressing possible resurgence in cases and in expediting vaccination. We have to be ready for the possible spillover risks resulting from differentiated and divergent recoveries. We need robust institutions and sustained policy discipline to see us through this COVID fog. Moving forward, the BSP will continue to promote price and financial stability. On monetary policy: the BSP will remain vigilant over the current inflation dynamics to ensure that the monetary policy stance continues to support economic recovery to the extent that the inflation outlook would allow. We will carefully scan the economic landscape with a forward-looking perspective, to move preemptively to address any risks to our price stability mandate. On the financial sector: the BSP will intensify its monitoring and surveillance over its supervised institutions to ensure that they remain responsive to emerging risks and to promote the continued soundness, stability, resilience and inclusivity of the banking system, particularly through the pursuit of enhanced digitalization. Third, on the external sector: the BSP will support policies that will help build the economy’s resilience to external shocks, including that of maintaining a market-determined exchange rate, keeping a comfortable level of reserves, and keeping the country’s external debt manageable. Finally, allow me to end my presentation with these key points: 1. Recent assessment by the IMF indicates that world economy is improving in 2021. Greater vaccine deployment and sustained fiscal policy support are expected to lend support to this prospect for the year. However, recovery is divergent, with significant downside risks. Greater scarring may be seen among the emerging and developing economies. 2. The Philippines is expected to recover in 2021 and recent economic indicators support this. 3. Positive economic outlook for the country requires strong institutions and policy discipline. The BSP contributes by remaining committed to its mandates. The BSP also supports the National Government with its inclusive growth goals. Thank you very much.

Title: Opening Remarks of BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno during The BSP Corporate Financial Trends Survey (CFTS) 2021 CFTS Information Session
Occasion: BSP Corporate Financial Trends Survey (CFTS) 2021 CFTS Information Session
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Our partner respondents from the private sector, guests, colleagues, ladies and gentlemen, good morning! It gives me great pleasure to welcome all of you in this important event – The BSP Corporate Financial Trends Survey (CFTS) Information Session. I say important because this endeavor aims to address one of the most pressing issues that policymakers are facing today, the need for timely, reliable, granular and accurate financial data on the country’s corporate sector. As most of you here would agree, a necessary condition for any sound decision is getting the right information. Our ability to formulate the appropriate policy largely depends on our capacity to correctly identify causal relationship between two or more variables. This, in turn, depends on our ability to access and generate relevant, timely and accurate statistics. In this regard, the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) conducts the CFTS to gather financial data from the domestic economy’s important driver of growth – the corporate sector. Our goal is to improve our understanding of the recent financial trends and conditions of various industries. In particular, the CFTS aims to gather more granular data on corporates’ sources of funds, spending activities, and profitability. This survey helps address some of the data gaps the BSP encounters due to long lags in financial reporting of private firms. The availability of more granular data enhances our capacity to undertake the macrofinancial surveillance necessary in pursuing policies that foster monetary and financial stability. The BSP recognizes that monetary policy causes changes in the macroeconomic and financial market conditions, which in turn, affect corporates’ borrowing behavior, investments and production activities. Thus, having information on the corporate production and leverage allows us to effectively assess the transmission mechanism of monetary policy and its impact on the real economy. Moreover, developments in the corporate sector could have important implications on the overall stability of the financial system. Literature and experience have shown that balance sheet weaknesses and excessive leverage in the corporate sector could exacerbate the costs of economic and financial crises. For instance, in the aftermath of the Global Financial Crisis (GFC), corporate balance sheet weaknesses and high levels of debt contributed to a more sluggish and protracted recovery of the global economy. Clearly, the stability of the corporate sector is vital to the stability of the whole economy. The sector accounts for a large share of employment and output. As such, the monitoring and understanding of the financial conditions of corporations are vital in the early detection of weaknesses in the sector and in the prevention of these weaknesses to develop into a full-blown crisis. The information we gather from the survey would allow us to identify potential vulnerabilities of the corporate sector. In turn, these could lead to the formulation of policies that would help safeguard macroeconomic and financial stability. This is particularly important as the domestic economy, including the corporate sector, continues to grapple with the challenges of the Covid-19 pandemic. Our preliminary assessment indicates that the disruptions in business operations due to the COVID-19 pandemic and the lockdown measures implemented by the National Government (NG) resulted to a sharp fall in corporate income. In turn, this weakened markedly the ability of firms to service their debt. Based on available 2020 annual reports of PSE-listed firms, the exposure of corporates to the pandemic resulted in some sectors showing vulnerabilities in their operating performance, liquidity and leverage. In terms of profitability, most firms from industrial, holdings, services and financial sectors reported declines in revenues. The 2020 GDP growth numbers show that many sectors, such as, accommodation and food service, transportation and storage, mining and quarrying, real estate, construction, manufacturing, education, and professional and business services have contracted. Lower corporate incomes could potentially expose firms to balance sheet weaknesses and vulnerabilities such as increased leverage and reduced ability to service debts. It is therefore imperative that regulators closely monitor the borrowing behavior of corporates and their capability to service their existing loans during the pandemic. This is to allow us to accurately assess the magnitude and extent of the pandemic’s impact on the country’s corporate sector, and in turn, measure the corporate sector’s spillovers and spillbacks to the financial system and the economy.
