Moving forward, the conduct of the Corporate Financial Trends Survey will serve as an important tool to accurately assess the state of the country’s corporate sector. The survey would allow the BSP to conduct a more intensified surveillance to enhance its monitoring of corporate financial conditions in a more comprehensive and timely manner. Moreover, results of the survey will enhance the BSP’s assessment of various corporate exposures and vulnerabilities and their sensitivity to shocks such as supply-side disruptions, and changes in financial market conditions. Indeed, the CFTS is a step further to developing a corporate sector database necessary for formulating appropriate policies that help promote a stronger and more stable macroeconomy and financial system. In closing, I thank all our participants for attending this information session and for your invaluable support to this important undertaking. Welcome once again and good morning. ​​​

Title: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno's Message of Support for the RBAP and RCBC MOU Signing
Occasion: RBAP and RCBC MOU Signing
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: The BSP welcomes the collaboration of RCBC and RBAP using the BANKONNECT, an innovative product that will help boost current efforts towards digitalization of financial services and further increase financial inclusion. As you all know, the BSP is proactively working on these two objectives prominently indicated in our Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap (DPTR) for 2020-2023. Using the Roadmap, we would like to achieve strengthened customer preference for digital payments, which we intend to pursue by increasing the volume of digital payments to 50%, and for 70% of the adult population to own a transaction account in order to see financial inclusion for a great majority of the population. We also advocate to see more innovative and responsive digital financial services, which will be achieved by fostering the innovation-driven use of consumer data for product development. The BANKONNECT, a “banking-as-a-service” (BaaS) digital platform, suitably responds to our DPTR objectives, and is truly a welcome concerted effort from RCBC and the rural banking industry. It is also a testament to RCBC’s continuous support of the BSP's efforts to modernize the digital retail payments landscape since the launch of the National Retail Payment System several years back. This laudable effort towards digital transformation has also been recognized internationally in 2020 as RCBC was named as the Philippines’ “Most Innovative Internet Banking Service Provider” by the London-based, The Global Economics Limited, and the country’s “Best Digital Bank” by the Hong Kong-based institutional investment magazine Alpha Southeast Asia. The BSP recognizes that innovation is truly imperative for today’s banking. There are growing operational cost pressures coming from running a day-to-day business, intense competition within the industry, and fast-changing customer preferences and expectations. The BSP welcomes the collaboration of RCBC and RBAP using the BANKONNECT, an innovative product that will help boost current efforts towards digitalization of financial services and further increase financial inclusion. As you all know, the BSP is proactively working on these two objectives prominently indicated in our Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap (DPTR) for 2020-2023. Using the Roadmap, we would like to achieve strengthened customer preference for digital payments, which we intend to pursue by increasing the volume of digital payments to 50%, and for 70% of the adult population to own a transaction account in order to see financial inclusion for a great majority of the population. We also advocate to see more innovative and responsive digital financial services, which will be achieved by fostering the innovation-driven use of consumer data for product development. The BANKONNECT, a “banking-as-a-service” (BaaS) digital platform, suitably responds to our DPTR objectives, and is truly a welcome concerted effort from RCBC and the rural banking industry. It is also a testament to RCBC’s continuous support of the BSP's efforts to modernize the digital retail payments landscape since the launch of the National Retail Payment System several years back. This laudable effort towards digital transformation has also been recognized internationally in 2020 as RCBC was named as the Philippines’ “Most Innovative Internet Banking Service Provider” by the London-based, The Global Economics Limited, and the country’s “Best Digital Bank” by the Hong Kong-based institutional investment magazine Alpha Southeast Asia. The BSP recognizes that innovation is truly imperative for today’s banking. There are growing operational cost pressures coming from running a day-to-day business, intense competition within the industry, and fast-changing customer preferences and expectations. As you know, the Covid-19 pandemic has also accelerated the public’s preference for contactless digital transactions, with consumers now less likely to go out of their way to brave traffic and health safety risks to do payments and banking transactions in person. These circumstances thus present an opportunity for consumers to more likely embrace digital payments services. A trend that is expected to last even after the pandemic. This significant development offers tremendous opportunities to transform banking better, particularly in terms of offering products and services that are customized to the specific needs and personalized banking solutions responsive to each customer’s preferences. Open banking is ushering in an era of productive collaboration between banks, non-banks, fintechs and consumers, as compared to the outdated model of competition among banks serving a market where products and services are definitive rather than responsive. The rapid rise of technology and the internet expanded the reach of the market for financial services, which rightly, now includes all income classes of the society. It has also altered the tastes and preferences of consumers, thus raising the bar of expectations and demand for more innovative and responsive financial services. This bodes well for smaller financial institutions like rural banks which can now take advantage of BaaS to increase their customer base, offer innovative products and services, as well as increase their revenues and profit. Open banking can enable ‘last mile’ providers like rural banks to realize their full potential as agents of financial inclusion. We are excited to see rural banks catering to the unique customer banking needs of a rural customer thru BANKONNECT. We will be more than happy to see rural banks provide customized financial products and services that are tailor-made to the business banking needs of micro, small and medium enterprises, particularly those engaged in agriculture and agri-value chains. We will be very supportive to see rural banks provide customer-centric banking services that will offer conveniences and safety in payments, remittances, and bills payments, among others. As Open Banking continues to develop, flourish and deliver results, it is useful to keep financial inclusion in mind. The BSP certainly welcomes collaborations in the financial services industry that will enable banks to work together in delivering innovative and responsive products and services for the unserved and underserved. Congratulations to RBAP and RCBC, and we look forward to the successful implementation of the BANKONNECT.​

Title: Message of BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno for the "Saya ng Buhay, Level App" Campaign
Occasion: konek2CARD FB Live Event
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ A good day to the members of CARD Mutually Reinforcing Institutions led by Founding Chairman, Dr. Jaime Alip, and to the officers, personnel, and clients of CARD Bank, Incorporated led by President and Chief Executive Officer, Ms. Marivic Austria. We extend our congratulations for reaching one million registered konek2CARD users. CARD Bank has been a longtime partner and champion of financial inclusion, and we are glad to mark this achievement with you today. The growth of digital financial services in general highlights the necessity and advantages of digitalization. For one, going digital enabled Filipinos to transact at the safety and convenience of their homes during this pandemic. We were fortunate to witness the beginnings of konek2CARD, along with digital cash machines and cash agent operations – all of which were on introductory rollout only a few years ago. The significant gains in a short period reveal the vast opportunities available to financial service providers when they maximize enabling policies and adopt an innovative, financial inclusion-oriented mindset. We thus set our goals higher and build on the momentum of the current digital wave. By adopting digital financial inclusion as a priority, we now focus on empowering Filipinos to ensure they thrive in the New Economy. Guided by the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap 2020-2023, we are advancing digitalization initiatives that will have practical benefits and transformative impact through and beyond the pandemic. Let me share some of them. With key government and industry partners, we recently launched the QR Ph Payment-to-Merchant pilot to boost digital payments and digitalization of our small businesses. We envision its usage to extend all the way to the low-income segment. Sa pagpapalawig ng paggamit ng QR Ph, maari tayong magbayad sa tindahan, palengke, pati ng pamasahe gamit ang ating cellphone. Hindi na kailangan ng barya sa umaga, sakto pa ang bayad. With the Philippine Identification System or PhilSys, every Filipino will have a reliable proof of identity. The PhilSys effectively dispenses with documentary requirements and makes the onboarding process faster and less costly. Sa pamamagitan ng PhilSys, maaring mag-avail ng bank account at ibang financial services gamit ang ating PhilID. Hindi na kailangan ng ibang ID. Mapapadali rin ang pagtanggap ng ayuda mula sa gobyerno. We recognize the need for reliable mobile and internet connection to bring financial services within easy reach of Filipinos, especially those in the last mile. Thus, we are pushing to mainstream technologies like satellite internet and open doors to more players in our broadband market. These reforms efforts specifically aim to make digital financial services affordable and accessible to the public. Layunin nating magkaroon ng maasahan at murang mobile and internet connection para maraming pang Pilipino, saan man sa bansa, ang mahikayat na gumamit ng digital financial services. While the COVID-19 pandemic has disrupted the way we live, work, and play, it also created exceptional opportunities to boost digital financial inclusion in the country. The phenomenal growth of the konek2CARD clientele amid the pandemic sends an unmistakeable message to the industry at large; And that is: financial inclusion makes good business sense. It also shows how digitalization can improve the lives of the common man. Think of a housewife who can now get micro-insurance with just a few clicks on the phone; or a farmer who can now check his account while working in the field.[*] Indeed, by embracing our financial inclusion mission, we can improve lives and create shared prosperity in ordinary and extraordinary times. And so, to the officers and personnel of CARD Bank, keep up the outstanding work. Thank you and good day. [*] Examples based on an actual interview of CARD Bank clients conducted in August 2019, in Barangay Manggahan, Dolores, Quezon Province.​

Title: One with the Nation: Forging Public-Private Partnerships toward Digital Financial Inclusion in the Philippines
Occasion: GCash Event
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ A good day to the organizers and participants. Our thanks to GCash for inviting us to this important event. Today’s webinar with the theme: “One with the Nation: Forging Public-Private Partnerships toward Digital Financial Inclusion in the Philippines” comes at an extraordinary but opportune time. The COVID-19 pandemic has led to unprecedented, adverse effects expected to persist for years to come. It has disrupted our economy and daily life. However, it has also opened a path of opportunities for broader inclusive development. One of these opportunities is accelerated digitalization of the financial services and their clients. Digitalization in the financial system can create a catalytic, transformative impact by making essential financial services less costly and more affordable for most people. The BSP has therefore made digitalization and digital financial inclusion a major and urgent priority, especially in light of the ongoing pandemic. From a macro perspective, digital financial inclusion creates a wider and diversified client base for financial services who not only can benefit from, but also contribute to, the system’s strength and dynamism. More people having access to welfare- and productivity-enhancing financial services can likewise boost livelihood opportunities and economic dynamism in communities across the country. To an individual, the benefits of digital financial inclusion are manifold. These would include: access to a wider range of financial services tailored to the customer’s needs; lowered transaction costs; better protection against fraud. All these represent greater empowerment for the financial consumers, especially the marginalized ones. Now we ask: can we as a country achieve digital financial inclusion? My short answer is yes. The 2019 Financial Inclusion Survey showed that even prior to the pandemic, account ownership, and with it - the use of digital financial services – has increased. Account ownership among Filipino adults rose from 23% in 2017 to 29% in 2019, translating to an additional 5 million Filipinos being financially included in the span of two years. More important, we saw an impressive increase in account ownership among the poorest or the socio-economic class “E” segment. From just 14% in 2017, it doubled to 27% in 2019 – a good indicator of this group’s readiness for digital finance. The uptake in e-money account ownership also grew from 1% in 2017 to 8% in 2019. The percentage of account holders who use their accounts for payment transactions also doubled – from just 18% to 39% in the same 2-year period. Indeed, we saw a rapid shift by customers, businesses and the government to digital payments. Cash withdrawals and check transactions significantly fell. While electronic fund transfers went up based on comparative date for 76 days before and during the enhanced community quarantine period. E-money also experienced a 61.6% growth in the number of active e-wallets at 14.2 million as of third quarter 2020, versus the 8.8 million level posted in 2019. We expect this growth to persist as more Filipinos transact online. These developments have been made possible by the foundations we have put in place for an inclusive digital finance ecosystem, which is characterized by three things: First is the democratization of account ownership through the no-frill, low-cost basic deposit account and e-money accounts. The BDA was introduced in 2018 to address the needs of the unbanked for an account with zero required maintaining balance, simple documentary requirements, and minimum opening deposit. As of fourth quarter 2020, 133 banks are offering BDA to 6.6 million depositors, up from 4 million depositors as of end-2019. There is also of course our pioneering e-money regulation, BSP Circular Number 649 issued in 2009, which led to the creation of non-bank e-money issuers such as G-Cash providing innovative mobile wallet services for the mass market. The second element is the expansion of access points, particularly for cashing in and out. These access points serve as a bridge that facilitate the transition of certain segments of consumers from cash transactions, to agent-assisted digital transactions, then to mobile financial transactions. This has been made possible through BSP’s agent regulations allowing banks and other financial service providers to tap retail outlets such as pharmacies, grocery stores, even pawnshops from which clients cash-in or cash-out from their BDA or E-money wallets. There are now more than 17,000 cash agents, more than 54,000 e-money agents, resulting in 91% of local government units being serve by at least one agent. The third key element of our inclusive digital finance ecosystem are the digital payment rails of Instapay and PESONet, which enable electronic fund transfers from one account to any account. As of end-April 2021, there are 82 participating financial service providers for PESONet and 52 for InstaPay. Building on these payment rails are the innovative payment solutions that we are now pursuing with industry, including the QR-code. QR code facilitates secure and cost-efficient solution for merchant digital payment acceptance as well as person-to-person or P2P real-time payments. What do all these mean for an individual - your constituents? The inclusive digital finance ecosystem has made opening of a transaction account – which is a basic step to financial inclusion – not only accessible and affordable but also compelling and practical, even for low-income individuals. Let’s imagine Aling Nena, a market vendor who previously does not have an account because aside from not having enough money to meet the maintaining balance, she did not really see the need to open an account because there is not enough money to save anyway. Why else would she need an account? Add to that the inconvenience of going to a bank branch during her store hours. But now because of e-money and BDA, agents and Instapay, Aling Nena finds that she can easily open an account even with just Php 100, in a convenience store just beside the wet market. In fact, she can open it by just downloading an app on her secondhand smartphone. She has also changed her mind about the necessity of owning an account. She now knows that having an account is useful and practical because with it, she can instantly pay her utility bills with a few clicks on her phone, and no longer having to waste time queuing up under the sun. She can even now invest in a money market fund for as little as Php 50 through her mobile account. And when she had to send money to her family in the province for emergency, she was able to do so instantly, anytime, anywhere. More important, as a market vendor, she now accepts digital payment from many of her ‘suki’ who prefer cashless transactions for health and security considerations. In fact, her sales have been going up! But that’s not the end of it. Aling Nena realized that with her digital payment transactions, she has been able to build a rich digital profile that a formal lender used to assess her credit worthiness. That’s why she was able to get micro-credit line at much better terms than what the 5-6 lenders offer. A mobile phone and an account have brought Aling Nena new opportunities and opened doors. We want to empower more Aling Nenas in the country. In fact, we have set as a target in our Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap. Our target is that by 2023, at least 70% of Filipino adults should have an account. This is an ambitious target but we are confident of hitting it. The remaining roadblocks are being addressed, not only by the BSP but also by financial inclusion advocates in government and the private sector. For instance, the the 20-member interagency Financial Inclusion Steering Committee chaired by the BSP helped successfully push for the issuance of Executive Order No. 127 which was signed by President Duterte in March 2021. The executive order is a policy reform that aims to address internet connectivity challenges in many unserved rural areas by liberalizing the satellite broadband market. With improved internet access, financial service providers can deploy and connect automated teller machines, agents, and other access points to serve more Filipinos, especially those in rural communities. Another critical digital gamechanger for financial inclusion is the Philippine Identification System or PhilSys. As a foundational ID system, PhilSys will not only address the long-standing documentary barriers faced by many unbanked but also enable seamless and cost-efficient digital onboarding. With the PhilSys online authentication and eKYC features, identity proofing will be done digitally without needing to manually verify, and photocopy identification documents. This will enable financial service providers to tap the low-income mass market as a viable and strategic segment. To encourage more people to open transaction accounts, it is not enough that we make it easy and affordable. Equally important is demonstrating account ownership as a basic necessity. This means we need to integrate the use of transaction account in their financial lives – whether for receiving emergency cash assistance from government or friends, or getting paid by their employers or customers. Thus, the BSP continues to build use cases that show the use of an account and shift the preference of the public from cash-based to account-based payment transactions. Toward this end, the BSP is collaborating with the Department of Social Welfare and Development (DSWD), Department of Labor and Employment (DOLE), and Department of Transportation (DOTr) to promote account-based transactions and digital payments in cash assistance distribution, wage payments, and public transport. These initiatives are supported by our financial and digital literacy campaigns. These campaigns are undertaken with various partners, including the Department of Education and the DSWD. Title: Welcome Remarks of BSP Gov. Benjamin E.Dioknio Asia for the School of Business Online Course for the FSCC
Occasion: Asia for the School of Business Online Course for the FSCC
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: Good morning to everyone and welcome to the first course structured by the Asia School of Business for the Financial Stability Coordination Council. I’m very pleased to welcome a dear friend of the BSP, former Negara Malaysia Governor DR. ZETI AKHTAR AZIS who now co-chairs the ASB. Let me also extend my warm greetings to MR. CHARLES FINE, President and Dean of ASB, DR. TRIWIT ARIYATHUGUN, Assistant Professor of Economics, DR. HANS GENBERG, Professor of Economics and Senior Director of Banking and Finance Program, and of course to my old friend who needs no introduction, DR. ELI REMOLONA, the Director of Central Banking at ASB. The Financial Stability Coordination Council has been actively looking at secondary market asset prices for signs about the market and their potential systemic risk concern. We have had active discussions about our yield curve and its incentive structure as market condition evolve, both in the Philippines and in established offshore markets. As part of our intent to better survey risk behaviors, we have been exploring various opportunities for learning, to enhance the capacity of our FSCC colleagues, and to address our mandate. The bar of accountability on that mandate has been raised, with the President signing Executive Order No. 144. With the legal authority to now issue guidelines and regulations related to managing systemic risks, it is important more than ever for the FSCC to see what market players see, and for us to influence risk behaviors in ways that develop markets and build resilience. This is why this course is important, particularly at this critical time. We hear of the divergent paths of recovery from Covid-19 and how movements in Advanced Economies are affecting Emerging Economies and Developing Economies. No matter how you work on the analysis, this divergence should be evident in market prices, alongside portfolio flows and reflows. The global economy is patently interconnected through the pipeline of bond and credit markets. While economists are comfortable with comparative advantage on the trade side, risk behaviors in financial markets are much more fluid, and respond more quickly to changing incentives. Those incentives, again, are all about visible prices in the open market and government securities provide the immediate natural platform. In this interconnected world, partnerships are critical. I am excited for the FSCC to build this partnership with the ASB. We leverage this market of experience and ideas from the best. Of particular interest to me is that ASB itself is a partnership that brings together the best talents and the broadest experience from different parts of the world. This puts the FSCC in an envious position. We are partnering with the best to better ourselves so that we too can, one day soon, be the best at what we do. This is not about status. It is about making FSCC an engine of improving the welfare of the Filipino public. This is the vision of the FSCC for why managing systemic risks and the pursuit of financial stability matter. Let me wish all the participants an enjoyable 10 hours of learning. Eli has not shared with me the answer key to his questions. But knowing him for so long, I assure our FSCC colleagues that you’re learning from the best in the field. Finally, let me thank those who patiently set up the logistics for getting this course off the ground. To LILY WONG of ASB, thank you for your patience. To our FSCC Secretariat, I look forward to seeing the fruits of this course in your next Systemic Risk Review. Thank you Governor ZETI, CHARLES, HANS, ELI AND TRIWIT. I welcomed everybody to this FIRST collaboration because I do hope to benefit from all succeeding partnerships between ASB and FSCC. Thank you and good morning to all.

Title: Keynote Speech of BSP Gov. Benjamin E. Diokno for the BSP Research Fair
Occasion: BSP Research Fair
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Good morning to our speakers and participants. Thank you for gracing this milestone event for the BSP, a timely testimonial of the BSP’s support for robust and evidence-based research, especially as we celebrate 72 years of central banking in the Philippines, and 28 years as the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas. This Research Fair represents many firsts for the BSP: It is our first International Research Fair organized by the recently established BSP Research Academy. As you know, we have a Main Conference for Day 1 and a less formal Research Café for Day 2, co-hosted with The SEACEN Centre. It is also the first completely virtual international conference organized by the BSP, where various researchers from local and international institutions are presenting their papers. This is also the first international conference of this nature we have organized since the onset of the pandemic. This year’s theme, “Central Banking in the Time of Pandemic” is the most pressing issue facing central banks today. The repercussions of the COVID-19 pandemic are unprecedented in our global economic history, even when compared to the Spanish Flu of 1918—the most devastating pandemic of all time in terms of the number of deaths. This is also because the COVID-19 pandemic has, in effect, caused a trio of shocks—a supply shock, a demand shock, and a shock to financial systems, as well. The COVID-19 pandemic is the first epidemic in the world’s history that has caused significant damage not only to people, the health sector and the macroeconomy, but also posed a considerable potential threat to the stability of financial sectors. Financial market reaction to the uncertainty and risks during the acute stages of the COVID-19 outbreak have led to significant strains in global financial markets. In the first quarter of 2020, there were sharp downfalls in the equity markets in advanced economies, as well as in emerging market economies. The speedy and substantial monetary and fiscal responses of policymakers have prevented a liquidity crisis, which would have had dire and pervasive economic and financial stability implications. Specifically, central banks reacted rapidly to market turmoil starting in March 2020 by adopting a wide-ranging set of emergency liquidity facilities and engaging in long-term government asset purchases. While monetary policy only plays a complementary role, it is a critical one. The BSP was among the central banks to be the first to respond to the crisis through the enactment of liquidity-enhancing measures. In fact, our policy rate cuts have amounted to a total of 200 basis points since March 2020. BSP’s accommodative monetary policies were aimed at alleviating any expected tightness in liquidity conditions, uplift business and consumer confidence, and help maintain the orderly functioning of the Philippine financial system. We also implemented some extraordinary measures including provisional advances to the National Government (NG) and the purchase of government securities in the secondary market. On the regulatory front, we implemented time-specific and well-targeted regulatory and operational relief measures to encourage BSP-supervised financial institutions to continue supporting the domestic economy. Specifically, the BSP reduced the reserve requirement rate and provided for alternative reserve compliance for lending to Micro- and Small and Medium Enterprises or MSMEs. We treated loans to MSMEs as compliance to the reserve requirement, increased the single borrower’s limit, and raised the ceiling for real-estate loans. We also allowed a grace period for loan settlement and restructuring of rediscounted loans. Such moves provided relief to banks and their borrowers. IMF Chief Economist Gita Gopinath, at the 3rd APEC Structural Reform Ministerial Meeting last June 16, 2021, in fact commended the Philippines as among the ASEAN-6 economies which effectively provided liquidity support to firms, including through loan moratoria and government guarantees. These various policies, she continued, have “…helped preserve future living standards, and benefit disproportionately lower-skilled workers and SMEs that would have been worse off otherwise.” Just as the BSP acted quickly to mitigate the economic and financial impact of the pandemic, and we are now carefully assessing the appropriate timing for the transitioning from our accommodative stance. We realize that doing this too late or too early could have serious repercussions on the economy. Success on the public health front combined with carefully targeted fiscal support will help boost consumer and business sentiment and lead the economy to its sustainable growth path. Building confidence in the fundamental strength of the Philippine economy in an environment of uncertainty and volatility requires skillful handling of our policy levers. Moving forward, we anticipate the communication challenges related to the scaling back of the accommodative monetary policies, when necessary, as economic recovery gets underway. Monetary policy needs to be flexible to shifts in economic and market conditions. As the BSP closely monitors any emerging risks to financial stability, various macroprudential measures and monitoring tools remain in place. We also look at external risk factors, including global economic growth and shifts in monetary policy in major economies, as we assess how they can impact domestic financial and growth conditions. More importantly, an integral part of the BSP’s policy agenda is making sure that the Philippines is able to get back on a balanced and sustainable growth path as soon as possible. In this regard, the BSP is working closely with the national government to advance the structural reform agenda, including transforming the financial landscape so that it is future-ready through digitalization and environmentally sustainable practices. Digital payments transformation has been helping us cope with the mobility restrictions put in place. It is a good thing that the country had been gradually embracing financial technology even before the pandemic hit. The rapid acceleration of digital transformation was in fact catalyzed by the COVID-19 pandemic. In 2020, the volume of PESONet transfers surged to 15.3 million, up by 376 percent year-on-year. The value of PESONet transactions rose by 188 percent, year-on-year, to reach nearly 951.6 billion pesos—equivalent to about 5.3 percent of the country's GDP. In the same period, the number of payments made through InstaPay reached 86.7 million, up by 459 percent year-on-year. Said transactions were valued at 463.4 billion pesos, which was a 340-percent increase year-on-year. This amount of InstaPay transactions is equivalent to about 2.6 percent of GDP. The BSP took this opportunity to advance initiatives that would push digitalization in the financial industry even further. To this end, launched the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap last year. The roadmap identifies two critical strategic objectives. The first objective, also my personal goal as BSP Governor—is that at least 50 percent of the country's total financial transactions be done digitally, and at least 70 percent of Filipino adults must have financial accounts. The second objective involves the availability of more innovative digital financial products and services. These products and services, designed to be responsive to consumers' needs, will be enabled by a digital PhilSys ID and supported by a next-generation payment and settlement system that facilitates the real-time processing of financial transactions. All these measures are integral parts of the strategy to become the central bank that understands the needs of the Filipino people and help them achieve their aspirations in life. While the ongoing crisis has subjected us to extraordinary challenges, it has also given us the opportunity to accelerate our efforts in bringing the BSP closer to the Filipino people. Through digitalization, we help create opportunities for them to improve their lives and participate in the formal sectors of the macroeconomy and the financial system. This way, we also promote financial inclusion, as digital payments help consumers confidently engage in economic activities with ease and security. In fact, in a report titled “Global Microscope 2020: The role of financial inclusion in the Covid-19 response” by the research arm of The Economist Group, the BSP was recognized for its initiatives to mitigate the adverse economic impact of the current global health emergency. In closing, I would like to highlight three strengths that we are pitting against the trio of shocks we are facing: • First, we continue to maintain strong macroeconomic fundamentals. Together with carefully thought-out public health responses and an efficient rolling out of the vaccination program, these will carry the Philippine economy toward sustainable recovery. Monetary policy will remain accommodative until domestic demand and overall macroeconomic activity recovers, keeping in mind our price and financial stability objectives. • Second, targeted fiscal support remains central to the COVID-19 response during the pandemic, and these are meant to minimize any permanent scars on the Philippine economy. • And third, we reiterate our continued support for structural reforms that are aimed at raising the country’s competitiveness and helping transform the financial landscape so that it is future-ready through digitalization and green finance. In this time of the pandemic, we realize more than ever before that the production of objective and comprehensive research and the promotion of the research culture in the BSP is important. Central banks have long benefitted from academic contributions to monetary policymaking and many other aspects of central bank practice. Academics and independent researchers, and the institutions that they come from, provide guidance in theoretical foundations as well as i

Title: Keynote Speech of BSP Gov. Benjamin E. Diokno for the Signing of the Multilateral MOA on the Implementation of a Centralized System for Accreditation/Selection of External Auditors in the Financial Sector
Occasion: Signing of the Multilateral MOA on the Implementation of a Centralized System for Accreditation/Selection of External Auditors in the Financial Sector
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Good morning. It is my pleasure to welcome our Financial Sector Forum (FSF) Principals, Emilio Aquino, Chairman of the Securities and Exchange Commission; Dennis Funa, Commissioner of the Insurance Commission; and Roberto Tan, President of the Philippine Deposit Insurance Corporation. Our partners from the Professional Regulatory Board of Accountancy headed by Chairman Noel Quinañola, to witness another milestone for the accountancy profession and the Financial Sector Forum. It was in 2009, when the FSF member-agencies and the Board of Accountancy (BOA) forged a partnership on the accreditation or selection process for external auditors. The FSF member-agencies and the BOA hurdled significant roadblocks to come up with a unified stance that only those external auditors who meet the qualification criteria set and adhere to by recognized standards in auditing will be allowed to express an opinion on the financial statements of financial institutions. This is recognition of the critical role of external auditors in promoting the fairness and integrity of financial statements and in strengthening market discipline in the financial industry. Since then, the FSF and the BOA continued working together to capacitate the external auditing profession and in promoting awareness of relevant developments in the regulatory sphere. As the business and the regulatory environments evolved, new challenges and opportunities emerged that required us to revisit the arrangements we have put in place more than 10 years ago. This led to the adoption of a centralized or a “one stop shop” accreditation framework which further streamlined the accreditation and selection process and promoted ease of doing business. Under the new framework, external auditors only need to file their applications with the Securities and Exchange Commission, while the FSF member-agencies leverage on the information sharing platform between and among agencies to complete the accreditation process. As such, let me take this opportunity to thank Chairman Emilio Aquino and the Securities and Exchange Commission for accepting the role as the central agency for this process. In line with this development, we would like to reaffirm our commitment by signing a new Memorandum of Agreement on the Implementation of a Centralized System for Accreditation/Selection of External Auditors in the Financial Sector. The MOA provides the respective roles and responsibilities of the member-agencies under the one-stop shop framework as well as the arrangements on the conduct of the Nationwide Regulator’s Forum to apprise external auditors of the latest regulatory issuances and internationally recognized standards relevant to the accounting and auditing profession. All said, let me conclude by sharing a quote from the American anthropologist, Margaret Mead. She said that “Never doubt that a small group of thoughtful, committed people can change the world. Indeed, it is the only thing that ever has.” Congratulations to the FSF member-agencies and the Board of Accountancy for taking this step to implement this change for the benefit of the Filipino people. Thank you and good morning!​

Title: The Management of Reputational Risks in Banks
Occasion: Bank Marketing Association of the Philippines General Membership Meeting
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Bank Marketing Association of the Philippines President Miguel Angelo Villa-Real, BMAP directors and officers, distinguished members and everyone joining this webinar, a pleasant morning. I would like to thank BMAP for inviting me as your guest speaker for this month’s general membership meeting. This is an opportune time to share about the BSP’s expectations on the reputational risk management framework for banks. As you know, continued trust and confidence in the banking system is crucial to securing economic recovery and growth. The COVID-19 crisis has also accelerated digital transformation within a financial landscape. As such, it is imperative for banks to adopt a holistic risk management approach to address emerging threats, especially risks that may affect their reputation and impair the sustainability of their operations. Allow me to first begin with the macroeconomic backdrop, followed by a snapshot of the condition and performance of the Philippine banking sector. Second, I will discuss the evolving governance landscape and the BSP’s reform agenda. Third, I will share the highlights of the recently issued guidelines on reputational risk management as embodied under BSP Circular No. 1114. I will then tackle some of the strategies for managing reputational risk in the digital age. Let me conclude this presentation with some key takeaways. A financial system that is sound and resilient reinforces a country’s strong macroeconomic fundamentals. In the Philippines, the domestic economy contracted by 4.2 percent in the first quarter of 2021—a sustained improvement from the 8.3 percent decline recorded in the last quarter of 2020. For the full year 2020, gross domestic product fell to its sharpest decline at negative 9.6 percent since 1946. As the Philippine economy further reopens and vaccination coverage improves, we project growth to range between 6.0 percent to 7.0 percent this year. The latest inflation forecasts indicate that average inflation is likely to settle at 3.9 percent in 2021, which is within the 2 to 4 percent target band. Gross international reserves posted a 14.7 percent increase to $107.0 billion as of end-May 2021, indicating adequate external liquidity buffer equivalent to 12.2 months’ worth of imports of goods and payments of services and primary income. Cash remittances from overseas Filipino workers amounted to US$7.6 billion. This rose by 2.6 percent in the first quarter of 2021 compared to US$7.4 billion during the same period a year ago as host economies began to ease pandemic-related restrictions. Meanwhile, the peso averaged P48.28/US$1 in Q1 2021 depreciating slightly by 0.03 percent from the Q4 2020 average amid the surge in COVID-19 infections in the country. Amid this backdrop, the National Government and the BSP have been working closely with the industry and other key stakeholders in instituting stimulus measures to help address the unprecedented challenges caused by the pandemic. We expect improvement in the country’s macroeconomic fundamentals in 2021 with the country’s accelerating rollout of the COVID-19 vaccination, the easing of quarantine restrictions and reopening of the economy. Meanwhile, the Philippine banking sector remains stable and resilient. Banks have strong capital position with ratios registering well above the BSP thresholds and that of international standards. As of end-March 2021, capital adequacy ratios of banks stood at 16.8 percent and 17.4 percent on solo and consolidated bases, respectively. The industry’s solo liquidity coverage ratio was at 194.3 percent as of end-March 2021 which was above the regulatory minimum of 100 percent. This indicates banks’ ability to fund its requirements during short-term liquidity shocks. Minimum liquidity ratios of stand-alone thrift, rural and cooperative banks stood at 33.8 percent, 58.9 percent and 38.7 percent, almost three times the minimum requirement of 16 percent. Trust and confidence in the banking system remain high as shown by the sustained year-on-year growth in its assets and deposits of 3.9 percent and 6.0 percent as of end-April 2021, respectively. In line with the slowdown of our economy last year, the gross total loan portfolio (TLP) of banks decreased year-on-year by 2.5 percent to P10.6 trillion as of end-April 2021, slower than the 7.3 percent growth rate posted in April 2020. In terms of asset quality, the Philippine banking system’s gross non-performing loans ratio rose to 4.4 percent as of end-April 2021 compared to the 2.3 percent posted a year ago. Nonetheless, this was matched by higher loan loss provisions resulting in a non-performing loan coverage ratio of 81.5 percent. The passage of the Financial Institutions Strategic Transfer Act is expected to help keep the non-performing loans ratio of banks in check. We believe that the banking system’s capital and liquidity buffers complemented by sound corporate and risk governance and the BSP’s prudent financial supervision, would enable the industry to withstand the challenges that will come our way as the country pushes towards a solid recovery. The sustained strength and resilience of the Philippine banking system is largely due to the progressive implementation of financial sector reform agenda, particularly on the areas of corporate and risk governance. The BSP implemented this reform agenda in three phases. First, we defined the fit and proper requirements and laid down our expectations from the board of directors and senior management in setting the tone of good governance from the top and ensuring that this is consistently adopted across the organization. Second, we established standards for checks and balances, which include the internal audit and compliance functions. Third, specific guidelines on the management of key risk areas such as credit, market, liquidity, operational, information technology, as well as environmental and social risks were issued by the BSP, all geared towards fostering strong risk governance in supervised financial institutions. The BSP recognizes that building and sustaining a good reputation is a critical element in promoting a bank’s safety and soundness. Hence, we released Circular No. 1114 to articulate the principles and practices underpinning sound reputational risk management. What exactly is reputational risk? It refers to the risk to earnings, capital, and liquidity arising from the negative perception on the bank by various stakeholders such as the customers, shareholders, investors, market analysts, or media, that can adversely affect the ability of the bank to maintain existing business relationships, establish new businesses or partnerships, or continuously access varied sources of funding. Simply put, reputation is the collective perception of a bank’s relevant stakeholders whose expectations, if not satisfactorily met, may result in severe adverse impact on the banks’ business and bottom line. As Warren Buffet said, “It takes 20 years to build a reputation and five minutes to ruin it. If you think about that, you’ll do things differently.” Considering the implications of a reputational risk crisis, as well as the immense effort and resources to rebuild stakeholder trust, it is crucial for banks take a proactive stance in managing the risks to reputation. This is aligned with the extraordinary diligence required of the banking system. The reputation of an organization may be affected by stakeholder perceptions on the bank’s corporate and risk governance, the integrity of its officers and personnel, staff competence, organizational culture, business practices, product or service quality, employee and customer relations, financial soundness or business viability, and legal or regulatory compliance. The BSP guidelines highlight how reputational risk is closely linked to other risk exposures such as credit, market, liquidity, and operational risks, such that reputational risk can be triggered by a risk or a host of other risks. In the same way, reputation events can have spillover effects to other risk categories. Reputational risk may also arise from environmental, social and governance issues, especially now when banks are being called to respond to the challenge of meaningfully embedding sustainability in their strategies and business models. While it remains to be seen whether green banks will outperform non-green banks in terms of financial and operational performance, there is no doubt that climate change concerns are a relevant concern of key stakeholders. And beyond business, this is something that we must address for future generations. Banks belonging to a conglomerate must also be vigilant of external reputation events affecting the parent bank, subsidiaries, affiliates, and other related companies. Similarly, business relationships with key clients and counterparties which are engaged in unethical or unlawful acts, as well as with major service providers that have substandard service quality or lax controls may give rise to contagion effects on reputation. Other external events, such as unfounded rumors and accusations may likewise have a damaging impact on the financial institution’s reputation and the level of public confidence. Due to interdependencies with other risk types, financial institutions should take a holistic approach in managing reputational risk, one that leverages on existing corporate and risk governance frameworks. Circular No. 1114 sets out the supervisory expectations for BSP supervised financial institutions on the identification, assessment, and management of reputational risks. Reputational risk management should be integrated not only in the bank’s enterprise-wide risk management system, but also in the organization’s crisis management discipline. In any case, banks are afforded the flexibility to design their reputational risk management function depending on how reputational risk exposures are being managed within the organization. In fact, we are aware t

Title: Opening Remarks of Governor Benjamin E. Diokno for the Satellite Broadband and Financial Inclusion Webinar
Occasion: Satellite Broadband and Financial Inclusion Webinar
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: Good morning ladies and gentlemen. As you know, the BSP is committed to advancing financial inclusion in the country. What started as an advocacy program has evolved into an institutionalized policy and strategic objective of the BSP in support of its vision of improving the quality of life of all Filipinos. Financial inclusion, at its core, is about ensuring every Filipino has access to financial services as tools that can help them better manage their finances, transact safely and conveniently, seize income opportunities, and protect their family and businesses against financial losses. In pursuit of this, we launched the 3-year Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap (DPTR) in 2020 as a tangible demonstration of our commitment to promote financial inclusion and digital payments. Under the Roadmap, we aim to strengthen customer preference for digital payments with at least 50% of the total volume of retail payments transacted digitally and at least 70% of adult Filipinos owning a formal account. We also want to see the provision of more innovative digital financial services that are truly responsive to the needs of consumers. We are optimistic that we will achieve these bold targets with the concerted efforts and collaboration of the BSP, the industry, and the government. As an example, the progress in the ongoing PhilSys registration has resulted in the onboarding of almost 3.8 million unbanked Philsys registrants as part of the Philippine Statistics Authority’s partnership with the Land Bank of the Philippines. Under BSP’s directive and guidance, the payment services industry led by the Philippine Payments Management, Inc. (PPMI) held a pilot launch of the QR Ph for Person-to-Merchant (P2M) payments last April 30 2021. With QR Ph P2M, consumers need not memorize details of their bank accounts as the relevant information will now be securely stored in the QR code, thus facilitating easy and convenient retail digital payment transactions. The coronavirus pandemic has quickened our digitalization agenda. It highlighted the significance of cashless and digital transactions—not just for convenience, but as a necessity. In fact last year, at the height of the lockdown, the Department of Social Welfare and Development implemented account-based distribution of the 2nd tranche of the social amelioration program with participating financial service providers. This has resulted in a speedy and efficient distribution of aid to our most affected pandemic-hit countrymen. We have also seen the quantum jump in the use of digital payments as indicated by the exponential rise in InstaPay and Pesonet transactions. Yet, as digitalization gains momentum, the pandemic also highlighted digital connectivity as a basic need for a truly inclusive new economy. Internet access, or the lack of it, has been a constant challenge, especially in the countryside. Thus, the BSP could not overemphasize the significance of the recently issued Executive Order No. 127 by President Rodrigo Roa Duterte to our digital financial inclusion agenda. The policy now allows non-enfranchised but duly registered internet service providers (ISP) and value added service providers (VASP) to directly access satellite systems to build and operate broadband facilities to offer internet services. By promoting greater competition and investment in the country’s satellite broadband services market, this new policy can bridge the massive internet infrastructure gaps in rural and remote communities within a short period of time. The BSP, and the Financial Inclusion Steering Committee which I chair, are honored to have played a role in the passage of this new policy. The BSP has benefitted greatly from the paper of The Asia Foundation and the Better Broadband Alliance on the country’s ICT policy landscape and emerging technologies, including satellite broadband. I also wish to acknowledge Monetary Board Member (MBM) Bruce Tolentino for his proactive stance in taking up this initiative to support the furtherance of the BSP’s financial inclusion initiative. We have seen the potential of satellite broadband in the experience of Bank Rakyat Indonesia (BRI), whom we invited to grace this webinar. And finally, we thank the Department of Information and Communications Technology under the leadership of Secretary Gregorio Honasan III, and the National Telecommunications Commission (NTC) under the leadership of Commissioner Gamaliel Cordoba for supporting this game-changing reform initiative. We envisioned this webinar as a venue for our financial service providers to recognize and seize the opportunities that the new policy brings for their digital financial inclusion strategy. Lastly, it is our hope that the participants here become active advocates for policy reforms and programs toward reliable and affordable internet access for all. Before I end, allow me to thank all the resource persons who have accepted our invitation to share their knowledge and experiences. Let me also thank The Asia Foundation for co-organizing this webinar with the BSP and for its valuable work in promoting our shared goal of improving the lives of Filipinos. I wish you all a wonderful and meaningful discussion ahead. Thank you and a pleasant morning to everyone.​

Title: Tatag, Tapang, Talino: Para sa Bawat Pilipino
Occasion: BSP's 28th Anniversary
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E Diokno
Content: ​ Members of the Monetary Board, BSP officers, and colleagues, good morning. It's wonderful to slowly see a sense of normalcy again. As you know, last year, we had a simpler celebration. But simpler as it was, it was still a momentous occasion being the BSP's first virtual anniversary celebration. By now, we have all gotten used to this way of doing things. “You are on mute," or “Can you see my screen?" have become common phrases as we go about our daily work. But I am sure that just like me, you are also missing the warmth and pleasure that you can only get with each other's physical presence. And so, I hope that—through the efforts of our BSP COVID-19 Task Force and the Employees Association in getting vaccines doses for us—we can celebrate our future milestones together without masks and not necessarily within 2 meters of each other. But today, we celebrate our 28th Anniversary with the theme, “Tatag, Tapang, at Talino: Para sa Bawat Pilipino". While this is our second virtual anniversary celebration, I can't help but feel a sense of pride for what the BSP has accomplished in pursuit of our mandates and bringing BSP closer to the people amid the challenging landscape. Our theme for this anniversary is very fitting. It reflects the qualities we exemplified for the past year. “TATAG" or resilience - The BSP never wavering despite the challenges that we have encountered in the past year. We remain resilient. Amid the several restrictions brought about by the lockdown, we continued to perform our duties and responsibilities to keep the economy afloat and the financial system well-functioning. The BSP remained true to its mandate. This, in turn, translated to the stability on all fronts of the economy. The Philippines' investment grade credit ratings with “stable" outlook have remained intact throughout the pandemic—in the midst of a wave of rating downgrades and negative outlook revisions globally. This has helped the government secure funding for vital COVID-response measures from both local and foreign financial markets at relatively cheap cost. “TAPANG" or boldness – True to its sworn duty as the guardian of the economy, the BSP soldiered on amid the unprecedented effects of the pandemic. Indeed, the BSP has done some heavy lifting in helping ease the impact of the pandemic on lives, livelihoods and the overall economy. The BSP has been quick and decisive in its policy and regulatory responses to the pandemic. Through our various measures, the BSP helped ensure sufficient liquidity in the financial system, helped keep the banking system stable, and helped maintain confidence in the financial markets. To date, the BSP has injected about ₱2.2 trillion, or US$45 billion, equivalent to 12 percent of GDP, to the financial system through its liquidity-enhancing measures. Moreover, the short-term, provisional advances we extended to the National Government helped provide emergency assistance for its COVID-response measures. The BSP also advocated the following legislation to complement our monetary policy and support our recovery—the Corporate Recovery and Tax Incentives for Enterprises (CREATE), and the Financial Institutions Strategic Transfer (FIST) Act. Both signed into law this year. In the middle and despite of the pandemic, we persevered to achieve our goals. On digitalization: The BSP, in October last year, launched the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap. Under the Roadmap, we have the two lofty goals of converting half of financial transactions into digital form and onboarding at least 70 percent of Filipino adults into the formal financial system through ownership of a transaction account by 2023. Consistent with this roadmap, we have issued a regulatory framework for digital banks. We have also launched programs that promote the use of QR codes for payments and that allow digital payment of transactions fees with government institutions. All these drive us further in the goal to shift from a cash-heavy to a cash-lite society. And in line with our massive push for digitalization as a key enabler for financial inclusion, the BSP plays an active role in the implementation of the Philippine Identification System or PhilSys. The Philippine government's vision of One Phil ID for every Juan would not only enable the financial onboarding of the unbanked, but would also facilitate efficient business and government transactions. To this end, we have proposed the integration of the PhilSys into all government systems and databases to enhance the acceptability of Phil IDs. We have also recently completed our realignment of the new organizational structure. And one of the changes made is the establishment of the Payments and Currency Management Sector to spearhead our efforts in digital payments transformation. We also pushed through with our priority programs and projects within the BSP. This year, we signed the lease with BCDA for the 31-hectare property that will house our new eco-friendly BSP Complex. With a vision for a smart, green, and modern facility, we launched the design contest that will help conceptualize this for us. We also had our first virtual “Bankers Reception" of sorts with “Ulat ng BSP sa Bayan." The virtual mode allowed us to reach thousands more of our colleagues, partners, and stakeholders through Facebook. From our usual friends from the banking and diplomatic communities, “Ulat ng BSP sa Bayan" was joined by youth groups and coalitions, MSMEs and fintech groups, and various non-government and industry associations. To date, there has been almost 60 thousand views of that momentous occasion. “TALINO" or intelligence – the BSP has and will continue to be at the forefront of thought leadership. We released the very first Statement on the State of Financial Stability in the country—a development that recognizes BSP's extensive role in ensuring financial stability in the country. BSP is also ahead of the curve by championing sustainability in the financial system. This is in recognition of the dire effects of climate change in the world as we know it. We have commenced capacity building, issuance of relevant regulations, launching of an internal Sustainable Central Banking Program, and collaboration with various government agencies, all geared towards a more sustainable approach to how we do things here in BSP. As such, we adopted sustainable central banking as one of BSP's strategic objectives. Part of this is advocating for green finance, which funds green economic activities and climate change mitigation and adaptation projects—this is something I am proud to be part our legacy in the BSP. This year, we also published four BSP publications: BSP Unbound, a publication on Central Banking Amid the Pandemic; No One Left Behind, a book on the Philippine Financial Inclusion Story; Yaman, a coffee table book on the History and Heritage in Philippine Money; and Cool Minds and Brave Hearts, a coffee table book on the people of the Central Bank—no easy feat, especially with the quarantine restrictions that we had to overcome. In addition, we established the BSP Research Academy to further bolster its ability to achieve central banking excellence and establish BSP's presence in the research community. The Research Academy is intended to provide valuable inputs to BSP policies, which are ultimately aimed at promoting a high quality of life for Filipinos. We had several milestones within the BSP, as well. These milestones, among others, are aimed towards a BSP geared towards continuing learning and complemented with competent talents. Last year, for example, we admitted the pioneer batch of 32 Central Bank Associates to the BSP Young Professional Programs. The success of the YPP and the prospects of the program to the BSP's talent management strategy inspired us to call for a second batch. In May, we had the first graduation of 32 employees from 22 Departments and Offices in the 28-week Business Intelligence and Data Analytics Program of the Carnegie – Melon University of Australia. This is consistent with our massive digitalization program to enable the BSP to “walk the talk" of digitalization. Aligned with this, we launched the BSP EDGe Learning Experience Platform, to provide employees with easy access to various learning content—a first in government service, if I may say so myself. We are continuing our talent engagement reforms to address career progression—we are doing this because we recognize that BSP's best asset has always been its people. While there are a lot to celebrate, let's always keep in mind the more important part of our 28th Anniversary theme: Para sa Bawat Pilipino. Anong silbi ng tatag, tapang, at talino kung hindi ibabahagi at pagyayamanin upang makapaglingkod sa kapwa Pilipino? So let this be our driving force in the work that we do, the struggles we face, and the triumphs we achieve—we are doing this for each and every Filipino. Last Christmas, I said to take to heart, “Para sa Bayan". Today, let's revise that to be “Para sa bawat Pilipino." Everyone, please repeat after me: “Para sa bawat Pilipino." Happy 28th Anniversary, BSP. Mabuhay ang Bangko Sentral, mabuhay tayong lahat! ​

Title: Message of BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno for the 2021 Youth Summit
Occasion: 2021 BSP Youth Summit
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Sa libu-libong kabataang Pilipino na kasama natin ngayon sa BSP Youth Summit, magandang hapon sa inyong lahat! Nagtuturo ako dati sa mga kagaya n'yong college students kaya masaya ako na kasama ko kayo ngayon. At dahil namimiss ko na rin ang pagiging professor, hayaan n'yong magkwento muna ako ng konting history ng ating financial system. Hindi pa ipinapanganak ang karamihan sa inyo nang magsimula ang electronic banking sa Pilipinas noong 1980s. During that time, when we said e-banking, limitado ito sa mga automated teller machines, o ATM—na uso pa rin naman hanggang ngayon at nakikita sa mga shopping malls, convenience stores, bank branches, at kung saan-saan pa. Noong 2000s ay naging laganap ang paggamit ng Internet, na naging game-changer sa larangan ng financial technology. Ito ang naging daan upang mas nagging maayos ang serbisyo ng mga bangko at iba pang financial service providers. Sa tulong ng Internet banking facilities, nagkaroon ng 24/7 access ang mga kliyente ng bangko sa kanilang mga accounts. Maaari na silang mag-fund transfer, magbayad ng bills, mag-apply ng loan, at magsagawa ng iba pang banking at payment transactions gamit lamang ang kanilang personal computers, at ngayon, pati mobile phones. Isang trivia para sa inyo—alam n'yo bang sa Pilipinas nag-umpisa ang paggamit ng mobile money services? Ang konseptong ito ay unang ipinakilala ng isang telecommunications company noong 2001. Ito ay para sa mga Pilipinong malalayo na hindi naaabot ng serbisyo ng mga bangko. Gamit ang SMS technology, ang isang tao ay maaaring mag-fund transfer, magbayad ng bills, at bumili ng kanyang mga kailangan. Noon pa man, interesado na ang Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas sa mga ganitong klase ng teknolohiya. Bilang texting capital of the world, nakita at ginamit natin ang mobile technology upang maabot ang mga kababayan nating nasa malayo at isali sila sa mga tinatawag nating “financially included.” Noong 2015, ipinakilala ng BSP ang National Retail Payment System Framework na siyang nagbigay-daan sa interoperable fund transfers sa pagitan ng iba't ibang bangko. Ito ang dahilan kung bakit nagkaroon tayo ng mga automated clearing houses—ang PESONet at ang InstaPay. Ang PESONet ay ginagamit sa pagta-transfer ng malalaking halaga ng pera habang ang InstaPay naman ay ginagamit sa real-time fund transfer ng mas mababang halaga ng pera. Sa panahon ng pandemya, malaki ang naitulong ng PESONet at InstaPay, lalo na’t kailangan nating manatili sa loob ng bahay upang maiwasan ang panganib ng COVID-19. Unti-unting natutuklasan ng mga Pilipino na posible palang gumawa ng mga financial transactions kahit hindi gumagamit ng aktwal o pisikal na pera. Safe na, convenient pa! Dahil sa digital at online banking, nagawa nating i-monitor ang ating mga bank accounts at mag-transact sa kabila ng mga community quarantines at lockdowns. Sa pamamagitan ng electronic money, nakakapag-"add to cart" tayo. Nakaka-order tayo online ng pagkain, gamot, damit, at kung anu-ano pa, at nai-dedeliver pa right to our doorsteps. Gamit ang mga digital payment channels, naipamahagi rin ng pamahalaan ang ayuda nito sa ating mga kababayan. Marahil ay marami sa inyo ang nakagawa na ng mga digital transaction at naranasan na rin ninyo kung gaano ito ka-convenient at ka-efficient. Sa ngayon, patuloy pa rin ang paggamit ng digital payments sa ating bansa. Nagiging bahagi na ito ng tinatawag nating “New Economy.” May ilang mga layunin ang BSP sa ilalim ng “New Economy.” Kabilang dito ang mga sumusunod. Una, by 2023, at least 50 percent ng kabuuang financial transactions sa bansa ay digital na at, pangalawa, 70 percent ng Filipino adults ay magkaroon na ng financial accounts. Sinisiguro din ng BSP na ang payment systems ng bansa ay safe, efficient, reliable, at secure sa pamamagitan ng pag-issue ng mga polisiya at pagsuporta sa mga kinakailangan pang mga batas. Kasama rin dito ang aming ginagawa pagdating sa mga kailangan ng mga consumers sa larangan ng payment systems tulad ng Interoperable Bills Pay at Request to Pay facilities, at ang QR Ph. May ilan na siguro sa inyo ang nakasubok bumili ng mga bagay sa convenience store, botika, o shopping mall gamit ang QR code. Patuloy din tayong nakikipagtulungan sa Philippine Payments Management, Inc., upang mas maging reasonable ang mga fee sa paggamit ng mga digital payment systems at mas magkaroon ng mga innovative payments-related products and services. Pagdating naman sa digital infrastructure, sinusuportahan ng BSP ang Philippine Statistics Authority sa pag-iimprenta ng ating National ID na tinatawag na Philippine Identification System o PhilSys. Umaasa tayo na sa tulong ng PhilSys, magiging mas madali ang pagbukas ng bank accounts at mababawasan ng requirements ang pag-apply sa trabaho. Mahalaga rin para sa amin sa BSP ang financial education kaya naman nariyan ang PisoLit, ang ating Facebook page na gagabay sa ating personal finances. Ipinapakilala rin namin ngayon ang kaibigan ni PisoLit na si Ekolit na syang tutulong sa inyong mas maintindihan ang mga kumplikadong economic concepts. Sina PisoLit at Ekolit ay nariyan upang tulungan tayong mas maintidihan ang epekto ng bawat economic at financial decision natin. Sana ay matulungan nila tayo sa pag-unlad ng ating mga pamilya, at maging ng ating bansa. I-follow nyo na lang ang kanilang mga social media account, pati na rin ng BSP. Ang kinabukasan natin ay nasa inyong mga kamay. Let us maximize all the possibilities and opportunities of digitalization. Nakita natin kung gaano kabilis magbago ang teknolohiya. Kailangan nating makisabay o mas mauna pa para hindi tayo mahuli sa takbo ng mundo. May kasabihan nga: “daig ng maagap ang masipag.” Kaya natin maging pareho. Sa loob ng ilang taon, kayo na ang mamamahala sa ating ekonomiya at sa ating bansa. Mag-aral kayong mabuti. Magmasid, mag-eksperimento, at matutong hamunin ang mga nakagisnan kung kinakailangan. At sa pakikibahagi at paglahok ninyo sa Youth Summit na ito, punong-puno ng pag-asa ang aking puso at diwa. Maraming salamat sa organizers, sa mga teams na nag-submit ng proposal, at sa inyong lahat na narito ngayon. The challenge is on: ibangon natin ang ekonomiya, i-digital mo na! ​

Title: BSP’s Monetary Policies amid the Pandemic and Vision Beyond COVID-19: Journey through Digitalization and Financial Inclusion
Occasion: Joint Foreign Chambers Economic Briefing
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ To the esteemed members of the diplomatic corps, and Board Directors and members of the organizing group, as well as to my other colleagues in government, and to all guests, a pleasant day. Thank you for this opportunity to speak in this economic briefing for the foreign chambers of the Philippines. As delivered to Joint European Foreign Chambers of Commerce 1. Advantage Austria 2. Australian - New Zealand Chamber of Commerce 3. British Chamber of Commerce of the Philippines 4. Dutch Chamber of Commerce in the Philippines 5. European Chamber of Commerce of the Philippines 6. French Chamber of Commerce and Industry in the Philippines 7. German-Philippine Chamber of Commerce and Industry 8. Nordic Chamber of Commerce of the Philippines 9. Philippines-Swiss Business Council (PSBC) 10. Polish Investment and Trade Agency (PITA) 11. Spanish Chamber of Commerce in the Philippines (La Camara) After the World Health Organization (WHO) declared COVID-19 as a pandemic about 1 year and 4 months ago, we can now say that the worst is behind us. The development and global distribution of vaccines within such a relatively short period since the start of the pandemic has proven once again the great crisis-coping ability of mankind. But with the uneven vaccine rollout globally, risks persist. After an estimated contraction of –3.3 percent in 2020, the IMF expects the global economy to grow at 6 percent in 2021 and then moderate to 4.4 percent in 2022. The growth forecast for the global economy by other institutions are also shown in this slide. In the case of the Philippines, we have started to see green shoots as early as the third quarter of last year. Foreign direct investments (FDIs) surged by 45.1% percent for the first three months of the year. Further, investment pledges with the Board of Investments increased by 65.6% percent in Q1 2021 and with the Philippine Economic Zone Authority (PEZA) by 53.9% percent in Q1 2021 from a year ago. As you know, investment approvals are a good leading indicator for actual investments, two or more years down the road. The passage of CREATE is expected to help attract more investments, both foreign and local, moving forward. Further, investment pledges with the Board of Investments increased by 65.6% percent in Q1 2021 and with the Philippine Economic Zone Authority (PEZA) by 53.9% percent in Q1 2021 from a year ago. As you know, investment approvals are a good leading indicator for actual investments, two or more years down the road. The passage of CREATE is expected to help attract more investments, both foreign and local, moving forward. Further, investment pledges with the Board of Investments increased by 65.6% percent in Q1 2021 and with the Philippine Economic Zone Authority (PEZA) by 53.9% percent in Q1 2021 from a year ago. As you know, investment approvals are a good leading indicator for actual investments, two or more years down the road. The passage of CREATE is expected to help attract more investments, both foreign and local, moving forward. Trade has picked up. In the first four months of this year, exports and imports expanded by 19 percent and 21.9 percent, respectively, from the same period last year. Meanwhile business confidence weakened for Q2 and Q3 2021, primarily due to concerns on the surge of COVID-19 cases in March and April this year. Country’s consumer sentiment, on the other hand, continued to improve citing expectations of more jobs and permanent employment, additional/higher income as government continues to ease mobility restrictions amidst accelerated vaccine rollout and the sustained implementation of measures to address the challenges from the pandemic. Remittances jumped by 4.8 percent for the first four months of the year from a year ago. This highlights the sustained resilience of overseas Filipino remittances. Last year, they declined by a mere 0.8 percent versus the double-digit decline projected by private sector analysts. The contraction in our gross domestic product (GDP) is on a decline, and according to the National Economic and Development Authority (NEDA), we an look forward to a positive GDP growth in the second quarter of this year despite the stricter quarantine restrictions due to the recent virus surge. Crisis Response from the BSP Going into the crisis, the Philippines had ample fiscal and monetary buffers to deal with shocks—and these have been used extensively and wisely to aptly respond to the crisis. On the monetary front, manageable inflation allowed the BSP to cut the overnight borrowing rate by 200 basis points from 4.0 percent to a record low of 2.0 percent. The BSP also cut the reserve requirement further to 12 percent from 14 percent, thus freeing up more funds for lending to businesses and households. On top of these conventional monetary actions, the BSP implemented unprecedented measures to squarely respond to the crisis. We granted provisional advances worth Php540 billion (US$ 11.2 billion) to the National Government to augment its resources for COVID response. The BSP’s charter allows us to extend lifeline support to the government in times of crisis, with proper safeguards. This facility, which allows short-term financing, does not serve as a long-term source of funds for the government because they are temporary, time-bound, and capped. The BSP has also purchased government securities in the secondary market to lift market confidence. However, this activity has been scaled down, as the economy recovers. In sum, the BSP has so far injected Php2.2 trillion (US$45 billion) into the financial system, equivalent to about 12 percent of GDP (as of June 10, 2021). Domestic liquidity has remained ample through the liquidity-easing measures of the BSP. Preliminary data show that domestic liquidity expanded by 4.7 percent year-on-year to about ₱14.3 trillion in May 2021. Bank lending growth, however, remains tepid. Preliminary data show that outstanding loans of universal and commercial banks fell by 4.0 percent in May following a 5.0-percent decline in April. The BSP also implemented various time-bound regulatory relief measures so banks and their customers can manage the impact of the crisis on their balance sheets and finances, and so there is more room for lending. Among these are grace periods for loan payments, capping of interest rates on credit card usage, staggered booking of loan losses, counting of loans to micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs) as compliance to the reserve requirement, increase in the single borrower’s limit, and higher limit on real-estate loans. Meantime, on top of the time-bound regulations to help manage the impact of the pandemic, the BSP also continues the task of enhancing the regulatory environment for financial sector and overall economic development. One of our most recent initiatives is the enhancement of the Know Your Employees (KYE) guidelines to enhance operational risk management of banks. Another is the set of amendments to regulations on securities custodianship and securities registry operations, consistent with the aim of strengthening investor protection and developing the domestic capital market. We also recently launched the Rural Banking Strengthening Program (RBSP), under which an Interagency Working Group (IAWG) chaired by the BSP was created to help fulfill the objective of rural banking development. An initial package of proposed policies, programs and reforms for the rural banking industry will be submitted to the BSP’s Monetary Board by the end of 2021 Exit Strategy Meantime, given the enormous stimulus from the BSP, the question on unwinding always surfaces. We recognize that economic recovery is still in its nascent phase. As such, we will keep our monetary policy supportive of growth and allow previous monetary easing to work its way to the economy. The BSP will withdraw monetary support only when there are indisputable signs of solid economic recovery amid a manageable inflation environment, as well as a sustained downtrend in community transmission of the virus. When it comes to exit strategy, the BSP recognizes the necessity of carefully balancing the need to ensure sustainability of recovery and the need to guard against risks to the BSP’s price and financial stability objectives. Build Back Better For the Philippines, the theme that has resonated amid the pandemic is “Building Back Better.” This means we do not merely aim to recover from the crisis; instead, we want to have a “New Economy” that is stronger, more technologically advanced, and more inclusive than before the COVID crisis. When talking about building back better, two things come to fore: digitalization and sustainability.
