Although there is clearly room for optimism, economic recovery will depend largely on the speed of the vaccination rollout, the expansion of the capacity of our healthcare system, and the citizens’ commitment to observe the health protocol. These will allow us to safely re-open the economy and resume more economic activities. The challenges of the pandemic may stay with us for a while, but we are hopeful that we will get back to where we were before the pandemic by the second half of next year. Now, more than ever, each one of us need to walk together and watch each other’s back so that no one gets left behind in this continuing journey. As Henry Ford said, “Coming together is a beginning; keeping together is progress; working together is success.” I trust that this session will enrich all of us and equip us with the right information and mindset that we can share with our colleagues or family members later. I hope that in our next RMCS event in Central Visayas, we can all meet in person, but until then, please stay safe and healthy. Maayong buntag ug daghang salamat!​

Title: The Philippines: Recovery and Beyond
Occasion: Nomura Fireside Chat Session
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ A pleasant day to everyone. I thank Nomura for inviting me to update its investor clients and guests on the Philippine economy. Let me start by providing a glimpse of where the economy is right now, as the world continues to cope with the COVID-19 crisis. The Philippine economy is slowly recovering. In the second quarter of this year, the Philippines grew by 11.8 percent, finally ending the pandemic-induced recession. This was not solely due to base effects. Growth was broad-based, driven by household consumption, investments, consumption, and public construction. Manufacturing has been improving. The purchasing manager’s index (PMI) was in an expansion mode from June to July. The PMI, however, declined in August in view of the recent lockdown imposed to slow down the spread of the covid 19 delta variant. By the way, the fall in the PMI waws Asia-wide. Unemployment rate, which peaked 17.6 percent in April last year, dropped to 7.7 percent in June this year. On the monetary front, inflation has been slowing down, settling at 4.0 percent in July to bring the average for the first seven months of this year to 4.4 percent. Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas expects inflation to average slightly above the upper end of the 2.0- to 4.0-percent target band this year due to supply issues, such as rising global oil prices and constraints in meat importation. Then, we see inflation easing toward the midpoint of the target range by next year and 2023 amid government measures to improve supply of meat and other food items. Our inflation projections are in line with expectations of relevant observers. Given manageable inflation, and in support of economic recovery, BSP kept policy rates steady during the policy meeting last August 12. External accounts remain strong with the Philippines’ usual sources of foreign exchange remaining resilient amid the pandemic. Overseas Filpino remittances in 2020 reached USD30 billion, the same as in 2019, defying forecasts that it will fall by double-digit rates. In the first half of this year, it grew by 6.4 percent to USD15 billion. In 2020, business process outsourcing (BPO) receipts declined by only 1.3 percent to USD22.4 billion. In the first quarter of this year, BPO revenues grew 3.0 percent to USD5.5 billion. Philippine exports and imports have rebounded alongside the recovery of its trading partners. In the first half of the year, imports rose by 29.8 percent, largely due to higher demand for imported raw materials and intermediate goods. Exports jumped by 20.9 percent in the same period amid recovery in global demand. Actual investments are also rising, which bodes well for job creation. For the first five months of the year, foreign direct investments (FDIs) jumped by almost 38 percent. The overall balance of payments (BOP) posted a small deficit in the first semester, driven mostly by the rise in imports mostly of capital goods amid the recovery process. Nonetheless, we expect BOP to end this year with a surplus of USD7.1 billion, equivalent to 1.8 percent of GDP. External liquidity buffers continue to be more than adequate. The gross international reserves (GIR) remains hefty amounting to about USD107 billion as of end-July this year, providing more than one year worth of import cover. At US$107 billion, the GIR exceeds the Philippines total external debt which stood at USD97 billion dollars as of end March 2021. The peso is relatively stable. It has depreciated by 3.5 percent against the US dollar since the start of the year. But such depreciation is broadly in line with the behavior of other emerging market currencies. Our hefty gross international reserves, the steady inflows from remittances, BPOs, and recovery of exports and FDIs will support the peso. The market determined exchange rate policy has been our first line of defense against external shocks. International debt market continues to warmly receive Philippine government bonds. Credit default swap (CDS) spread for the Philippines’ 5-year bonds remain lower than our rating peers. Moving on the banking sector - the banking system is stable and resilient, with manageable asset quality and ample capitalization. It benefitted from regulatory reforms of the past two decades. Non-performing loans (NPL) ratio of universal and commercial banks, rose to 4.0 percent as of end-June this year, up from the pre-pandemic level of 1.6 percent. Yet, the latest NPL number is still far from the levels seen right after the Asian financial crisis. Capital adequacy ratio (CAR) of UKBs stood at 16.8 percent on solo basis and 17.4 percent on consolidated basis as of end-March this year. These numbers are better than the 15.3 and 15.8 percent, respectively, recorded in the same period last year, and much higher that the 10 percent threshold set by BSP and 8 percent level prescribed by the Bank for International Settlements. All these developments suggest that the Philippine economic recovery is underway. Yet, we recognize that the road to full recovery is a bumpy one. Last month, the government imposed another strict lockdown in Metro Manila and other areas to contain the spread of the more contagious COVID Delta variant. Consequently, the government had to bring down its economic growth projection for this year to a range of 4 to 5 percent, from 6 to 7 percent previously. But for the succeeding years, robust growth projections were retained at 7 to 9 percent for 2022, and 6.0 to 7.0 percent for 2023 and 2024. This means that while challenges remain significant over the near term, our medium- to long-term growth prospects remain bright owing to its sound economic fundamentals and the government’s steadfast commitment to structural reforms. The much-improved supply of COVID-19 vaccines from multiple sources and quickened vaccination rollout are key to accelerate economic recovery. Highest number of jabs of 710,482 in a day was recorded last August 5. In the month of August, average daily jabs reached 426,653. These performances suggest that the administrative machinery needed for a massive vaccination drive exists. More than 100 million doses of vaccines are expected to arrive on or before the end of the year. At this rate, the Philippines’ goal to reach “population protection” by the end of the year appears doable. . The accelerated inoculation drive will help boost business sentiments as well as consumer sentiments and sustain the momentum for investments, job recovery, and overall economic growth. Now allow me to share other official macroeconomic assumptions for the Philippines. Inflation will remain manageable, thus provide an enabling environment for investments and consumption. Latest estimates from the central bank show that full-year average for this year will settle at 4.1 percent. This is slightly above the 2.0 to 4.0 percent target range, driven by supply-side factors including rising global oil prices. For next year and in 2023, inflation is seen to ease to 3.1 percent. Exports will rise by 8.0 percent this year and 6.0 percent next year. Imports will grow by 12.0 percent this year and 10.0 percent next year. Remittances will grow by 4.0 percent this year and next. GIR will rise further to USD115 billion by the end of this year and USD117 billion next year. The banking sector will remain stable, thus able to support economic recovery and growth through financial intermediation. The new Financial Strategic Transfer (FIST) will provide additional support for banks in managing soured debts. Based on our baseline projections for the short term, the average NPL ratio of UKBs will stay within single digit levels. Moving on, I would like to touch on COVID response measures, which have helped fuel the economic recovery process. In the Philippines, there is a whole-of-government approach to winning the COVID-19 battle. On the economic front, significant support has come from both the fiscal and the monetary sides. On the fiscal side, the National Government adjusted its budget deficit program to accommodate the huge cost of response measures. From the pre-pandemic level of 3.0 percent of GDP, the budget deficit rose to 7.6 percent last year and is programmed to reach 9.3 percent this year. The national government’s outstanding debt stood at 60.4 percent of GDP as of end-June this year, which remains manageable. As shown in this graph, general government debt, as percent of GDP, is several percentage points lower than national government debt. As of end-September 2020 shows general government debt stood at 45.1 percent of GDP. Throughout the COVID-19 crisis, fiscal authorities have endeavored to strike an appropriate balance between recovery needs and fiscal prudence, with the priority given to health, social, and infrastructure spending. On the monetary side, the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) has done quite a lot. We cut the policy rate to its historic low of 2.0 percent, following a series of rate cuts last year. The reserve requirement is now down to 12 percent following the 200-bp cut last year. We continue to buy government securities in the secondary market to ensure ample liquidity in the system. However, the volume of our purchases, has declined from last year’s, given much improved liquidity conditions. The BSP has also extended bridge financing to the National Government to boost its resources for COVID response. After previous ones were settled, the latest outstanding amount stands at PHP540 billion (about USD10.8 billion). This lifeline support is short-term—as allowed under the BSP’s charter during times of crisis. This will not cause permanent increase in the monetary base and will not be a regular source of funding for the government. In total, the BSP has infused over PHP2.2 trillion (or USD44 billion) in liquidity to support the economy, equivalent to 12 percent of GDP. The liquidity enhancement measures have helped buoy market confidence. Meantime, besides the liquidity enhancing measures, the central bank has also implem

Title: Welcome Message of BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno for the NCDA Webinar Empowering Persons with Disabiliites Through Digital Financial Inclusion
Occasion: NCDA Webinar on Empowering Persons with Disabilities Through Digital Financial Inclusion
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Good morning to all the participants in today’s NCDA webinar. I am happy to be here with you again. Alam natin na hindi sapat ang paglago lamang ng ekonomiya. Dapat maramdaman ng lahat ng Pilipino ang mga benepisyo nito. All Filipinos should be able to make better lives for themselves as the economy improves. This requires that we continuously work toward the NCDA’s vision of “full participation and equality of rights and opportunities for persons with disabilities.” This is why financial inclusion is among the top advocacies of the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas. We believe that everyone should have effective and easy access to a wide range of financial services. Over the past few years, the BSP has been laying the foundations of an inclusive digital financial ecosystem. As financial transactions are increasingly conducted through digital channels, more and more consumers gain convenient access to legitimate financial services, making them less vulnerable to onerous practices, such as the nefarious 5 - 6 scheme. The theme of this webinar is both noble and timely. We consider the empowerment of our countrymen across all sectors as a vital outcome of digital financial inclusion. This inclusion will also help us recover from the adverse economic impact of the pandemic and make us better prepared for a post COVID-19 economy. An exciting development is the emergence of sacheted financial services. Such novel and groundbreaking services include micro-savings, micro-insurance and investments, micro-credit and remittances, which are designed to be affordable and accessible to all, with far-reaching benefits. In order to access these services, one needs a transaction account in the form of a bank or e-money account. Such a transaction account is the gateway to financial inclusion. That is why one of our main strategies is to make it easy to own a Basic Deposit Account or BDA, which is especially designed to meet the needs of the unbanked. With a minimal deposit of just one hundred pesos and simplified identification requirements, anyone can open an account through their nearest bank, cash agent, or even via mobile app. Presently, 133 banks in the country offer BDAs. As part of our commitment to protect the interests and welfare of the persons with disabilities sector, the BSP has issued Memorandum No. M-2021-040 to all banks and BSP-Supervised Financial Institutions or BSFIs to implement non-discrimination policies. We have likewise introduced enhanced features in our banknotes to help the visually-impaired in identifying denominations. To further boost these initiatives, the BSP supports the implementation of the Philippine National ID System or PhilSys. Makakatulong ang PhilSys na pabilisin ang pagbukas ng account, pagtanggap ng ayuda mula sa pamahalaan, at iba pang mga proseso. The BSP also supports the passage of the Financial Consumer Protection Bill, which aims to strengthen protections available to financial consumers. The initiative has far-reaching benefits, especially amid the rise in digital transactions. We also applaud the issuance of Executive Order 127, signed by President Rodrigo Roa Duterte last February. EO 127 seeks to liberalize access to satellite Internet technology, enabling our kababayans in rural areas to connect and benefit from the digital economy. The BSP invites you to be active and dynamic participants in the shaping of a stronger and more vibrant post-COVID-19 economy by using and promoting legitimate digital financial services. Digital financial inclusion means that no one is left behind. Kapag kabalikat namin kayo, mas madali nating maibabangon ang ating ekonomiya tungo sa pag-unlad para sa bawat Pilipino. Maraming salamat po!

Title: Keynote Address of BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno during the Makati Business Club's Webinar Series on Good Governance
Occasion: Makati Business Club's Manila Webinar Series on Good Governance
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ On behalf of the Anti-Money Laundering Council (AMLC) and its Secretariat, it is my pleasure to join today’s virtual forum, “Out of the Grey List: Meeting Global Standards Against Financial Crime and Corruption Prevention,” hosted by the Makati Business Club, Center for International Private Enterprise, and K2 Integrity. As you may all know, the AMLC is the Philippines’ Financial Intelligence Unit, mandated to implement the Anti-Money Laundering Act of 2001, as amended; the Terrorism Financing Prevention and Suppression Act of 2012; and the Anti-Terrorism Act of 2020. The AMLC is tasked to protect and preserve the integrity and confidentiality of bank accounts and to ensure that the Philippines will not be used as a site for the money laundering of proceeds of any unlawful activity and for terrorism financing. Money laundering, in particular, is defined as a crime, where the proceeds of an unlawful activity are transacted, thereby making the proceeds appear to have originated from legitimate sources.: It only occurs after a predicate crime has been committed. And among these predicate crimes are those related to corruption, specifically:​ • Violations against the Anti-Graft and Corrupt Practices Act; • Plunder; • Bribery and corruption of public officers; and • Malversation of public funds and property. Although the AMLC may already scrutinize suspicious activities prior to the determination of a predicate crime, investigations on predicate crimes are primarily handled by law enforcement agencies. And in the case of those related to corruption, the Office of the Ombudsman also steps in as required by their mandate. Investigations on money laundering, on the other hand, are primarily handled by the AMLC as the country’s Financial Intelligence Unit. Unlike most Financial Intelligence Units of other countries, the Philippines’ AMLC is a hybrid one. Not only does the AMLC receive and analyze covered and suspicious transaction reports, it also investigates money laundering and terrorism financing and causes the filing of forfeiture proceedings and money laundering and terrorism financing cases. As the threat of money laundering and terrorism financing are evolving, there is a need to adequately manage and monitor the level and direction of these risks in coordination with various stakeholders and to further strengthen this hybrid-type of organizational structure. As such, the AMLC then underwent a two-phase reorganization in 2017 and 2018. This expands the financial intelligence analysis functions to address specific areas as mirrored in the Financial Action Task Force (FATF) recommendations, such as the Compliance and Supervision Group and other specialized units. One of the AMLC’s investigation units is, in fact, dedicated to anti-corruption. As a result of this renewed structure, the AMLC now works as a fully functional Financial Intelligence Unit; a reliable partner in law enforcement, prosecution, and asset forfeiture; and an efficient supervisor. Now, let me share some highlights of the AMLC’s efforts and initiatives this year in undertaking its anti-money laundering and counter-terrorism financing (AML/CTF) operations. The AMLC requires and receives covered and suspicious transaction reports (STRs) from covered persons. The AMLC also collects, evaluates, and analyzes financial information, regarding potential money laundering and terrorism financing; and disseminates financial intelligence reports to law enforcement agencies, foreign financial intelligence units, and other AMLC units to support and assist investigations. Using its database and other sources of information, the AMLC has released the following risk assessments, strategic studies, and typologies in 2021 for the guidance of government and law enforcement agencies; and covered persons: 1. Terrorism and Terrorism Financing Risk Assessment, which is an update of the Second National Risk Assessment, particularly on the understanding and assessment of terrorism and terrorism financing risks in the country; 2. Foreign Terrorist Fighters Study using Suspicious Transaction Reports; 3. Money Service Business [MSB]: 2021 Money Laundering and Terrorism Financing Sector Risk Assessment, which indicates how certain services and products of MSBs are exploited by criminals to facilitate and expand illegal activities; 4. Real Estate Sector: A Money Laundering/Terrorism Financing/Proliferation Financing Assessment, which examines the potential criminal threat environment and vulnerabilities associated with the real estate sector; 5. An Assessment of the Philippines’ Exposure to External and Internal Threats Based on Suspicious Transaction Reports for 2018 to 2020, which looks into the country’s risk and exposure to money laundering, terrorism financing, and different predicate offenses. 6. An Analysis of Suspicious Transaction Reports with Possible Links to Tax Crimes, which is a study on the potential exposure of the Philippines to tax crimes prior to the inclusion of tax evasion as a predicate offense to money laundering by focusing on selected suspicious transaction reports (STRs); and 7. Suspicious Transaction Report Quality Review: 2017 to 2020 Data Discovery that covers 2.4 million STRs, which the AMLC received from 2017 to 2020 from covered persons and that aims to provide a trend analysis for the covered periods; assess the technical compliance and investigative value of the STRs; and identify the reporting gaps and challenges of covered persons to provide guidance on how to improve the quality of STRs. Most of these are available in the AMLC’s website for the guidance of our stakeholders. From January 1 to June 30, 2021, the AMLC has filed 63 cases, varying from money laundering and terrorism financing cases; civil forfeiture cases; and even administrative cases. Through these cases, the AMLC has deprived money launderers and other criminals of over PhP3.3 billion and various assets, including 121 hectares of real properties that were subject of freeze orders related to terrorism and terrorism financing and a five (5)-hectare land from two (2) United Nations Security Council Resolution-designated individuals frozen in 2019. In a Council-issued Resolution in March 2021, real property owned by a certain alleged associate of a designated-terrorist group was also subject of a freeze order. Further, this year, there has been a money laundering case conviction predicated on the violation of the E-Commerce Act in relation to cybercrime. As an efficient supervisor, the AMLC adopts necessary policies, procedures, and guidance, using its assessment of money laundering and terrorism financing trends and standards. The AMLC ensures compliance of covered persons with AML/CTF laws and their corresponding rules and regulations by conducting regular and targeted onsite examinations and offsite supervision of covered persons. So far in 2021, the AMLC has issued several AML/CTF regulations to strengthen the Philippines’ AML/CTF supervisory framework. Among these are: 1. 2021 AMLC Registration and Reporting Guidelines; 2. Amendments to Certain Provisions of the 2018 Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9160, as amended; 3. 2021 Anti-Money Laundering/Counter-Terrorism Financing Guidelines for Designated Non-Financial Businesses and Professions; 4. Amendments to Certain Provisions of AMLC Regulatory Issuance No. 4, Series of 2020; 5. 2021 Sanctions Guidelines: Targeted Financial Sanctions Related to Terrorism, Terrorism Financing, and Proliferation Financing [of Weapons of Mass Destruction]; 6. Guidance for De-Listing and Unfreezing Procedures; 7. Notice of AMLC No. TF-42, Series of 2021, directing the issuance of sanctions freeze order to take effect immediately against the National Democratic Front, also known as the National Democratic Front of the Philippines, pursuant to its designation as terrorist by the Anti-Terrorism Council (ATC) Resolution No. 21 (2021); 8. Notice of AMLC Resolution No. TF-41, Series of 2021, directing the issuance of sanctions freeze order to take effect immediately against members of the Abu Sayyaf Group and other terrorist groups, pursuant to their designation as terrorist by ATC Resolution No. 20 (2021); 9. AMLC Resolution TF No. 35, Series of 2021 on Anti-Terrorism Council Resolution No. 10, Series of 2020 on the Designation by Adoption of the Consolidated Lists under UNSC Resolutions 1267 (1999), 1989 (2011), 2253 (2015) and their Successor Resolution; 10. AMLC Resolution No. TF-39, Series of 2021 on sanctions freeze order against property or funds that are owned or controlled, directly or indirectly, including property or funds derived or generated therefrom, by the subject of domestic designations by the ATC under Resolution Nos. 16, Series of 2021; and 11. AMLC Resolution No. TF-40, Series of 2021 on sanctions freeze order against property or funds that are owned or controlled, directly or indirectly, including property or funds derived or generated therefrom, by the subject of domestic designations by the ATC under Resolution Nos. 17, Series of 2021. The AMLC’s core functions are supported by the AMLC’s overarching support services, that, among others, ensure the country’s compliance with international AML/CTF standards. In 2018, the Philippines underwent its Third Mutual Evaluation, where the country was assessed on its levels of technical compliance with international AML/CTF standards; and effectiveness compliance of its existing AML/CTF mechanisms. It will be recalled that the country’s First and Second MEs only involved technical compliance. With the passage of the Anti-Terrorism Act of 2020; the passage of Republic Act No. 11521, which further amends the Anti-Money Laundering Act of 2001; and the issuance of necessary implementing rules and regulations last year, the Philippines was able to address its technical deficiencies. Adopting compliant laws and regulations, however, is not sufficient. The effectivenes

Title: Amplifying Momentum Towards a Strong PH Economic Recovery
Occasion: DBCC Briefing to Senate Committee on Finance
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Senate President Vicente Sotto III, Committee Chair Juan Edgardo Angara, members of the Senate Committee on Finance, members of the economic team, colleagues, ladies and gentlemen, good morning. The Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas, as a resource institution of the Development Budget Coordination Committee, will present the macroeconomic assumptions underlying the fiscal year 2022 proposed National Government budget along with the key developments and prospects supporting our assessment on monetary, external and banking sectors. More than a year since the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, the Philippine economy is gradually recovering. The unprecedented crisis called for an unprecedented response and the whole-of-government acted promptly and decisively. The government’s mass vaccination program is picking up its pace and scope and is expected to accelerate further. The Bayanihan 1 and 2 as well as the provisions under the 2021 National Budget were designed to support the safe re-opening of the economy. Moreover, the government passed into law reforms that could foster a strong and faster economic rebound while limiting the scarring effects of the pandemic on the economy. This includes the passage of the Financial Institutions Strategic Transfer or FIST Act (RA 11523) and the Corporate Recovery and Tax Incentives for Enterprises or CREATE Act (RA 11534). Meanwhile, the proposed Government Financial Institutions Unified Initiatives to Distressed Enterprises for Economic Recovery or GUIDE Bill aims to strengthen the capacity of government financial institutions such as the Philippine Guarantee Corporation, Land Bank of the Philippines, and the Development Bank of the Philippines, to assist micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs) cope with the effects of COVID-19. Given that the nature of the current crisis is primarily an issue of public health, the government’s public health measures, fiscal stimulus, and structural reforms have stood as the country’s major response against the pandemic. Complementing the government’s responses, the BSP has implemented temporary and time-bound monetary policy measures to ensure ample liquidity in the financial system, restore financial market functioning, and shore up market confidence. These measures include a mix of decisive monetary policy adjustments and extraordinary liquidity measures along with regulatory relief and forbearance measures. On its recent policy meeting, the Monetary Board decided to keep its monetary policy settings unchanged. The Monetary Board remains keen on sustaining monetary policy support for as long as necessary in order for the momentum of economic recovery to gain more traction as well as to help boost domestic demand and market confidence, especially as risk aversion continues to temper credit activity. As of 2 September 2021, the BSP’s measures have injected into the financial system about ₱2.3 trillion in liquidity, equivalent to 12.7 percent of the country’s full year nominal GDP for 2020. Given the whole-of-government response and bolstered by years of resolute reforms, the resulting 2020 key economic indicators show that the domestic economy had remained resilient against the adverse effects of the global pandemic. The country’s macroeconomic fundamentals remain generally intact as evidenced by the quarter-on-quarter improvements in gross domestic product outturn towards the year-end; within-target inflation; ample financial system liquidity; and robust external payments position. At the same time, the banking system continues to be stable and resilient while the public sector debt remains manageable. In the next slides, I will go through the recent trends and developments for 2021 in the real, monetary, financial, and external sectors of the economy. The Philippine economy begins its positive growth. After five consecutive quarters of economic contractions, the economy posted a positive growth in the second quarter of 2021, with real GDP expanding by 11.8 percent from a 17.0 percent contraction in Q2 2020. In terms of production, the expansion was mainly contributed by manufacturing (Q2 2021: +22.3%, Q1 2021: +0.5% ), construction (+25.7%, -22.6%) and wholesale and retail trade (+5.4%, -3.4%). By expenditure, growth was driven by household consumption (+7.2%, -4.7%), public construction (+49.7%, +25.3%), and investment in durable equipment (+89.2%, -10.3%). Almost all sectors and expenditure segments of the economy bounced back during the quarter. This seems to indicate that the country is learning how to live with the virus by managing risks through the safe re-opening of the economy and calibrated re-imposition of granular quarantine restrictions only where it is necessary. Headline inflation rose to 4.9 percent in August 2021 from 4.0 percent in July, bringing the year-to-date average inflation to 4.4 percent. The increase in headline inflation was driven mainly by higher price increases of key food items, particularly vegetables and fish, reflecting impact of strong monsoon rains. Meanwhile, increased electricity rates due to higher generation and transmission charges along with increased prices of LPG contributed to the rise in non-food inflation. Core inflation, which excludes selected volatile food and energy items to depict underlying demand-side price pressures, also increased to 3.3 percent in August from 2.9 percent in July. Average inflation is projected to settle slightly above the upper end of the government target band in 2021. Nevertheless, with the continued and timely implementation of direct supply measures and programs to mitigate supply-side pressures on meat and other food prices, inflation is projected to ease towards the midpoint of the target range in 2022 and 2023. Latest assessment likewise indicates that the risks to the inflation outlook remain broadly balanced over the policy horizon. Moving on…. Domestic liquidity in the financial system remains ample. M3 expanded by 5.9 percent year-on-year in July 2021, slower than the 6.5-percent growth recorded in June. Meanwhile, bank lending activity shows some indications of improvement. Preliminary data show that outstanding loans of universal and commercial banks (U/KBs), net of reverse repurchase (RRP) placements with the BSP, decreased by 0.7 percent year-on-year in July, slower compared to the 2.0-percent contraction in June. On a month-on-month seasonally-adjusted basis, outstanding universal and commercial bank loans, net of RRPs, went up by 0.5 percent. However, credit activity has remained weak due to risk aversion and weak economic sentiment. Bank credit continues to decline as the necessary measures to address the still elevated number of COVID-19 cases constrained domestic economic activity and dampened market sentiment. On the whole, Philippine banks have continued to rein in the NPL ratio within manageable level, reflecting gains from prudent reforms and improvements in banks’ credit risk management systems. Gross non-performing loans ratio increased to 4.5 percent while the NPL coverage ratio of the banking system declined to 82.4 percent in June. Moving forward, the BSP anticipates that the non-performing loans of the banking system will remain within manageable levels. The existing regulatory relief measures serve as an interim measure pending the full operationalization of the FIST Act in 2021. The FIST Act will reinforce banks’ capital and liquidity position in the long-term by allowing financial institutions to dispose their non-performing assets, increase their risk-bearing capacity, and enhance their capability to provide financial services to productive sectors of the economy. Moreover, Philippine banks remain well-capitalized. As of the first quarter of 2021, the capital adequacy ratio (CAR) of the banking system is at 16.9 percent, well above the 10 percent minimum threshold set by the BSP. The BSP stands committed and ready with its enhanced onsite and off-site surveillance tools and prudential policy toolkit to promote the financial sector reform agenda for sustaining the sound and stable financial system conducive to a strong, dynamic and inclusive economic growth recovery of the country. On the external sector, the gross international reserves (GIR), which stood at US$107.2 billion as of July 2021, continue to provide more than adequate external liquidity buffer. The GIR level is equivalent to 12.2 months’ worth of imports of goods and payments of services and primary income. It is also about 7.7 times the country’s short-term external debt based on original maturity and 5.2 times based on residual maturity. The GIR is supported by improving inflows of cash remittance from overseas workers. On the foreign exchange market, the recent peso depreciation is partly influenced by broad US dollar strength driven by risk-off sentiment over the spread of the Delta COVID-19 variant and to markets’ perceived shift to a hawkish tone by the US Federal Reserve. In addition, the observed pick-up in corporate dollar demand as the economy gradually re-opens and the Fitch Ratings’ outlook downgrade for the country’s sovereign credit rating added pressure on the peso. However, the more than adequate gross international reserves is expected to temper the peso depreciation. In addition, we expect the peso to be supported by structural FX flows through recovery in exports, overseas Filipino remittances, and business process outsourcing receipts. FX inflows related to foreign direct investments are also expected to help shore up the currency. Mr. Chair and the members of the Senate Committee on Finance, let me now present the macroeconomic assumptions underlying the FY 2022 National Government budget: As mentioned, inflation is projected to settle close to the high end of the inflation target for 2021 and decelerate near the midpoint of the target band for 2022. On global crude oil prospects, elevated global oil prices could persist in the near term given improve

Title: Paving the Way to Recovery and New Post-COVID-19 Economy
Occasion: Virtual Philippine Economic Briefing in Japan (2021)
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Ambassador Jose C. Laurel, fellow officials and colleagues from the economic cluster of the Philippine government, officers and staff of SMBC Group, guests, ladies and gentlemen, good afternoon. I thank our Japanese audience for being here and for the interest on the Philippine economy. The covid-19 pandemic has brought unprecedented challenges across the world. The Philippines was not spared, as the economy contracted last year after 21 consecutive years of growth. But a year and a half since the onset of the health crisis, we have learned to better deal with its challenges. To expound on this, I will cover three things in my speech: • First, macroeconomic, monetary, and external accounts updates. • Second, banking sector developments. • And third, our vision of a Post-COVID 19 Economy. On macroeconomic, monetary, and external accounts updates… The Philippine economy is recovering. It rebounded strongly in the second quarter, with a growth of 11.8 percent, thus ending the pandemic-driven recession. Inflation averaged 4.4 percent for the first seven months of this year. The Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) expects inflation to average slightly above the upper end of the 2.0- to 4.0-percent target band this year due to supply issues, such as rising global oil prices and constraints in meat importation. Then, we see inflation easing toward the midpoint of the target range by next year and 2023 amid government measures to improve supply of meat and other food items. Our inflation projections are in line with expectations of relevant observers. Given manageable inflation, and in support of economic recovery, BSP kept policy rates steady during the policy meeting last August 12. We will again review the policy settings on September 23. The policy rate stands at a record low of 2.0 percent after BSP did a series of policy rate cuts last year. We also cut the reserve requirement and did other actions to ensure ample liquidity in the financial system, which in turn buoyed market confidence during a critical time. These include purchases of government securities in the secondary market, as well as advance remittance of dividends and extension of short-term financing to the National Government. In total, the BSP has so far infused PHP2.2 trillion (or US$44 billion) to the financial system, equivalent to 12.5 percent of the country’s GDP. Recovery is at its nascent phase. We are mindful that unwinding policy support prematurely will be costly. As such, as the inflation outlook allows, BSP commits to keeping monetary policy accommodative until we see hard evidence of full recovery. The country’s external accounts remain robust. Gross international reserves (GIR) are hefty at US$107.2 billion as of end-July this year. This is 7.7 times the country’s short-term external debt and provides 12.2 months’ worth of import cover, which is above the internationally accepted threshold of 3 months. GIR is supported in part by overseas workers’ remittances, which have been resilient throughout the pandemic. Overseas Filipino remittances in 2020 reached USD30 billion, the same as in 2019, defying forecasts that it will fall by double-digit rates. Remittances grew by 6.4 percent in the first semester and are expected to grow by 4.0 percent for the full year 2021. Japan is a steady source of remittances, accounting for over 5.0 percent of total last year. Receipts from business process outsourcing (BPO) industry is another steady source of foreign exchange. In 2020, BPO receipts remained resilient, declining only by 1.3 percent and it is projected to increase by 5.0 percent this year. Foreign direct investments (FDIs) have risen, reflecting improving investor confidence. Japan has been a vital source of FDIs, more so during this pandemic. Bucking trends, Japan’s FDIs to the Philippines more than doubled last year to nearly US$700 million. Moving forward, we expect FDIs to rise further given latest developments, such as enactment of the Corporate Recovery and Tax Incentives for Enterprises (CREATE) law. As the Finance Secretary will expound later, this law improves the competitiveness of the country’s corporate tax and fiscal incentives regime. Driven by the sources of FX, plus the rise in imports mostly of capital goods amid the recovery process, the overall balance of payments (BOP) posted a small deficit in the first semester. We expect BOP to end this year with a surplus of US$7.1 billion, equivalent to 1.8 percent of GDP. Healthy external accounts will continue to support the exchange rate. The peso, together with other emerging market currencies, depreciated lately due to strengthening of the US dollar amid anticipation of policy normalization by the US Fed. But the peso volatility has been manageable. Moving forward, the peso will be supported by our hefty GIR, exports revenues, remittances, FDIs, and BPO receipts. The country’s external accounts are also supported by enabling policies. BSP further liberalized FX rules last month. We now allow electronic submission of documents, use of e-signatures, and funding of peso deposit accounts of non-residents with peso receipts from loans and investments. We also lifted the prior approval requirement for derivatives transactions of non-bank government entities. Constant enhancement of FX rules supports overall efforts toward capital market development. BSP’s bilateral arrangements also promotes strong external payments position of the Philippines. The BSP has signed a Bilateral Swap Arrangement and a Cross Border Liquidity Arrangement with the Bank of Japan. The BSP is keen on pursuing other areas of cooperation with Japan such as the establishment of a Philippine Peso-Japanese Yen Direct Settlement Framework. Moving on to banking sector developments… Philippine banks have shown resilience to the COVID-19 crisis, benefiting from risk-management and other regulatory reforms of the past two decades. Banks have ample buffers against shocks. Capital adequacy ratio (CAR) is at 17.4 percent, well above the 8.0 percent prescribed by the Bank for International Settlements (BIS) and the BSP’s 10.0 percent minimum requirement. Exposure to bad debts remains manageable. Average non-performing loans (NPL) ratio stood at 4.0 percent as of end-June this year, much lower than the double-digit figures in the aftermath of the Asian financial crisis. Banking sector stability throughout the pandemic was also aided in part by BSP’s regulatory relief measures for banks and their customers. The time-bound relief measures will be in place until such time that the new Financial Institutions Strategic Transfer (FIST) Act, signed earlier this year, becomes fully operational. Under this new law, financial institutions may dispose their non-performing assets through FIST corporations, thereby maintaining soundness of their asset quality. BSP’s support measures, together with government’s pandemic response, have supported confidence and optimism in the banking industry. The latest Banking Sector Outlook Survey showed that banks are optimistic for the next two years, expecting double-digit increase in assets, net income, investments, deposits, and loans. Now let me talk about our vision of a “Post-COVID-19 Economy.” The Philippines aims not only to regain what was lost from the COVID crisis. We want the economy to be stronger, more technologically advanced, more sustainable, and more inclusive than ever before. BSP is actively contributing to this vision. One way we are doing so is through financial digitalization. BSP has launched the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap, under which we aim to achieve two major objectives by 2023: • At least half of financial transactions in the country are done digitally; and • At least 70 percent of Filipino adults have financial accounts. But with the pandemic as the catalyst, we expect to achieve both goals sooner than planned. Financial digitalization accelerates income growth and makes affordable financial products and services accessible to more people. To achieve the twin goals of financial digitalization, BSP has several initiatives. We have issued the “Open Finance Framework,” which allows consent-driven use of customers’ information for the creation of innovative financial products and services catering to their needs. We have also released regulatory guidelines for digital banks, thus providing an enabling environment for growth of digital banking. So far, BSP has approved license applications of five purely digital banks. We are confident of hitting our goals under the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap. The volume of electronic payments has grown by leaps and bounds. Also, since the onset of the pandemic had limited mobility, over 4 million electronic financial accounts have been created. One positive, unintended consequence of the lockdown, and the associated mobility restrictions caused by the pandemic is that people have embraced the convenience of digital payments. As we build our digital finance ecosystem, the Philippines hopes to attract foreign investments, which may bring advanced technologies and technical expertise. Foreign investors are welcome to do business in the Philippines, to take advantage of untapped potentials in the financial services space and, in doing so, be our partners in achieving our economic growth and financial inclusion objectives. Another way BSP is pursuing the post-COVID-19 economy is through work on sustainable finance… As the world endeavors to end the pandemic and move on to the “new normal,” mankind is obligated to ensure this does not mean reverting to ways that harm the environment. BSP is supporting legislation that will count loans to green projects as part of banks’ compliance with the mandatory lending for agriculture. We have also issued the Sustainable Finance Framework, under which banks are encouraged to design instruments that will help mobilize funds for green or sustainable projects. Moving forward, we will i

Title: Moving from Crisis, to Recovery, and further to the 'New Economy'
Occasion: Official Monetary and Financial Institutions Forum
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: Officers and staff of the Official Monetary and Financial Institutions Forum (OMFIF), fellow central bankers and regulators, other public and private-sector guests, ladies and gentlemen, a pleasant day to all of you. I thank OMFIF for inviting me again to speak before your stakeholders. My task here is to update everyone on economic developments in the Philippines—a year-and-a-half into the global COVID-19 crisis. My presentation will be divided into three parts: (i) where the Philippine economy is; (ii) where we envision the Philippine economy will be; and (iii) how we intend to reach our desired destination. The Philippine economy has started to recover, growing by 11.8 percent in the second quarter of 2021, thus ending the pandemic-induced recession. The second quarter growth was not solely due to base effects. Growth was broad-based, driven by manufacturing, construction and wholesale and retail trade on the production side and by household consumption, public construction and investment in durable equipment on the expenditure side. Unemployment rate improved to 6.9 percent in July this year, the lowest since the pandemic’s historic high of 17.6 percent in April 2020. Manufacturing activity was on expansion mode, with the purchasing manager’s index (PMI) at 50.8 in June and 50.4 in July this year. But it suffered a temporary setback in August owing to the imposition of stricter lockdown to curb the spread of the Delta variant. By the way, the decline in PMI is Asia wide. Meanwhile, inflation rose to 4.9 percent year-on-year in August. The year-to-date average inflation rate of 4.4 percent in the first eight months is above the upper bound of the Government’s annual inflation target of 3.0 percent ± 1.0 percentage point. The increase in headline inflation was driven mainly by higher price increases of key food items, particularly vegetables and fish due to supply disruptions from damages caused by monsoon rains in parts of the country. Meanwhile, increased electricity rates due to higher generation and transmission charges along with increased prices of liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) contributed to the rise in year-on-year non-food inflation. The latest CPI outturn is consistent with the BSP’s assessment that inflation could settle close to the high end of the target range in the near term before decelerating back to within the target by year end. In 2022-2023, inflation is expected to decline towards the midpoint of the target, supported by the continued and timely implementation of non-monetary measures and reforms to address directly supply-side pressures on key food items. Our inflation projections are in line with expectations of relevant observers. Given manageable inflation, and in support of economic recovery, BSP kept policy rates steady during the policy meeting last August 12. Latest figures on actual and pledged investments are encouraging. Foreign direct investments (FDIs) jumped 40.7 percent from January to June. Foreign investment applications approved in Q2 2021 grew by 45.5 percent, reaching PHP22.5 billion (USD445.5 million). The top foreign nationals investing in the country came from United Kingdom, South Korea, USA, Netherlands and China. Trade has recovered from last year’s contraction… Imports grew by 24.0 percent in July. With bulk of the country’s imports being raw materials and intermediate goods, imports recovery is a welcome signal of rising investments in anticipation of higher demand. Similarly, exports jumped by 12.7 percent in the same period. Overseas Filipino remittances in 2020 reached almost USD30 billion, slightly less compared to 2019, defying forecasts that it will fall by double-digit rates. During the first six months of this year, remittances grew by 6.4 percent. The uptrend in remittance inflows is expected to continue as COVID-19 vaccination levels increase domestically and in OFW host economies, travel restrictions ease further and countries safely open to foreign workers. External accounts remain robust. Gross international reserves (GIR) are equivalent to more than one year of imports—versus the received doctrine that three months of import cover is already sufficient. The banking system remains stable and resilient, thanks to regulatory reforms over the past two decades. Loan quality remains manageable, with the non-performing loans ratio of universal and commercial banks was at 4.0 percent as of end-June this year. This is far from the double-digit figures seen in the aftermath of the Asian financial crisis. Banks have ample buffers, with the capital adequacy ratio of 17.4 percent. This is well above the international standard of 8 percent and the central bank’s regulatory requirement of 10 percent. All these indicators show that the Philippines is well into the recovery process, aided by COVID-response measures from the fiscal and the monetary side. The Central Bank implemented extraordinary measures to ensure ample liquidity in the financial system, support orderly functioning of the financial market, and shore up market confidence. These measures include a mix of decisive monetary policy adjustments, liquidity enhancing measures and regulatory relief and forbearance measures. The BSP has so far infused PHP2.2 trillion or US$44 billion—equivalent to about 12.5 percent of GDP—in liquidity to the financial system. To maintain stability of the financial system and ensure public access to financial services, we implemented a long list of measures to ease the impact of the crisis on the balance sheet of banks, so they can help their crisis-hit customers as well. With the Philippines’ economic recovery underway, we remain optimistic that the country’s medium – to long-term growth prospects remain robust. Nevertheless, we recognize that the road to full recovery is not a walk in the park. With the spread of the more contagious Delta variant in many Asian countries in recent months, the Philippines’ COVID cases have also spiked. We are proactively containing the spread of the Delta and other variants through a risk-based approach in quarantine restrictions allowing important businesses/sectors to operate, including public transportation, but restricting non-essential activities. We are also using this period to vaccinate as many as we can. The lockdown is expected to dampen the Philippine economy’s nascent recovery —but only temporarily. There is basis for cautious optimism. Nationwide vaccinations have accelerated. The Philippines is now inoculating more than 400,000 individuals per day. At this rate, the country may achieve “population protection” by the fourth quarter of this year. Moving on to my second point on where we envision the Philippine economy to be… Before the pandemic, the Philippines was poised to transition from “low middle income” to “upper middle income” status in 2020. We also had achieved great progress in poverty reduction. Poverty rate fell from 23.5 percent in 2015 to 16.7 percent in 2018—and was poised to drop further to 14 percent by 2022. Unemployment rate was down to a record low of 4.5 percent in October 2019. The economy posted 21 years of uninterrupted growth up to 2019, expanding even at the height of the Global Financial Crisis. These achievements were made possible in part by price and financial stability, aided by the BSP’s sound monetary policy and financial sector regulation. With the Philippine economy starting to recover, the National Government’s fiscal and public health measures remain crucial in sustaining the momentum. The road to economic recovery requires purposeful and coordinated efforts - a whole of government approach. The BSP—complementing the stimulus from the fiscal side—will continue supporting the economy, until the recovery fully gets underway. Looking ahead, we do not aim to simply regain the economic losses from the pandemic. We aspire for a “Post-COVID-19 Philippine Economy” that is stronger and more resilient, more technologically advanced, and more inclusive than ever before. The BSP supports the realization of the "Post-COVID-19 Economy” through commitment to its price and financial stability mandates, and push for innovation in the financial sector. The Philippines also aims to be a technologically advanced economy that has high regard for the environment. On sustainability, the Philippines last April submitted its commitment with the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change to reduce its projected carbon emission by 75 percent by 2030. By then, we want sustainability deeply engrained in the consciousness of Filipinos and factored in by businesses in their operations On my third point – on how we are moving towards our desired destination The Philippines is pursuing its goals in many ways, but three are top of mind. First is by further liberalizing the economy and making it even more investor friendly. The Philippines is a signatory to the Regional Comprehensive Economic Partnership (RCEP)—a mega free-trade deal signed by select Asia-Pacific economies in November last year. RCEP signatories account for nearly a third of global output. Once it takes effect, RCEP will open the gates of member economies to much more trade and investments. RCEP is important for the Philippines, more so because it will drive growth of our micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs), which account for two-thirds of employment in the country. Businesses operating in RCEP economies will be able to source raw materials within the bloc at cheaper cost, and export finished goods to other RCEP members under preferential arrangements. Also, RCEP will help the Philippines attract export-oriented firms that want to take advantage of free trade with our neighbors. There is much economic energy waiting to be unleashed in the coming period. Our recovery will also get a boost with the implementation of the CREATE law or the Corporate Recovery and Tax Incentives for Enterprises. This is the largest economic stimulus program for businesses

Title: The Philippine’s Path to Economic Recovery Amid the Pandemic
Occasion: Joint General Membership Meeting of ACI and MART
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ To the officers and staff of the Financial Markets Association; Fund Managers Association of the Philippines; Investment House Association of the Philippines; Money Market Association of the Philippines; National Association of Securities Broker Salesmen, Incorporated; Trust Officers Association of the Philippines; and partners and stakeholders in the banking and finance industry; ladies and gentlemen, good afternoon. Thank you for this opportunity to share the good news as our country continues on its path towards economic recovery. It has been a year-and-a-half since the onslaught of the pandemic. Uncertainties remain. But the country’s sound economic fundamentals give us reason to be optimistic during these times. Let me give you an overview of the state of the economy right now, how we at the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas have responded towards recovery, and how we defy the odds as we push forward. Overall, we have started this year in a position of strength. We saw signs of recovery when our country’s gross domestic product or GDP recorded a double-digit growth in the second quarter of 2021. You see there in red five quarters of pandemic-induced recession before we hit the 11.8-percent growth. This second quarter of 2021 saw most industries and services, including public transportation, continuing its operation amid the reimposition of the Modified Enhanced Community Quarantine in the National Capital Region. As I mentioned in the previous slide, the 11.8-percent growth is a strong rebound as it marked an end to 15 months of economic slump. Our labor force continues to recover as we build on this growth in the second quarter. Unemployment rate fell to its lowest since the start of the pandemic—down to 6.9 percent in July 2021. We also see favorable trends in our manufacturing indicators particularly in the Purchasing Managers Index, the volume and value of production indices, as well as the average capacity utilization rate. In terms of confidence in the market, we have observed improvement in both the overall consumer and business sentiment. However, the less optimistic outlook in the second quarter of this year was attributed to the surge in covid cases, the reimposition of the lockdown, and higher prices due to supply constraints. Another source of optimism is the credit rating upgrades and affirmations, which are votes of confidence in the government’s ability to stage a strong recovery from the health crisis. For August, inflation rate rose to 4.9 percent. This is at the high end of BSP’s monthly forecast range of 4.1 to 4.9 percent, and above the target band of 2 percent to 4 percent, which is consistent with the BSP’s assessment that inflation could settle above the high end of the target range in the near term before decelerating back to within the target by year end. Moving forward, we will continue to monitor price developments to help ensure that we achieve our primary mandate of price stability. Moving on to the external sector…. The export and import of goods both increased by more than 10 percent in the first quarter of 2021. These amounted to US$12.9 billion and US$ 24 billion, respectively, reflecting an US$ 11.1 billion trade deficit as imports outpace exports. This development reflects the sustained recovery momentum of the trade sector in the wake of the government’s containment measures at the onset of the pandemic. We also see a 37.8-percent increase in the foreign direct investment inflows for the period January-June 2021 as compared to the same period last year. However, there is a decline in net inflow in May, which reflected the renewed investor concerns on the rising cases of the new COVID-19 variants. The seven-percent increase in June in cash remittances from overseas Filipinos hit its highest level in six months, climbing to nearly US$15 billion the year-to-date remittance level. We can expect the uptrend in remittance inflows as travel restrictions ease further and global economies now reopen. Meanwhile, our country’s gross international reserves reached US$ 108.05 billion by end-Aug 2021. This reflects a more-than-adequate level of external liquidity buffer equivalent to 12.3 months’ worth of imports of goods and payments of services and primary income. Our outstanding external debt narrowed to US$ 97 billion in March 2021. Related to this, our external debt-to-GDP ratio of 26.7 percent in the first quarter of the year shows our strong position to service foreign borrowings in the medium to long-term. It ensures that our debt payments remain manageable and sustainable. Liquidity and credit conditions also remain supportive of growth. Domestic liquidity or M3 remains ample. M3 expanded by 5.9 percent year-on-year to about P 14.4 trillion in July 2021. Meanwhile, bank lending activity shows some signs of improvement. The year-on-year rate of decline in outstanding loans is slower at 0.7 percent relative to the previous month, while the month-on-month change is positive. Total outstanding loans for production activities expanded by 0.8 percent in July following a contraction of 0.6 percent in the previous month. This is the first time that outstanding production loans has increased since the reported growth rate of 0.5 percent in November 2020. Philippine banks remain well-capitalized. The capital adequacy ratio of the banking system in the first quarter of the year is at 16.9 percent, well above the 10-percent minimum threshold set by the BSP. On the whole, banks have continued to rein in the non-performing loan ratio within manageable levels, reflecting gains from prudent reforms and improvements in banks’ credit risk management systems. More than maintaining these indicators, the Bangko Sentral is committed to sustaining a sound financial system conducive to a strong and inclusive economic recovery. We keep up with the whole-of-government tempo with these three major interventions: First, the BSP put in place measures to boost market confidence on cost and availability of credit resources, such as cuts in the policy rate and the reserve requirement. The BSP reduced its policy rate by a total of 200 basis points to help bring down the cost of borrowing and shore up market confidence. Meanwhile, the lower reserve requirement—or the proportion of deposits banks must keep as reserves in the BSP—increased the volume of loanable funds. The BSP also allowed banks to count newly granted loans to micro, small and medium enterprises, and large enterprises as alternative compliance to reserve requirement. Second, the BSP adopted extraordinary liquidity measures. These include purchases of government securities in the secondary market, remittance of P35 billion in advance dividends, and provisional advances to the national government. In sum, the BSP has infused about P 2.23 trillion in liquidity, equivalent to 12.5 percent of the country’s GDP for 2020. Third, the BSP implemented regulatory and operational relief measures to maintain stability of the financial system and ensure public access to financial services were implemented. The BSP issued time-bound and targeted regulatory and operational relief measures to encourage BSP-supervised financial institutions to continue their support to the economy, with particular emphasis on the MSME sector. Indeed, we have done much in implementing extraordinary measures. Yet our action will remain anchored to our mandate of promoting price and financial stability, working on appropriate policy responses as necessary. On monetary policy: we will remain vigilant over the current inflation dynamics to ensure that the monetary policy stance continues to support economic recovery. We will carefully scan the operating environment with a forward-looking perspective to move in a pre-emptive fashion to address any risk to our price stability mandate. On the financial sector: we will intensify its monitoring and surveillance over BSP’s supervised institutions to ensure responsiveness to emerging risks. At the same time, we will intensify the pursuit of financial digitalization to enhance the soundness, stability, resilience, and inclusivity of the banking system. Third, on the external sector: we will remain supportive of policies that will help strengthen the economy’s resilience to external shocks, including that of maintaining a market-determined exchange rate, and keeping a comfortable level of reserves and manageable external debt. On top of these initiatives, we will also promote financial digitalization and a sustainable finance. The desired outcome is a “post-COVID-19 economy” that is stronger, more technologically advanced, and more inclusive than ever before. Towards this end, the BSP launched the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap for 2020-2023 last year. With this roadmap, we aim to transform the country into a cash-light society through digitalization of payments and increasing the number of Filipinos with access to financial services. The use of digital payments also helps reduce the need for mobility and prevent health risks from face-to-face and over-the-counter financial transactions. The recent figures confirms that the value of transactions done through InstaPay expanded by more than 180 percent, and through PesoNet, by more than 80 percent in the first seven months of 2021. While we move towards a post-COVID-19 Economy, we will continue to complement the national government’s broad-based pandemic-relief initiatives and provide the needed liquidity to support economic activity and maintain the favorable market sentiment. There is no doubt that we have already made solid economic strides amid these trying times. We reaffirm that the monetary policy stance will remain accommodative until economic recovery is fully underway. Thank you. ​

Title: The Rise of Challenger Banks
Occasion: MAP Webinar Series
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: A pleasant day to everyone! Thank you for inviting me to speak at this webinar entitled “The Rise of Challenger Banks”. Indeed, this event by the Management Association of the Philippines is an excellent opportunity to collaborate and see the digital revolution from different perspectives. But first, allow me to share with you the country’s digital payments landscape based on the 2019 update and 2020 preview on Philippine digital payments conducted by the Better Than Cash Alliance or “BTCA” in partnership with the BSP. In terms of the total volume of payments in the country, 17 percent were made digitally in the first half of 2020, as compared with 10 percent in 2018. Consumer payments make up 78 percent of the 4.6 billion monthly payments, followed by corporate payments at 21 percent. Consistent with the previous diagnostics, the government continues to lead the way in digitization with some 87 percent or P319 billion equivalent of payments by the government (G2X) already in digital form, up from 64 percent in 2018. The same is followed by payments made by people (P2X) and businesses (B2X) at 35 percent and 15 percent, respectively. Noteworthy is the rise in volume of digital merchant payments by 66 percent since 2018. Essentially, this increase pushed the strong upward trend in the overall volume of merchant payments driven by electronic fund transfers and the heightened usage of prepaid cards. As you know, the BSP launched in 2020 the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap, a blueprint for the development of an efficient, inclusive, safe and secure digital payments ecosystem that supports the diverse needs and capabilities of individuals and firms alike. In this journey, two strategic outcomes are emphasized. First is the goal to convert at least 50 percent of the total volume of retail payments into digital form, and second is to make available varied innovative and responsive digital financial products and services. The roadmap also aims to onboard at least 70 percent of Filipino adults to the financial system through the ownership and use of a transaction account. Over time, the increased usage of transaction accounts will enable Filipinos to build financial profiles that further unlock access to more complex financial services. According to World Bank article entitled “Digital Financial Services” published in April 2020, access to affordable financial services is critical for poverty reduction and economic growth. Countries with deeper, more developed financial systems enjoy higher economic growth and larger reductions in poverty and income inequality. Fintech-enabled Digital Financial Services (DFS), has the potential to lower costs, increase speed, security and transparency and allow for more tailored financial services that serve the poor at scale. DFS would also allow governments to provide welfare grants and other forms of financial assistance to vulnerable people in a fast and secure way. With the issuance of Digital Banking Framework, the Philippines is a step closer to reaching its strategic outcomes. On our end, the BSP has long recognized the role of digital platforms in finding greater efficiency in the delivery of financial products and services. We also see the recent issuance of the digital banking framework as an integral building block in promoting an enabling regulatory environment that fosters responsible innovation, promotes cyber resilience and contributes towards advancing the digitalization of the financial sector. As such, we established a specific classification for digital banks. Due to their digital-centric business model, the operation of these banks should be underpinned by sound digital governance, robust, secure and resilient technology infrastructure, and effective data management strategy and practices. This digital banking framework is particularly relevant as billions of people affected by the COVID-19 pandemic are driving a “historic and dramatic shift in consumer behavior”. That’s according to the latest research from PricewaterhouseCoopers or PwC. The consulting and accounting firm's June 2021 Global Consumer Insights Pulse Survey reported a strong shift to online shopping as people were first confined by lockdowns, and continued to work-from-home. Another significant finding from the report is that consumers do not think they’ll go back to their old ways of shopping once the pandemic is over. This may change the business models of companies. This remarkable result us supported by a the study conducted by Visa in the Philippines which also showed that around 70 percent of Filipinos who used digital payment channels during the lockdown will likely continue to do so. To date, the Monetary Board (MB) has already approved the applications of six (6) banks. The Monetary Board approval refers to the first of the three-stage licensing framework of the BSP on the establishment of banks. • On March 25, 2021, the MB approved the first application which is from the Overseas Filipino Bank (OFBank) seeking to convert its banking license from a thrift bank to a digital bank. • On June 3, 2021, the MB approvedthe applications of (1) Tonik Digital Bank, Inc., for conversion of its rural banking license to a digital bank license, and (2) UNObank, Inc. for the establishment of a new digital bank. • On July 15, 2021, MB approved the application of Union Digital Bank, a wholly-owned subsidiary of UnionBank of the Philippines. • On August 12, 2021, MB also approved the application of GoTyme Bank, a partnership between JG Summit Holdings and a South African banking group. • And lastly, on September 16, 2021, MB also approved the application of Maya Bank, Inc., an affiliate of PLDT’s Voyager Innovations, Inc. Based on their profiles, these digibanks intend to serve the OFWs, underserved, unbanked and the mass market, but are open to venture into investments, insurance, and payment services, to broaden their reach. On August 19, 2021, the BSP issued Memorandum No. M-2021-046 announced the closure of the application window for new digital banks, including converting banks after August 31, 2021. This is to closely monitor the digital banks’ performance and contribution to the country’s financial inclusion goals, impact on the banking system, and level of competition. Following such announcement until August 31, the BSP received nine applications from new players and existing banks. These are on top of the two digital bank applications which were under evaluation prior to the announcement. These nine applications are processed on a first-come, first-served basis. The application documents are also assessed by the BSP for completeness and sufficiency against the documentary requirements and applicant bank’s compliance with the licensing criteria for the establishment of digital banks. Currently, with the recent approval of Maya Bank, only one digibank license remains to be contested by nine aspirants. In any case, this new category is not without its unique challenges. Data privacy concerns, money laundering, and electronic frauds are among the issues that can undermine the confidence in this policy initiative. We therefore expect that the key stakeholders particularly the financial institutions, to adopt adequate measures and controls to manage such risks. Foremost of which is the conduct of an effective information campaign to ensure that customers are aware of the risks and how they can perform their transactions securely. I want to emphasize that consumers must be made aware of consumer protection mechanisms. On the other end of spectrum, other government agencies are also expected to collaborate and address the digital divide. It entails ensuring access to IT infrastructure by people from all walks of life to achieve inclusivity. Given all these developments, what does it mean for MAP? As the COVID-19 wreaked havoc and put every facet of life on a virtual standstill, MAP’s theme for the 2021 “The Great Reset: Leading for the Common Good” sets the tone on how we should act and face the reality head on. Along with this great reset is the rise of challenger banks that can change the game on how the brick-and-mortar banks deliver their products and services. Intuitively, these digital banks can be viewed by traditional banks as formidable contenders which can reduce their market share. But there are strong arguments for allowing the establishment of digital banks. First, it can usher prosperity and alleviate poverty. Second, it can advance financial inclusion as the new entrants can provide affordable financial services to the mass market. Lastly, it can nudge the incumbent bank’s digital transformation moves as a way of staying relevant and competitive. In all of these, it’s the customers and the business community who will reap the benefits. More than the challenger banks, we believe that the journey of our BSP-supervised financial institutions towards digital transformation is another equally important aspect that the MAP members can draw inspirations from. In particular, the BSP publication entitled “BSP Unbound: Central Banking and the COVID-19 Pandemic in the Philippines” released in 2020, showed that investing in the right technology and tools, involving all departments in developing the strategy, and investing in staff training are the critical factors towards the success of digital transformation. In the same publication, we noted how the pandemic made our supervised electronic money issuers and virtual asset service providers realize the importance of introducing new products suitable to the new normal, strengthening business continuity framework and pursuing digitalization initiatives. Clearly, such way of thinking is also the gist of a Harvard Business Review article in 2020 that highlighted the importance of pivoting to business models conducive to short-term survival along with long-term resilience and growth. It emphasized the need for disintermediation by going directly to th

Title: Achieving Financial Inclusion for the Filipino People
Occasion: Financial Executives of the Philippines 53rd Annual Conference
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Good morning! Thank you for inviting me as a speaker to this 53rd Annual Conference of the Financial Executives Institute of the Philippines. With all the disruptions in the current environment, the challenge is for us to transform these developments into something that is beneficial for the country. That is precisely what the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas has been doing—leveraging on technological progress to hasten economic growth and financial inclusion. Even before the coronavirus pandemic, we have embraced financial technology to bring more Filipinos inside the financial loop and help improve their lives. These developments have enabled the Filipino people to continuously transact business within the safety of their homes during this time of the pandemic. Though the pandemic hit us hard, the Philippine economy is expected to recover in 2021 and 2022. In fact, the economy has started to rebound: The economy grew by 11.8-percent in the second quarter. Based on the latest government projections, the economy will grow anywhere between four and five percent this year and expand to a range of seven to nine percent next year. The economic recovery will be supported by manageable inflation, stable banking sector, and robust external payments sector. Estimates show that inflation is expected to slightly exceed the two- to four-percent target range this year due to supply factors, though this is expected to ease by next year. This manageable inflation will continue to provide an enabling environment for investments that will result in more jobs and higher income. Philippine banks are sound and well-capitalized. As such, banks will remain capable of supporting economic growth. The country’s external liquidity buffers continue to be more than adequate. Our gross international reserves of 108 billion US dollars, the steady inflows from remittances and business process outsourcing, and the recovery of exports and foreign direct investments will support the peso. However, the BSP does not only intend to simply to be back where the economy was before the pandemic. We aspire for a stronger, more resilient, more technologically advanced, and more inclusive post-COVID-19 economy. We are working towards this goal in three ways. The first is through the pursuit of relevant reforms that will support economic recovery and growth and build its resilience. These reforms include the passage of the performance-based fiscal incentives under the Corporate and Recovery and Tax Incentives for Enterprises, or the CREATE Act, which will attract foreign direct investments in the long run, and the Financial Institutions Strategic Act, or FIST, which will keep the financial sector stable amid the pandemic. The second way is by contributing to infrastructure development through the adoption of regulatory measures that will make it easier for infrastructure companies to finance projects, such as increasing single borrower’s limit and deepening the capital market. Third is through the inclusion agenda. The BSP has put in place regulations and programs so that micro enterprises can have easier access to credit and other financial services, and thus boost digital financial inclusion in the country. We also continue to work on our financial literacy programs to improve the knowledge of Filipinos on savings and investment. Indeed, the pandemic has highlighted the importance of inclusive digital finance. Early groundwork on digitalization has enabled many Filipinos to shift easily and rapidly to digital platforms.
