The foundations of the digital finance ecosystem—the Basic Deposit Account, the network of low-cost touchpoints, and the National Retail Payment System—earned remarkable gains during the pandemic. The number of basic deposit accounts opened in 133 banks has reached 6.6 million accounts amounting to 4.71 billion pesos in 2020. With 61 electronic money issuers in the country, around 34.7 million active e-money accounts were recorded last year. We have also noted a continued expansion of low-cost touchpoints enabling access to financial services. Last year, there were 58,000 cash agents and over 84,000 e-money agents nationwide. This translates to 91 percent of local government units with access to agent banking services that enable financial institutions to serve a wider client base, particularly in low-income and rural areas, at greater efficiency but less cost. Physical and mobility restrictions heightened the use of electronic fund transfer schemes. The volume of PESONet transactions totaled 31.6 million for the first half of 2021, representing a 164-percent growth. Similarly, the volume of InstaPay transactions for the same period increased to 200.7 million, posting a 223-percent growth. We now have a total of 87 participants for PESONet and 55 participants for InstaPay, and these figures are growing. Serving as the cornerstone of the BSP’s digitalization agenda, the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap outlines priority policy initiatives and strategies to advance an efficient, inclusive, and secure digital finance ecosystem. It also seeks to drive innovation in the delivery and development of financial services catering to various client needs and capabilities. By 2023, the roadmap hopes to achieve the following—First is the conversion of 50 percent of the total volume of retail payments into digital form. Second is the expansion of account ownership to 70 percent of Filipino adults. To build use cases for account ownership and digital payment rails, the BSP has collaborated with different government agencies such as the Department of Labor and Employment, Department of Social Welfare and Development, and Department of Transportation. Furthermore, the BSP launched the national Quick Response standard or QR Ph to promote interoperability and reduce costs of digitalization for small businesses. The BSP is also expanding engagements with other target segments to promote inclusive digital finance. We are engaging the National Council on Disability Affairs for greater financial inclusion of the persons with disability sector. Our initiatives include information sessions on the benefits of financial inclusion, and issuance of regulation on upholding non-discriminatory policies, providing mobility ramps in bank premises, and Braille on automated teller machines. We have also invited the Department of Interior and Local Government to be the 21st member of the inter-agency Financial Inclusion Steering Committee in July 2021, given its unique position to promote financial inclusion among the local government units. To sustain the efficiency of delivery of financial services, the BSP has advocated initiatives to strengthen the country’s digital finance infrastructure. The BSP supports the rollout of the national ID System or PhilSys, which targets 50-70 million Filipino registrants by year-end. The PhilSys provides a unique and verifiable digital identity that can be used as an official document for availing financial services, such as opening an account and receiving government assistance. A co-location arrangement between the Philippine Statistics Authority and the LandBank of the Philippines has allowed PhilSys registrants to open a bank account onsite. This arrangement has resulted in the onboarding of over 5.3 million unbanked Filipinos to date. The BSP is involved in efforts to mainstream PhilSys adoption through policy development and issuance of relevant industry regulations such as acceptance of PhilSys ID as an official and sufficient proof of identity. The BSP, as the Chair of the Financial Inclusion Steering Committee, endorsed Executive Order No. 127, which promotes the use of satellite broadband technology. This technology will be able to address digital connectivity gaps in rural and remote communities, as well as reduce the cost of delivering digital financial services nationwide. While digitalization is safe, convenient, and effective, the BSP is aware that it comes with risk. Accordingly, the BSP issued the governance policy for operators of payment system, as part of the phased-in implementation of the National Payment Systems Act. The governance policy provides for the regulatory expectations and governance standards that operators of payment systems must follow, considering their critical roles in ensuring the smooth circulation of funds in the economy in a safe and efficient manner. In addition, the BSP also requires designated payment systems to adopt international standards to further protect public interest and promote confidence in the use of payment systems. This is embodied in a circular on the Principles for Financial Market Infrastructures developed by the Bank for International Settlements and the International Organization of Securities Commissions. For your information, the country’s first designated payment system is the Peso Real-Time Gross Settlement System operated by the BSP. This was approved by the Monetary Board in July 2021. The proposed Financial Consumer Protection Act is also necessary as it has far-reaching benefits to consumers especially amid the rise in digital transactions. This will allow regulators to further ensure that appropriate mechanisms are in place to safeguard the interest of financial consumers. The BSP has upgraded the country’s 17-year-old real-time gross settlement system with a next-generation system—the PhilPaSSplus, which went live last July 26. With the upgraded system, PhilPaSSplus is able to settle larger numbers and complex financial. This will help the financial services industry keep pace with the rapidly progressing digital transformation of both the local and global economies. PhilPaSS Plus shall significantly enhance risk reduction, security levels, business continuity provisions, liquidity management, and the generation of data and information. Among the many features of PhilPaSS Plus is the adoption of the ISO 20022 international message standards, which shall adv

Title: Open Finance Towards a Collaborative and Inclusive Digital Finance
Occasion: European-Philippine Business Summit
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Good afternoon to all the participants of the 2021 European-Philippine Business Summit. I’m glad to be here to talk about the opportunities and potential of Open Finance. As you know the BSP has always allowed innovations to flourish, while safeguarding effective risk management. But before I dive into the BSP’s Open Finance Framework, let me take you quickly to the BSP Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap. The BSP developed the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap 2020-2023 to harmonize efforts towards building an inclusive and robust digital payment ecosystem in the country The Roadmap is anchored on three pillars and is envisioned to transform the Philippines from a cash-heavy into a cash-lite economy. First, the Digital Payment Streams embody the BSP’s key initiatives of creating compelling, large scale digital payments use cases, in collaboration with strategic partners in the government and the private sector. Second, the Digital Finance Infrastructure seeks to enhance key infrastructures that support the expansion of an inclusive digital payments’ ecosystem. Third, the Digital Governance and Standards aims to provide overarching principles on the digital governance expectations and ethical use of data. Let me walk you through the milestones and the BSP’s initiatives under each pillar. The BSP has been collaborating with the payment services industry led by the Philippine Payments Management, Inc. and other government agencies in order to develop more digital payment streams that cater to the needs of the local economy. The QR Ph P2M, which expands the use case of QR Ph to cover person-to-merchant payments or P2M, will be rolled out by Q4 2021. This will facilitate efficient digital payments to the small unbanked vendors such as sari-sari store owners and tricycle drivers. Aside from this, we are pushing for several other payment streams such as the Multiple Batch Settlement for PESONet that is set to be operationalized by Q4 2021. The Bills Payment Facility, which is set to be rolled out by early next year, will enable a customer to settle recurring payments even if the customer's Payment Service Provider is different from the biller's Payment Service Provider. The Request to Pay Facility, which is projected to be launched by Q2 2022, allows payees to initiate collections by simply sending a “request to pay” to the payor. The Direct Debit, set to be implemented next year, will enable billers to pull funds from the account of the payors for authorized recurring payments. Meanwhile, the BSP and the Department of Labor and Employment have been working for the digitalization of wage payments through remittance to transaction accounts. Likewise, the BSP also supports the Department of Transportation’s Project on Automated Fare Collection System, which is set to be implemented towards the end of 2022. Indeed, there is a big opportunity in digital payments. In fact, as of August 31, 2021, payment providers have increased with 87 BSP-Supervised Financial Institutions participating in PESONet and 55 in InstaPay. The participating institutions include thrift and rural banks, as well as non-bank e-money issuers, indicating a diverse reach of these services. From Q1 2021 to Q2 2021, the combined PESONet and InstaPay transactions increased by 17% in volume and 14% in value. During that period, the volume grew from 107 Million to 125 Million while the value increased from Php1.5 trillion to Php1,7 trillion. Since the start of the pandemic in 2020, the average quarterly growth rate for volume and value is 28% and 19%, respectively. More evidence shows our progress in widening the adoption of digital payments. In its first ever diagnostic for the Philippines in 2013, The Better Than Cash Alliance revealed that out of the 25 million monthly retail payments in the country, only 1% is electronic. Fast forward, the early estimates from the first half of 2020 suggest that digital payments rose to 17% or 782 million of all monthly payments, propelled by payments made to merchants and person-to-person payments. During the same period, the average monthly value of digital payments rose to 25% or 29 billion USD. The second pillar of the BSP’s Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap pursues key initiatives to develop digital finance infrastructure. To this end, the BSP upgraded the country’s 17-year-old PhilPaSS, the country's Real-time Gross Settlement, with a next generation system called PhilPaSSplus. Launched in July 2021, the PhilPaSSplus settles larger value, diverse, and complex financial transactions at a faster speed and with comprehensive functionalities. The BSP continues to support the implementation of the Philippine Identification System or PhilSys. The PhilSys ID is seen to address the challenge of proper identification with the added benefit of reducing customer onboarding costs. According to the Philippine Statistics Authority, about 26.3 million Filipinos have already completed Step 2 registration (which involves the capture of biometric information, as well as facial photographs) for the Philippine Identification System (PhilSys) as of August 18, 2021. Out of these registrants, 4.47 million have already been onboarded for bank accounts with the Land Bank of the Philippines. The accounts have no initial deposit requirement and comes with a Landbank Agent Banking Cards free of charge. In addition, Information and Communications Technology policy reforms aim to address digital connectivity gaps to promote fast and reliable internet connectivity. In combination with the roll-out of the PhilSys ID and e-KYC facility, greater internet access will allow more unbanked rural clients and low-income sectors to use digital financial services and benefit from digital innovations. As you know, the goal of our rapid digitalization efforts is financial inclusion. And we are working to address this gap in financial services. Through BSP’s efforts, there are now 133 banks offering basic deposit accounts to around 6.6 million depositors as of fourth quarter of 2020. At the same time, the number of e-money accounts has reached 34.7 million offered by 61 e-money issuers nationwide. We also have over 58,000 cash agents and over 84,000 e-money agents in the country. These agents have enabled financial institutions to efficiently serve wider client base, particularly in low-income and rural areas. As of the fourth quarter of 2020, 91% of local government units already have access to an agent. In addition, our efforts to develop the Digital Finance Infrastructure are gradually unlocking financial technology innovations offering alternative source of financing to MSMEs. For instance, instead of depending on collateral, P2P lending provides non-collateralized loans to small businesses at competitive rates using credit scoring models and big data analytics to assess the credit worthiness of MSME borrowers. Equity crowdfunding supports business startups by raising capital using online platforms. In 2019, the Philippine Securities and Exchange Commission issued the regulations on crowdfunding that will help support MSMEs while providing alternative investment opportunity for the investing public. E-commerce finance offers working capital loans to small businesses selling goods on e-commerce platforms, while invoice financing allows them to monetize outstanding receivables in a quicker, easier, and more flexible manner than selling their receivables to a third party. Indeed, fintech has the potential to help MSMEs grow and become competitive. The final pillar of the BSP’s Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap commits BSP to providing an enabling regulatory environment that fosters digital innovation and adoption while ensuring effective risk management. The Digital Banking Framework, which was issued through Circular No. 1105 dated December 2, 2020, will support the expansion of the use of digital financial and payment services. A digital bank offers financial products and services that are processed end-to-end through a digital platform and/or electronic channels with no physical branches. To date, the Monetary Board has approved the application of 6 digital banks. To further strengthen BSP-supervised financial institutions’ cybersecurity capabilities, the BSP shall be issuing amendments to the electronic banking guidelines to upgrade the baseline security controls for electronic payment and financial service providers. On August 26, 2021, the Monetary Board has approved the local adoption of the Principles for Market Infrastructure among BSP designated payment systems essential in fortifying their resilience against financial crises and participant defaults. The latest set of foreign exchange reforms that were issued through Circular No. 1124 dated August 10, 2021 allow authorized agent banks and foreign exchange corporations to sell foreign currencies, without prior BSP approval, if the foreign currencies will be used for digital payments for e-commerce transactions. The BSP has also issued the policy on the governance arrangements and standards to be adhered to by all Operators of Payment Systems. At this juncture, let me dive deeper into the Open Finance Framework. Under Circular No. 1122 dated 17 June 2021, the BSP Open Finance Framework aims to facilitate the move of the local digital payments landscape towards an open finance ecosystem. An open finance ecosystem fosters data sharing scheme that espouses consent-driven data portability, interoperability, and collaborative partnerships among incumbent financial institutions and new third-party players who adhere to the same standards of data security and privacy. With access to consumer data, banks and third-party providers could tailor a broader array of financial products such as, banking products and services, investments, pensions, and insurance. The Framework sees customers as owners of their transaction data, which can be shared if they wish to do so. With more data s

Title: The Philippines’ Response and Economic Recovery Amid the Prolonged Pandemic
Occasion: Meeting with Credit Rating Agencies
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ The Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas welcomes this opportunity to update you on BSP's policy measures amid the pandemic as well as latest developments on the monetary, external and banking sectors. As with the rest of the world, the Philippines continues to face challenges brought on by the pandemic. Let me first discuss the BSP's policy interventions to address the pandemic-induced challenges. When the health crisis started early last year, the BSP acted swiftly to ensure ample liquidity in the financial system, support orderly functioning of the financial market, and shore up market confidence. We implemented a mix of decisive monetary policy adjustments, extraordinary liquidity enhancing measures, and regulatory relief and forbearance measures. In particular, to maintain stability of the financial system and ensure public access to financial services, we implemented a long list of measures to ease the impact of the crisis on the balance sheet of banks, so they can help their crisis-hit customers as well. We have discussed most of these measures with you last Spring and in subsequent reviews. As an update, BSP's policy and liquidity-easing measures has injected into the financial system about ₱2.3 trillion liquidity in total, equivalent to 12.7 percent of the country's full year nominal GDP for 2020 as of 26 August 2021. As the Philippine economy recovers, the decision on the timing and circumstances under which BSP will dial back its pandemic-induced support measures would depend primarily on evolution of various domestic factors, as well as global developments and spill overs. When domestic developments warrant a withdrawal of policy support, the BSP will ensure a smooth transition in winding down its time and state-bound measures. At all times, the BSP will coordinate closely with fiscal authorities to ensure adequate policy support remains for the economic recovery to be sustained. Moving on to the latest developments on the monetary front Headline inflation in September slightly eased to 4.8 percent from 4.9 percent in August. The slower inflation was mainly due to lower annual rate of increase in transport index. Year-to-date inflation has averaged 4.5 percent. The BSP expects inflation to settle at the upper end of the target range of 2-4 percent in 2021 at 4.4 percent. Inflation is seen to ease towards 3.3 percent for 2022 and 3.2 percent for 2023. We see the risks to the inflation outlook remaining broadly balanced over the policy horizon. The increase in inflation this year is not unique to the Philippines. Our neighbors are also experiencing the same. Given a manageable inflation path, the BSP has kept the key policy interest rate at 2.0 percent in its recent policy review last month. The Monetary Board has decided that the revailing monetary policy settings remain appropriate given the manageable inflation environment and the nascent economic recovery. Together with appropriate fiscal and health interventions, keeping a steady hand on the BSP's policy levers will allow the momentum of economic recovery to gain more traction by helping boost domestic demand and market confidence. On the banking system Philippine banks have shown resilience to the COVID-19 crisis, benefiting from risk-management and other regulatory reforms of the past two decades. Banks continue to have ample buffers against shocks. The capital adequacy ratio (CAR) remains well above regulatory and international standards. Exposure to soured debts remains manageable. Average non-performing loans (NPL) ratio of the banking system stood at 4.5 percent as of August this year, much lower than the double-digit figures in the aftermath of the Asian financial crisis. The BSP estimates that NPL ratios of the banking sector will settle between 5.0 percent to 6.0 percent by end-December 2021. Going forward, we expect NPLs to remain manageable. The FIST Act, which facilitates the sale/transfer by banks and other financial institutions of their non-performing assets is projected to reduce average NPL ratio of the banking system. Liquidity in the financial system remains ample, expanding by 6.9 percent year-on-year in August 2021—faster than the 5.9 percent growth recorded in July. The BSP ensures that domestic liquidity remains adequate in support of economic activity. The outstanding loans of universal and commercial banks (U/KBs) increased by 1.3 percent year-on-year in August. This is the first reported expansion in outstanding loans of U/KBs after eight consecutive months of contractions. This improvement in may be attributed to the accelerated vaccine rollout and the gradual easing of quarantine restrictions. The BSP's consumer expectations survey andbusiness expectations survey shows broad improvements in outlook for the coming months. Meanwhile, the external sector remains strong. The Philippines' foreign debt remains manageable. As of end-2020, the total external debt-to-GDP ratio of the Philippines was 27.2 percent much lower than Malaysia's 69.1 percent, Indonesia's 39.4 percent and Thailand's 37.8 percent. As of end-June 2021, our external debt had declined to 26.5 percent of GDP. Gross international reserves continue to be hefty at US$108 billion, as of end-August, equivalent to 10.8 months' worth of import cover. At US$108 billion, GIR exceeds total external debt worth Php101.7 billion. We expect the overall BOP position to post moderate surpluses in the next two years, supported by resilient structural flows. Overseas Filipino remittances in 2020 reached almost US$30 billion, slightly less compared to 2019, defying forecasts that it will fall by double-digit rates. From January to July this year, cash remittances increased year-on-year by 5.8 percent to US$17.8 billion. The increase in remittance inflows is expected to continue as COVID-19 vaccine rollout accelerates in the Philippines and in OFW host economies as travel restrictions ease further and countries safely open to foreign workers. Similarly, BPO receipts reached US$10.8 billion as of the first half 2021, 7.1 percent higher than the Php10.1 billion level a year ago. Meanwhile, net foreign direct investments rebounded by 43.1 percent to US$5.6 billion in the first seven months of 2021. On the exchange rate, the peso's performance is broadly in line with the behavior of other regional currencies. The peso has depreciated by 5.02 percent (as of 7 October 2021 close) against the US dollar since the start of the year. Our hefty gross international reserves, the steady inflows from remittances, BPOs, and recovery of exports and FDIs—all these will support the peso. The BSP remains committed to a flexible exchange rate regime. It will intervene only to smoothen the volatility in the exchange rate. Moreover, the BSP will also continue to rely on measures such as maintaining a healthy level of foreign reserves as buffers and reviewing and calibrating existing macro-prudential measures to maintain the stability of the peso. We cannot overemphasize enough that the Philippines came into the pandemic with ample policy space. This has enabled the government to address the challenges from the pandemic head on. Moving forward, the BSP will continue to pursue appropriate policy actions responsive to the needs of the time. On monetary policy: the BSP will remain vigilant over the current inflation dynamics to ensure that the monetary policy stance continues to support economic recovery to the extent that the inflation outlook would allow. It will carefully scan the operating environment with a forward-looking perspective to move in a pre-emptive fashion to address any risks to our price stability mandate. On the financial sector: the BSP will intensify its monitoring and surveillance over its supervised institutions to ensure that they remain responsive to emerging risks and to promote the continued soundness, stability, resilience, and inclusivity of the banking system. Third, on the external sector: the BSP will remain supportive of policies that will help strengthen the economy's resilience to external shocks, including that of maintaining a market-determined exchange rate, keeping a comfortable level of reserves, and keeping the country's external debt manageable. Thank you and I look forward to your questions.​

Title: QR Ph P2M: Bringing Digital Payments Closer to the Filipino People
Occasion: QR PH P2M Launch
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Good afternoon to our colleagues from the government, to our partner institutions, ladies and gentlemen. We are very pleased that you are with us today, albeit virtually, to witness the virtual full launch of the QR Ph P2M: Bringing Digital Payments Closer to the Filipino People. With today’s event, are well on our way to becoming a cash-lite society. This milestone is testament to our commitment to overcome the challenges of broadening digital transformation in the country in our pursuit of the delivery of universal access to safe, affordable and convenient digital payment services for all Filipinos. The simplicity of QR code-enabled payments gives it the potential to reach even small players because it requires less expensive infrastructure for onboarding - only a smartphone and the internet are needed. The use of QR code payments were bolstered with the development of the National QR Code Standard, dubbed as “QR Ph” in accordance with BSP Circular No. 1055. QR Ph aims to transform the fragmented QR-driven payment services into interoperable payment solutions. It was in November 2019 when we launched the QR Ph for person-to-person payments or “QR Ph P2P” which primarily catered to remittances and payments within the informal sector. From only 6 pilot participants during the launch, we now have 23 participants by the end of August 2021. From the second quarter last year, transactions have likewise surged. The volume expanded by almost thirteen times its Q2 2020 level and the value surged by more than forty-five times its Q2 2020 level, exhibiting an average quarterly growth rate of 110% and 229% for volume and value, respectively, over the period. To complement the P2P component of the QR Ph, the QR Ph for person-to-merchant or “QR Ph P2M” was developed and its pilot run started in April this year. QR Ph P2M enables merchants such as department stores, pharmacies, supermarkets, hardware stores, and restaurants to accept e-payments with ease even in the safety of their customers’ homes. QR Ph P2M may also make bills payments faster and more convenient. For this full launch, customers who have accounts with – 1) AllBank, 2) AUB, 3) Cebuana Lhuillier Rural Bank, 4) Chinabank, 5) Landbank, 6) RCBC, 7) StarPay, 8) Union Bank, and 9) USSC Money Services, Inc. — can now make QR Ph payments in over 30,000 locations of more than 20,000 participating merchants who have accounts with – 1) AllBank, 2) AUB, 3) BDO, 4) Cebuana Lhuillier Rural Bank, 5) Metrobank, 6) PayMaya, 7) RCBC, 8) StarPay, 9) Union Bank, and 10) USSC Money Services, Inc. (A final list of participating institutions and the final number of merchants/locations will be provided.) We anticipate that more payment service providers and merchants will be onboarded to the QR Ph P2M platform after today’s event. Indeed, the use of QR codes for payments has been gaining traction as an alternative to the traditional debit and credit card payments, given the ease, convenience and speed of merely scanning the code compared to having to bring a card, tapping, dipping or swiping it, and signing a charge slip in many cases. QR Ph empowers consumers by giving them the freedom to choose their digital payment service through the interoperability feature of QR Ph-payments, and this is aligned with the United Nation’s principles on the responsible use of digital payments. So, what are the features of QR Ph and why do we advocate its adoption? • First is that QR Ph is cost-effective. With the QR technology, all you need is a smartphone. Small entrepreneurs need not invest in costly electronic data capture equipment like point of sale (POS) terminals. • Merchants only need to print the QR Code that their payment service providers will generate for them, display those codes, and obtain payments by simply asking their customers to scan the codes. • Second is that QR Ph minimizes encoding errors. By simply scanning the merchants’ QR code, both merchants and customers can be assured that the payment is sent to the correct account number of the merchant. • Third is the convenience of QR Ph. Compared to other modes of payment, QR code is extremely quick and easy. Customers need not bring their physical wallets to pay or to manually input the merchant’s account information since such is already embedded in the merchant’s QR code. • Fourth is that QR Ph is secure. QR codes are one of the safest payment methods. Customers do not have to store sensitive payment information and data transferred via QR codes is encrypted, thus making payments secure. • Fifth is the contactless feature of QR Ph payments. With the ongoing pandemic, customers now prefer using digital payments like QR Ph because they can pay for purchases without having to touch shared surfaces like payment terminals, pens, or change. This helps the customers feel safer knowing the only thing they will touch is their own device. • Last, but not the least, is that QR Ph allows you to pay the exact amount. With QR Ph, customers can pay the exact amount for the purchase of goods and services. Merchants looking for “change” or barya will be a thing of the past. • With this, we will be able to move towards our envisioned coinless state as we transition towards a cash-lite economy. Needless to say, QR Ph P2M is a key digital payment stream developed to further advance digital payments adoption. We just recently announced the results of the e-payment measurement for the full year 2020, reporting that we even slightly surpassed the initial “20% by 2020” target of the BSP when we launched the National Retail Payment System (NRPS) in 2015. In the 2020 report and in the previous diagnostics, P2M has consistently been among the key drivers for growing digital payments usage in the country. This trend is expected given that merchant payments comprise over 70% of the total monthly retail payments in the country. As such, we are prioritizing merchant payments given its huge potential to further advance digital payments adoption in the country. Since the P2M use-case covers a big consumer base – both individuals and businesses – it stands to provide a far-reaching impact to society in terms of the benefits that may be realized when merchant payments are digitalized. Imagine onboarding the micro and small enterprises that comprise over 98% of registered businesses in the country to offer QR Ph P2M to their consumers. We will not only drive digital payments usage, but we will also empower micro and small businesses to realize greater opportunities for growth which, in turn, could positively impact our country’s overall economic growth. Ten (10) years ago, it may have seemed daunting to even set a target of transforming 50% of retail payments into digital form by 2023. This is due, in large part, to the study that showed that, in 2013, out of the 2.5 billion payments made by Filipinos in a month, only 1% are done electronically. We also did not have any laws or holistic regulatory frameworks in place governing the provision of digital payment services. Looking back, it really seemed impossible to achieve this target. However, given the landmark regulatory reforms implemented these past several years, significant developments were realized in our bid to modernize the country’s payments landscape. As a result, we have seen the accelerated rate of increase in the progression of digital payments adoption from 2013 to 2020. From 2018 to 2020, we witnessed a continuous rise in the share of digital payments in the country, with the volume growing significantly from 10% in 2018 to 20.1% in 2020, up by more than 10 percentage points compared to the more than 9 percentage point increase in the previous 5-year period from 2013 to 2018. Considering the developments and the strengthening partnership between the public and private sectors in reforming our national payment system, the BSP is optimistic that it will realize its objective under the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap of transforming at least 50 percent of total payments into digital form by the year 2023. The BSP will continue to play its catalytic role by issuing supportive regulations that aim to strike the right balance between fostering innovation while ensuring the overall safety, efficiency and reliability of the national payment system. Aligned with this payment system oversight approach, the BSP recently issued policies that promote good governance and sound risk management standards for the critical participants in the national payment system. These regulations set the BSP’s expectations on the payment system participants’ adherence to the Principles for Financial Market Infrastructures to help them become more resilient to financial crises, and likewise prescribed the quality of stewardship among operators of payment systems. Moreover, we continue to develop appropriate cyber security and anti-money laundering policies to protect the financial consumers. We have also launched digital literacy programs to educate consumers on the responsible use of digital payments, thereby reducing their vulnerabilities to scams and frauds, and fortifying the confidence in digital payments. But the BSP cannot create a well-functioning digital payments ecosystem alone, everyone has a role to play. We enjoin you to be a part of this big change. Let us work together to build an inclusive and vibrant digital payments ecosystem as we forge pathways towards our vision of a cash-lite society. So next time you need to pay for something, just SCAN QR Ph. Let’s go digitALL! Thank you and have a great day!

Title: Responding to Today's Crisis and Building Foundations for a Better Tomorrow
Occasion: Standard Chartered Bank’s Sovereign Investor Forum
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: Finance Secretary Carlos Dominguez III, officers and staff of Standard Chartered Bank, and all the participants of this virtual investor forum, warm greetings to all of you. I’m here today to present updates on the Philippine economy under the purview of the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP), to help guide you in your assessment of the country’s economic outlook. I’ll begin by saying that while the road to full recovery remains bumpy for the Philippines and many other economies at present, the medium- and long-term prospects for the Philippines are promising. This opinion is shared by relevant third-party observers, including credit rating agencies. As Secretary Dominguez had also shown earlier, throughout the pandemic—amid a wave of rating downgrades worldwide—the Philippines has maintained its investment grade credit ratings. Beyond the short-term challenges of the pandemic, debt watchers say that sound fundamentals and policies will help the Philippines return to a sustainable growth path once the dust settles. Let me share some of the latest economic indicators and macroeconomic projections for the short term. Gross domestic product (GDP) grew by a robust 11.8 percent in the second quarter, thus ending the pandemic-driven recession. Growth was broad-based, fueled by manufacturing, construction, and wholesale and retail trade on the supply side, as well as household consumption, public construction, and private-sector investments on the demand side. Unemployment rate had improved to 6.9 percent in July. However, with re-imposition of stricter quarantine classification on affected areas due to the threats from the more transmissible Delta variant, unemployment rate rose to 8.1 percent in August. But with calibrated approach to the lockdowns, more economic activities were allowed which helped bring down unemployment from the pandemic’s historic high of 17.6 percent in April 2020. Foreign direct investments (FDIs) jumped by 40.7 percent in the first semester, signaling return of investor confidence. Meanwhile approved investment applications from foreign investors rose by over 45 percent in the second quarter, with bulk of the investments going into industries with high potential for job creation such as Information and Communication, construction and manufacturing. Manufacturing activity is improving, as shown by the recovery of Purchasing Manager’s Index in September after a temporary setback in August due to stricter quarantine measures to curb the delta variant. Factory output sustains an upward trend with a triple-digit growth in volume and value of production indices, as well as, sustained average capacity utilization rate. Merchandise trade has recovered. For the first seven months, exports and imports sustained double-digit growth. Remittances, which have a been a steady source of foreign exchange throughout the pandemic, grew by 5.8 percent in the first seven months. Gross international reserves are hefty and provide adequate liquidity buffer. Meanwhile, the Philippines’ exposure to foreign currency debt remains manageable, at only 26.5 percent of GDP as of end-June 2021, and low compared to neighbors. Inflation averaged 4.5 percent in the first nine months of the year. This was slightly above the target range of 2.0 to 4.0 percent on account of supply-side pressures, but nevertheless remains manageable. The BSP expects inflation to settle at the upper end of the target range of 2-4 percent in 2021 at 4.4 percent due to the impact of recent transitory supply-side pressures. However, inflation is seen to ease towards around the midpoint of the target at 3.3 percent for 2022 and 3.2 percent for 2023. We see the risks to the inflation outlook remaining broadly balanced over the policy horizon. The banking system remains sound. Banks remain adequately capitalized as the average capitalization of banks exceed minimum regulatory requirement while the pandemic-driven increase in non-performing loans is manageable. In view of the resurgence of covid cases in the third quarter and the imposition of calibrated mobility curbs, the government had to trim its growth projection for 2021 from 6.0 to 7.0 percent to 4.0 to 5.0 percent. For 2022, the government retained its 7.0-9.0 percent growth projection. In any case, the Philippine Government has taken a whole of government approach in tackling the pandemic-induced challenges. There have been massive responses from both the fiscal and monetary sides. On the part of the BSP, we have done quite a lot to help safeguard livelihoods, maintain order in the financial markets and put the economy back to a robust growth path. Besides slashing the reserve requirement and the key policy rate, which now stands at a historic low of 2.0 percent. We have extended liquidity support to the National Government, purchased government securities in the secondary market, among others. So far, the BSP has infused a total of ₱2.3 trillion (or US$ 44 billion) in liquidity to the financial system, equivalent to 12.7 percent of GDP. As the favorable inflation outlook allows, the BSP will continue to support the economy until there is clear evidence of robust and sustainable progress to economic recovery. The decision on the timing and circumstances under which BSP will dial back its pandemic-induced support measures depend primarily on the evolution of various domestic factors, as well as global developments and spill overs. BSP’s policies are data-dependent to avoid the premature withdrawal of policy stimulus, while also safeguarding against emerging risks to our price and financial stability objectives. When domestic developments warrant a withdrawal of policy support as economic recovery gains traction, the BSP will ensure a smooth transition in winding down its time and state-bound measures. At the same time, the BSP will continue to coordinate closely with fiscal authorities to ensure enough policy support remains for the economic recovery to be sustained. Here are some of the latest near-term macroeconomic assumptions which reflect our confidence on the economic recovery: For next year and in 2023, growth is expected to accelerate to a range of 7.0 to 9.0 percent and 6.0 to 7.0 percent, respectively. Inflation will provide an enabling environment for higher consumption and investments, as this is seen within the 2.0- to 4.0-percent target range next year and in 2023. FDIs are expected to reach US$7.0 billion for the full year this year, and US$ 7.5 billion by next year. This will be aided in part by government efforts toward easing doing business, supported by a liberalized FX environment put in place by the BSP. Trade is expected to pick up further amid recovery in global demand and improving business sentiment here and abroad. Exports are expected to grow by 14.0 percent this year and 6.0 percent next year; and imports by 20.0 percent and 10.0 percent over the same period. The country’s external accounts will remain robust, with the GIR staying well above USD 100 billion. This will continue to be supported by steady inflows of remittances and business process outsourcing (BPO) receipts. Speaking of the country’s strong external payments position, this will continue providing support to the peso. I would like to point out that the peso’s recent depreciation is manageable and in line with the behavior of regional and emerging economies currencies. While short-run fluctuations in the peso are affected by market sentiment, its medium- to long-term movements are largely supported by economic fundamentals as indicated by the relative stability in the movement of the real effective exchange rate of the peso. As a matter of policy, the BSP does not target any particular level of the exchange rate and in fact, maintains a market-determined exchange rate. Nevertheless, the BSP retains some scope to intervene in foreign exchange (FX) markets in response to excessive FX volatility in order to ensure orderly market conditions, avoid excessive volatility in the exchange rate, and therefore minimize any undue impact on inflation and inflation expectations. When warranted, the BSP can provide dollar liquidity to ensure that legitimate demands for foreign currency are satisfied. The banking sector is expected to remain resilient to the pandemic. We expect bad debts to stay below 10 percent—far from the levels seen following the Asian financial crisis. The Financial Institutions Strategic Transfer (FIST) law enacted earlier this year—which allows banks to unload bad assets to FIST corporations—serves as a pre-emptive measure against any material rise in non-performing assets. Let’s move on to my next point on the Philippines’ medium and long-term prospects. The Philippines does not simply aim to recover from the crisis. There is a whole-of-government effort toward a post-COVID Philippine economy that is better than ever. This means an economy that is stronger, more technologically advanced, more inclusive, and sustainable. I will briefly tackle each of the four aspects and how the BSP is contributing to their attainment. First, a stronger economy. Government has implemented various measures geared toward investment and job creation, including policies on ease of doing business, tax reforms, and critical infrastructure projects. It is also investing heavily on human capital development. The government also continues to pursue critical legislative measures such as further liberalization of the economy as earlier mentioned by Finance Secretary Dominguez. The BSP has a range of initiatives complementing these efforts. For instance, to name just a few. Several waves of foreign exchange liberalization help enhance the ease in doing business. Properly calibrated limits on bank loans support infrastructure development. Opening of the banking sector to more foreign players help boost economic activities and bring in innovative financial technology. Prudent management of external accounts and structural curren

Title: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno's Message for the Philippine Sustainable Finance Roadmap & Guiding Principles Launch Event and Donors’ Forum
Occasion: Philippine Sustainable Finance Roadmap & Guiding Principles Launch Event and Donors’ Forum
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Her Majesty’s Ambassador to the Republic of the Philippines Laure Beaufils, Department of Finance Secretary Carlos G. Dominguez III, Ernst & Young Director Russell Marsh, our colleagues from the Green Force, guests, partners, and fellow sustainability champions, good afternoon! Today marks a significant milestone in our sustainability journey as we launch “The Philippine Sustainable Finance Roadmap” and “The Sustainable Finance Guiding Principles”. The BSP is honored to be part of this milestone brought to life by the Green Force members with the support of the United Kingdom Government. The BSP and the banking industry, consider the Roadmap and the Guiding Principles as key enablers in unlocking financing to sustainability projects and initiatives for three reasons. First, this will facilitate implementation of the Sustainable Finance Framework, which was issued in 2020 to mainstream sustainability principles in the banking sector in line with our national aspirations for sustainable development. While multiple efforts are underway to rethink and reset banking strategies to meet the expectations under the Framework, there is an undeniable need for a clear and strategic direction from the government side. The Sustainable Finance Roadmap will provide the necessary guideposts that will complement the objectives of the Sustainable Finance Framework. More importantly, it will harmonize future policy initiatives, regulatory interventions, and business strategies towards a genuinely sustainable and low-carbon transition. Second, this initiative will promote participation and strengthen the confidence of market players. The BSP recognizes the considerable progress in the adoption of green or sustainable finance among banks, as seen in the increase in green, social and sustainability bonds issuances by major banks. However, we can do more in for green or sustainability loans. This type of financial product not only provides an opportunity for smaller banks to participate in sustainable financing, but also promotes inclusivity as it caters to a wide variety of borrowers including the unserved sector. Like the Roadmap, the Guiding Principles serve as a platform for the common understanding of varied stakeholders in identifying economic activities that are considered ‘sustainable.’ This will open up various opportunities and accelerate the mobilization and reallocation of capital towards green and sustainable activities. Third, this will steer the banks’ climate and environmental and social risk management. The Roadmap and the Guiding Principles serve as the green light for stakeholders to pursue priority areas which will contribute to low carbon transition. This will enable financial service providers, businesses, and investors to make parallel actions and optimal shifts in their capital allocations, thereby minimizing transition risks. At the same time, this will promote adequate and meaningful disclosures among market players, thereby supporting the stakeholders’ decision-making processes. Ladies and gentlemen, while we celebrate these two key accomplishments of the Green Force, this is only the beginning. We are set to release the second issuance on the Sustainable Finance Framework, which aims to provide granular expectations in managing environmental and social risks in relation to credit and operational risk areas. Likewise, we are completing our various initiatives under the Sustainable Central Banking Program, which aims to embed sustainability principles in the BSP’s operations and functions. We are only a few weeks away from the 26th Conference of the Parties or COP26, which is a much-anticipated event for climate action around the globe. This is where the Philippines will prove its commitment to reduce carbon emissions and fight climate change. On this note, I would like to enjoin everyone – the policymakers, regulators, banking industry, business sectors, development agencies, and partners - to continue our collaboration and turn our ambitions into realizations for the future generation. Thank you and Mabuhay!​

Title: Building on Strong Macroeconomic Foundations Toward a Stronger, Digitalized, and Inclusive Economy
Occasion: The Asset 16th Philippine Summit
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ To the officers and staff of The Asset, our stakeholders in the banking and finance industry, and everyone joining us in this virtual gathering, good afternoon. Thank you for inviting me to the 16th Philippine Summit. This is a timely occasion for the industry to share insights and best practices, as we pave the way for recovery and growth. My speech this afternoon is divided into three parts. First, I will briefly discuss the latest developments on the Philippine economy. Second, I will tackle the BSP’s response to the crisis brought about by COVID-19. And last, I will share insights on the BSP’s way forward. A year-and-a-half since the pandemic, we are getting back on our feet. We have learned to live with the virus better with the government shifting to a risk-based approach to mobility restrictions. This strategic shift has resulted in better economic and health outcomes. In the second quarter of this year, the Philippines’ Gross domestic product (GDP) grew by a robust 11.8 percent. This strong recovery reflects more than just base effects but the impact of the fiscal and monetary policy support during the pandemic and the calibrated imposition of mobility restrictions, which enabled many businesses, including public transportation, to continue operations. Manufacturing is on the rise, with the purchasing managers index on expansion mode for 6 out of 9 months this year. It suffered a temporary setback again in August similar to April and May owing to the imposition of stricter lockdown to curb the spread of the virus but recovered strongly in the month of September. Unemployment rate improved to 6.9 percent in July from the pandemic’s historic high of 17.6 percent in April 2020. However, with the re-imposition of stricter quarantine classification on affected areas due to the threats from the more transmissible Delta variant, unemployment rate rose to 8.1 percent in August. What our GDP and employment numbers show is that once the number of covid cases taper off, and restrictions are eased, the economy has the dynamism and flexibility to bounce back quickly and restore incomes and jobs. A stable inflation environment is also necessary to support our nascent economic recovery. While supply-side pressures pushed the average inflation rate this year to levels slightly above target, latest estimates show it will ease and settle at around the mid-point of the official target range of 2.0 to 4.0 percent next year and in 2023. The country’s external accounts remain sound. Gross international reserves are hefty and provide adequate buffers against external headwinds. Remittances remain resilient throughout the pandemic and continue to be a steady source of foreign exchange. With the improvement in global trade, merchandise imports and exports increased substantially in the first seven months of the year. Foreign direct investments (FDIs) have similarly rebounded reflecting confidence on the Philippines’ recovery and growth prospects. The Philippines’ strong external payments position, driven in part by steady inflows of remittances, recovery of exports and investments, as well as the progress of our economic recovery will provide ample support to the peso moving forward. May I note that the peso’s depreciation lately—which has happened amid the anticipated policy normalization by the US Fed—remains manageable and is broadly in line with the behavior of other emerging market currencies. The banking sector remains sound and continues to support the financing requirements of the recovering economy. Capitalization of banks remain well above regulatory requirements. While non-performing loans may have risen during the pandemic, they remain manageable. Lending has improved, expanding by 1.3 percent in Aug from a year ago. This was the first expansion following the declines posted since December last year amid improvements in sentiment brought about by the continued rollout of COVID-19 vaccines and the gradual easing of quarantine restrictions. Along with these indicators, the country has kept its investment grade credit ratings intact throughout the pandemic. This is a testament to the confidence of debt watchers on the Philippine economy’s medium- and long-term growth prospects. Moving on to the BSP’s crisis response. The BSP has implemented a long list of crisis-response measures, amid a whole-of-government effort to address the challenges brought by the pandemic and to accelerate economic recovery. First are measures to boost market confidence on the availability of credit. The BSP lowered the reserve requirement and slashed the key policy rate by a total of 200 basis points last year to a record low of 2.00 percent. Second are extraordinary measures meant to ensure sufficient liquidity at a critical time. These include extension of provisional advances to the National Government, purchases of government securities in the secondary market, and advance remittance of dividends. And third, time-bound regulatory relief measures meant to help banks and their customers cope with the crisis. These include counting of loans to micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs) as compliance to the reserve requirement, staggered booking of loan losses, and increase in the single borrower’s limit, among others. The BSP has done quite a lot to respond to the crisis. Nevertheless, we will continue to support the economy until there is clear evidence of robust and sustainable progress toward full economic recovery. In total, BSP’s policy and liquidity-easing measures has injected into the financial system about ₱2.3 trillion liquidity in total, equivalent to 12.7 percent of the country’s full year nominal GDP for 2020 as of 26 August 2021 The decision on the timing and circumstances under which BSP will scale back its pandemic-induced support measures would depend primarily on the evolution of various domestic factors, as well as global developments and spill overs. BSP’s policies are data-dependent to avoid the premature withdrawal of policy stimulus, while also safeguarding against emerging risks to our price and financial stability objectives. When domestic developments warrant a withdrawal of policy support, the BSP will ensure a smooth transition in winding down its time and state bound measures. At all times, the BSP will coordinate closely with fiscal authorities to ensure adequate policy support remains for the economic recovery to be sustained. The BSP is one with the government in the pursuit of a post-COVID Philippine economy that is better than ever. Let me cite two of our initiatives toward this end. One is the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap for 2020-2023. We aim to transform the country into a cash-lite society, where at least 70 percent of Filipino adults have transaction accounts—to be achieved largely through digital services—and where at least half of financial transactions are done electronically. Data show that we are on track with these twin goals. Second is our sustainability agenda. As you know, climate change poses drastic socioeconomic problems if left unaddressed. It is incumbent upon everyone to act with a sense of urgency. On the part of the BSP, we have issued the Sustainable Finance Framework, which calls on on banks to institutionalize sustainability principles in their operations. We will be issuing more enabling environmental, social, and governance (ESG)-related guidelines moving forward. Moving forward, the BSP’s actions and policy thrusts will continue to be anchored on its core mandates of promoting price and financial stability. Toward this end, the BSP will continue to pursue appropriate policy actions responsive to the needs of the time. On monetary policy: the BSP will remain vigilant over the current inflation dynamics to ensure that the monetary policy stance continues to support economic recovery to the extent that the inflation outlook would allow. It will carefully scan the operating environment with a forward-looking perspective to move in a pre-emptive fashion to address any risks to our price stability mandate. On the financial sector: the BSP will intensify its monitoring and surveillance over its supervised institutions to ensure that they remain responsive to emerging risks and to promote the continued soundness, stability, resilience and inclusivity of the banking system, particularly through the pursuit of enhanced digitization. Third, on the external sector: the BSP will remain supportive of policies that will help strengthen the economy’s resilience to external shocks, including that of maintaining a market-determined exchange rate, keeping a comfortable level of reserves, and keeping the country’s external debt manageable. In closing, I would like to highlight three take-away messages. First, the Philippines’ economic fundamentals remain sound; inflation is manageable, external payments is robust; and the banking system remains resilient and stable. Second, as indicators show, the Philippines has begun the recovery process. The central bank will keep its policy settings and regulatory relief measures supportive of the economy as needed. The withdrawal of policy measures will only be done once full recovery is underway. And third, the monetary authorities will continue to provide an enabling environment for the attainment of the Philippines’ long-term economic goals, such as through price and financial stability, and promotion of financial digitalization and sustainable finance. While the Philippine economy draws on solid fundamentals to boost the recovery, we encourage concerned stakeholders to take the opportunities from this pandemic challenge to help ensure the benefits from digitalization and financial sustainability reverberate in the economy and our people’s lives sooner than later. Thank you very much for your attention. I wish you the best for this virtual Philippine Summit.​

Title: Thrift Banks: Moving Forward to the Next Economy
Occasion: Chamber of Thrift Banks 2021 Virtual Convention
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Officers of the Chamber of Thrift Banks led by the Convention Chairman Lorenzo T. Ocampo and President Cecilio “Paul” D. San Pedro, our partners from the thrift banking industry, guests, ladies and gentlemen, good morning! I am honored to be your keynote speaker in this year’s virtual convention of the Chamber of Thrift Banks. I am grateful for this opportunity to engage with every one of you, our stakeholders, and discuss our shared goal of moving forward to the next economy. I will start my presentation today by providing an overview of our current economic landscape and the performance of the thrift banking industry. Next, I will cover the BSP’s temporary relief and structural measures that will help propel the industry forward, including our sustainable finance initiatives and financial inclusion efforts. Regarding the updates on the economy - The country's gross domestic product in the second quarter of 2021 reached 11.8 percent and is seen to average between 4 and 5 percent this year. GDP growth is projected to further improve to a range of 7 to 9 percent in 2022. Our macroeconomic fundamentals remain sound and are expected to register strong recovery. We anticipate interest rates to remain low and the peso to be stable. Inflation is expected to remain manageable. Headline inflation slowed to to 4.8 percent year-on-year in September 2021 from 4.9 percent in the previous month. The year-to-date average inflation rate of 4.4 percent was above the target range of 2 to 4 percent. The latest inflation outturn is consistent with the BSP’s assessment that it could settle close to high end of the target range in the near term before decelerating back to within the target by year-end. Overseas Filipino remittances are expected to rebound this year. In the first half of 2021, the cumulative remittances rose to US$16.6 billion, up from the US$15.6 billion recorded in the comparable period in 2020, or by 6.7 percent. The country’s Gross International Reserves of US$108.1 billion as of end-August 2021 is hefty. It represents 12.3 months’ worth of imports of goods and payments of services and primary income. It was also 7.8 times the country’s short-term external debt based on original maturity and 5.4 times based on residual maturity. Thrift banks are strategically positioned to be a catalyst for financial inclusion with an extensive network of 2,744 banking offices as of end-August 2021 that are spread in different regions throughout the country. Amid the pandemic, the thrift banking industry remained sound and stable as shown by sustained growth in assets and deposits, strong capital position, adequate liquidity buffers, and profitable operations. Total assets of the industry registered at PHP1.2 trillion as of end-July 2021, posting a 6.8 percent year-on-year growth rate. Meanwhile, deposit liabilities in the same period grew by 7.9 percent, highly indicative of the public’s continued trust and confidence in the industry. The risk-based capital adequacy ratio of the industry stood at 18.8 percent as of end-June 2021. This is above the minimum thresholds set by the BSP and the Bank for International Settlements. The minimum liquidity ratio of the industry was 36.5 percent as of end-June 2021, surpassing the 16 percent minimum. Net income registered at PHP6.6 billion in the six-month period ending June 2021, higher by 15.3 percent compared to last year’s level. We are optimistic that the thrift banking industry is well-equipped to withstand the risks posed by the COVID-19 crisis. Recovery in the country’s macroeconomy and the industry’s financial performance will serve as a strong foundation for thrift banks in charting their course under the new economy. Let me now focus on the key enablers which will help support the thrift banking industry as it navigates the post-COVID economy. First, the BSP’s time-bound regulatory relief measures remain in place until end of this year. This includes the easing of prudential requirements which will help thrift banks support financing requirements of micro, small and medium enterprise clients. The BSP allowed the recognition of peso-denominated loans to MSMEs as alternative compliance with reserve requirements against deposit liabilities and deposit substitutes. In addition, the BSP reduced the credit risk weights of loans granted to MSMEs that are current in status to 50 percent until end-December 2021. The BSP deferred the implementation of the amendments to the risk-based capital adequacy framework for stand-alone thrift banks, rural banks and cooperative banks to 1 January 2023, instead of 1 January 2022. Moreover, the BSP reduced the minimum liquidity ratio of stand-alone thrift banks, rural banks and cooperative banks from 20 percent to 16 percent until the end of December 2021. Overall, these measures aim to provide thrift banks with sustained capacity to expand lending activities and support the recovery of local communities. Second, thrift banks can take advantage of the fiscal incentives under the FIST Act to manage the level of non-performing assets, increase liquidity and support financing requirements of productive enterprises and households. The BSP recognizes the vital role of thrift banks in promoting the development of MSMEs. The third enabler relates to initiatives aimed at enhancing the country’s credit infrastructure and improving access of the MSME sector to formal financial channels. The BSP is working on a Credit Risk Database project in partnership with the Japan International Cooperation Agency. The project will improve access to credit by small and medium enterprises by promoting collateral-free and risk-based lending through the use of statistical scoring models that will facilitate risk profiling and assessment of the paying capacity of these enterprises. In order to improve banks’ risk assessment and accelerate turnaround time in the processing of loan applications, the BSP is also working closely with key government agencies and the private sector, including the banking industry, in the adoption of a Standardized Business Loan Application Form. This project aims to make the loan application process simple, more streamlined and borrower-friendly, particularly for small enterprises. Fourth, the COVID-19 pandemic provides a window of opportunity to bolster the integration of green or sustainable finance in the country’s recovery response. Toward this end, the BSP is working on policy reforms that will promote sustainable finance and mitigate climate-related risk. The BSP has issued enabling regulations that will promote green finance through the Sustainable Finance Framework for banks. The regulations require banks to integrate sustainability principles into their strategic objectives, corporate governance, risk management systems, and in their operations. In addition, the BSP has strengthened engagement with various stakeholders domestically and internationally in line with its intensified campaign to adopt the sustainability principles. Based on the results of the latest Banking System Outlook Survey, more than half of thrift bank respondents view sustainable finance as highly important. Although the willingness to finance sustainable project is high, thrift banks, in particular, indicated that the lack of demand for these projects and the lack of expertise or skill as the major barriers in pursuing sustainable financing. As such, the BSP is providing support in this area by developing the capacity of banks with the help of developmental institutions and training partners. Fifth, the BSP recognizes the potential of digital technology to further advance the financial inclusion agenda. As such, the BSP is committed to develop a digital finance ecosystem that supports the evolving needs of the public, particularly the low-income and unserved and underserved segments. One reform area where financial institutions, including thrift banks, can leverage on is participation in the National Retail Payment System. As of end-July 2021, there were only 17 thrift banks offering PESONet and 11 offering InstaPay electronic fund transfer services. And so we encourage thrift banks to leverage on these technologies since this will further expand your customer reach and maintain competitiveness. Lastly, the BSP is pursuing key policy issuances and initiatives to strengthen the digital infrastructure, promote safe and efficient digital payment streams, safeguard electronic customer data, and build digital mindset and skillsets inline with the BSP’s digital transformation roadmap. Related to this, the BSP has issued the Open Finance Framework which espouses consent-driven data portability, interoperability, and collaborative partnerships among incumbent financial institutions and third-party providers. Through “permission-based” access to customer financial information, financial institutions and third parties will gain access to financial information necessary to develop innovative products and services that are suited to the changing consumer needs. The forthcoming BSP policy issuances on outsourcing and regulatory sandbox or the “test and learn” framework are expected to further facilitate digitalization among smaller institutions such as thrift banks The BSP is also considering developing a course on digital transformation to assist thrift banks in preparing for a digital-ready culture and environment. The BSP also continues to support the implementation of the Philippine Identification System, or PhilSys, which will provide every Filipino with a unique, verifiable and biometric-based digital identity. This will not only address the persistent onboarding issues due to lack of acceptable IDs, but also boost account ownership by enabling remote and more seamless onboarding. The BSP is working on complementary initiatives that will facilitate digital onboarding of clients through the operationalization of the PhilSys electronic Know Your Customer, or e-KYC, and authen

Title: Revving up for Economic and Social Recovery Through Evidence-Based Policies
Occasion: Closing Event of the National Statistics Month
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ As we conclude the 32nd National Statistics Month (NSM) celebration, allow me first to thank all of you for all your hard work. The month-long activities that we have carried out highlighted our commitment in producing relevant, timely, and accurate statistics, as well as in promoting awareness and understanding of these statistics. Through these activities, we hope to gain public support for the continued enhancement of the country’s statistical system, especially amidst uncharted challenges brought about by the COVID-19 pandemic. Let me share some key points relevant to this year’s NSM theme, “Revving up for Economic and Social Recovery Through Evidenced-based Policies.” Allow me also to briefly provide you with comparative snapshots of the Philippine economic situation since the pandemic began, where we are now and the BSP’s policy actions to address the spill-over effects of the pandemic on the economy. The economy since the pandemic in March 2020 As you know, the country’s strong macroeconomic fundamentals before the pandemic provided the needed economic buffer against the onslaught of COVID-19. Predictably, we were not spared from the global economic slowdown. Looking back to when we first felt the brunt of the economic consequences of the pandemic in 2020, the country’s output contracted 17 percent in the second quarter, the lowest since 1981. The strict lockdown and mobility measures imposed to curb the spread of the virus in March 2020 took a heavy toll on our economy. The impact of which, promptly fed into the labor market causing unemployment to peak at 17.6 percent in April 2020. Nonetheless, we were able to keep inflation low and stable during the entire year, which averaged at 2.6 percent in 2020. This was because of the decisive and prompt monetary policy actions that kept financial markets functioning and the system liquid. On the external front, the overall balance of payment (BOP) position in 2020 straightaway captured the effects of the disruptions in global demand and supply chains. The country posted a balance of payments surplus of US$16.0 billion in 2020, driven by the surplus in the current account amounting to US$11 billion as our imports significantly dropped. Despite the crisis, we continued to build up our Gross International Reserves (GIR), capping 2020 with US$110.1 billion in reserves, equivalent to 12.3 months of import cover. More than a year later, we are now gradually seeing positive results of the implementation of our data-driven policies. After five-quarters of economic contractions, the country’s real GDP grew by 11.8 percent in Q2 2021. The unemployment rate declined to 8.1 percent in the latest August 2021 Labor Force Survey. Meanwhile, inflation averaged at 4.5 percent in the first nine months of 2021, mainly caused by supply-side factors, including the rise in world oil prices. While the inflation path is seen to remain above the target range of 2 – 4 percent up to October 2021, it is expected to decelerate to within the inflation target range of before the end of the year as the non-monetary measures of the government will help moderate the impact of supply bottlenecks. Meanwhile, as domestic activity gradually resumed, the country’s current account and the balance of payments for January to June 2021 reverted to a deficit amid strong imports of raw materials and capital goods for productive purposes, indicating pick-up in economic activity moving ahead. Nonetheless, the latest gross international reserves as of September 2021 remain ample at US$107.2 billion, equivalent to 10.8 months’ worth of imports of goods and payments for services. However, business sentiments remained cautious due to the highly transmissible delta variant, re-imposition of lockdowns, and rising inflation rate due to supply constraints. By contrast, consumer confidence is gradually improving, based on the Q3 2021 Consumer Expectations Survey. The optimism stemmed from their expectations of improvements in job creation, additional income sources, speedier vaccine roll-out, and provision of financial assistance. Economic recovery through policy coordination. All these gains were achieved with the a whole-of-government approach in addressing the crisis. On the part of the BSP, it implemented temporary and time-bound policy measures to support the economy. First were measures to boost market confidence on availability of low-cost credit resources, such as cuts in the policy rate and the reserve requirement. The lower policy rates were meant to influence banks to reduce their own lending rates; thereby, promoting credit-taking activities. Meanwhile, lower reserve requirement levels is intended to increase the volume of loanable funds. Second were extraordinary liquidity measures to the National Government (NG) to help fund the pandemic-response measures. These include provisional advances to the NG, purchases of government securities (GS) in the secondary market, and remittance of advance dividends to the NG. Third were regulatory and operational relief measures to maintain stability of the financial system and ensure public access to financial services. These included first, allowing loans to micro, small, and medium enterprises as compliance to the reserve requirement; second, increasing the single borrower’s limit; and third, raising the ceiling for real-estate loans to promote bank lending. Indeed, while economic activity has improved, but the overall momentum of the economic recovery remains tentative as the threat of COVID-19 infections continues. Still, the BSP believes that sustained monetary policy support for domestic demand should help to sustain the economic recovery. Looking ahead, the BSP will continue to support to the economy for as long as necessary to ensure the country’s strong and sustainable economic recovery. The BSP will also remain vigilant against any emerging risks to the outlook for inflation and growth and will adjust its policy settings as needed. A key element in our continued surveillance and policy actions is the availability of accurate, relevant, and timely statistics or data. And as part of the Philippine Statistical System and one of the major producers of economic and financial data, the BSP shall continuously enhance its statistics, in terms of coverage, granularity and timeliness, by leveraging on the advancements in information technology and data analytics. Before closing this month-long celebration, I would like to highlight three key messages: - First, our solid fundamentals allowed us to weather this pandemic, while our evidence-based policies and measures paved the way for our gradual recovery a year after. - Second, we, at the BSP, remain steadfast to achieving our mandate of maintaining low and stable inflation to safeguard the Filipinos’ purchasing power, ensuring an efficient payments and settlements system, and safeguarding the country’s financial system. - And finally, it is imperative that we all work together as, one nation, with the shared objective of sustaining the economic recovery. On behalf of the BSP, I would like to congratulate and thank all those that helped make this month-long event a success. To the PSA, we look forward to partnering with you again at next year’s event. Hopefully, we would be able to hold a physical event of some sort by then. Again, thank you and mabuhay tayong lahat!​

Title: Closing Remarks of BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno for the Turn-over Ceremony of Donation of the Anti-Money Laundering Council and Its Secretariat
Occasion: Turn-over Ceremony of Donation of the Anti-Money Laundering Council and Its Secretariat
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Commissioner of the Insurance Commission, Atty. Dennis B. Funa. Chairman of the Securities and Exchange Commission, Atty. Emilio B. Aquino. Executive Director of the Anti-Money Laundering Council (AMLC) Secretariat, Atty. Mel Georgie B. Racela. Administrator of the Hospicio De San Jose, Sister Maria Socorro G. Evidente, DC. Rector of the National Shrine of Our Mother of Perpetual Help, Father Victorino A. Cueto, CSsR. On behalf of the AMLC and its Secretariat, I would like to thank the Hospicio de San Jose and the National Shrine of Our Mother of Perpetual Help—also known as Baclaran Church—for its efforts and initiatives, especially during this unprecedented time. We hope that our donation today can help further your endeavors to help those who are most in need. As you may know, the AMLC is mandated to protect the country from terrorism financing, money laundering, and related crimes. While law enforcement and government agencies, such as the AMLC, are there to promote peace and order, civic and charitable organizations are just as crucial. Through various programs, these groups not only provide refuge to the impoverished, the downtrodden, and the marginalized but also open doors to opportunities and maybe even a new lease on life. They say crime is a reflection of a society. If so, then perhaps, wellbeing among its people can protect a society from the ills of lawlessness. And this is where we come together, despite the differences in our mandates and responsibilities. We are grateful for such humanitarian organizations. May the Hospicio de San Jose and the National Shrine of Our Mother of Perpetual Help continue to bless our country with their altruism and may many others follow their lead. Thank you.

Title: Message of BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno for the AMLC 20th Anniversary
Occasion: Anti-Money Laundering Council 20th Anniversary
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Anti-Money Laundering Council (AMLC) Members, Insurance Commissioner, Atty. Dennis B. Funa; Securities and Exchange Commission Chairperson, Atty. Emilio B. Aquino, AMLC Secretariat Executive Director, Atty. Mel Georgie B.
