The digitalization of payments has been instrumental in boosting the country's economy during this pandemic. The launch and development of digital payment streams, such as PESONet, InstaPay, QRPh P2P, and P2M, created gateways for retailers and their customers to transact digitally. These digital payment solutions allow businesses to sustain their operations under the new economy by enabling them to offer safe and secure payment options to their customers. Recent trends show a sustained growth in the volume of PESONet and InstaPay payments. As the trend toward payments digitalization continues, the PESONet payments volume may overtake check payments over time. The volume of InstaPay payments has already overtaken that of ATM off-us cash withdrawals or withdrawals made with another bank's ATM machine. In fact, the 2020 e-payments measurement report shows that one out of five - or about 20.1 percent - of the monthly volume of retail payments have already been transformed into digital form. The growth in digital payments usage was mainly driven by high-frequency, low-value retail transactions, such as merchant payments and person-to-person or P2P payments. Among payments by individuals, P2B or merchant payments and P2P transactions hold the biggest potential in further accelerating e-payments adoption. From 2019 to 2020, the monthly digital payments volume for merchant payments increased by 47.8 percent, while P2P monthly digital payments volume grew by 18.1 percent. The growth in these sub-use-cases could be attributed to the increased usage of transaction accounts for e-commerce and P2P remittances. These data suggest that payments digitalization has been broadly observed among retailers for merchandising transactions. Research shows that the use of financial technology for purchases and digital channels for making payments drive trade in goods and services and helped offset economic losses brought about by the COVID-19 pandemic. It also tells us that receiving payments through digital channels, purchasing goods or paying bills online spur expansion of business activities, thus, stimulating economic growth. To further support retail payments digitalization, the BSP, in collaboration with the Philippine Payments Management, Inc. or PPMI developed the QR Ph payment facilities. In accordance with BSP Circular No. 1055, this facility uses the national QR code standard, “QR Ph," to transform the fragmented QR-driven payment services into interoperable payment solutions. Launched in November 2019, the initial use case was QR Ph for person-to-person payments or “QR Ph P2P." From only six pilot participants during its launch, we now have 28 participants as of end-February 2022. We likewise saw a gradual increase in its volume last year, with a steeper growth starting in the second quarter of 2021. From the first to the third quarter of 2021, QR Ph P2P volume and value rose, on average, by 126 percent, and 209 percent, respectively. Meanwhile, the BSP and PPMI launched the QR Ph Person to Merchant or the “QR Ph P2M" facility in October 2021 to bring the benefits of digital payments to businesses, including MSMEs, and to consumers in a simple, convenient, low-cost, and safe way. Using QR technology, small entrepreneurs need not invest in costly equipment, such as point-of-sale (POS) terminals, to capture electronic data. Instead, merchants only need to print the QR code generated by their payment system providers, display those codes, and obtain payments by simply asking their customer to scan the codes. As of end-February 2022, there are 16 participating payment service providers or PSPs in the QR Ph P2M facilities and more of them shall be onboarded in the coming months. Moving forward, the BSP continues to collaborate with the payments industry for the development of new digital payment streams that are relevant to the payment needs of the retail trade industry. Bills Pay and Request To Pay are forthcoming digital payment streams that will run on the InstaPay rail to make person-to-business - or P2B - and business-to-business - or B2B - payments, such as payments for goods, services, bills, and utilities, more convenient. Another initiative utilizing the PESONet rail underway is direct debit. This can facilitate more efficient settlement of recurring payments to billers. Both multiple batch settlement and direct debit initiatives can facilitate P2B payments, such as loan amortizations and lease rentals; B2B payments, such as supplier payments; and payments made by individuals, businesses, and the government to other government institutions. These facilities can be used by consumers even if the transaction accounts of the customers and merchants/billers are maintained in different payment service providers. We will also enhance cross-border payments that will facilitate retailers' participation in a globalized economy. Amid these advances, we continuously develop appropriate cybersecurity, anti-money laundering, and financial consumer protection policies to promote the welfare of businesses and consumers and sustain their confidence in the national payment system. To safeguard information security and data privacy, the BSP has been developing relevant cybersecurity policies that require BSP-supervised financial institutions, or BSFIs, to adopt robust technology risk management systems and effective cybersecurity resilience measures. To ensure the confidentiality and integrity of customer information, the BSP advocates a "zero trust" model which requires that any access to a BSFI's digital infrastructure shall be regarded as a suspicious access and must be resolved in such a way that it undergoes verification and security screening. The BSP likewise complements the existing regulations on financial consumer protection with an intensified financial literacy campaign that promotes the responsible use of digital financial services and with an improved means to elevate concerns to the BSP. We are also pleased that Republic Act No. 11595, otherwise known as An Act Amending Republic Act No. 8762 or the Retail Trade Liberalization Act of 2000, has been signed into law on December 10, 2021. Under this law, the capital requirement for foreign retailers is lowered from around P125 million to P25 million. This structural reform measure will attract foreign investors and boost the domestic economy. As we continue the path to recovery, the retail trade sector is more than ready to bounce back. This is hinged on the fact that physical movements are expected to return to pre-pandemic levels. Google Mobility Indices showed that physical movements have significantly increased, especially when most of the country was placed in Alert Level 2 in February, and when 38 areas were further eased to Alert Level 1 for the first half of March. Mobility, particularly in key establishments such as pharmacies and groceries, has grown. With improved mobility, we expect the economic numbers to rise significantly, accompanied by the developments in the digital payments space. The National Economic and Development Authority estimates that t

Title: Message to PDS Group for 17th Annual Awards
Occasion: PDS 17th Annual Awards
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ PDS Group President and CEO Ramon Monzon, PDS Group Chairman Emeritus Cesar Virata, SEC Chairman Emilio Aquino, Treasurer Rosalia De Leon, representatives from the financial services sector, ladies and gentlemen, good evening. First of all, let me extend my warmest congratulations to this year's awardees. Despite challenging and uncertain market conditions, you pushed tirelessly forward to achieve growth, serve clients, and set milestones for your organization. But as we celebrate your well-deserved achievements tonight, let us not forget to look back at our past in order to come to a greater appreciation of the present circumstance and chart a better path forward. When the pandemic first hit, the financial sector was among the first to feel the pinch. The markets collapsed, liquidity dried up, and investors fled to safe havens. Businesses, big and small, had to shut down and consumers tightened spending. To augment the national government's efforts, the BSP acted swiftly and decisively to respond to the crisis. Our combined actions – from cutting the policy rate, extending provisional advances to the national government, to purchasing government securities in the secondary market – amounted to 11.1 percent of GDP. Altogether, these measures helped calm the market, eased liquidity conditions, and restored financial market activity. By now, we are seeing bright spots that suggest that the economy is on its way to recovery. The economy grew 5.6 percent in 2021, higher than government estimates and market expectations. Unemployment dropped to a low of 6.4 percent in January 2022 as businesses opened and adjusted to the new economy. Several other indicators, from mobility indices to manufacturing activities, suggest that full recovery is well on its way. While there are certain risks clouding our outlook, I remain optimistic about our prospects because the Duterte administration didn't sit idly by as the virus rages. We made sure to invest in long-term structural reforms that would ensure the sustainability of our recovery. Our goal is not only to regain what was lost to the pandemic, but to embark on a more sustainable path of development. In this vision, financial institutions will play an important role. As a key player in the movement of capital, we hope that we can count on your full support in our push to deepen the financial markets along the route of sustainable finance. We trust that you can lend us your expertise to mobilize capital in a way that does not only serve the interests of your shareholders; but also helps us address the urgent challenges of our time as partners in recovery and nation-building. Again, congratulations to the winners of this year's 17th Annual Awards at mabuhay po kayong lahat! #

Title: Speech for the Philippine Economic Briefing
Occasion: Philippine Economic Briefing 2022
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Good morning to everyone joining us today here and online. It is my privilege to speak before you in today’s Philippine Economic Briefing, with the theme "Six Years of Institutionalizing Game-Changing Reforms and Shaping the New Economy.“ Over the past six years, we have achieved many accomplishments and faced a few serious challenges. In 2020, economies across the globe suffered severe downturns due to the coronavirus pandemic. And this year, just as we were adjusting to the new normal, Russia’s invasion of Ukraine and the global response present new challenges and uncertainty. But we are optimistic as we see clear signs of recovery. But for this morning, let me first discuss key developments in the BSP’s focus areas over the last 6 years, then conclude with our outlook. The BSP remains committed to its mandate of maintaining price and financial stability, and a safe, efficient, and inclusive payments and settlements system. At the same time, the BSP maintained a reform-oriented mindset. The COVID-19 pandemic did not deter us from pursuing game-changing reforms. In fact, the pandemic had proved to be a catalyst that accelerated many of our reforms, such as digitalization and sustainability in the financial system. On the monetary front, the BSP adopted the Interest Rate Corridor framework in 2016 to strengthen the transmission of changes in the monetary policy stance to the rest of the economy. In addition, the inclusion of BSP securities in standard monetary operations provided an additional instrument for managing liquidity in the financial system. In 2019, President Rodrigo Roa Duterte signed Republic Act No. 11211, amending the BSP Charter. This granted the BSP the authority to issue its own debt securities. This law embodies measures to align the BSP’s operations with international standards and global best practices, thus improving the BSP’s corporate viability and enhancing its capacity for crafting proactive policies, among others. Another key reform passed to address the impact of the pandemic is the “Financial Institutions Strategic Transfer Act”. The FIST Act is a pre-emptive measure to ensure banking sector stability in case of significant spikes in loan defaults. The passage of R.A. No. 11439 or the Act of Providing for the Regulation and Organization of Islamic Banks, marked an important milestone in the history of Islamic banking in the Philippines. Islamic finance will help attract funds from Islamic investors looking for opportunities to support infrastructure requirements particularly in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao. In line with our efforts to promote greater financial inclusion, the BSP introduced the basic deposit accounts framework. This enables the account holder to access a wide range of financial services, including savings, credit, insurance, investments, and remittances, with limited requirements. As of the SECOND quarter of 2021, the number of basic deposit account holders reached 7.4 million. This brings deposit account ownership to a total of 90.5 million Filipinos. The BSP launched the National Retail Payment System or the NRPS, which promotes interoperability among payment service providers, allowing for efficient and seamless transactions. The NRPS is a safe, efficient, reliable, and interoperable retail payment system and is a critical enabler of our financial inclusion initiatives. The BSP launched the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap 2020-2023, charting strategies for an efficient, inclusive, safe, and secure digital payments ecosystem. The roadmap aims to convert half of the total volume of retail payments into digital form and onboard 70 percent of Filipino adults into the formal financial system using payment or transaction accounts by 2023. We are now seeing the fruits of our efforts to digitalize the country’s payments system. As of end-2020, the share of monthly digital payments volume reached 20.1 percent, slightly exceeding the BSP’s target of “20 percent in 2020” set in 2015. This indicates that the adoption of digital payments remains on track to reach at least the 50 percent mark by 2023. The issuance of the Digital Banking Framework also aims to advance financial inclusion and the adoption and growth of digital financial services in the country. As of end-2021, the BSP granted digital bank licenses to six financial institutions. This is expected to further accelerate the creation of e-money accounts. We also launched the Sustainable Central Banking Program, which aims to foster environmentally responsible and sustainable policies and work practices in our key functions and operations. The BSP also issued the Sustainable Finance Framework and the Environmental and Social Risk Management System Framework. Sustainability is central to achieving the BSP’s core mandates of maintaining price and financial stability, given the potential impact of climate disasters on supply of goods and services, as well as on incomes. As such, the BSP invested US$550 million, to date, in the green bond fund of the Bank of International Settlements. Against the backdrop of all these milestones and reforms, the Philippine economy remained strong. From 2016 to 2019, the Philippine economy expanded annually by 6 to 7 percent. However, as a result of the COVID-19 pandemic, the economy contracted by 9.6 percent in 2020, following 21 years of uninterrupted growth. The economy rebounded strongly, growing at 12-percent in the second quarter of 2021, and has managed to sustain growth since then. With mobility restrictions easing, the economy is returning to pre-pandemic growth levels. In any case, the BSP pursued initiatives that supported the economy throughout the pandemic. First were measures to boost market confidence, such as cuts in the policy rate to promote credit-taking activities and the reserve requirement to increase loanable funds. Second were extraordinary liquidity measures, such as provisional advances and remittance of dividends to the national government, and purchases of government securities in the secondary market. So far, the BSP has infused over PHP2.2 trillion to the financial system, equivalent to about 11.1 percent of GDP. Third were regulatory and operational relief measures to maintain stability of the financial system and ensure public access to financial services. The Philippines’ long history of implementing critical structural reforms strengthened the economy’s resilience to face the challenges of the pandemic. Despite the impact of the pandemic on the economy, credit rating agencies have maintained the Philippines’ investment grade ratings—amid a wave of negative rating actions globally. This shows confidence in the whole-of-government efforts, including the BSP’s response measures toward recovery from the COVID-19 crisis and a better post-COVID Philippine economy. Moving on to key developments in the monetary, external and banking sectors, during the periods 2016-2021, the BSP has been able to keep inflation low and stable. Manageable inflation provided an enabling environment for growth. From January to March this year, headline inflation stood at 3.4 percent, well within the target range of 2 to 4 percent. While upside risks exist including shortages in domestic pork and fish supply, as well as from the potential impact of higher oil prices on transport fares, average inflation is projected to settle within the target band in 2023. Meanwhile, the country’s strong external position continued to be a key credit strength, inspiring investor confidence. Remittances from overseas Filipinos registered a 5.1 percent growth in 2021. At the height of the pandemic in 2020, they only contracted by less than one percent. The BPO industry remains a steady source of foreign exchange. BPO receipts in 2021 grew by 9.5 percent. In 2021, net FDI inflows increased by 54.2 percent to USD10.5 billion. As of February 2022, Gross International Reserves stood at 107.8 billion dollars, which continue to provide a liquidity buffer equivalent to 9.5 months’ worth of imports of goods and payments of services and primary income. As bank regulator, the BSP ensures that the Philippine banking system is sound and stable. Indicators show the continued growth of assets, deposits, and capital, as well as net profit, capital and liquidity buffers. Banks have ample loan-loss reserves and a manageable loan quality. These allow the banking system to support the economy. Bank credit grew seven consecutive months since August 2021, reaching 8.8 percent last February and reflecting expansion in business activity. This supports the view of an even stronger economic rebound this year. Now, let me briefly touch on our economic outlook and the BSP’s policy normalization strategy. The government expects economic growth to accelerate to a range of 7 to 9 percent this year and 6 to 7 percent next year. There are strong bases for this optimistic outlook. Growth will be supported by the implementation of the 2022 national budget, Build, Build, Build Program, the Corporate Recovery and Tax Incentives for Enterprises law, and Executive Order No. 166, adopting the Ten-Point Policy Agenda to Accelerate and Sustain Economic Recovery from the Pandemic. Moreover, game-changing legislation, such as the amended Public Services Act will drive economic growth, moving forward. This economic outlook is supported in part by the BSP’s efforts toward financial digitalization and inclusion, and promotion of a stable inflation and financial environment conducive to economic growth. However, the favorable economic outlook comes with risks. One of the key risks to domestic and global economic growth is the ongoing conflict between Russia and Ukraine. Its direct impact is consequential, yet its indirect impact through higher world oil prices and slower global growth could be significant. The BSP supports the national government’s fiscal interventions to address risks to inflation and maintain the

Title: Closing Remarks for the IPON Conference
Occasion: IPON Conference: What to Do in 2022
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​Secretary Leonor Magtolis-Briones, dear teachers, students, families, and guests from the Department of Education (DepEd), good afternoon. Being a long-time professor and student myself, I know that educators leave a lasting impact on a student's life. As strong influencers, they impart not just academic knowledge but also valuable lessons that students can carry throughout their lifetime. More than just being an authority figure in the classroom, they are leaders, mentors, and motivators who nurture young leaders who can make a difference in improving the quality of life of our people. But teaching is not an easy profession. The COVID-19 pandemic and the sudden shift to distance learning added to the existing challenges of our educators. With many teachers heavily indebted and living from paycheck to paycheck, delivering on the demands of the profession becomes a difficult task as financial distress can affect health and workplace productivity. But even before the pandemic, Filipinos had often been unprepared for financial shocks. The 2019 Financial Inclusion Survey revealed that nearly half or 49 per cent of adults could not meet everyday spending needs and regularly borrowed money to meet their financial obligations. Only 23% had insurance to protect themselves and their families from risks. The same survey indicates that only half (55%) of Filipino adults knew the effect of inflation on their purchasing power. At the same time, two-thirds had little to no knowledge of the impact of compounding interest rates on the value of their bank deposits or their loans. Studies have shown that a person's financial health and wellness are closely related to his or her level of financial literacy. While it is true that an individual with greater financial capacity is more likely to be financially healthy than someone with less, just as crucial would be a person's financial behavior related to saving and planning ahead. This is why the Bangko Sentral prioritizes financial education and literacy among its advocacies. Together with DepEd, we recognize the importance of financial education as one of the means to hone the financial skills of our teachers. A World Bank Study on financial capability shows that "savings habits and preferences formed in early life stay with Filipinos into adulthood". Thus, for the education sector, our approach is two-pronged. First, we embed financial literacy concepts in the basic education curriculum to ensure that children are taught the value of saving and basic money management skills as early as kindergarten. Second, we integrate personal financial management in the regular training of DepEd teachers and non-teaching personnel. As you may know, last June 03, 2021, DepEd issued the Financial Education Policy, which strengthens the integration of financial education in the K-to-12 curriculum. This policy complements BSP's economic and financial education program. The BSP, along with other financial sector regulators, is also working with the Commission on Higher Education (CHED) to embed personal finance classes at the tertiary level. Moreover, the BSP has already forged strategic partnerships with other key government agencies to ensure that our financial education programs are sustainable and scalable, with multiplier effects. We need a whole-of-society approach to financial education—recognizing the critical role of all relevant stakeholders, such as individuals, households, communities, and public and private sector partners in advancing our goal of a financially healthy and literate citizenry. And so, we call on you, our dear educators, to remember that more than just a profession, teaching is a responsibility. But unfortunately, we cannot teach something we have yet to learn ourselves. Thus, we must continue taking steps in the right direction to regain control of our financial lives. Teachers are also role models who exemplify good financial habits—whose dedication and perseverance to get back on track with their finances, manage debt responsibly, and turn from debtors to investors—are worthy of respect and admiration. Once again, thank you for your dedication and congratulations to all the participants and organizers of the IPON Conference. Maraming salamat, at mabuhay po tayong lahat.#​

Title: Speech for the 25th Founding Anniversary of the Fund Managers Association of the Philippines
Occasion: 25th Founding Anniversary of the Fund Managers Association of the Philippines
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​Let me extend my warmest greetings to the Fund Managers Association of the Philippines on its 25th Founding Anniversary. It is a testament to your organization’s resilience and relevance that you have achieved this milestone in the face of challenging market conditions. From its humble beginnings in April 1997, FMAP has weathered countless turns of business cycles to grow into the respected organization that it is today. Throughout the years, FMAP has served as the voice of the fund management industry and contributed to the formulation of valuable policies and guidelines from regulatory agencies that include the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas. As we cautiously mark what appears to be the tail-end of the pandemic, I trust that FMAP will continue to lend its support as we chart our way out of this crisis and start the rebuilding process. Your anniversary theme, “Silver Linings: Road to Recovery” reflects our hope and confidence that the Philippine economy is on a strong recovery path. On the part of the BSP, you can rest assured that we will continue to promote an enabling regulatory environment for the institutions under our supervision. Digitalization and sustainable finance are priority areas for us at the Bangko Sentral and we will continue to steer the financial industry in this direction. Another important development is the passage of the Financial Consumer Protection Act by Congress. If this is signed by President Duterte, I trust that FMAP will help us implement this law to better protect the interests of the investing public and reinforce trust and confidence in the financial system. This year and beyond, I trust that the FMAP will help us realize our vision of inclusive and sustainable financial markets. Again, congratulations on your 25th founding anniversary! #​

Title: Monetary Policy and Central Banking Toward a New Economy
Occasion: 3rd Philippine-Singapore Business and Investment Summit
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Ambassador Joseph del Mar Yap, fellow officials and colleagues from the economic cluster of the Philippine government, guests, good day. Thank you for this opportunity to provide updates on Philippine monetary policy and how we have continuously prepared and made ourselves ready to compete in the new economy. The BSP implemented a wide range of monetary and regulatory measures, which complemented the massive relief efforts of the national government. First, the BSP shored up market confidence and ensured adequate liquidity and credit. Second, the BSP complemented government programs through extraordinary liquidity measures. Third, the BSP implemented regulatory and operational relief measures. All these contributed to getting us back on track to economic recovery. First, we cut our policy rate by a cumulative 200 basis points resulting in a record low of two (2) percent reduced RRP rate, which helped stimulate provision of credit to productive activities. We also reduced our reserve requirement against deposit and deposit substitute liabilities For Universal and Commercial Banks and Non-Bank Financial Institutions with Quasi-Banking Functions, effective April 03 2020, we reduced it from 14 percent to 12 percent. For Thrift Banks (TBs) and Rural/ Cooperative Banks (RCBs), effective 31 July 2020, we reduced it from 4 percent to 3 percent. Second, we extended time-bound liquidity support to the national government to help finance its pandemic-response programs. We also purchased government securities in the secondary market during this critical time. So far, the BSP has infused over PHP2.2 trillion into the financial system. This is equivalent to about 11.1 percent of GDP. Third, the BSP put in place key regulatory and operational relief measures for banks, which helped maintain the financial system's stability and ensure the public's continued access to financial services. BSP will continue to engage banks in monitoring developments and risks surrounding the impact of CoVID-19. These actions taken by the BSP, as part of a whole-of-government approach, contributed to the economy growing by 5.7 percent in 2021, reversing the pandemic-driven recession in 2020. Some key economic developments include the rebound of manufacturing activities to their highest level in three years as demand and production picked up. Pandemic relief measures and sustained economic recovery helped improve the labor situation. The unemployment rate dropped to 6.4 percent in February 2022 from 8.8 percent in February 2021 and from a peak of 17.6 percent in April 2020. The government continuously invested in infrastructure, which provided support to the labor market and overall economic activity. Infrastructure spending will increase economic capacity and efficiency, improving the mobility of people and goods. The increase in debt resulting in part from higher infrastructure spending is expected to pay off. The infrastructure projects are designed to propel economic recovery and growth. It is worth noting that despite the increase in public debt, it remains manageable as the Philippines entered the pandemic in a position of fiscal strength. The resilience demonstrated by the economy amid the pandemic enabled the Philippines to maintain its investment-grade ratings despite a wave of downgrades globally. Throughout the pandemic, all our sovereign ratings were affirmed by credit rating agencies. Against this backdrop, we find that business sentiment and consumer outlook have become buoyant. The BSP’s consumer and business expectations surveys suggest broad improvements in outlook for the coming months largely due to the improving state of the economy and an uptick in the volume of demand and sales. On the monetary front, headline inflation increased to 4.0 percent year-on-year in March 2022, higher than the previous month’s rate of 3.0 percent. The resulting year-to-date average inflation of 3.4 percent for Q1 2022 is within the government’s inflation target range of 2.0-4.0 percent for the year. The BSP is mindful that as the economy recovers and gradually returns to normalcy, the extraordinary measures will need to be scaled back. The timing and conditions of BSP’s exit strategy for its pandemic interventions will continue to be guided by the inflation and growth outlook over the medium term, the state of public health, and domestic and global risks to the economy. On the external front, we expect our external payments position to remain a source of strength. The country’s robust external accounts and structural foreign exchange inflows are expected to mitigate potential negative impacts from advanced economies’ monetary tightening, as well as headwinds from the Ukraine-Russia conflict. Gross international reserves (GIR) stood at USD108.5 billion as of end-March 2022. The GIR level is equivalent to 9.6 months’ worth of imports of goods and payments for services and primary income. Meanwhile, external debt-to-GDP ratio settled at 27.0 percent as of end-2021 compared to approximately 60.0 percent in mid-2000s. In 2021, overseas workers’ remittances grew by 5.1 percent and is expected to grow by 4 percent this year. Overseas Filipinos in Singapore are a steady source of remittances, accounting for 7.0 percent of total remittances last year. The business process outsourcing (BPO) industry is another steady source of foreign exchange. BPO services in 2021 grew by 9.5 percent as the sector rapidly adjusted to new-normal work conditions. In 2021, net foreign direct investments increased by 54.2 percent to USD10.5 billion, reflecting positive foreign investor sentiment and improving confidence in the country’s long-term growth prospects. This augurs well for business activity and job creation. Singapore has been a vital source of FDIs. Singapore’s FDIs to the Philippines more than tripled last year to USD761 million from USD237 million in 2020. As the economy recovers, foreign investment pledges reached PHP192.3billion or 71.5 percent higher than in 2020. These foreign investment pledges are projected to create over 66,000 jobs. Singapore was the top source of approved investments in 2021 with a total of PHP80.2 billion, followed by the Netherlands, Japan, UK, and the US. The recently passed amendments to the Retail Trade Liberalization Law, Foreign Investments Act and Public Service Act will further open the economy to attract steady FDI flows. Now moving on to the Philippine banking sector’s performance… The regulatory reforms implemented in the past have helped banks build resilience to shocks. Asset quality remains sound with non-performing loans at manageable levels despite the pandemic. Banks also have ample buffers against credit losses. Philippine banks have generally observed prudent lending standards amid a sound regulatory environment. Also, the Financial Institutions Strategic Transfer Act or FIST Act, which enables banks to dispose of bad assets via asset management companies, serves as a preemptive measure to manage any increase in loan defaults. Capitalization remains well above the minimum regulatory requirements, providing banks with sufficient capacity to absorb shocks. Meanwhile, bank lending has recovered strongly with the easing of mobility restrictions and steady progress in our vaccination rollout. We are also making headway in financial digitalization. Our goal is that by 2023, at least 50 percent of retail payments should be done digitally and at least 70 percent of Filipino adults should have transaction accounts. Complementing this reform is the Financial Consumer Protection Act intended to strengthen the protection of financial consumers against cybercrimes. In line with our digital transformation roadmap, the BSP has issued six digital banking licenses. We expect digital banks to start strong as they capitalize on the growing demand for cross-border digital remittances from the large overseas Filipino workforce across Asia and the Middle East. In November 2021, the BSP and the Monetary Authority of Singapore signed an enhanced FinTech Cooperation Agreement. This agreement benefits the Philippines as Singapore is a key trading partner and a major source of remittances. This move is also a step toward linking our domestic payments system with our regional peers. In line with global efforts toward climate change adaptation and disaster risk mitigation, the BSP promotes a more sustainable future for the Filipino people. We have issued regulations requiring banks to incorporate sustainability principles into their operations. We are also among the early investors in the Bank of International Settlements’ Green Bond Fund and the Asian Green Bond Fund. Taking all these into consideration, we are optimistic that the Philippine economy will post an even better performance in 2022. We expect it to grow by 7.0 to 9.0 percent this year and by 6.0 to 7.0 percent next year. Recent key economic developments give us confidence in the country’s ability to sustain recovery in the near-term. Growth will be supported by the implementation of the 2022 national budget, the Build, Build, Build projects, the Corporate Recovery and Tax Incentives for Enterprises law, as well as amendments to the Retail Trade Liberalization Act, the Foreign Investments Act, and Public Services Act. However, like the rest of the world, we are also closely monitoring the ongoing Ukraine-Russia crisis. So far, we expect the conflict's impact on the country's trade, investments, and remittances to remain negligible given the minimal economic linkages of the Philippines with Russia and Ukraine. Meanwhile, the BSP supports the Philippine government’s implementation of social protection measures to mitigate the adverse impact of rising crude oil prices on vulnerable sectors. In closing, let me thank our partners in Singapore who have played significant roles in the Philippine economic narrative. We invite you to continue working with us as we move into the next chapter

Title: The Philippines: On the Road to Full Economic Recovery
Occasion: Bank of America Emerging Markets Investor Conference
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Good afternoon. Thank you for this opportunity to provide updates on the Philippines’ recent economic performance and the BSP’s initiatives to ensure a strong and sustainable recovery in the new economy. Following the pandemic-driven recession in 2020, the Philippine economy is now on its way to full recovery. The economy strongly rebounded, growing at 12.1 percent in the second quarter of 2021. Since then, it has continued its positive performance. For the full year of 2021, the economy grew by 5.7 percent, which is well above the government’s target range of 5.0 percent to 5.5 percent. The country’s labor market conditions have also improved, with the unemployment rate declining to 6.4 percent in February this year from 8.8 percent in the same period last year and a peak of 17.6 percent in April 2020. Developments in the manufacturing industry are also encouraging, as the purchasing manager’s index hit a three-year high of 53.2 in March 2022. Moving on to monetary and external developments. For the first quarter of the year, inflation averaged at 3.4 percent. Inflationary pressures are mostly due to surging energy costs, but this was offset by the continued slowdown in the price increases of food. In the last policy-setting meeting, the BSP’s Monetary Board kept the policy rate at a historic low of 2.0 percent to help sustain the momentum of economic recovery, as inflation expectations remained manageable. We are closely monitoring the ongoing conflict between Russia and Ukraine and its potential impact on the domestic economy. While its direct impact is manageable, its indirect impact - higher oil world prices and slower global growth - could be more serious. The BSP supports the national government’s fiscal interventions to address inflation risks and maintain the economic recovery momentum. On the Philippine banking system (PBS), banks in the country have remained sound and stable throughout the COVID-19 pandemic, with liquidity and credit conditions supportive of growth and well above regulatory requirements. Outstanding loans of universal and commercial banks grew by 8.8 percent in February this year, from 8.4 percent in the previous month. As of end-February 2022, the gross non-performing loans or NPL ratio remained manageable at 4.2 percent. The peso depreciated by 2.02 percent to ₱52.05 as of April 11, from ₱50.77 at end-2021. This was due to the negative market sentiment affecting emerging markets across the globe amid the Russia-Ukraine conflict. The BSP keeps its market-determined exchange-rate policy and intervenes only to ensure orderly market conditions and prevent excessive short-term volatility in the exchange rate. Meantime, the country’s external accounts continue to provide adequate buffers against external headwinds. The increase in overseas Filipino cash remittances by 5.1 percent, business process outsourcing (BPO) services by 9.5 percent, and net foreign direct investments (FDIs) by 54.2 percent in 2021, all strongly add to the country’s external foothold. The latest gross international reserves (GIR) of USD108.5 billion as of end-March 2022, represent a hefty buffer equivalent to 9.6 months’ worth of imports of goods, payments of services, and primary income. Our low external debt-to-GDP ratio of 27 percent as of end-2021 also acts as a cushion against external shocks. Amid favorable macroeconomic fundamentals, the Philippines has kept its investment-grade credit ratings, despite a wave of downgrades globally. This is a vote of confidence for the Philippine economy’s medium- and long-term growth prospects. We expect continued improvement in economic indicators as economic recovery sustains its momentum. The government targets economic growth to accelerate to a range of 7.0 to 9.0 percent this year, and 6.0 to 7.0 percent next year. This will be driven by improved business sentiment on the Philippines’ management of COVID cases and accelerated vaccination program, supported by vital legislation to further liberalize the economy that can potentially fuel FDIs; and increased investments by domestic firms. The government’s continued investments in infrastructure and social services are also seen to support this prospect. We are also making headway in financial digitalization. Under the BSP’s Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap, we aim to achieve two major objectives by 2023 -- First, at least half of financial transactions in the country are done digitally and second, at least 70 percent of Filipino adults have transaction accounts. We are hopeful that digital banks will help accelerate our goals. We now have six (6) purely digital banks in the country after the issuance of the digital banking framework in 2020. We are also committed to advancing sustainable finance. Sustainability is central to achieving the BSP’s core mandates of maintaining price and financial stability, given the potential impact of climate disasters on supply of goods and services, as well as on incomes. While the Philippines’ macroeconomic fundamentals remain sound, and our medium- and long-term growth prospects look bright, we recognize that achieving full recovery requires a whole-of-government approach. The BSP remains cognizant of the need to strike a balance between providing adequate stimulus to sustain economic recovery and managing the risks to price and financial stability. Thank you for your attention.​

Title: The Philippines’ Path to Full Economic Recovery
Occasion: Barclay’s Emerging Markets Investor Roundtable
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Thank you for this opportunity to provide updates on our outlook for the Philippine economy and whole-of-government initiatives to ensure a strong and sustainable recovery in the face of domestic and global risks. After contracting by 9.5 percent in 2020, the economy bounced back, growing by 5.7 percent in 2021 and reversing the pandemic-driven recession. Bright spots are showing across critical sectors. In terms of labor market conditions, the country’s unemployment rate improved to 6.4 percent in February from the same period last year and from a peak of 17.6 percent in April 2020 The banking system has remained sound throughout the pandemic. Banks have maintained sufficient capital and liquidity buffers. Bank lending has been improving, and banks have kept their exposure to bad debts manageable. Our external sector accounts continue to provide sufficient buffers against external headwinds. The country’s gross international reserves continue to provide a hefty buffer against unforeseen demand for foreign exchange liquidity. Improved cash remittances from overseas Filipinos, business process outsourcing receipts, and foreign direct investment inflows are supporting the country's dollar reserves. The Bangko Sentral is actively contributing to a post-COVID Philippine economy that is more resilient, technologically advanced, sustainable, and inclusive than ever before. Under our Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap, we aim to achieve two major objectives by 2023 – First, at least half of financial transactions in the country are done digitally and second, at least 70 percent of Filipino adults have transaction accounts. We are also committed to advancing sustainable finance. Under the Sustainable Finance Framework, banks are encouraged to design instruments that will help mobilize funds for green or sustainable projects. Moving on to inflation. For the first quarter of the year, inflation settled at 3.4 percent, within the government's 2 to 4 percent target range, on account of surging energy costs, which were offset by the continued slowdown of food inflation. We have observed that domestic economic activity has gained stronger traction with the further easing of mobility restrictions and steady progress in our vaccination efforts. However, heightened geopolitical tensions and a resurgence in COVID-19 infections in some countries have also clouded the outlook for global economic growth. Supply-chain disruptions could also contribute to inflationary pressures. We will continue to closely monitor developments in this area and make timely interventions, as needed, to address potential second-round effects. Having said this, the Monetary Board sees scope to maintain the BSP’s policy settings in order to safeguard the momentum of economic recovery in the context of increased uncertainty. In terms of policy adjustments, allow me to reiterate that we do not necessarily have to move in step with the monetary policy adjustments of the US Federal Reserve. The BSP calibrates its monetary policy settings in response to external developments only to the extent that they influence the outlook on growth and inflation. Monetary policy decisions have been, and will continue to be, data-driven and anchored on evolving domestic developments to avoid unintended consequences associated with protracted easy monetary policies. The timing and conditions under which the BSP will unwind its pandemic interventions will continue to be guided by the growth and inflation outlook over the medium term, and relevant risks to the economy. While the BSP expects supply-side pressures to subside, we will continue to watch for any signs of inflation becoming broader-based while we continuously advocate for the various non-monetary interventions of the national government. The Philippine economy is now on its way to full recovery. To further support recovery efforts, the Philippine Congress has passed key legislative reforms to further improve the country’s competitiveness and to support increased foreign investments. These reforms, along with our long-track record of sound macroeconomic management, lead us to believe that there will be no fundamental shift in the country's economic landscape. The peso depreciated by 2.02 percent to Php52.05 as of April 11, from Php51.0 at end-2021. This was due to the negative market sentiment affecting emerging markets across the globe amid the Russia-Ukraine conflict. The BSP keeps its market-determined exchange-rate policy, and intervenes only to ensure orderly market conditions and prevent excessive short-term volatility in the exchange rate. For the BSP, pursuing policy continuity requires preserving its institutional independence, with the Monetary Board acting as a collegial body. The BSP will continue to pursue monetary and financial sector policies that are data-driven, evidence-based, and attuned to the evolving market environment. In this regard, future monetary policy decisions will continue to be anchored on evolving domestic and external developments to avoid unintended consequences associated with prolonged monetary accommodation. The BSP implemented extraordinary measures at the onset and height of the pandemic to squarely address the unprecedented crisis; now that the economy is recovering, we are implementing our exit strategy, gradually and in a carefully calibrated manner. The BSP will continue to take into consideration the need to support economic recovery, while also guarding against any emerging risks to its price and financial stability objectives. As I close, let me leave you with these key take-aways: • First, the Philippine economic fundamentals remain solid, and this momentum will carry us through full recovery. • Second, BSP will keep its policy settings and regulatory relief measures supportive of the economy, subject to our data-driven approach to policymaking. • Lastly, the BSP remains supportive of measures that will propel the economy toward full recovery. Thank you for your attention. ​

Title: Forging Ahead Toward a More Technologically Advanced and Inclusive Economy
Occasion: JP Morgan Investor Seminar
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ To the JP Morgan officers and staff, special guests, ladies and gentlemen, good afternoon. Thank you for this opportunity to provide updates on the Philippines’ recent economic performance, and the BSP’s initiatives to ensure a strong and sustainable recovery in the new economy. The Philippine economy has demonstrated its resilience and fundamental strength. After the COVID-19 crisis dragged us into recession in 2020, we are now in much better shape. The economy rebounded strongly, growing at 12.1-percent in the second quarter of 2021 and has continued to grow since then. For the full year of 2021, the economy grew by 5.7 percent, above the government’s target range of 5.0 to 5.5 percent. The country’s labor market conditions also improved, with the unemployment rate declining to 6.4 percent in February this year from 8.8 percent in the same period last year and from a peak of 17.6 percent in April 2020. Developments in the manufacturing industry are also encouraging, as the purchasing managers’ index hit a three-year high of 53.2 in March 2022. Moving on to monetary and external developments. For the first quarter of the year, inflation averaged 3.4 percent. Inflationary pressures are mostly due to surging energy costs, but this was offset by the continued slowdown in the price increases of food. We are closely monitoring the ongoing conflict between Russia and Ukraine and its potential impact on the domestic economy. While its direct impact is manageable, its indirect impact shown in higher oil world prices and slower global growth could be more serious. The BSP supports the national government’s fiscal interventions to address inflation risks and maintain the economic recovery momentum. Meanwhile, the country’s external accounts continue to provide adequate buffers against external headwinds. The latest Gross International Reserves (GIR) of USD108.5 billion as of end-March 2022 represent a hefty buffer equivalent to 9.6 months’ worth of imports of goods, payments of services, and primary income. Our low external debt-to-GDP ratio of 27 percent as of end-2021 also acts as a cushion against external shocks. The increase in overseas Filipino cash remittances by 5.1 percent and the steady growth of business process outsourcing services, increasing by 9.5 percent in 2021, strongly add to the country’s external foothold. Foreign direct investment (FDI) net inflows sustained their growth momentum in 2021, with a significant increase of 54.2 percent year-on-year, equivalent to 10.5 billion US Dollars. Let me move on to the banking sector. The Philippine banking system remained sound and stable throughout the pandemic, with liquidity and credit conditions supportive of growth and well above regulatory requirements. Bank lending has been growing since August 2021. Latest data of 8.8 percent year-on-year growth in February indicate that credit activity continued to improve amid a more favorable economic outlook from businesses and households. As of end-February 2022, the gross non-performing loans (NPL) ratio of the Philippine Banking System remained manageable at 4.2 percent. As of the third quarter of 2021, the capital adequacy ratio of universal and commercial banks reached 17.4 percent on a consolidated basis, well above the minimum thresholds of 10.0 percent set by the BSP and 8.0 percent by the Bank for International Settlements. All these indicators show that the Philippines is well into the recovery process, aided by COVID-response measures from the fiscal and the monetary side. Central to the country’s recovery is its whole-of-government approach. On the part of the BSP, this was implemented through its wide range of monetary and regulatory measures, which complemented the massive relief efforts of the national government. First, the BSP shored up market confidence and ensured adequate liquidity and credit. Second, the BSP complemented government programs through extraordinary liquidity measures. Third, the BSP implemented regulatory and operational relief measures. All these contributed to getting us back on track to economic recovery. First, we cut our policy rate by a cumulative 200 basis points resulting in a record low of two (2) percent reduced RRP rate, which helped stimulate provision of credit to productive activities. We also reduced our reserve requirement against deposit and deposit substitute liabilities. For Universal/ Commercial Banks (UKBs) and Non-Bank Financial Institutions with Quasi-Banking Functions (NBQBs), effective 3 April 2020, we reduced it from 14 percent to 12 percent. For Thrift Banks (TBs) and Rural/ Cooperative Banks (RCBs), effective 31 July 2020, we reduced it from 4 percent to 3 percent. Second were extraordinary liquidity measures, such as provisional advances and remittance of dividends to the National Government to help finance its COVID-19 response, and purchases of government securities in the secondary market. With our liquidity-easing measures, the BSP has infused over PHP2.2 Trillion (or approximately USD43 billion) into the financial system, equivalent to 11.1 percent of the country’s gross domestic product. Third were regulatory and operational relief measures to maintain stability of the financial system and ensure public access to financial services. Moving on to the economic outlook… After the solid rebound last year, the government projects that the Philippine economy will grow by 7.0 to 9.0 percent this year, and by 6.0 to 7.0 percent next year. The latest baseline forecasts indicate that inflation will settle a little above the target range at about 4.3 percent for this year, then within target range at 3.6 percent for next year. Overseas Filipino remittances growth is expected to stabilize at 4.0 percent this year, aided by further reopening of economies. Net foreign direct investments are projected to rise to 11 billion US Dollars this year amid expectations of improved domestic economic activity and implementation of investment friendly reforms. Meanwhile, the current account is expected to post a deficit equivalent to 3.8 percent of GDP in 2022, driven by rising demand for imported capital goods and inputs for production as the economy continues to recover. Along with these indicators, the country has kept its investment grade credit ratings throughout the pandemic, a testament to the Philippine economy’s medium- and long-term growth prospects. We expect continued improvement in economic indicators as economic recovery sustains its momentum. Finally, allow me to discuss the BSP’s plans moving forward. Consistent with the BSP’s data-dependent approach to policymaking, the timing and conditions under which the BSP will unwind its pandemic interventions will continue to be guided by the inflation and growth outlook over the medium term, and relevant risks to the economy. We are also making headway in financial digitalization. Under the BSP’s Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap, we aim to achieve two major objectives by 2023: First, at least half of financial transactions in the country are done digitally; and second, at least 70 percent of Filipino adults have transaction accounts. We are also committed to advancing sustainable finance. Sustainability is central to achieving the BSP’s core mandates of maintaining price and financial stability, given the potential impact of climate disasters on supply of goods and services, as well as on incomes. While the Philippines’ macroeconomic fundamentals remain sound, and our medium- and long-term growth prospects look bright, we recognize that achieving full recovery requires a whole-of-government approach. Nonetheless, the BSP remains cognizant of the need to strike a balance between providing adequate stimulus to sustain economic recovery and managing the risks to price and financial stability. Thank you for your attention.​

Title: Digital Currencies: Risks and Opportunities for Financial Inclusion
Occasion: 14th Annual Group of Twenty-Four (G-24) / Alliance for Financial Inclusion Policymakers' Roundtable at the 2022 IMF-World Bank Springs Meetings
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: Good afternoon. Let me first thank and commend our organizers for setting a timely and highly interesting topic, at least among central banks, for this meeting, i.e., the risks and opportunities of digital currencies for financial inclusion. This subject touches on several major strategic objectives of the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP). Digitalization of the payment system is one of the major goals of the BSP. Under its Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap, the BSP targets to digitalize half of total retail payments by 2023. Faster processing of payments speeds up economic activities as well as capital turnaround, and thus, income growth. Inclusive finance is another major goal of the BSP. In the Philippines, the BSP leads the implementation of initiatives under an interagency National Strategy for Financial Inclusion (NSFI) which aims for a coherent nationwide approach in accelerating financial inclusion in the country. The potential of digital finance and capabilities of digital technologies in achieving financial inclusion are deeply embedded in the NSFI. Ultimately, we aspire to transform the Philippines into a more technologically advanced, more inclusive, and more sustainable economy. Alongside these, the BSP takes a proactive stance in learning digital technologies that drive the emergence of alternative payment instruments. As more than 80 central banks worldwide are exploring central bank digital currencies (CBDCs), the BSP has also taken major steps in understanding CBDCs and knowing the benefits, risks, as well as policy implications for issuing a Philippine CBDC. Allow me to briefly share what the BSP has done so far as regards to this initiative. The BSP initially conducted an exploratory study in 2020 to determine and evaluate the issues that surround CBDC issuance based on the literature and learnings of other central banks. This study covered the prospective implications for the pillars of central banking in the Philippines, the legal aspects, as well as the potential of CBDCs in furthering financial inclusion. From this comprehensive study, we understood that a potential motivating factor for the BSP to explore CBDC issuance is to enhance the safety and efficiency of the national payment system. In 2021, we proceeded with identifying the use cases for CBDC issuance in the Philippines by comparing the functional features of the country’s existing retail and large-value payment systems with the use of CBDCs. Our goal was to identify the areas where a CBDC can yield the greatest value-adding benefit to the Philippine payment system. Based on this review, the BSP determined on the one hand, that there is minimal value added of the use of retail CBDC in the Philippines in the short term given the progress in our widespread implementation of retail payment digitalization and financial inclusion reforms. On the other hand, the BSP identified the value of a wholesale CBDC in addressing pain points in the national payment system in the following areas: First are the frictions on large cross-border foreign currency transfers. We believe that we need to address this issue by reducing transaction costs, shortening processing times, and enhancing the transparency of such transfers. Second is the settlement risk exposure arising from the use of commercial bank money in our equities market. We intend to mitigate this risk with CBDC, being a central bank money. Last is the challenge of operating an Intraday Liquidity Facility (ILF) that we have yet to automate end-to-end. The CBDC may play a critical role in facilitating the settlement of ILF availments and the subsequent collection of the draw-down. Thereafter, in light of the recommended use cases, we decided to pursue the implementation of a pilot wholesale CBDC under the initiative dubbed as Project CBDCPh. The main objectives of this Project are, as follows: First, we want to build the BSP’s capacity so we can have hands-on knowledge on the functionality, architecture, technology, operational, and organizational requirements of a CBDC; and Also, we wish to leverage on the learnings from the pilot for pursuing further CBDC projects. The pilot project covers the experimentation of the CBDC’s use to transfer large-value financial transactions on a 24x7 basis, across a limited number of financial institutions but possibly covering both banks and non-bank institutions. The Project CBDCPh is led by an intersectoral project management team in the BSP to ensure that we are able to cover the major critical areas in operating CBDC. These areas include policy and regulatory considerations, technological infrastructure, governance and organizational requirements, legal matters, payment and settlement models, reconciliation procedures, and risk management. Equally important, the CBDCPh Project Team also includes external advisers from international standard-setting bodies and multilateral institutions to build on trainings and knowledge sharing on CBDC development and implementation all over the world. The pilot is a major step for both the BSP and the Philippine financial industry towards understanding the potentials and risks of a wholesale CBDC. The learnings from the pilot are critical in constructing the BSP’s medium to long-term roadmap for more advanced wholesale CBDC projects that shall further strengthen the Philippine payment system. Ladies and gentlemen, emerging digital technologies do not alter the mandates of central banks. Instead, emerging technologies facilitate the means for central banks to achieve their mandate of providing safe, efficient, and inclusive payment systems. At the core of these developments are capacity building activities and/or knowledge sharing sessions, such as this major event, that help ensure that central banks are in pace and fully understand emerging digital technologies. For this reason, I thank again our organizers and participants for this important event. Good afternoon again to all.​

Title: Message for the MOA Signing on the Establishment of Shariah Supervisory Board in BARMM
Occasion: MOA Signing on the Establishment of Shariah Supervisory Board in BARMM
Speaker: MOA Signing on the Establishment of Shariah Supervisory Board in BARMM
Content: ​ DOF Undersecretary Bayani H. Agabin, BARMM Minister Mohammad S. Yacob; NCMF Commissioner Yusoph J. Mando; colleagues in government service, our friends from the Press Corps who are joining us online, guests, ladies and gentlemen, good morning and Assalamu Alaikom. First of all, let me extend my congratulations to all the stakeholders present today, especially the Founding Agencies of the Shari’ah Supervisory Board (SSB). Despite the disruptive effects of the pandemic, here we are— celebrating the fruits of our perseverance as we achieve another great milestone for Islamic banking and finance in the country. Today, the Philippines will be among a handful of jurisdictions that have institutionalized a Shari’ah governance oversight body for Islamic banking and finance through the creation of a Shari’ah Supervisory Board or the SSB. Actually, the SSB in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao (BARMM) was formally established last month when the Founding Agencies signed the Joint Circular on its creation. Upon its signing we published the Joint Circular to convey to the domestic and global markets that the Philippines is continuously building the necessary infrastructure to accommodate an expanded Islamic banking and finance ecosystem. The MOA which we will be signing today supplements the Joint Circular. The MOA prescribes the agreement of the government agencies with respect to the roles and responsibilities of the SSB and the members thereof and the mandate of the SSB, among others. Nevertheless, the said MOA is meant to be dynamic and evolving as the Islamic banking and finance industry progresses. Today’s MOA signing is also a testament of our gains under a whole-of-government approach. The relentless collaboration and continuing cooperation of the BSP, Department of Finance, Bangsamoro Government, and National Commission on Muslim Filipinos led us to fulfill our joint mandate enshrined under the Bangsamoro Organic Law to promote Islamic banking and finance. However, the collaboration does not end with the signing of the Joint Circular and the MOA. The Government agencies and private sector must continue to collaborate until our vision of an Islamic banking and finance ecosystem that viably operates alongside the conventional system, is realized. That said, the SSB will serve as a strong arm in reinforcing BSP’s commitment on financial stability grounded on sound corporate governance in the delivery of financial products and services in the country. By issuing Shari’ah opinions on Islamic banking products and services, the SSB will play a key role in driving the business model of Islamic financial institutions and the sustainability of the Islamic banking products or services that will be made available to all Filipinos. The creation of the SSB can also boost trading activities, participatory financing schemes and entrepreneurial undertakings with financial institutions that are committed to provide end-to-end Shari’ah compliant products and services. Clearly, the SSB can shape the Shari’ah governance oversight on Islamic banking and finance and complement existing conventional governance frameworks. By now, we may already have visualized the potential and opportunities, as well as the challenges, that this endeavor brings. While the tasks ahead are new and numerous, these are birth pains which I am optimistic of overcoming, given the strong commitments of the Founding Agencies. Rest assured that the Joint Circular and MOA have provided effective and efficient mechanisms to address prudential concerns and ensure a dynamic framework that can work for the SSB and its stakeholders. In the last two years since the enactment of RA 11439 or the Islamic Banking Law in 2019, the BSP has issued the key implementing regulations on licensing, Shari’ah governance framework, liquidity risk management and prudential reporting requirements for Islamic banks and Islamic banking units. On the other hand, the DOF and the Bureau of Internal Revenue have issued the implementing regulations on the tax neutrality provision of the law which in essence provide that Islamic banking transactions should not be taxed more heavily nor more lightly that their conventional counterparts. Recently, the Insurance Commission has issued its baseline guidelines on Takaful or Islamic Insurance undertakings. Today, the establishment of the SSB is the anchor that can hold all of these regulations together. Thus, as we sail on to our next milestone, let us not waver in our support to the SSB and in promoting Islamic banking and finance, in general. As Founding Agencies, let us remain committed to the spirit and letter of the Memorandum of Agreement that we are about to sign. Thank you and Eid Mubarak!​

Title: Message for the Signing of Purchase Agreement Between the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas and OceanaGold (Philippines), Inc. (OGPI or OceanaGold)
Occasion: Signing of Purchase Agreement Between the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas and OceanaGold (Philippines), Inc. (OGPI or OceanaGold)
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ Today, we mark a momentous occasion in Philippine central banking and its pursuit of a sustainable and inclusive economic growth with the signing of the Purchase Agreement between the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) and OceanaGold Philippines, Inc. or OGPI This agreement contributes to BSP’s enhanced gold buying program that is underpinned by the principles of responsible gold sourcing and sound reserve management. The program aims to promote livelihood, productivity and sustainable gold production by expanding BSP’s gold sourcing beyond small-scale mining or SSM to include large scale mining or LSM. The BSP aims to be the preferred buyer by both the SSMs and the LSMs. Purchases of gold from LSMs are bolstered by policies adopted by the BSP, on one hand, and the national government, on the other, namely: 1. Purchase of responsibly sourced gold is based on prevailing gold buying price set by the BSP; 2. Sale of gold to the BSP by LSMs registered with the Board of Investments (BOI) are considered by the BOI as constructive export; and 3. Sale of gold to the BSP is VAT-exempt under Republic Act. No. 10963 or the TRAIN Law and as confirmed by the BIR notwithstanding the change of classification from VAT zero-rated to VAT exempt. The transactions under this Purchase Agreement and future agreements with other LSMs are guided by the same policies. For this reason, we laud the efforts of the Philippine Government and OGPI for the execution of an agreement through Financial or Technical Assistance Agreement (FTAA) No. 001. This requires OGPI to offer for purchase to the BSP, not less than twenty five percent (25%) of its annual doré production of the Didipio Gold and Copper Mine at fair market price and pursuant to the terms and conditions as may be agreed upon by OGPI and BSP. This effort supports the BSP’s strategic direction of further strengthening the country’s management of its gross international reserves which support economic activities and effectively cushion against adverse impacts to the economy. We appreciate OGPI’s enthusiasm and cooperation in this Agreement. Maraming salamat at mabuhay!​

Title: Finance Forecast 2022: Opportunities and Recovery
Occasion: Ignite: The Strength of Rising Together Eastern Communications e-Huddle Series
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ To all the thought leaders, fellow speakers, and everyone here present, good day! Thank you for inviting me to the second year of the E-Huddle webinar series. This provides an effective platform for the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas to convey our insights and ongoing initiatives. Today, I will discuss— First, the macroeconomic landscape and highlights of the performance of our banking system. Second, the key regulatory reforms to ensure smooth transition post-pandemic, particularly BSP initiatives in promoting sustainable finance and digitalization as essential elements in the recovery and operational resilience of BSP-supervised financial institutions. Third, the BSP legislative agenda. Finally, I will cap my presentation with some key takeaways. Now, let me start by saying that our economy is fundamentally strong and resilient. Despite the recession in 2020 due to the pandemic, we managed to bounce back stronger. The economy posted a 5.7-percent full-year growth last year, above the government’s target range of 5.0 to 5.5 percent. Inflation remains manageable, settling at 3.4 percent in the first quarter of this year, well within the government’s target band of 2.0 to 4.0 percent. Our gross international reserves, or GIR, which serve as buffer against external headwinds, remain hefty at 108.5 billion US dollars as of end-March this year. The GIR is considered adequate if it can cover at least three months’ worth of imports. Ours provides 9.5 months of import cover. The job situation has recovered. The number of employed Filipinos now exceeds pre-pandemic level. The unemployment rate dropped to 6.4 percent in February this year from a peak of 17.6 percent in April 2020. Moving on to the banking sector…. Banks remain the pillar of strength of our economy. As of February this year, the total resources of the banking system expanded by 7.0 percent year-on-year to 20.7 trillion pesos. Credit activity registered an increasing trend for seven straight months, up by 5.4 percent year-on-year. We expect improving economic condition and market sentiment to further spur domestic credit expansion. Non-performing loans remain manageable with NPL ratio at 4.2 percent. The NPL coverage ratio, which shows the banks' allowance for potential losses from bad loans, is high at 86.1 percent. Meanwhile, the capital adequacy ratio of universal and commercial banks reached 17.4 percent on a consolidated basis. This is well above the minimum thresholds of 10.0 percent set by the BSP and 8.0 percent by the Bank for International Settlements. Finally, the net profit of the banking system is up by a remarkable 44.8 percent year-on-year as of December 2021. Against this backdrop, our country has kept its investment-grade ratings all throughout the pandemic. This is despite a wave of downgrades globally, which validates the resilience demonstrated by our economy amid the pandemic. When the pandemic struck, the BSP stepped in to provide additional emergency support to the government’s broad-based health and fiscal programs. On top of this, we implemented a wide range of regulatory relief measures aimed at: First, extending financial relief; Second, boosting bank lending; and Third, promoting continued access to financial delivery and services. The BSP extended some of these measures until yearend to sustain the credit growth momentum and ensure continued access to financial services. At the height of the pandemic, we strengthened our earlier digitalization efforts through the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap. We have twin targets by 2023, which I consider personal goals. First, that at least half of financial transactions in the country are done digitally… …and second, that at least 70 percent of Filipino adults have transaction accounts. I am optimistic about hitting these two objectives as the economy shifts from a cash-heavy to a cash-lite economy. Now, let me cite some of our initiatives. Together with the Philippine Payments Management Inc. or PPMI, we launched the QR Ph Person-to-Merchant facility in October 2021. We are also collaborating with PPMI to broaden the use cases for three digital payment streams this year. First is the interoperable Bills Pay Facility, which will address the existing fragmented bills payment mechanism. Second is the Request to Pay Facility, which will empower payees to initiate collections of non-recurring receivables. Third is the Direct Debit, which will allow customers to better manage recurring payments. We have also issued the open finance and digital banking frameworks to accelerate digital transformation and financial inclusion. The Open Finance Framework promotes consent-driven data portability, interoperability, and collaborative partnerships among entities that adhere to the same standards of data security and privacy. In line with our digital transformation roadmap, the BSP has issued licenses to six purely digital banks. The BSP has also commenced initiatives in the digitalization of offshore payments. We are prioritizing the establishment of interoperable cross-border real-time retail payment systems among ASEAN member states. Moving on to sustainable finance... For us at the BSP, sustainability will serve as our guidepost in rebuilding the post-COVID 19 economy. It is crucial that sustainability principles are part of how institutions are governed. As such, the BSP is actively collaborating with government agencies and regulators through the Interagency Technical Working Group on Sustainable Finance or what we call the “Green Force” to form a cohesive action plan to institutionalize and accelerate the growth of sustainable finance. Data shows that 24 percent or 119 out of 499 banks have adopted their respective sustainable transition plans. Banks have issued around 1.15 billion US dollars and 152.9 billion pesos worth of green, social and sustainability bonds since 2017. In leading by example, the BSP has invested 550 million US dollars in the Green Bond Funds of the Bank for International Settlements to promote investments in sustainable or green finance assets. We also acknowledge the critical legislation complementing our regulatory reforms and initiatives. These bills include Financial Consumer Protection Act, which will afford the financial sector regulators the authority to enforce prudent and customer-centric standards of conduct, including the power to adjudicate customer complaints. The FCPA is in the Office of the President for signature. The Agri Agra Bill is seen to enhance access by rural communities to private sector financing. This bill is pending for bicameral conference. Meanwhile, the Digital Payments Bill seeks to promote digital payment systems, particularly in all government agencies. This is currently approved in the House of Representatives on third reading and pending in the Senate committee on banks. The Government Financial Institutions Unified Initiatives to Distressed Enterprises for Economic Recovery Act or the GUIDE Bill seeks to strengthen the capacity of the Landbank and Development Bank of the Philippines to provide access to credit to distressed enterprises as a result of the pandemic. This bill is approved in the House of Representatives on third reading and pending in the Senate committee on banks. There is also the Financial Accounts Regulation Bill, which aims to regulate the use of bank accounts and e-wallets in online transactions to address proliferation of cybercrimes. This is also pending in the Senate committee on banks. Finally, let me highlight key points of my presentation. The country is on the road to full economic recovery; Banks continue to be in a position of strength to support economic recovery; The pandemic has helped accelerate the sustainability agenda and digitalization of the financial system; And, amid all the challenges and uncertainties, the BSP remains committed to provide a conducive environment for digital innovation and sustainability. ​​

Title: Accelerating the Country’s Post-Pandemic Recovery
Occasion: Money Market Association of the Philippines (MART) First General Membership Meeting
Speaker: BSP Governor Benjamin E. Diokno
Content: ​ To the Money Market Association of the Philippines, guests. ladies and gentlemen, a good day to all. It is a pleasure to speak to you again as you set the agenda for the year ahead. The last time I spoke with MART was when I inducted a new set of officers for 2022. During that time, I asked you to keep in mind that the actions you take today will inevitably shape the future yet to come. In the few months that have passed, much has changed. Fortunately, the economy is in much better shape than it was since we last spoke. I remain optimistic that full recovery is well underway. Please allow me to share the reasons behind my optimism. After a pandemic-driven recession in 2020, the economy staged a strong turnaround and grew 5.7 percent in 2021. This year, the economy is expected to grow 7-9 percent and 6-7 percent in 2023 on the back of easing mobility restrictions and structural reforms. Several indicators – from unemployment levels to manufacturing activity to business and consumer sentiment – suggest broad improvements.
