When the economy was recovering, we were very aggressive in making sure that the pandemic did not [cause the economy to] contract more than [what could have been] generated by the lockdowns. We brought down the policy rate to 2 percent. I’ve been with Monetary Board for 11 years; 3.5 percent is, more or less, the normal policy rate. That’s not surprising since the midpoint of our inflation target is 3 percent, so it’s only logical that we have a positive, real policy rate. Of course, the pandemic changed everything – we went to a 2 percent policy rate, lent P600 billion to the national government interest-free, and then, we saw markets panicking and fleeing to cash because of the uncertainty. In other words, markets were going in the “wrong” direction. The pandemic is expected to reduce capex and make people want to save more. The “correct” reaction of the market should have been to lower interest rates, but they were fleeing to cash because of the sense of uncertainty, To ease further upward pressure on interest rates, we purchased a trillion pesos of bonds from the secondary market. In relation to our balance sheet of P4.5 trillion, that’s quite big. All along, we had an exit plan. It was [supposed] to be a gradual liftoff of rates – probably starting the last three months of the year. [But] we were surprised by developments in the US. We were surprised by the movements in commodity prices due to the Ukraine war. These were not part of our forecast. Thus, our exit came sooner and bigger [than expected]. We started with two 25 bps, an off-cycle 75 bps, and then another 50 bps. If you compare us with Thailand and Indonesia, we are among the central banks in Southeast Asia that was the most aggressive. Why? If you look at it relative to the original exit plan, we would have done at least 100 bps anyway, but [we] could have done it in the last half of the year. In reality, the policy rate is still quite supportive of growth. Indeed, this is happening when there are no lockdowns. When you look at Google Mobility indices, you can see [the improvement]. In the BSP, our COVID numbers, we noticed, and this is exactly the same [trend] as the rest of the nation: low hospitalization. Our source of confidence that growth will take place is, first, that people have learned to live with the virus, and second, vaccinations and previous infections make them more resilient. The fact that the virus affects the upper respiratory tract rather than the lower makes it more transmissible but less deadly. In that context, we are quite optimistic that [economic] growth is taking place. Why the surprise [slower-than-expected growth] in Q2 2022? I was personally expecting 8 percent; what happened was much lower. If you look at growth from a seasonally adjusted quarter-on-quarter basis, we’re on a slight reduction. The answer has nothing to do with the virus; it has to do with the fact that when people are spending lots more of their money on transportation and electricity, they have less money for other things. That is what the data shows. When you take out essentials from consumption, what remains is non-essential. The growth of non-essentials already expanded to pre-pandemic levels and is now low again compared to pre-pandemic levels. The slower growth is not due to monetary policy; it’s due to the fact that between driving less, using less electricity, being less likely to go to restaurants [as people have less to spend after spending for non-discretionary items], the economy would slow down. The slow economy is due to the high cost of imports and, unluckily, the high cost of some local agricultural products, [which] has reduced money for discretionary spending. This, together with the policy rate increases, give us an even more chance of seeing below 4 percent inflation next year. Our policy rates are not in response to the current inflation per se; they are forward-looking because we take very seriously our inflation-targeting mandate. If inflation is high next year, this would not be due to external supply shocks anymore but due to second-order effects. We notice, for instance, that wages are rising and transport fare petitions. There’s very little we can do about this. But absent other shocks, we expect inflation to be below 4 percent next year and closer to 3 percent than to 4 thereafter. My point is we did not take away growth [with our tightening]. What we did was create bigger growth opportunities next year and the next. The other way to look at it is: we might have lost a little bit of output this year, but if we had postponed our actions, we would have lost more in the following years because the pressure to raise rates would have been even greater if we had not acted on inflation this year. In Tagalog, mas mabuti ang maaga kaysa magaling. Don’t see our moves as being anti-growth. We’re actually laying the groundwork for better growth in the coming years. Another point: our banks are in a good position to absorb losses: there’s enough provisioning, and capitalization is quite high – much higher than what’s required. Not all non-financial corporates are healthy, but that’s the very nature of capitalism. In any distribution of firms in any capitalist economy, some will be doing much better or worse than average, but on average, [the banks are] quite strong. In my experience with the Monetary Board, never were we told by the government that our policy rate was too high. Never were we told by the government to be gentle to a bank. The independence of the central bank is well understood by authorities, most of all by the current Secretary of Finance. Thank you

Title: Taking steps to improve coin recirculation in the Philippines
Occasion: MOA signing with Participating Retailers for the Coin Deposit Machine Project
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ SM Retail Inc. Controllership and Financial Planning Senior Vice President Jonathan H. Ng, Filinvest Land Inc. Deputy Chief Finance Officer Janeth De los Reyes, and Robinsons Retail Holdings Inc. – Robinsons Supermarket Managing Director Stanley Co.; fellow BSPers, partners, ladies and gentlemen, good afternoon. The phrase “barya lang po sa umaga” – suggests there’s really a shortage of coins. I remember when I was growing up, as a 15-year-old first-year student in a school nearby, I would take the bus from Lipa to La Salle Taft, where my boarding house was nearby. I would wonder about the skill of the conductor. During those times, the buses did not have aisles; they had continuous seats all the way to the side. In other words, one side of the bus is completely closed, and the other side is completely open. The conductor has no change. What he does is he swings from outside the bus, literally like an acrobat, moving from one side to the other, asking everybody where they’re going. After having asked that, he punches all the tickets. Alam niya kung ilan ang Lipa, ilan ang Batangas City, ilan ang Malvar. Fantastic memory. And then he will collect all the payments. A lot of people overpay because they don’t have exact change. In the end, he will now get all the coins, and somehow, he’s able to remember who to give the money back to. So, in other words, to solve the coin problem, you need a very smart bus conductor. We seem to have the same problem now: the problem of recirculating coins. When I look at this project, everyone is benefitting: us [at the BSP], we’re going to save a lot of money on the cost of minting. Let me give a small lecture on seigniorage: it’s nicer to print P1000 [banknote] because the P6 gives you P1000. If the P1000 earns a little bit of interest, you recover the P6. That’s called positive seigniorage. On the other hand, the coins have a small face value, yet the cost of production is so high. So you’re helping us solve this problem. But you’re also helping the public because there’s a sense of fairness. Ayaw ng mga tao ng sukli ay kendi. This is solving problems for everyone. The only question I want to ask myself is, with something as good as this, why did it just come only now? The question is why and the reason is somebody has to think of it. And thank God, enough people thought about it, and now people came together and talked about it, and here we have it! We in the BSP think that [with] our investment in the machines plus the creativity of our partners, everyone will benefit. In other words, partial ideas put together can become full-blown ideas that work. I’m very, very optimistic that after a two-year pilot run, we will be able to deploy these machines, and hopefully, I’m quite confident that the benefits will be realized. The lesson from this is: what other things here in BSP could follow this model? And I’m thinking, what about the fact that we are so “dependent” on the P1000 bill because the ATM is the best way to distribute currency? Banks don’t like to put P100 bills in ATMs because it’s very expensive, right? Such that the P100 bill tends to just keep circulating [in the informal economy] even if it’s no longer fit. So, another version of this [model]: maybe we will have fitter notes that would use the very principle we used here [in the coin deposit project] and find the very same partners we have here. When we think this way and work together this way, we, together, can do a lot of things. Thank you very much, and good afternoon.

Title: Keynote Address for 2nd BSP International Research Fair
Occasion: 2nd BSP International Research Fair
Speaker: Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: Professor Athanasios Orphanides – who we all know was the former Governor of the Central Bank of Cyprus – Dr. Ozer Karagedikli of the Asia School of Business, members of the Monetary Board, esteemed speakers and discussants, my colleagues at the BSP, ladies, and gentlemen, good morning. While many things that are now changing outside the Philippines are, to say the least, very challenging, especially to an inflation-targeting central bank like the BSP, I always like to remind myself that its core policymaking remains guided by forecasts of GDP growth and inflation rates, how our policies can affect them, and the lag between the policies and their effects. We, of course, know that our forecasts can be wrong. To make myself feel better, let me recall two years when our forecasts were way off in the pleasant direction. In 2015 and 2016, we forecasted that inflation will be very close to the mid-point of our 2 to 4 percent target. But the actual inflation was, in fact, 0.7 percent and 1.3 percent, while GDP growth was 6.3 and 7.1 percent, respectively. And GDE, the sum of consumption, investment, or expenditures, was growing even faster. We told the public that we were wrong because import prices, which were inputs into our models, that is, to economists exogenous to our models, turned out to be much lower than expected. Of course, this is now happening in the opposite direction. The [deviation] in the inputs and our forecasts are way, way up. And they were reassured when we told them that when we did runs that used the actual import prices, our forecasts of inflation turned out to be much closer to what actually happened. The entire time that prices were below the target, people readily accepted our mantra: that monetary policies need not respond to transitory supply shocks. And they actually accepted that two years was transitory; that’s how kind they were to us. And never did we hear that we were behind the curve and that, from hindsight, they never said that monetary policy was too tight. Fast forward to the present, and the situation is, of course, much more difficult. People don’t want to hear that our tools are not very good at addressing supply shocks. They don’t care who; they want somebody to solve the problem. They, or at least the noisier part of the gallery, also want us to raise interest rates much more in response to what was initially unanticipated by US monetary policy hawkishness. In short, they don’t want us to be like the textbook version of an inflation-targeting central bank. So we need a lot of educating to do in making the markets accept volatility and live with it. We need to do more and more of that gradually over time. I asked some people, “What is the optimal volatility?” They always tell me, “More than what you had yesterday.” The asymmetry of responses to good and bad supply shocks is, of course, not just a political phenomenon. Clearly, in a country that has rarely experienced low inflation, the likelihood of a downward wage-price and exchange rate spiral is perceived to be close to zero. On the other hand, the same spiral can be very powerful going upwards. Indeed, it was already in the textbooks for many decades now. In modern jargon, we call it the disanchoring of inflationary expectations. Influencing inflation expectations is, therefore, half the battle, especially during episodes of high inflation when central banks cannot afford to look unconcerned or inattentive. Of course, we should be transparent to explain why when we are wrong. When caught early enough, the appropriate policies could rein inflation expectations towards the inflation target. By the way, our studies show that private sector forecasts follow our forecasts with a lag – a very, very short lag. We do not want to lose that. But we must admit that inflation expectations depend not just on monetary policy and even fiscal policy but on what happens outside the Philippines. To repeat, safeguarding price stability is not just an essential condition for preserving macroeconomic stability; it can also contribute to higher economic growth. Of course, it is a pure coincidence that the inflation-targeting period of the BSP is also the highest growth rate [of the country]. For instance, the longer end of the Peso bond market is now much larger because of our IT credibility. But to properly fulfill their mandates, it’s important that central banks reach their intended audience. Effective communication is key to credibly assure the markets that fundamentals are intact and that appropriate policy actions will be taken when needed in a pre-emptive fashion. In this sense, it has been pointed out that “central bank communication can be used to manage expectations both by “creating news” and “reducing noise.” Now, let me briefly touch on the final point. While global challenges,, like climate change and food security, are primarily within the remit of fiscal authorities, they affect our forecasts of the trajectories of variables of interest and their long-run values. This, of course, has implications for our policy decisions. Thus, central banks might do well to be supportive of other things that are important in operations — like improving capacity in big data, and of course, developing our people, and providing avenues for regulated entities to participate in green funds. In closing, while I believe central banks should attend to immediate concerns, we must also keep one eye on and devote resources to ensuring that we build our credibility, our skills, and our technological capacities to be equal to the task at measure. I wish you a pleasant time at this conference. Be safe and Mabuhay! ###

Title: Speech for BSP Young Professionals Program (YPP) Batch 1 Graduation
Occasion: BSP Young Professionals Program (YPP) Batch 1 Graduation
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Magandang hapon sa inyong lahat. This brings me back to my UP days when I used to enjoy graduation ceremonies. BSP colleagues, guests, ladies, and gentlemen, of course, most importantly, our dear graduates. Let me start by congratulating this pioneering batch of the BSP YPP – or Young Professionals Program – for successfully completing the program. Today, we celebrate and honor you for what you have accomplished – completing what I see is a really hard, rigorous 18 months that most likely forced all of you to immerse yourselves in different areas of central banking and see it from the point of view of various departments in the bank. You were able to successfully complete two projects, I was told, with your sponsoring departments, which I am sure will contribute to our processes, systems, and knowledge base here in the BSP. So the next question is: what now or now what? Dear graduates, it’s time for you to build your own career not just as central bankers but as public servants in the truest sense of the word. You’ll be lucky to be in an institution that, in our view, serves the people because of the importance of its three pillars. As you help BSP achieve them and improve the performance of the economy through its three pillars, you have also served the country. Of course, there are other ways of serving the country, like being a good brother to your younger siblings or, just like me, being a good husband and father to my children. But there’s nothing like working for an institution whose mandates are so clearly defined and whose tools are largely in its own hands. Of course, there are things beyond our control, like any other institution that has to deal with a very complex environment, but it’s very clear what the metrics are. If you’re a BSPer, you will have a methodology for removing what we call exogenous variables. Which of the nice things that happen are due to us? Which of the nice things that happen is pure luck? But it could also go the other way: which of the bad things that happen are just pure bad luck? And which of the bad things are things that suggest that we should get better? I’m certain that there’s more to come — more opportunities to learn and grow, to contribute, and to add value. By the way, do not use NBA metrics. In NBA, you know exactly what the performance of a team is when you’re in or out of the game. They call this the plus-minus statistics, ‘we without you.’ Some players are plus 10; some players are minus 30, meaning every time they’re in, the game is losing badly. Even in that particular case, it need not be your fault, meaning you’re a young player, and you’re used only when the team is losing. Even in that particular case, where pluses and minuses are so easy to compute, how much it is when you’re in or out of the game, nothing can really be measured for certain. That’s why values, beliefs, vision, and dreams are important because pluses and minuses cannot be your true being. What is it then that [you need] in this journey where measuring your output is so hard? This entire field of economics [is] where you can measure the output of the thing but never measure the output of the members of the team. Sometimes, it could be frustrating; sometimes, it could be exciting because your team did very well, and you knew you contributed. What are these things that you need? Of course, you must have self-discipline. We often confuse this with zest and enthusiasm. Why? Much of the work is not like reading a book, then it turns out to be boring, so you put it down. Or you might join a weight-loss program and then give up, saying, ‘I’ll make up later,’ or enrolling in a new course and backing off midway when your interest wanes. In other words, don’t be ningas-kugon. True commitment demands from us the self-discipline to finish what we started and, most of all, to complete it without sacrificing quality. Now, the second quality is perseverance, but I don’t have to explain this too much. The fact that you’re here. You finished the program. You have this. The third is loyalty. Loyalty does not mean big, heroic actions because sometimes that may be the requirement. We’ve seen that happen to our employees when despite great disasters, they’re able to [keep things running and] prevent the branch from elements disabling [it]. You don’t need to be heroic all of the time, sometimes it calls for you to just show up when expected and do the needful: be punctual in your appointments, make time for people, especially the members of your own team, and, of course, keep your word. These acts of loyalty will make you important men and women of honor and important assets of the BSP. Mahal kayo ng BSP. Mahalin niyo ang BSP. The adjective “mahal” has at least two different meanings. Mahal is an adjective for beloved. The other one is pricey or expensive or high value. But they’re not contradictory. When you say, dahil mahal niyo ang BSP, binibigay niyo ang lahat mahahalagang bagay para sa kanya. After this, you will go back to your respective offices and do what you have to do, finish the task that you have to finish. But I hope that you will go home with a renewed sense of commitment to finish strong in everything that you do and be committed to doing work that you will be proud of because you always deliver quality work. As they say, “Para sa Pilipino, para sa bayan at ang BSP ay para sa bayan.” Before I end, let me thank DG Ed Bobier, YPP Chairperson Lou Sicat, and YPP Vice Chairperson Jayzle Ravelo for putting this excellent program together. Once again, to Batch 1 of the BSP Young Professionals Program, congratulations, and we look forward to many great things from all of you. Maraming Salamat.

Title: Prospects for Financial Stability in the New Normal
Occasion: BSP-UPSE Distinguished Lecture Series 2022
Speaker: Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: Good afternoon. It’s an honor to welcome Professor Thorsten Beck to deliver the first BSP-UPSE Distinguished Lecture, which has the theme “Prospect for Financial Stability in the New Normal.” It is also my great pleasure to acknowledge Monetary Board Member Anita Linda Aquino of the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas, Mr. Frederico Rafael Ocampo, Senior Vice-President of BDO Unibank and Chief Investment Officer of the BDO Trust and Investments Group, and Dr. Ramon Moreno, formerly of the Bank for International Settlements and currently a member of the BSP Research Experts Panel. On behalf of the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas and the UP School of Economics, where I used to serve as both Dean and Professor, I welcome all of you to this Lecture. These are truly very challenging times even as we approach what seems to be the tail-end of this pandemic. Policymakers have to contend with elevated inflation, policy normalization in advanced and emerging economies, geopolitical tensions, and financial market frictions that complicate their policy decisions. There is much scope to take stock, evaluate, and proactively manage the formation of systemic risk and exogenous shocks. On the home front, we expect the Philippine economy to sustain its recovery. We note that quarterly private consumption expenditure levels have surpassed pre-pandemic levels already and continue to grow as the economy further opens up. In this respect, the employment picture improved significantly from pre-pandemic levels, thereby providing support for future economic growth. In this context, the risks to the inflation and growth outlook come from domestic and external shocks. Supply disruptions have triggered a surge in global fuel and food commodity prices, raising import bills. These sustained price pressures have resulted in petitions for transport fare and wage hikes, posing further risks to inflation and exchange rate stability. However, the greater risks emanate from policy spillovers as monetary authorities respond to inflationary pressures with hikes in policy interest rates. Last week, we raised our policy rate by 75 basis points at a regular Monetary Board meeting, bringing the policy rate to 3.25 percent. By the way, don’t be misled by the term “regular.” It’s regular because the topics of the regular Monetary Board meeting are topics other than the policy rate. This was an off-cycle decision which we rarely do. We were not supposed to meet until August, but we recognize that such action was necessary to arrest the broadening of price pressures. By taking urgent action, we aim to anchor inflation expectations and temper mounting risks to the inflation outlook. At the same time, we back non-monetary efforts by the National Government to mitigate the impact of supply-side pressures on commodity prices. Another pocket of risk is the possible reimposition of local COVID-19 restrictions amid an uptick in infections. The confluence of events poses challenges to risk pricing and market valuations of financial assets. The BSP remains vigilant to the possible spikes in financial instability and stands ready to employ all the available tools to maintain the orderly conduct of transactions in our financial system and in the broader economy. Amid this backdrop, we are privileged to have Professor Thorsten Beck of the Florence School of Banking and Finance and Professor of Financial Stability at the European University Institute to share his insights. Ladies and gentlemen, let me now call on Deputy Governor and also newly appointed Head of the BSP Research Academy, Francisco Dakila, Jr., who incidentally is an alumnus of the UP School of Economics, to introduce our distinguished lecturer. DG Francis, please. ###

Title: Closing Remarks for APEC Webinar on Strategies and Initiatives on Digital Financial Inclusion: Lessons from Experiences of APEC Economies
Occasion: APEC Webinar on Strategies and Initiatives on Digital Financial Inclusion: Lessons from Experiences of APEC Economies
Speaker: Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: To representatives of APEC member economies, esteemed speakers, and my colleagues at the BSP, ladies, and gentlemen, good afternoon. Seven years ago, the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas set in motion the modernization of the retail payment system with the development of the National Retail Payment System (NRPS). Under the NRPS, we activated two Automated Clearing Houses, which we now call InstaPay and PESONet. As you may have heard, we achieved several milestones since then. By the end of 2021, nearly a third – or 30.3 percent - of total payment transactions were digital. To provide some perspective – in 2013, monthly digital payment transactions in the Philippines represented only 1.0 percent of total payment transactions. It rose to 20.1 percent by the end of 2020 as the pandemic accelerated digital payments adoption. By 2021, the share of digital payments, in terms of value, represented 44.1 percent of all retail payments. At the core of this successful arrangement is the partnership between BSP and the payments industry. What we learned from this experience is that by acting early, the BSP was able to put in place the infrastructure for digital payments. This proved to be essential in the event of the pandemic. Thus, digital payments, which was not originally construed for ensuring business continuity, provided an unexpected lifeline to struggling consumers and businesses navigating the Covid-19 pandemic. Going forward, the BSP continues to implement the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap and work towards its two key targets by 2023: first, transform half of the volume of retail payments into digital form. And from the figures I cited earlier, we are close to meeting our objective. Second, onboard 70 percent of adult Filipinos in the formal financial system through the opening of transaction accounts. The BSP backs several initiatives to achieve the following goals: first, wider acceptance and use of digital payments services; second, increased digital payments adoption through better security, efficiency, and interoperability within the payments ecosystem; and lastly, financial stability and consumer protection through the issuance of cohesive regulatory and prudential standards. Along with our regional peers, we will continue to advance digital inclusion via a conducive policy and operating environment that empowers the unserved and underserved segments of the economy to move up in life. We will continue to engage our stakeholders in the public and private sectors to foster innovation and promote efficiency and competition while preserving financial stability, financial consumer protection, and market integrity. In closing, let me express my sincere appreciation to APEC for partnering with the BSP. I would also like to thank the moderators, panelists, and participants for sharing their ideas, insights, and experiences. I trust we can convert the rich insights from this webinar into actionable strategies and new campaigns as we continue our work. Salamat at mabuhay tayong lahat! ###

Title: Bringing Paleng-QR PH Plus to Baguio
Occasion: Paleng-QR PH Launch in Baguio
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Good morning everyone. Like Mayor Benjie, I’d like to skip the formal introduction… Mayor Benjamin Magalong, I’d like to thank you for a beautiful conversation yesterday evening. Of course, I’d like to greet Congressman Mark Go. It is extremely important that the correct legislation that supports digitalization all happen, and we will need your support. And all the other partners - I have to greet the representative of DILG Regional Director Araceli San Jose - and all our private sector partners; this cannot happen without the private sector. As you know, procurement in government is very difficult. Buying anything takes months, but with the private sector, which has its own system, has a great advantage over us in this respect, and that’s why we need them very much. I noticed people from both Maya, Globe, and GCash. I was thinking of what quotation I will use to start this speech. And I was initially thinking of Neil Armstrong. When he landed on the moon, he said, “One small step for man, giant step for mankind.” Of course, they never went back to the moon because it was very expensive. But nonetheless, a lot of lessons were learned. Indeed, until now, they left behind a metal disk that can be detected and targeted by a laser beam to the moon, and now we have very precise measures of how far the moon is because of that device. And there are many things they have learned by going to the moon that can also be applied elsewhere. But that kind of quotation is not really appropriate for this project because it turns out Baguio has not taken one step, it already has taken many steps. So, we started Paleng-QR in the right city. Of course, we are hoping and praying that it will spread throughout the Philippines and it will reach the sidewalk vendor, the tricycle driver, and the jeepney driver. By the way, the jeepney driver is very important. When I used to take the jeepney, I would notice sometimes the wife was seated in front. Sabi ko, “That’s not too wise.” They lose one revenue [with the] seat [taken], right? Then I found out the wife is auditing the revenue of the husband. Now with Paleng-QR, hindi na niya kailangang sumakay ng jeep, tingnan niya na lang ang transaction record ng asawa niya. Now, of course, that also creates new risks. Like what if all these information gets in the wrong hands? That’s why the Data Privacy Law is extremely important, but at the same time, the implications in the future are staggering. Can you imagine if every merchant that was willing to waive his right tells the bank, “You can look at my records.” The bank will see his transaction, and without asking for collateral, can already make an assessment of how much loan can be granted to that merchant. By the way, this is not a very distant future, and BSP already started on the project with JICA on a credit scoring system. If you look at all the data that you have on anyone, you can already calculate a credit score. And imagine a world where you no longer need collateral. Because the biggest thing that holds back the poor is a chicken-egg question: wala kang property kaya hindi ka maka-utang, pero kaya hindi ka makautang dahil wala kang property. So [Paleng-QR Ph] will break it – that really very bad, negative circle. But we want our positive circles. I’m really so impressed with Baguio. This is the place where the positive circles are happening. Of course, I will not mention any more what the Baguio App is already doing - I am very impressed - for instance when somebody needs assistance, all he has to do is tap one icon there, and the location device of your cellphone turns on…and it already knows where you are. If it happens that there is a camera in that place, the city will actually see you. So, if there is any place where we want to start [Paleng-QR Ph], we picked the right place. Of course, success is not automatic. As the Chinese say, “cross the river by feeling the stones.” Kasi malalim yung river. Pero kung maraming bato ka na pwedeng tungtungan at palukso-lukso ka sa bawat bato, kahit pa gaano kalalim yung river, mako-cross mo. Eh paano kung sa gitna wala ka nang makitang bato? Atras, diba? And tweak. I also heard that word ‘tweak’ so many times. So, I’m so optimistic. We are truly excited to roll out the Paleng-QR Ph here in Baguio City, and we look forward to everyone’s support for the successful implementation of this program, not just in Baguio but everywhere in the Philippines. Maraming salamat at magandang umaga sa inyong lahat.

Title: BSP-DLSU Research Collaboration
Occasion: MOU Signing BSP-DLSU Research Collaboration
Speaker: Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: Brother Bernard Oca, President of the De La Salle University, Dr. Raymond Girard Tan, Vice President for Research and Innovation of DLSU, Deputy Governor Francis Dakila, members of the BSP Research Expert Panel, officials, and colleagues, good afternoon. I speak to you from the perspective of a former professor of economics and a central banker. Having been both for quite some time – by the way, I’d like to add, I am an alumnus of De La Salle where I got my undergraduate degree in economics – I have long recognized the importance of connecting the theoretical and the empirical and in turn, the theoretical and the empirical with the operational and on-the-ground conditions. After all, academics and researchers provide valuable theoretical insights and empirical experience that inform how we carry out our core mandates – from the conduct of monetary policy to the crafting of bank regulations and the enhancement of the country’s payment system. Those three are what we call our three pillars. It’s against this backdrop that I am proud to say that the BSP has always attached great value to interactions with the academe and research institutions, especially top institutions like De La Salle. An example is the BSP Sterling Chairs which has been in place since 2006. The BSP Sterling Chairs aim to help maintain and promote a critical mass of productive research scholars in economics, statistics, business administration, finance, accounting, agriculture, and other related fields – which we do with the University of the Philippines. The BSP also conducts regular Environmental Scanning Exercises to foster open discussions on various critical and current issues. In 2021, we established the BSP Research Academy – or BRAc, for short – to strengthen our research capacity. We hope that BRAc will help foster a thriving research culture in the BSP by promoting and conducting objective, rigorous, independent, and policy-relevant research. We hope to achieve the level of objectivity and rigor of academics and come up with evidence-based research that can inform policy formulation and decisions. We also intend to explore emerging issues and engage in blue skies research that can help the BSP become future-ready. And this is where collaboration is important. Through this Memorandum of Understanding on research collaboration with De La Salle, the BSP affirms the importance of collaboration and intellectual exchange in research. Under the MOU, we may explore sharing of information and resources, co-authorships, joint publications, and sponsorships of conferences and other research events. Finally, I would like to express my gratitude to our research partner, my alma mater, DLSU, for participating in this valuable and noteworthy endeavor. May this mark the beginning of a long, productive, and collegial relationship toward building a body of high-quality research. Maraming salamat at mabuhay! ###

Title: The Philippine Economy: Navigating the Challenging Economic Landscape
Occasion: The Asset 17th Philippine Summit
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​​The BSP’s Pillars Help Create Growth-Conducive Macroeconomic Environment I will skip all the greetings and go directly to the point. That is correct; we are an inflation-targeting central bank, and we take that [first pillar] very seriously, but we have two other pillars; banking stability [is the other]. We make sure that banks are adequately capitalized relative to the risk they are taking so that when they lose money, they lose their own money, not the depositors’ money… And then, of course, there is the great importance of the payment system to the banking system. In the past, this used to be very boring: printing paper money and minting coins. Now, of course, the importance of digital payments is certainly an important pillar. We have our Deputy Governor Mert Tangonan here to expound on that. So those are our three pillars. These pillars do not change. What will change, though, are the scenes and the environment of these three pillars… which, in some cases, make it easier to support and sometimes harder to support. And this [where we are today] is about as testing a global environment as one can have. The three pillars cannot make the economy grow faster than its potential. But at the very least, it can make the natural performance as close to its potential as possible. Indeed it would be dangerous [for the economy] to grow faster than potential because it could overheat. So the capacity of the economy to grow is explained by many things – many of which are from the supply side. Of course, the supply side case is also determined by institutions, governance, and things like that. Given that, the central bank can have monetary policies that are too tight for [cases where] output [is] over the potential or monetary policies that are too loose [otherwise] and therefore creating price instability and price pressures. So those are the things that we watch out for. Price Stability: Challenges are largely external and supply side, but BSP has the tools But I will talk about price stability, because right now, it is the most challenging. As you can see in the chart, we have revised our inflation targets [at our latest meeting.] By the way, the inflation target is the average of 12 year-on-year monthly inflation rates. It is very simple: the price index this month compared to the price index in the same month last year. There will be 12 of those; the average of those 12. And right now, we are sure it will be much higher than the target - 5.4 percent [for 2022], later revised to 5.6 percent. The reason, of course, is that the recent [inflation] prints are much higher than 4 percent – our tar​​​​​get is 2 to 4 percent. But next year, we will have a fighting chance, [based] on my own gut feel, if we do what we have to do, [there is] at least a 50 percent chance of having an average inflation rate of 4 percent next year and an average inflation rate of 3 percent – right at the middle of our target - by 2024. So now the reason we will have a hard time; [why] we [will] have a 50/50 chance of missing the target next year is that current inflation rates are high, and this high inflation rate, year-on-year inflation rates [in 2022], will continue to the second half of next year. By the second half of next year, inflation rates will be within target, but there is a 50 percent chance that such lower rates would not be enough to offset the [elevated inflation in the] first six months. That is why it is 4.1 percent [for 2023] because [of] the policies that we have put up, and hopefully, with the policies of the government on the supply side, [there is] more than a 50-50 chance, we will miss our 2-4 percent average inflation rate [in 2023]. So I guess despite that, it can be said that we have hiked our policy rates enough to put us on a target-consistent path of inflation. Will we have to do more? Well, of course, this depends on the data, but the thing we are watching out for the most is what the Fed will do. It is actually very simple; capital can freely flow from the Philippines to the rest of the world and vice versa. And when I do the following survey, I asked every person I know, suppose interest rates in the Philippines are the same as in the US, ‘Where will you put your money? Those who will keep it in the Philippines, please raise your hands.’ Nobody? Our own finding is we need a premium of 100 bps for it to be an “even” contest. This is called the impossible duality. It is very hard to have an independent interest rate policy when your capital markets are open because, assuming that all countries have the same risk rating, the country that has higher interest rates will attract money, and the country that has lower interest rates will lose money. That is the other side of it. Of course, risk ratings are not the same; US risk ratings are always higher than ours because the US is the currency issuer of the world and the biggest safe haven. Of course, what BSP is hoping for is that the government would relax restrictions on imports and food products – these are called supply-side interventions. On the part of the BSP, we did the following: as you can see, this chart compares what we have done compared to what the US has done. We have raised our policy rate by an aggregate of 225 bps; the US has increased it by 300 bps. Now that partially explains why the peso is weak. The other reason is that many of our imports became more expensive - oil, coal, food, that alone meant 15 billion dollars in extra demand for dollars. So we started raising rates by 25 bps because we thought [we would have a] gradual exit. By the way, we had a very low policy rate. Why? Because we wanted to counter the effects of COVID. We brought down the policy rate to 2 percent, and in addition, we supported the bond market. We bought trillion of bonds in the secondary market. We knew that that policy must have an exit plan. We had an exit plan – a gradual exit – but it turns out that is not possible because of two developments. One is the Ukraine war; the other is the US pivot from saying, ‘Inflation is low and transitory.’ Now, their story is what? It is high and persistent. Now you have on the right-hand side of the chart, historically, the [Fed’s] fastest rate of [rate] increase. Of course, [it is] not yet the highest rate, but the level of the rate increase is unprecedented – [the Fed raised by] 25 bps, 50 bps, 75 bps, 75 bps, 75 bps. And maybe, [they might do] two more 75 bps, and one more 50 bps. By the way, they are [the Fed] doing the right thing. Inflation is like a disease. If you do not take medicine early and the bacteria spreads, it will be harder to treat it. Inflationary expectations get deeply anchored, initially anchored at a low inflation rate, but if the inflation rate is high for quite some time, they would say, ‘No, we don’t believe the old anchors anymore. We expect inflation would be higher in the future.’ And that itself creates a self-fulfilling prophecy. ‘I must ask for a wage increase.’ Why? ‘We expect prices to increase.’ ‘I must raise my prices.’ Why? ‘Because I have to pay wage increases, and besides, my competitors and my suppliers are raising their prices.’ So, that has to be avoided. And you cannot blame the US for being so aggressive. The only problem, of course, is it makes policymaking in the country a little bit more difficult. As shown in the next chart, here, all the currencies are depreciating against the dollar. Of course, the Hong Kong dollar is pegged. The Singapore dollar is pegged to the Euro, the Yen, and the US dollar. And, of course, how much of the basket is US is a secret. And because of that, you can see that Singaporean Dollar is between Euro and zero versus the dollar. The Philippines, on the other hand, is right there in the middle of the pack, like Taiwan and Thailand – all the same. But that is how we are dealing with inflation, as I will discuss later. Now, of course, there are many things that are going for us: one of which is the overseas Filipinos [remittances] and BPO. Then the other one is Net Foreign Direct Investments are also rising. But the current account deficit [is expected to] increase from 5 billion dollars a year to 20 billion dollars a year. So, we have buffers but, the waves are strong. And what was our response? Adjust interest rates. Let the peso-dollar rate serve as a shock absorber – more generally, let the exchange rate serve as a shock absorber. Sell reserves. And, of course, as I already said, the BSP backs non-monetary measures, the most important of which is allowing greater imports. And the BSP is doing all of these, but my view is more can be done in the fourth tool [non-monetary interventions]. The following slide will show you how the Philippines has combined all three medicines. Remember, the fourth medicine is for the National Government; the first three medicines are ours: interest rate adjustment, exchange rate adjustment, and reserve selling. And as you can see, the Philippines, in terms of exchange rate adjustment, is lower than Korea but about the same as Thailand and Taiwan. And as I already said, Indonesia is partially benefitting from high commodity prices. Vietnam is a long story [about] why it is so successful. That will take three days to lecture on, so we will skip that. Similarly, we changed the policy rate than all other countries in that table. We [the Philippines] did 225 bps [in cumulative rate adjustments]. One of the next highest is India – 190 bps. We also sold more reserves except in relation to Thailand. Thailand sold 19 percent of its reserves. Banking System Stability: A Bright Spot and Anchor for Macroeconomic Resiliency Now, let me go to the banking system. The best way to summarize this table is [to say] that banks are adequately capitalized; they are growing without sacrificing safety. In addition, the BSP, of course, is moving towards a sustainability agenda. We are a rounding error in generating greenhouse gases. But clearly, our bank

Title: Banking on Solid Ground: The Role of Banks in Post-Pandemic Recovery
Occasion: Bankers' Association of the Philippines (BAP) 2nd CEO Forum
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: To our friends – my friends – in the banking industry, my own colleagues from the central bank who are here, guests, good afternoon. It is great to see familiar faces, especially after what I thought was a very joyful reunion that we had at the central bank after almost three years. Thank you for inviting BSP to your second CEO Forum and for the opportunity to speak to all of you. We at the BSP have always valued these kinds of discussions. Indeed, I look forward to what you have to say after my speech. Do not worry if it is critical of the central bank – I will not remember if it is you who said it; I will blame it on another bank. Clarity of Policy Intent I have always believed that the BSP is more effective when you understand us – not just what we do, but why we do it – especially at a time like this where there is so much confusion. “Is the central bank still an inflation-targeting central bank, or has it become an exchange rate-fixing central bank?" Because…if you are fixing the exchange rate, then your interest rate is [equivalent to] the US interest rate plus the risk premium. In the absence of risk, like depreciation, [and] because no country ever defaults on its own currency, then we just go to where it is [the interest rate] higher, as shown in Hong Kong. Hong Kong cannot have an interest rate different from the United States. Indeed, I ran a few regressions where the correlation between market rates in Hong Kong is much closer to the United States than any other country in the sample that I have for a very simple reason – it is a fixed exchange rate. So, this has to be explained: why does it seem like we are “too concerned” about the exchange rate? ​​Why were we not as concerned before? But before I do that, I must address the topic, which is your role. Congruence of Goals In summary, your role is very simple: do your job well. There is no other industry where doing your job well is as close as it can be to serving the country. Of course, that [also] applies to central bank governors because our job is to do things for the country. In your case, you are doing business [and] seeking profits, but if you do it well and do it under the rules, you are actually serving the country. That is not a new thing. There are times when self-interest and the country’s interest can coincide, and I cannot think of a better example of that than of a banker who follows the rules. Now, a colleague of mine... found that a collapse of a stock market bubble hurts the economy a lot less over the medium run than a collapse of a real estate bubble. The collapse of a real estate bubble is linked to a long period of abnormally low growth rates for three, four, even five years, while the collapse of the stock market bubble had [little to] no effects on the economy. Of course, she did not explain why that is the case, [but] I have a simple explanation: banks do not lend to stock market speculators. They lend a lot to real estate. So real estate... [when it slows], it hurts the banking industry, which in turn, results in banks being too risk-averse and, therefore, a prolonged period of slow growth. Indeed, this concept is not in the textbooks: the concept of balance sheet recession. When balance sheets get destroyed [by a crisis], the economy grows slower. Because as anyone who has taken a course in accounting knows, a lot of lending happens to be based on balance sheets, and there is nothing like a weak balance sheet to make people risk-averse. So, if it is the balance sheet of the banks that are weak, the impact on the economy is prolonged. During that period of balance sheet fixing, we experience abnormally low periods of growth. Fortunately, we learned that from the Asian Financial Crisis. Fortunately, our regulations, which we got [and enhanced] from previous dialogues with you, acknowledged that that was very important. [Deputy Governor] Chuchi [Fonacier] will testify to that. We had lots of debates about how quickly we will move towards Basel II. Maybe we are moving too fast? And we decided, no, there is no such thing as moving too fast. I think it’s now paying dividends. Of course, we are running out of acronyms to discuss all this: LCR, Net Stable Funding Ratio... I forgot all the acronyms that we watch. Of course, I have been told, “Sometimes, you are overwhelming us, bureaucrats. You are overwhelming us with tools!” But it turns out those tools work. My point is that banks play a huge role. Anybody in the private sector could have the same responsibilities as a public official. By the way, I take my oath as a public official very seriously. The last line says, “no intention of shirking from the responsibilities of performing your duties.” The last part has something to do with following God – even if you are an Atheist, you will take that oath seriously. Because even if you do not believe in God, you have your own conscience. Now, I will go quickly to the slides. Banks sustained solid footing during the COVID-19 pandemic: adequate capital buffers, expanding assets, ample liquidity, growing deposits, and lending have recovered. So, the best way to summarize this is that you have grown without sacrificing safety. You have grown without sacrificing stability. Our banks are growing in a healthy way without sacrificing the stability of the system and, at the same time, protecting depositors. Of course, that is the entire point of capitalization requirements. [Deputy Governor] Chuchi [Fonacier] always tells me this: capitalization should match the riskiness of your assets. Now, in other words, if you lose money, it should be your money, not your depositors’ money. It is hard to think of economic development without thinking of institutions. It was said, “The reason a market-based economy progresses is because people’s creativity is empowered.” And you want to do things that are good for yourself...but it is not just you that benefits, but [also] the entire society. But it was also said that for societies that are most progressive, a very important part of that are the things that you are not allowed to do. Of course, ‘Thou shall not kill.’ ‘Thou shall not steal.’ ‘Thou shall not covet your neighbor's wife.’ My point is that we must give credit to the industry, and we must give credit to all of you who were young folks during the Asian Financial Crisis. I think this is one reason why we are successful. Now, we have people who are running the banks [that] do not see the Asian Financial Crisis as a theoretical thing; they were actually there when it happened. They were not running the banks [at that time], but they were close enough to the action to know that that should never happen again. Regulators, too, said that should never happen again. We should have larger reserves despite [contrary] advice from the IMF. By the way, the Bank for International Settlements (BIS) – the other source of thinking – said Asian central banks were actually correct in building up reserves. Of course, the question now is, what do you do with those reserves now that times are getting challenging? If I were to lecture now and talk about the topic, that is it. You are doing a good job. You are doing it by following regulations. You are serving the country. And as we engage in dialogue to finetune and improve the regulations, we become an even better economy. A good sign is your own patience in us not fulfilling our promise to make the reserve requirement a single digit, that you are willing to wait until June 29, 2023. Note that at these trying times, the most effective thing to do would be to raise the reserve requirements. And we are not doing it. We think we should use market-friendly tools to reduce excessive liquidity. Of course, that is why we are growing our issuance of central bank (CB) bills. That is why we are hoping you will sell down some of your bills to your trust department. Because our research shows that sterilization of excess liquidity by borrowing from banks is the weakest form of sterilization because you can change your mind and lend the money [instead]. On the other hand, deposits in the trust accounts are more sticky; these tend to get rolled over. Fortunately, the law now allows the BSP to borrow from the public. Of course, it is more convenient that it has done in primary auctions where only the banks can participate. That is why we are simplifying the rules on prohibitions on foreign money and non-resident funds in our monetary operations facilities. [We issued a circular where] if 10 percent of the fund is non-resident, it’s still [classified as a] resident. This is the very thing – the regulators and the regulated industry have this constant dialogue. We understand each other, and you understand why we do what we do. I admire that you are working on corporate risk governance. Of course, we have dreams of incorporating environmental risks into the [financial] system. [From] my point of view, we are rounding error in global warming, but we should not forget that environmental risks may also affect default rates. We hope the banks will incorporate that when pricing their loans. That is my last message on our second pillar. Challenges on the road to a Target-consistent Inflation Path There are two more pillars. The first one, of course, is price stability. Now, let us go to the first pillar. What you can see is that we have adjusted our [inflation] forecast. What it is saying is that our [average inflation] forecast [for 2022] is 5.6 percent. The simple explanation for that is that while it [the latest inflation print] is 6.9 percent, the earlier months were much lower, so the [full-year] average is forecast to be 5.6 percent. If not for the earlier months, this [full-year figure] would have been 6 percent or even 6.5 percent. What we are saying now is the average inflation next year that we foresee – by the way, this does not take into account a lot more actions that we will take. I said, and I hope my colleagues in the Mo

Title: Keynote Speech for Philippine Payments Management Inc. (PPMI) Annual Membership Meeting
Occasion: Philippine Payments Management Inc. (PPMI) Annual Membership Meeting
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ To the officers and members of the Philippine Payments Management Inc., our partners in payments digitalization, good day. Thank you for inviting the BSP to your annual membership meeting. As a member of the Monetary Board for the past decade and now as BSP Governor, I could not help but reminisce and reflect on how far our collaborative partnership has come. It has been five years since we jointly built the National Retail Payment System Framework, which gave rise to PESONet and InstaPay. We have made significant progress since then, and we have you to thank for these milestones. With your support, we were able to significantly grow the share of digital payments from 10 percent in 2018 to 20.1 percent in 2020 and 30.3 percent in 2021. Fortunately, digitalization and financial inclusion go hand-in-hand. About 56 percent of adults in the country now own transaction accounts. This is equivalent to an additional 22 million Filipinos we had onboarded in the formal financial system, up from 2019, when only 29 percent of adult Filipinos had transaction accounts. What is more promising is that e-money accounts are now the most owned accounts. The proportion of Filipinos that own e-money accounts rose fourfold from just 8 percent in 2019 to 36 percent as of 2021. The rise in digital payments adoption was mainly supported by the sustained growth in PESONet and InstaPay, and QR Ph P2P payments, which were, in turn, driven by the broader participation of diverse financial institutions in the NRPS. The growth in the number of participants expanded the access points for more Filipinos to be onboarded in the digital payment ecosystem and benefit from digital financial services. We have continued to roll out more digital payment innovations. Allow me to go over two of them: First, the QR Ph Person-to-Merchant (P2M), which we rolled out in October 2021 to digitalize merchant payments. To promote the broader adoption of the QR Ph P2M facility, the BSP engaged with the Department of the Interior and Local Government for the Paleng-QR Ph program. We launched Paleng-QR in Baguio last month to allow consumers to pay at wet markets and tricycle hubs using contactless methods. Second, we have the PESONet Multiple Batch Settlement (MBS) in January 2022, which increases the daily batch settlement to two cycles within a banking day. Through MBS, PESONet users can now receive funds at an earlier time than the usual end of the banking day, enabling users to settle large value payments faster, and consequently increasing PESONet’s viability as an alternative to checks. Despite our achievements, we know that there are still many opportunities to bring the benefits of these innovations closer to more Filipinos. For instance, it is crucial for us to digitalize more business payments which account for 24.1 percent of total monthly retail payments in the country. While supplier payments made up about 89.98 percent of business payments in 2021, only less than four percent of these payments were in digital form. Business owners still have a strong preference for checks and are constrained by the current requirements for paper trails. Recognizing such concern, the BSP looks forward to further collaboration with the PPMI in rolling out more innovative digital payment streams that would successfully address these gaps in our payments digitalization agenda. We have three initiatives in the pipeline: • First, we will be able to launch Bills Pay within the year to help unify the currently fragmented payment mechanism we have today. It will also support the digitization of government collections as mandated by EO 170 or the Adoption of Digital Payments for Government Disbursements and Collections. By the way, the IRR of EO 170 is about to be issued. • Similarly, the Request to Pay and Direct Debit facilities will provide greater efficiency in carrying out person-to-business, or P2B, and business-to-business, or B2B, payments, such as payments for goods, services, bills, and utilities. • Another key initiative lined up is the enhancement of our cross-border payment corridors, wherein the BSP is working with PPMI members to establish bilateral payment linkages with our ASEAN peers. Our shared commitment towards digital transformation has immensely helped our fellow Filipinos experience the benefits of cashless payments. However, our greatest innovations still lie ahead of us. Let us continue to lay the strong foundations for an innovative, safe, and efficient national payment system in order to lift every Filipino’s capability to participate in the gains of the growing digital Philippine economy. Maraming salamat at mabuhay po tayong lahat!

Title: Trust: Evolving Amidst Challenging Times
Occasion: Trust Officers Association of the Philippines (TOAP) Summit
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: To the directors, officers, and members of the Trust Officers Association of the Philippines—or TOAP—ladies and gentlemen, good day. Thank you for inviting me to be your keynote speaker for this year’s TOAP Summit and for the opportunity to share with you our view on issues facing the trust industry, especially during this trying time. We at the BSP have always believed in engaging the industry through fora such as this to help you better appreciate our regulatory and supervisory approaches and understand why we do what we do. Providing Context to Policy Let me start with the economy to provide some context [to our policy actions]. Over the past ten years, the Philippine economy has been on a strong growth path. The expansion was both robust and inclusive: more than 6 percent growth, and poverty incidence was on the decline. COVID, however, put an end to this period of strong and inclusive growth. So we at the BSP got to work and took measures to support the nation through this crisis: • First, we cut the policy rate to a record low of 2 percent • Then, we lent ₱600 to the national government – interest-free • Lastly, we bought a trillion pesos in government securities Fortunately, it appears that the economy is on the mend. The economy grew by 5.7 percent last year, and we expect it to grow by 6.5-7.5 percent this year. Even before the pandemic, the BSP had an exit strategy in place. The initial plan was a gradual, careful, and deliberate exit. We were considering increasing rates sometime in Q3 or Q4 2022, and the plan was gradual. Then, as the securities we bought matured, we would slowly decrease our holdings. That was the plan, but as they say, “The plans of mice and men often go awry.” What happened? The sudden pivot of US monetary policy and the Ukraine-Russia conflict strengthened the case for more aggressive tightening. So it was important that we had to act sooner and take bigger actions. As of our August policy meeting, we have raised the policy rate by a cumulative 175 basis points. We started with two 25 bps, followed by an off-cycle 75 bps, then a follow-through 50 bps. As you hear this speech, the Board will have a policy meeting. Of course, we will do whatever is necessary to achieve a target-consistent inflation path. The national government has also paid back its provisional advance—ahead of schedule. And we scaled down our government securities holdings and shifted the GS window into a normal liquidity facility. Now, with regard to inflation… Based on our August policy meeting – and we will revisit these numbers at today’s meeting – inflation is forecasted to exceed our 2 to 4 percent target this year. Since a large part of inflation is largely driven by supply-side pressures, our monetary tools, which are aimed at stimulating or reducing demand, may not be as effective in responding to current inflation dynamics. Moreover, it’s known that monetary policy acts with a lag. Our tools take about 6-18 months to kick into full effect. That’s why we support targeted interventions by the national government to reduce the impact of inflation on the most vulnerable groups – from transport groups to the agri-sector. That said, we are confident that we will revert to a target-consistent inflation path. What does that mean? While inflation may exceed our target this year, it will start hitting close to the midpoint of the target by the middle of next year. Based on our August meeting, inflation is expected to average 4 percent in 2023 and slow down to 3.2 percent in 2024. Hopefully, we can put the worst behind us, but there’s no way to say that with full certainty. To put things into context, this situation of elevated inflation is not unique to the Philippines. The latest inflation prints are much higher than the average for the last ten years and, for most countries, higher than the target. So inflation is high, but relative to other countries, the increase in our inflation rate is relatively low. We believe that our credibility as an inflation-targeting central bank has helped in this regard, and it will help again in the coming years as we work to bring inflation back to a target-consistent path. As you can see from the previous chart, the US is one of the countries facing inflation much higher than the Philippines. That’s why the Fed had to act as aggressively as it did to bring inflation under control. As a small, open economy, the Philippines deemed it prudent to act and adjust its monetary policy settings, especially given the impact of the Fed’s actions and tightening global financial conditions on the exchange rate. We are monitoring these external developments closely in relation to how they affect the growth and inflation outlook. As always, our monetary policy decisions will continue to be guided by data. Zeroing in on the Trust Industry Now against this backdrop, how has the trust industry fared? We are pleased to hear that the trust industry has performed strongly, with assets under management (AUM) growing by a steady 3.4 percent in June. • When industry assets are broken down, we can see that recovery appears to be taking place despite the pandemic: Investment management accounts, or IMA, which represent almost half of trust assets, grew by 18.4 percent from pre-pandemic levels. • Unit investment trust funds, or UITFs, which represent a quarter of trust assets, grew by a remarkable 47.9 percent between December 2019 and June 2022. Let me discuss UITFs in more detail. For us at the BSP, UITFs are valuable in the promotion of financial inclusion and investment literacy since barriers to entry are lower compared to other investment alternatives. We are pleased to note that the number of UITF participants more than tripled to 2.2 million in 2021 from 657,500 in 2019. Much of this growth may have been driven by the accessibility of UITFs through digital platforms. Recognizing the potential of digital innovations, the BSP digitized PERA in 2020 to make it more accessible to the investing public. Two years since the launch, we are already seeing considerable progress, with the number of contributors tripling by the end of May 2022. We thank our partners from TOAP and the BSP PERA Technical Working Group for their work on the information drive and public awareness campaign for the digital PERA. You can rest assured that the BSP will continue to work to promote awareness and encourage even more Filipinos to consider investing in the digital PERA to build their nest egg and prepare for retirement. In addition to the digital PERA, we recognize several initiatives by TOAP as part of ongoing efforts to develop the market, preserve the interests of the investing public, and bring it in line with international standards. You can rest assured that the BSP will continue to engage the industry in conversation on how we can do better. Your feedback is very important to us in this regard. One thing that may rank high on your agenda is access to BSP Securities. After receiving a request from the TOAP for access to BSP Securities, the BSP issued Circular No. 1130 dated 23 November 2021. Said circular allows UITFs of eligible trusts to invest in the BSP Securities Facility via the secondary market starting December 2021. In the same circular, we allowed digital banks to participate in BSP monetary operations. What do we hope to achieve with this circular? • First, we aim to help develop the [capital] market; • Second, we envision this will help mop up excess liquidity from the system, especially after the BSP injected about ₱2 trillion into the financial system, equivalent to about 12 percent of nominal GDP during the pandemic; • Third, this is part of operational refinements to the interest rate corridor. While we do not allow investments funded by non-residents into the BSP securities facility, you might be pleased to hear that we are contemplating a relaxation of the implementation guidelines. Specifically, UITFs with a minimal share of funds from non-residents may be invested in BSP securities, subject to periodic monitoring and review. Why did we prohibit these in the first place? We sought to prevent opportunistic investment activities by non-residents. However, discussions are still ongoing on how we can operationalize this policy amendment. Rest assured that we are in talks with the industry for feedback on how we plan to implement this once the necessary approvals are in place. We anticipate increased participation of trust entities in the BSP Securities Facility when this facility amendment is implemented. We expect the role of trusts to significantly improve the effectiveness of our monetary operations. As you know, we sometimes purchase foreign exchange and government securities, but we do not want this to result in excessive growth of the money supply. And the best way to do this is for the BSP to sell short-term central bank (CB) securities, such as the 28-day CB bills. If the trusts are the main buyers of CB bills rather than the banks, we are more confident that our purchases of foreign exchange and government securities in the secondary market would not result in excessive growth of bank lending. Another initiative by TOAP that we commend is the UITF Certification Program or the UCP for UITF marketing personnel. This certification program will help ensure that personnel selling UITFs are equipped to help investors understand what they’re buying based on their risk profiles and risk appetites. The certification requirement for UITF marketing personnel will be fully implemented by 2023 after having the transition period extended for one year. The BSP supports efforts of the TOAP and individual trust entities to raise the UCP completion rate by year-end. As you know, the BSP takes consumer protection very seriously, especially with the enactment of the Financial Products and Services Consumer Protection Act or FCPA. Lastly, we acknowledge the trust industry’s initiatives to digitalize its offer

Title: Banking regulation in the era of accelerated digital transformation and post-pandemic economic recovery
Occasion: The Asian Banker – Finance Philippines 2022
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: Good morning everyone. Let me start by thanking “The Asian Banker” for the opportunity to share insights on the BSP’s role in regulating banks during an era of accelerated digital transformation and post-pandemic recovery. We at the BSP have always believed in engaging the industry through fora such as this to help you better appreciate BSP’s point of view and understand why we do what we do. As you may know, we have three pillars in the BSP: price stability, financial stability, and a safe, secure, and efficient payments and settlements system. The BSP makes sure to respond to developments in the economy and the financial sector, mindful of these core mandates. Our first pillar of price stability – where prices grow at a stable and predictable rate – provides a conducive environment for banks to do business and support growth. Given the theme of this event, the discussion will focus more on our second and third pillars, which define our role as a financial supervisor and overseer of the payments and settlements system. In my presentation, I will address two questions: • First, how does the BSP provide a conducive environment for banking through our three pillars? • And second, how do we design our regulatory and supervisory approaches in an era of accelerated digital transformation? Let me begin by sharing our economic narrative to provide context for our discussion. The economy grew by an average of 6 percent for a period of ten years before the pandemic put a very rapid end to this period of growth. Fortunately, as restrictions on mobility were relaxed, the economy grew by 5.7 percent in 2021 and by 7.8 percent in the first half of 2022, giving us confidence that the economy is well on its way to recovery. Let me share what the BSP has done to support the economy through this crisis: • We brought the policy rate to a record low of 2 percent, • We also extended ₱600 billion in provisional advances to the National Government—interest-free, • And lastly, we purchased ₱1 trillion in government securities in the secondary market. But we knew, from the very beginning, that we had to exit from these ultra-accommodative policies. Our initial plan was a gradual and careful one. But as you know, powerful global forces – from the pivot in the US monetary policy to the Ukraine-Russia conflict – changed our plans. So instead of the gradual approach, we had to exit sooner and take bigger and bolder actions: • As of our August policy meeting, we have raised the policy rate by a cumulative 175 basis points. We started with two 25 bps, followed by an off-cycle 75 bps, then a follow-through 50 bps. And as you hear this speech, the Board will have a policy meeting. Of course, we will do what is necessary to achieve a target-consistent inflation path. • Then, the National Government paid the provisional advances ahead of the actual maturity date. • Lastly, we scaled down our government securities holdings. Despite the normalization of policy settings, our policy rate is still accommodative. Remember: we're coming from 4 percent pre-pandemic [policy rate]. As you can see in this chart, our policy rate is still lower than it used to be. ​ If you compare the policy rate to inflation, the real rate is still negative. Achieving a target-consistent path of inflation is of great importance to us. Respectable growth is still possible under these terms, but for the BSP, price stability, one of our pillars, is the primary concern. ​ On the financial sector… ​Let me summarize this slide by saying that our banks are growing without sacrificing the stability of the financial system and the interest of depositors. We can see this reflected in all these positive statistics: growing assets, recovering loan growth, increasing deposits, and sustained profitability while remaining sufficiently capitalized and liquid. According to the BSP’s banking sector outlook survey, bank leaders maintain optimistic views on the economy’s growth prospects. Having said that, banks face several risks. According to our Banking Sector Outlook Survey for the second semester of 2021, our banks have identified credit risk, cybersecurity risk, and environment-related risk as top risks. For the BSP’s part, financial stability, our second BSP pillar, calls for us to also closely monitor these risks and craft relevant regulations. Based on our surveillance toolkit, our banks remain on stable footing. So far, banks have been able to manage risks at the institutional level. As of July 2022, the gross NPL ratio remains single digit at 3.57 percent from 3.60 percent a year ago. This was matched by ample NPL coverage ratio of 99.2 percent. Cybersecurity risk is another cause for concern. To counter cybersecurity threats while at the same time promoting digital innovation, the BSP has developed a Cybersecurity Roadmap to institutionalize cyber-resilience in the financial services industry. Now, onto digitalization… Our policy agenda on digitalization will not only make the financial system more inclusive but also more efficient. As you may know, we have welcomed the entry of digital banks into the system. These are new industry players that would further push the digital transformation of finance.​ To ensure that these banks safely operate, the BSP is about to issue the second set of rules that will clarify the prudential requirements applicable to digital banks. A digital financial marketplace model is being developed, which will set forth, among others, the criteria for the grant of authority to engage in digital marketplace operations and the related supervisory expectations. The model builds on the open finance framework. To be well positioned to respond to emerging risks and challenges, BSP also leverages supervisory technology or “Suptech” in our operations. Suptech applications support faster and more flexible data capture compared to the traditional template-based approach. In turn, this allows for earlier detection of potential risks and improved supervision. Related to this, the BSP has rolled out our regulatory sandbox framework to better understand and supervise emerging technologies and business models. Through the framework, BSP is able to ensure that financial innovations are scrutinized prior to deployment to the public. The banking regulations are accompanied by initiatives that aim to shift our country from a cash-heavy to a cash-lite economy. These programs fall under the BSP’s third pillar of safe and efficient payments and settlements system.​ The BSP's overarching strategy is embodied in the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap 2020 – 2023. The Roadmap has the twin objectives of converting at least 50 percent of the total retail transactions volume into digital form and 70 percent of Filipino adults onboarded to the financial system by 2023. There has been accelerated growth in the adoption of digital payments. For 2021, the share of digital payment transactions to total transactions grew to 30.3 percent. Meanwhile, the value of digital payments increased to 44.1 percent. We have several initiatives to further broaden digital payments adoption across several use cases. Last but not the least, we recognize the importance of sustainable finance. While we are a rounding error when it comes to greenhouse gas emissions, we have to recognize that climate change could create new risks for banks. So we tell banks to factor the environment and climate risk into their lending and credit decisions. The more industrialized countries that are contributing more to global warming are actively discouraging banks from lending to greenhouse gas-emitting industries. But our focus is more on how it may affect default rates. Moving forward, the BSP will adopt guidelines on climate stress testing and sustainable finance taxonomy, enhance disclosure requirements and prudential reports, and look into regulatory incentives to incentivize financing toward green or sustainable projects. In closing, regulating banks amid an accelerated digital transformation and post-pandemic recovery is very tricky. But with our three pillars guiding our actions, we are ready to face the challenge.
