Rest assured that the BSP will continue to foster an enabling environment that allows the financial institutions under our supervision to contribute to our three pillars of price stability, financial stability, and safe, secure, and efficient payments and settlements system while supporting economic growth. Thank you for your attention. Our Assistant Governor Lyn Javier will represent the BSP in the Q&A.

Title: Toward a sustainable Philippine economy
Occasion: Harvard Business School – Club of the Philippines Virtual Forum
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Good afternoon. Thank you to the Harvard Business School – Club of the Philippines for inviting me to speak at today’s event. While I did not go to Harvard, I am very pleased that I am able to speak with your community, and I’m also looking forward to hearing your views and questions. First, I will talk about what we are doing in the BSP. We, of course, value this opportunity to share our views on the economy. I think the more the country understands the BSP, the more effective it will be in doing its work. I know most of you may want to hear me speak on the interest rate and the exchange rate from the get-go – both pressing topics that deserve extensive discussion, and I will definitely be touching on these later on. But we at the BSP make sure to anchor our actions on what we call our three pillars. Anchored on Three Pillars Our three pillars are price stability, financial stability, and efficient and reliable payments and settlements system. As I always say, the three pillars are constant. What changes are the environment and, in the case of the payment system, the technology. Price stability: it is important to have low and predictable inflation because when inflation is low and predictable, it is easier for the government and the private sector to borrow. It is very hard to decide whether an interest rate is adequate or not if you do not know what the inflation rate would be. In some countries where inflation is very low, a 7 percent interest rate is high. In countries where their inflation rate is high, 7 percent may not be enough. So, if there is uncertainty about what the inflation rate would be…clearly, that will get in the way of more efficient functioning of the financial system. Financial stability: if banks are weak, they often become reluctant to lend. As we observed after the Asian Financial Crisis, banks with weak balance sheets are often correlated with slower growth of the economy. Indeed, there are studies showing that right after a financial crisis that is related to real estate, economies grow significantly lower than normal for as long as three to five years. Lastly, the importance of an efficient payments and settlements system is as old as time, but now, with new technologies, it is becoming even more important. Fintech will not only make the economy more efficient; it may make governance easier. Of course, it will most likely make it easier for the financial system to be more inclusive. My talk will focus on these three pillars. Current Inflationary Pressures, the Resulting Inflation Path, and the BSP’s Policy Actions Right now, because inflation is a big problem everywhere, it’s getting most of the attention. The chart that you will see shows our forecasts, which, of course, get revised every [Monetary Board] policy meeting. We meet approximately every six weeks to reassess our interest rate and monetary policy settings. The way we measure inflation on a yearly basis is the average of the twelve year-on-year headline inflation for the year. Our target is 2-4 percent. Clearly, we’re going to miss it this year. Our forecast of average inflation for this year is 5.4 percent…but because of the relatively high inflation prints [in recent months], we have actually revised the average inflation for the year to 5.6 percent. The only reason it is 5.6 percent and not 6.9 percent as in the latest print is [because] the earlier months had lower inflation. Note that we think that inflation will begin to subside, and our own forecast is that by October or November, the peak year-on-year inflation will have happened, and it should be going down from that point on. But since we’re coming from a very high level, [inflation for] the first half of 2023 will still be above 4 percent, but the second half will be below 4 percent – probably closer to 3 percent than to 4 percent. But as you average out, the relatively high inflation in the first six months [in 2023] may outweigh the lower inflation in the last six months such that we [full-year figure] may actually exceed slightly the average inflation target for 2023. But note that we expect inflation to go back to within target by 2024. [So] the path of our inflation is target consistent. We may be off [target] now because of very, very strong supply shocks, but with the monetary policy settings that we have, when the supply shocks eventually die out – meaning prices may have risen but will stop rising – then just like the sheer arithmetic of inflation computation [where] the denominator is higher, and the monthly change is lower; therefore the year-on-year inflation will be lower. We refer to that as the base effect. Inflation pressures are beginning to broaden such that what we call core inflation is now also above 4 percent. Core inflation, [explained] in the simplest way: suppose you take out all the extreme inflation risks – [items that are] too low and too high – what remains? The sign that it has broadened is that even if you take out the very high inflation and low inflation commodities, average inflation is now above 4 percent. Clearly, the central bank had to act to make sure that what we call second-order effects – high inflation begetting higher inflation – does not happen. [We had to make sure] that the month-on-month changes in prices eventually normalize, and the initial momentum of inflation will disappear and normalize. The way to do that, of course, is to manage demand. Although our interest rate hikes are not very effective in bringing down the current inflation – because the current inflation is largely due to supply [pressures] – the moment [the impact of] supply shocks dissipates, the high inflation that is caused by supply [shocks] would no longer not translate to an increased momentum… that inflationary expectations do not result in self-fulfilling expectations, where high inflation causes higher inflationary expectations, which in turn causes higher inflation in the future. The Philippines is not the only country that is suffering from this. [If you look at] the ratio of the current inflation to the ten-year inflation average, we are actually one of the countries with a relatively low ratio. Many other countries have even higher inflation than us. It is the rich countries that have higher inflation now relative to emerging markets. While the inflation was driven by supply concerns, we are now seeing second-order effects starting to manifest in terms of wage and transport hike petitions. That is, exactly what we’re trying to prevent: high prices beget higher prices and inflation gains momentum. Now, what have we done? Let me start with where we came from. We are coming from an extremely accommodative monetary policy, which was our response to COVID. We brought down the policy rate to a historic low of 2 percent; for a country that normally has 3 percent inflation on average, setting policy rates at 2 percent means we are setting a negative policy rate. After adjusting for inflation, we actually had negative interest rates. That was needed, of course, because COVID brought down the economy by nearly ten percent [in 2020]. We knew, of course, that we must have an exit strategy. As the economy recovers, we should be gradually withdrawing the monetary stimulus. Our plan was a very simple one: very gradual [rate liftoff], maybe every meeting 25 bps [adjustments], and winding down of stimulative policies that COVID made us do. Unfortunately, there were a lot of surprises. The two main surprises are [first] supply shocks which had two sources: the Ukraine-Russia war, which pushed up fuel, fertilizer, and wheat costs, and the other [source was] due to domestic supply shocks because of pork shortage, fish shortage, and more recently, bad sugar supply, which together with our very restrictive, protectionist attitude to sugar, that is sugar imports are not [based on] market decisions but decisions of politicians and bureaucrats. For instance, we see inflation related to sugar as high as 40 percent per year. [Second], the other problem is that it turns out that US inflation is much higher and much more persistent than anyone had imagined six months ago. So, the US has become determined to raise policy rates. They started doing this since March; they have already raised their policy rates by 300 bps. This caused a narrowing of the differential in the interest rate between the Philippines and the US. Since the US dollar is the safe haven currency, a narrower differential between PH and US interest rates will cause the peso to depreciate. To top it all, the global shocks increased the price of our imports, increasing our current account deficit from US$5 billion last year to our forecast of nearly US$20 billion this year. The current account is [what remains after it is] stripped of the payments and receipts of the Philippines from other countries of capital flows. So take out the principal but include interest and dividends: that is the current account. Our exports of goods are much smaller than our imports of goods. On the remittance and services side, it’s positive because of remittances and BPO receipts. When you combine the two, we went from a US$5 billion deficit to a US$20 billion deficit largely because of the high prices of imports. Our own calculations show that 2/3 of the increase in the current account deficit is due to high [import] prices and not due to higher volume. These are all happening at the same time: the US raising policy rates, strengthening the dollar, and high inflation. Our response was to increase policy rates. If we were just responding to local inflation, the policy rate [adjustments] might have been much lower. But given that the US is also increasing policy rates [at a rapid pace], then that required a bigger response. The most dramatic of these was that we had an off-cycle increase. There was no scheduled meeting, and yet we increased the policy rate by 75 bps. That was in July. Then, we followed it up with two

Title: Safeguarding price stability amid challenging times
Occasion: PH Dialogue/Philippine Economic Briefing in Washington DC
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Good day. As we in the central bank say, we have three pillars. Those three pillars [by] themselves cannot make the economy grow, but they create the conditions under which a well-run government and a good private sector can get the best results. One analogy is motor oil: it cannot make a small car bigger, but it certainly could make a high-performing car perform much better and, in its absence, will make the car weak. Our first pillar is price stability: predictable and low inflation rates [where] a central bank’s forecast about inflation is believed and used by people who plan. The second pillar is a strong and stable banking system because every economic crisis is related somehow to a banking crisis. Sometimes, the banking crisis causes an economic crisis; sometimes, it is vice versa. Another crisis related to two of them is a foreign exchange crisis or fiscal crisis. The third pillar is an efficient, safe, and reliable payment system. It used to be the most boring part of central banking; it is about a large factory that produces money, and the many branches distribute and collect it back. Now it is a lot more exciting because of fast payment systems: InstaPay and instead of wiring cheques, we have PESONet. Let me start with the first chart on inflation. Much of these are forecasts of the BSP. In 2022, it is no longer our forecast because we already know the first nine months, and it is easy to predict the last three; [they are] higher than the first nine. That is why we say, “We will certainly miss our target of 2-4 percent.” In times like these, we always say [that] it cannot be blamed on the central bank. Because if high inflation means the central bank created too much demand, its interest rate policies were too low, credit policies too liberal, and too much demand was created, we should fire the Governor. On the other hand, because oil prices are high – and our country is 80-90 percent dependent on oil – clearly, you cannot blame the central bank. If there is a pork shortage because of the [African swine fever] virus and the shortage will last very long because the entire stock has been wiped out, you cannot blame it on the central bank. The question is, “What will inflation be according to our forecasts?” Our forecast is that by 2023, inflation will normalize. In other words, supply shocks do not create very high inflationary expectations. People know that the high prices are not due to the central bank creating demand. People know that the causes of inflation are, hopefully, things that are one-off supply shocks. Sometimes, it goes the other way. The supply shocks are so pleasant, like in 2015-2016 when inflation was off-target [below-target]. The supply shocks could go either way [driven by] really good harvest and very cheap oil. The central bank wants to make sure that supply shocks do not cause high inflationary expectations – expectations that are self-fulfilling where prices are high because people expect them to be high. The record of the central bank is [that] people believe when it says, ‘These supply shocks are temporary unless there are new shocks, they will pass and expire, and we will have normal inflation again.’ Of course, we could be unlucky, and another shock will come. Our ability to predict supply shocks – anybody’s ability to predict – is very low because they’re essentially almost random: big typhoons, big droughts, something that happens outside the Philippines, and so on and so forth. So, our forecast is that by 2024, things will be normal. Indeed, by H2 2023, inflation will be normal, and the reason we’re above target in 2023 is because [numbers for] H1 2023 are so bad, and H1 2023 is roughly the continuation of the bad things that are happening in 2022. But right now, we are betting that it is more than a 50/50 chance unless we have good luck that we will miss our inflation targets this year. Before I talk about monetary policy, let me talk to you about where we’re coming from. We had a pandemic. The pandemic threatened to make the economy contract by 15-20 percent. To prevent the pandemic from having large multiplier effects, we had an extraordinarily stimulative monetary policy: • We brought policy rates to 2 percent, an all-time-low; • The bond markets were going crazy. People were so scared during the pandemic that they were fleeing to cash. But the people fleeing to cash were selling bonds. And if bond prices are falling, interest rates will rise. So, the central bank bought ₱1 trillion of bonds in the secondary market; • In addition, we lent ₱600 billion – interest-free – to the Government so they’ll have enough money to fund all these programs. To their credit, the national government has already paid back their debt. So from the get-go, we knew we had an extraordinarily stimulative monetary policy. And like any medicine, it must end. You cannot take antibiotics forever. We had an exit plan, and the [initial] exit plan was beautiful: 25 bps [rate hikes] or a quarter of a percent. In a year’s time, we will be [bring the policy rate] back to normal. But there were large shocks – the Ukraine-Russia war, and then, of course, American inflation was much higher than anybody expected. So the exit had to be shorter – it must happen over a shorter period of time – and larger. The Ukraine-Russia war caused the prices of fuel to rise. On the other hand, [as] America raised interest rates, it began to make the peso weaker. You will notice that the countries that did not respond and did not raise their policy rate as the US did suffer even more – Japan [for instance]. Where will you put your money if dollar interest rates are the same as peso interest rates? Who will move their money to dollars? Everyone, right? Because the dollar is also the safe haven currency. And for a good reason: America is the center of the global payment system. The supply of global securities is a very important feature of the global credit system. Naturally, when you have US interest rates so high, people will flee to the US dollar. Philippine policy rates should be at least 100 basis points higher than US policy rates to have some form of exchange rate stability. Those are the things that complicate monetary policy. We are fighting inflation, and in addition, the US battle against inflation is forcing our currency to depreciate, so we also had to raise interest rates as the Fed does. In summary, there are two reasons why the Philippines raised rates: first, we have our own inflation problem, and second, we are forced to respond to policy rate changes of the Fed. Everyone is saying that the Fed will raise policy rates by two more 75 bps. Everybody is complaining, asking why the Fed is doing that. And I say, of course, the US must do that. It must kill its own inflation, and we cannot complain. Because if I were [in] the US, I would do exactly the same thing. As you can see, all the currencies are depreciating. If you were to look at the Yen, Euro, and Pound, the Philippine peso is somewhere in the middle of the pack. But as you can also see, we have several things going for us: robust external cushion because we have remittances and BPO. Despite that [buffer], the currency is under pressure. To summarize, we responded by: • First, by adjusting interest rates; • Second, markets were dictating that the peso depreciate because of what the Fed was doing and the fact that high prices of oil and non-oil products increased the need for dollars; • The other thing we did was, luckily, we had large reserves to sell. When you sell reserves, it affects supply. The other thing it does is it reduce peso supply because the pesos come back to us as we sell [dollar] reserves. • Then, we tell the government, ‘Let markets work. Import food that you cannot produce.’ Because no matter how good the President is as Secretary of Agriculture, the effects of production – that is the thing about plants – they need time. The next slide shows the summary of the actions we’ve done: • Our currency has depreciated more than all the currencies in the chart, except the Korean Won. • We have increased the policy rate more than any other country in the chart. • And we sold dollars. Let me say something about the banking system. Fortunately, our banks are in a very strong position: growing assets, deposits, and loans, adequately capitalized, and after the crisis, falling non-performing loan ratio. That is the other thing going for us: the banking system is there to support the system, will not be a cause of problems, and will support the system as we [the economy] recover. We’re doing digitalization, improving the risk governance of banks, and doing sustainable finance. To summarize: • The BSP is committed to bringing inflation back to a target-consistent path. It will take all the necessary measures, including raising interest rates, to match the US Fed’s actions – hopefully not too much. Hopefully, the Fed will pause. • The banking system is sound, stable, and capable of supporting economic growth • And financial digitalization will bring about more benefits for the economy While the Philippine economy has problems, it can navigate these uncertain times. Thank you very much for your attention.

Title: Navigating post-pandemic recovery
Occasion: Standard Chartered Investor Conference at the sidelines of the IMF-WB 2022 Meetings
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Good morning. Thank you for this opportunity to share our views on the economy and the opportunity to explain the role of the central bank in economic policymaking in the Philippines. It is important to communicate that the Philippine central bank became an independent central bank. It used to be that Cabinet members dominated the Board. Right now, [there is] only one Cabinet Member who is a member of the Monetary Board – [Finance] Secretary Diokno. In early 2000’s, we also became an inflation-targeting central bank. The independent central bank is known by its Filipino name, Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas; the former central bank is known as the Central Bank of the Philippines. I think these are two very important developments in the Philippines. Now, let me talk to you about what is the most important among the three core pillars of the Philippine central bank – price stability. If you look at the chart, you can see that we expect inflation to exceed our target of 2-4 percent this year. That is practically a certainty. Indeed, the last year-on-year headline inflation [in September] is 6.9 percent. Because inflation was lower in the beginning of the year, we expect the average of the 12-month year-on-year headline inflation to be 5.6 percent this year. Next year, we expect year-on-year inflation to be closer to 3 percent than to 4 percent by Q3 2023. But because of relatively high prices in H1 2023, we might slightly miss our [full year] target in 2023. Our original forecast was 4 percent; now, we updated it to 4.1 percent because of external developments. However, in 2024, we expect inflation to go back to the mid-point of our target – closer to 3 percent than to 4 percent. But first, let me start with where we were coming from to provide context. Because of the pandemic, the economy shrunk by nearly 10 percent in 2020. Of course, we provided heavy monetary accommodation to prevent the economy from going down even further: • We brought down the policy rate to 2 percent; • We extended ₱600 billion in provisional, interest-free advances to the national government, which they have already paid; • And we bought a trillion pesos in government securities because the markets were acting exactly the opposite of what the fundamentals said they would do. Because of the pandemic, we expected a drop in capex, which should [in theory] result in lower interest rates because the motive to save, in fact, was higher because of the pandemic. However, there was a flight to cash because of the uncertainty. To save the market from going against the fundamentals, we bought a trillion pesos in government securities in the secondary market. From the get-go, we had an exit strategy. Our initial exit strategy was gradual, maybe 25 bps in a row and deliberate. Unfortunately, there were developments that pushed us to take bigger and bolder action. These were external supply shocks, mostly related to the Ukraine-Russia war. In addition, there were domestic problems: swine flu forced the hog raisers to kill a big part of their herd. We have yet to recover from that, so we had high pork prices. And then, we had bad fish catch, bad weather, all of those together with high oil prices, high import prices pushed up inflation. In addition, the high import prices caused our current account deficit to increase from US$5 billion to a projected US$20 billion this year. Of course, to make things even more exciting, the sudden pivot in US monetary policy in response to US inflation resulted in a strong dollar or, put it another way, a weak peso. Because if interest rates on peso and dollar securities are the same, naturally, investors will prefer dollar securities for the same tenor. In other words, we have to maintain a differential with our interest rates and the US. So we moved much faster than we anticipated six months ago. We had a series of [interest rate] increases: the first two were gradual ones, two 25 bps. And then, an off-cycle 75 bps followed by two 50 bps. We have already increased our policy rate by [a cumulative] 225 bps. This itself cannot reduce the inflation that is due to supply shocks. The problem was supply. However, we were afraid that supply shocks will have their own momentum and cause second-order effects. This is the reason we increased our policy rate aggressively. As you can see from the chart, our increases in policy rate is lower than what the Fed did – 225 bps vs 300 bps – but it [our cumulative policy rate adjustments] was much higher than what anyone did in the region. Now, the other thing that happened is the [widening] current account deficit, plus the big increases in policy rates, resulted in a weaker peso. That also fueled us to be more aggressive in raising the policy rate. But while we have acted—and I think we have acted decisively—we feel that as we respond to the Fed, we do not have to increase our policy rate in lockstep, point by point. In this chart, you can see that the peso has depreciated more than most currencies in the chart, but [is] doing better than the Euro, British Pound, New Zealand Dollar, South Korean Won, and Japanese Yen. If you expand the countries beyond the region, we will be roughly in the middle of the pack. We let the peso act as a shock absorber. The depreciation solves one problem: a weaker peso will send appropriate signals to the market to use foreign exchange more carefully and also substitute domestic products for imported products. But we cannot rely solely on depreciation because if the depreciation is too large, it may begin to disanchor inflationary expectations. What is going for us, of course, is two very reliable sources of foreign exchange: business process outsourcing and remittances. We also decided that we will not rely solely on policy rate and exchange rate adjustments because during the Global Financial Crisis, we accumulated a lot of reserves – from US$45 billion of reserves to over a hundred billion dollars. So we also use sales of foreign exchange to adjust to global shocks. In summary, our toolkit has four components: • adjust interest rates, • let the peso serve as a shock absorber [and] let changes in the peso improve efficiency of resource allocation, • sell reserves, and • lastly, back non-monetary measures by the National Government. For instance, there are still many heavily protected agricultural products where it is very hard to import because they are covered by non-tariff barriers. Sugar is the best example. In that particular case, the government moved to increase imports to reduce pressure in prices. Coupled with non-monetary measures, we hope that the combination of these four medicines will help us achieve a target-consistent inflation path with the least amount of pain or output loss. This chart summarizes how the Philippines compares to its neighbors in using its tools: • The peso depreciated more than all the currencies in the chart, except the Korean Won. • Our policy rate changes were greater than any other country in the chart [at 225 bps]. • Finally, we sold 10 percent of our reserves – that is lower only than Thailand’s. As you can see, we are using all our tools to achieve a target-consistent path of inflation. Now that I have talked about our first pillar – price stability – I must also mention the second pillar: stability of the banking system. The chart here can be summarized in one sentence: our banks are strong; they are well-capitalized. They are growing in a healthy way without sacrificing the stability of the system and at the same time, protecting depositors. In addition, we have some of our strategic policy priorities: • Improving corporate and risk governance of banks • Advancing sustainable finance • And promoting digital transformation. • Indeed, our goal is that [by 2023] 70 percent of Filipino adults will have accounts so that they may easily conduct transactions. In 2021, it is at 56 percent account ownership. [Another target is to have] 50 percent of transactions to be digital next year. Let me end with the following messages: • The BSP is committed to bringing inflation back to a target-consistent path. • The banking system is sound and stable. • Lastly, we will continue to promote digitalization to improve efficiency in the payments system. I conclude that the government actually is smart enough to realize that a strong and independent central bank that takes care of its three pillars is in its long-term interest. Because with that, the government can borrow long-term in pesos at fixed rates. For those who are old enough to remember, there was a time when 70 percent of total borrowing was very short-term. Now, it is exactly the opposite: we actually now have to tell the [National] Treasurer to borrow more short-term so that it can be used for pricing loans. But that is a separate topic altogether. Thank you very much and good morning.

Title: A Call to Action toward Sustainable Growth
Occasion: European Business Community (EBC) 7th Economic Briefing
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Good afternoon, everyone. Let me thank the European Business Community (EBC) for the opportunity to share the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas’ (BSP) insights on safeguarding price and financial stability amid challenging times. Today’s event highlights the importance of our collaborations domestically and abroad. Europe has been an important economic partner of the Philippines, and we invite the EBC to continue their investment and projects in our country. Our existing trade arrangements with Europe have partly contributed to the Philippines’ greater market access to Europe. For instance, under the EU Generalized System of Preferences Plus (GSP+), more than 6,000 Philippine exports to EU member states enter duty-free. Since the Philippines’ application to the GSP+ in 2014, exports to the EU have increased to US$8.5 billion in 2021 from just US$6.7 billion in 2014. So, I am often asked, how can the BSP contribute to the government’s growth and poverty reduction goals? In what way can the BSP support the national government achieve these ambitious growth targets? The answer is in by doing our job—our three pillars, as we call them—and doing them well. While it is not part of our direct mandates, the central bank contributes to growth by working toward our three pillars. When we deliver on our core mandates, we help promote an environment and create the conditions conducive to growth. So, growth is a natural by-product of making sure we do our jobs well. Let me go over how we do just that. Our first pillar is price stability, about which I will say more later. Our second pillar is financial stability. As you can see in this slide, banks are growing without sacrificing the stability of the system while preserving depositor interest—growing assets and profitability while remaining sufficiently capitalized and liquid. Banks are actively lending to productive sectors of the economy, indicative of recovering economic activity. When banks are strong, people are encouraged to save. These savings are lent out to support households, businesses, and communities. The third pillar is a safe and efficient payments and settlements system. Under our Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap 2020–2023, we aim to shift from a cash-heavy to a cash-lite economy. So, how does this pillar contribute to growth? Think of our role as plumbers. Good plumbing work would ensure smooth flow of water, same as having a safe and efficient payment system that ensures that transactions are settled and closed. It also strengthens confidence in the system. Now back to our first pillar: price stability. Let me backtrack to show you where we are coming from. At yesterday’s meeting, the Monetary Board raised the policy rate by 50 basis points (bps), bringing the overnight reverse repurchase rate to 3.75 percent. What was the Board responding to? Inflation rose to 6.4 percent in July, owing mainly to faster price increases of key food items like meat, fish, and sugar. As you can see from this slide, the latest baseline inflation forecasts suggest that inflation could remain elevated in 2022 and 2023. Based on our August 18 assessment, inflation is expected to average 5.4 percent for 2022, before slowing to 4.0 percent in 2023, and finally decelerating to within the target range at 3.2 percent for 2024. What we are worried about is that inflation gains momentum. Higher prices beget higher prices. Inflation results in more inflation—or what we call second-order effects. We have seen further second-round effects by way of wage and transport fare hike petitions, which strengthen the case for ongoing tightening by the BSP. That said, the Philippines is not alone in experiencing above-target inflation. This is clearly not unique to the Philippines. A growing number of countries—from advanced economies to emerging economies—are grappling with high prices. In response to higher-than-expected inflation in the US, we have seen the US Federal Reserve (US Fed) pivoting from initially seeing inflation as something transitory to making inflation their priority concern. So, the US Fed hiked by 225 bps year-to-date in a bid to control US inflation. The interest differentials between the Philippines and the US began to move in favor of the US dollar, which could lead to depreciation pressures on the peso. Given the magnitude of the US Fed’s policy actions, the Philippines could not not react. That is why we took action. But we do not have to move in complete lockstep with the US Fed and match their actions one for one. As an inflation-targeting central bank, we believe changes in the exchange rate are healthy and work in the correct direction. The BSP does not normally react to movements in the exchange rate in keeping with our market-determined exchange rate policy. But in recent periods, the peso depreciation, while remaining with regional peers, has been adding significantly to the build-up of inflationary pressures. I have already said, on separate occasions, that the BSP is ready to take more steps to bring inflation back to a target-consistent path. As shown in this chart, there are broadening pressures on inflation as denoted by the 12-month moving average (red line). While we have hiked by a cumulative 175 bps, our policy settings are still accommodative. Remember: we are coming from a 4.0-percent pre-pandemic policy rate, which was in January 2020. As you can see in the chart, our policy rate is still lower than it used to be. Achieving a target-consistent path of inflation is of great importance to us. Respectable growth is still possible under those terms, but for the BSP, price stability is the primary concern. In closing, we invite the members of the EBC to work toward the BSP’s vision of a stronger, more digitalized, and more inclusive Philippine economy. In particular, the creation of better-quality jobs and the promotion of green and sustainable investments will create opportunities for development. I trust that we can count on your support as we move toward these objectives and goals for the nation. Thank you for your attention. ###

Title: Microfinance: Thriving through Uncertainties
Occasion: Microfinance Council of the Philippines, Inc. (MCPI) 2022 Annual Conference
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Good morning. Thank you, Aris, Ed, and all the officers and directors of the Microfinance Council of the Philippines—both outgoing and incoming. I am glad to hear that you consider the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) as your number one ally. From our point of view, you are also a very important ally of the BSP. It is hard to think of financial inclusion without looking at microfinance. Thank you for inviting me to speak on behalf of the BSP at your annual conference. This year, the theme is “Microfinance: Thriving through Uncertainties.” This speaks of the key role of microfinance institutions—or MFIs—in expanding financial inclusion. Despite the challenges of the pandemic, you were able to continue serving the entrepreneurial poor, reinforcing your position as last-mile providers. Of course, the pandemic has hit you very hard just like the rest of the economy. From very high [loan] growth rates until 2019—averaging more than 20.0 percent—there was a contraction of 2.5 percent in 2020. Indeed, whether you look at the value of loans or the number of borrowers, you have not yet come back from pre-pandemic levels. Thank God, the trends are encouraging. Recovery is on the horizon. With all the dedicated people in microfinance, especially in your council, I am confident that by this year or the next, you will have more than fully recovered. For instance, in Q4 2021, you already had a growth rate of 4.1 percent [in microfinance loans]. Although repayment fell to a low of 56.5 percent in Q2 2020 from a more normal 98.9 percent before the pandemic, it has now gone back to 84.0 percent in February 2021. So, things are looking up. Hopefully, as the economy reopens, so will the activities and the success of the sector. It is very important because, for us, MFIs are reliable partners in our push for financial inclusion. MFIs are present in 80.0 percent of municipalities and cities and are located at the heart of communities. According to the BSP Financial Inclusion Survey, 9.0 percent of the adult population maintains an account with MFIs, making MFIs the third choice for account maintenance, next only to e-money issuers and banks. Tellingly, the same survey showed that 23.0 percent of Filipino borrowers secured loans from MFIs, compared to only 12.0 percent from government institutions, 8.0 percent from cooperatives, and 7.0 percent from lending-financing institutions, making MFIs the most favored formal sector loan provider in 2021. Let me emphasize the words, “formal sector.” You are the most important bridge from the unorganized or informal sector to the formal sector. Who are the providers of these microfinancing solutions? • There are 139 banks offering microfinance loans. There is CARD [Bank] for one. • Then, you have microfinance NGOs [non-governmental organizations] which have an outstanding portfolio of ₱50.4 billion and 6.2 million clients as of 2020. • Cooperatives, on the other hand, had an outstanding loan portfolio of ₱288 billion and 8.8 million members as of 2020. ​​In short, MFIs are a key component in achieving our financial inclusion goals, especially in reaching the most vulnerable. When traditional banks cannot grant loans to microentrepreneurs—due to various reasons, be it lack of collateral or lack of documents—microfinance institutions provide loans with simple requirements and zero collateral. Of course, loans may be smaller in value than extended by banks, but when one looks at them from the ground level, [they are] no less important. It is how many parents could have missed tuition payments; how many businesses could have missed buying their inventory. In other words…even though the loans are smaller, they are not of lower strategic value. Through microfinance, microentrepreneurs have been empowered, which allowed their modest businesses to grow into promising ventures. Indeed, some of them have “graduated” and are now regular borrowers of banks. Given your resilience, the theme of this event, “Microfinance: Thriving through Uncertainties,” resonates with every Filipino—umuunlad bagamat nagkaroon ng krisis. We want to honor that and help you along the path to growth, hopefully, as regulators, by creating the conditions for an enabling regulatory environment, [by observing] as [the late Governor] Nesting Espenilla calls it “proportionality,” in our regulations through:​ • ​First, expanding access to credit; and • Second, promoting digitalization. Under the refreshed National Strategy for Financial Inclusion, or NSFI, the BSP supports initiatives to enhance the financing ecosystem for micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs) and the agriculture sector. We have several initiatives to enhance credit access. One is expanding the PhilGuarantee’s [Philippine Guarantee Corporation] MSME Credit Guarantee Program. This is very important because many MSMEs have been hurt by the pandemic, and maybe the only way they can return to creditworthiness is if they are given another chance by having access to guarantees. Also helping with the collateral problem is the long-standing goal of the Credit Surety Fund, which was pioneered by the BSP. It is also important to expand alternative financing options for MSMEs. Oftentimes, the source of the solution is supply-side. They are better, more productive, more efficient, and more profitable—and therefore will have better access to credit. Digitalization is quite important. Here, we can improve the relative position of small lenders relative to the big ones. I should mention:​ • Paleng-QR Ph – This program taps LGUs’ [local government units] policy championship in promoting the use of QR Ph payments among small merchants. I was just in Baguio, and we launched it together with Mayor [Benjamin] Magalong. I actually bought taho using my e-wallet; • Basic deposit accounts; • Facilitation of technical assistance and matching grants supporting the digital transformation of rural financial institutions and other last-mile providers; and • Promoting internet connectivity in underserved areas. There are lots of beautiful stories here. During the last typhoon that hit Siargao, it was a rural bank that remained open—Cantilan Bank. When all the big banks were unable to provide services, here was a rural bank that had access to satellite [technology] and made people, some of them stranded, much better off. Here, our partners are DOST [Department of Science and Technology] and PhilSA [Philippine Space Agency]. There are two rural banks participating [in testing satellite technology via satellite internet service provider]: Progressive Bank of Malvar and Rural Bank of Cuenca. With your vast network and reach, your members are effective agents not only of financial inclusion but also of digital transformation and progress. You, the microfinance community, have made it possible to provide much-needed financial services, especially to those whose livelihoods were affected by stringent mobility and health restrictions. We thank you for helping keep our countrymen afloat during these difficult times, and we trust that you will stay true to that commitment. Rest assured of the BSP’s support. We appreciate and like what you are doing [to support MFIs], and we would be extremely dense if we do not realize that [your key role in development]. As they say, we rise together by lifting others. Maraming salamat at magandang umaga. ###

Title: Preserving Price Stability in a Challenging Global Environment
Occasion: UP School of Economics Alumni Association (UPSEAA) Forum
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ UP School of Economics Alumni Association (UPSEAA) President Jeffrey Ng, distinguished officers of the association, and fellow alumni, good afternoon. Let me start by saying that it is very important for the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) that we are able to communicate with groups such as the UPSEAA. As you well know, the School right now is overrepresented in the Cabinet relative to its number of graduates—the Finance Secretary, the NEDA [National Economic and Development Authority] Director-General, and the Budget Secretary, so much so that my friends tell me that if the economy does badly under the current president, they will blame UPSE. But of course, that is not true; that is like blaming sociologists for a crime. Let me say that, as suggested by the title, we are indeed facing challenging times, probably harder to find anything comparable to this if one looks only at our post-war history. First, the pandemic caused the economy to shrink by almost 10.0 percent in 2020. If one looks only at Q2 2020, the damage done was even higher. Never in the history of our country did we experience this kind of contraction. The response of the BSP was, to use the word by the IMF [International Monetary Fund] in their dialogues with us, “unconventional” monetary policies. Deputy Governor [Francisco] Dakila, another UPSE alumnus, told the IMF that it is not unconventional; it is in the BSP Charter. What we did was legal. But the IMF said, to the extent that you have never used it before, it is unconventional. What did we do? First, we brought the policy rate to a record low of 2.0 percent. We extended ₱600 billion in provisional, interest-free advances to the National Government. Finally, we purchased one trillion pesos worth of government securities on the secondary market. And it worked. The economy actually grew very rapidly in Q1 2022, and we expect the Q2 2022 [performance] to be quite good also. Indeed, we were so sure that our policies would work that we were sure that we needed an exit strategy. Whether or not inflation was high, we would start raising rates. The exit already began: the government paid back the ₱600 billion in provisional advances. We were thinking that we will raise policy rates, even if inflation is normal, starting the first month of Q3. The word we used then was a “gradual” exit from the ultra-low interest rates we had then. But as you now know, a lot of things have changed since then: The Russian war on Ukraine made a lot of things we import, oil or non-oil, quite expensive. Inflation [in the US] turned out to be much higher and much more persistent than even the US Federal Reserve (US Fed) had anticipated as late as three months ago. Therefore, the US Fed had to pivot from not worrying about inflation to being very hawkish. If capital is mobile and the interest rate in one country is about the same as the US, since the US dollar is the safe haven during bad times, funds will flow out and move to the US and, therefore, add extra pressure on currencies such as the peso. In addition, our economic growth increased faster than our trading partners’. So, our imports increased much faster than our exports. Add to that the higher price of imports and we have a fairly large current account deficit, which by itself will already cause the peso to depreciate. If that was the only reason that the peso was depreciating, then it is a good thing—produce more of what is scarce and demand less of it. Unfortunately, the currency is not just any other commodity because if you have a consistently rising exchange rate, people may no longer believe our inflation target and instead anchor their inflation expectations on the changing [exchange] rate. Now, if inflation were low and exchange rate changes were small, that would not be a problem. For small changes in the exchange rate, the elasticity is less than 0.1. However, if the changes are large, the relationship becomes non-linear, and it will change inflationary expectations and cause what we call second-order effects. Therefore, we had to act sooner and bigger because that will prevent the need to act even bigger in the future. We do not have to match the interest rate changes of the US Fed, but we cannot afford not to act. The exchange rate itself is beginning to add to inflationary pressures and inflationary expectations, so we decided to act. And we decided to act very quickly. Our action [75-basis points (bps) off-cycle rate hike] was a surprise in two ways: first, it was off-cycle; and second, we seldom increased rates by more than 50 bps. Fortunately, the increase in the policy rate will not, in our view, kill or significantly slow down what we see as nascent economic growth. Our own analysis is that the economy can absorb the impact of the rate hike. The fact that the economy grew by 8.2 percent in Q1 2022 and that we expect it to grow even faster in Q2 2022, we are confident that the economy will [continue to] grow rapidly this year. But while there are encouraging signs of recovery, we can see that there are other things that complicate the situation. The current account deficit was significantly larger than what we initially forecasted. In this chart, we revised our forecast of the current account deficit significantly because of higher oil and non-oil prices. The question becomes very simple: how much of our reaction will be policy rate changes and how much will be sales of foreign exchange? Both have their advantages and disadvantages. First, if you sell forex, it might backfire because the markets see that your reserves are low and say that “All the more reason the peso will depreciate.” But we also know that high interest rates may have an unintended effect on growth. Moreover, banks hold quite a bit of government securities. If interest rates rise, the price of​​ bonds, which banks hold, will fall. Therefore, the banks lose money, they have less capital, and they may be less inclined to lend. Those are the things that we are now balancing. There are two barrels in our weapon. Which do we rely on more? In this chart, you can see the different reactions of different countries: South Korea and the Philippines increased their policy rate the most—125 bps. On the other hand, as a percentage of their reserves, Thailand sold the most. Thailand did not even change its policy rate. Indonesia is quite different: it sold less reserves and did not increase policy rates. The difference between Indonesia and the Philippines is that they are not as dependent on oil imports as we are. Different countries have different ways of reacting, and the Philippines [stood] at the higher end of the policy rate and [ranked] quite high relative to the percentage reduction of reserves. What explains that is we are more dependent on imports of things that have become more expensive. In addition, because our growth rate is higher, we were less afraid to raise the policy rate, unlike other countries. It is a balancing act between the two tools: forex interventions and raising policy rates. But there are interesting questions: why did we not sell more [dollars]? Could we have done less than 125 bps [in policy rate adjustments]? But in our mind, the policy rate increases, especially the surprise off-cycle 75 bps, were actually very powerful in changing expectations and have already resulted in a much more stable peso. We did what we had to do with the comfort that we may reduce growth—but not significantly that we can still grow rapidly. If we postpone the adjustments and wait for the inflation to be too high, policy rates, later on, would have to rise even more, and output loss in the future would be greater than what we took now. We are quite confident that the 125-bps increase will be quite effective in reducing inflation and reducing depreciation of the peso. The midpoint of our inflation forecast for 2023 is 4.2 percent. If you do nothing, the probability that we [inflation] will exceed 4.0 percent is much more than 50.0 percent. We expect to hit near the midpoint of the inflation target in 2024. From the point of view of an inflation-targeting central bank, it is better if the midpoint of the inflation target for next year is below 4.0 percent, which, in our estimates, is the case now that we have taken the necessary measures, like the 75-bps increase, but [also] for another reason. The high rate increases in the US will most likely cause a recession. And even if they do not, [these will] significantly slow down the growth of the US economy that demand for petroleum and other products will fall, which will contribute to lower inflation sooner than as shown in the chart now. In short, safeguarding price stability is not just an essential condition for preserving macroeconomic stability. If you take a longer view, especially when inflation is high, it is better to control inflation sooner than later. Because if you wait till later, the output loss will be greater. If you take a longer view, there is no conflict between price stability and economic growth. More so, if you take an even longer-term view—if the central bank is credible in its inflation targets—it is easier for the government to issue longer-term peso-denominated bonds. If you look backward, you can see when we were so dependent on T-bills [Treasury bills] because it was so hard to sell longer-term bonds, since there was so much uncertainty over the future inflation rate. Finally, I would like to make the point that it is better if people understand why the central bank does what it does. A predictable central bank would be more effective. If they know what we are doing, the signals we send are clear and the direction of the economy will be more certain. But in addition to that, there are other things that we have to do. We have to get better as a central bank. One of the things we did was [to launch] a research academy, which has strong ties with UPSE. We recently had a seminar series where both faculty m

Title: The Road Ahead: Priorities for the Next Six Years
Occasion: Economic Journalists Association of the Philippines (EJAP) Virtual Forum
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Good morning. [Your theme] “The Road Ahead: Priorities for the Next Six Years” is a very easy question to answer for a central banker because we have our three pillars: price stability, financial stability, and an efficient and secure payments and settlements system. Those three pillars do not change; what does change is the environment and in the case of the payment system, the technology. In that sense, though our pillars are constant, the dynamic of the policies of the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) is very fluid. As you can see in the chart—note that these numbers are old data and do not reflect the latest data that we will use [at our policy meeting] tomorrow. That said, for this year, we will clearly miss our 2.0–4.0 percent inflation target. Due to the fact that the last two headline inflation numbers are higher than expected, you would expect an upward revision [to our forecast for the year]. However, it can also go the other way. Indeed, the actions we take are all meant to bring the current forecasts of 4.2 percent [for 2023] below 4.0 percent and 3.3 percent [forecast for 2024], closer to 3.0 percent. Of course, it is easier said than done. And if we focus too much on that [inflation] and ignore the effects on output, the output loss of our actions can be very large. That is why we call it flexible inflation-targeting. That is why it is a band—2.0–4.0 percent. If you are too focused [on hitting] the midpoint of the target, you will sacrifice a lot of GDP [gross domestic product]. I can already say that we should aim for a point lower than 4.0 percent next year and closer to 3.0 percent the following year. The rest of the term of the current president should be within 2.0–4.0 percent. Forecasting anything beyond 18 months is really futile. The normal distribution is about 95.0 percent probability, and that standard deviation gets larger and larger as the forecast policy horizon goes further. To give you an example, we were pleasantly surprised in 2016–2017 when inflation was lower than target. Why? Because the global environment was deflationary. So, these are the forecasts of private sector economists—they are not very far from ours. We ran a regression between our forecasts as the independent variable and private sector forecasts as the dependent variable, [and we found that] BSP forecasts influence private sector forecasts. It is our role to influence inflationary expectations. Our role is to tell the public: we will use everything in our toolkit to bring inflation to within the target band, in this case, 2.0–4.0 percent. It is just that there are times when supply shocks and global shocks are just too powerful. Of course, as shown by the sugar incident, both natural [factors] and [man-made] policy could affect prices—bad weather, expensive fertilizer, reduced sugar supply, etc. I keep saying that bureaucrats saying how [much] to import is always a formula for disaster because so many things [data] are needed that you end up with too much or too little imports. You can see that the latest inflation prints suggest intensifying inflationary pressures—whether you look at the year-on-year or the 12-month moving average, all the lines are going up. Even more alarming is that the share of items [within the inflation target] is getting smaller, whereas the share in purple [items above target] is rising. This is the current challenge that we are facing. We are also beginning to see what we call second-round effects. Naturally, if the inflation rate is high, people will ask for more adjustments in wages. Naturally, if fuels get more expensive, transport groups will ask for price adjustments. So, second-round effects are already being seen. The other way to look at this is to look at core inflation, which takes out volatile items. But even if you take out the volatile items, as you can see, the grey line is [still] going up and is closer to 4.0 percent than to 3.0 percent. To make things even more difficult, the peso has depreciated quite a bit. Of course, as you can see in the graph, we are in the company of many: in other words, it is a strong dollar, not a weak peso. But for the consumer, [most] things are getting expensive because imports are largely denominated in dollars. As the Americans say, “The dollar is our currency, [but] it is your problem.” Normally, we do not care too much about the exchange rate movements because they are healthy adjustments. But there are times, if it is [depreciation] too much, it might actually disanchor inflationary expectations. For instance, in the experience of Latin America during the bad old days, depreciation has actually contributed to a balance of payments deficit because every time the currency depreciates, inflation goes up by an equivalent amount. If you look at the ratio of the exchange rate and the price index, every time the numerator goes up, the denominator goes up, too. So, the ratio does not change. If that happens, you can even have hyperinflation—inflation causing exchange rate changes and exchange rate changes causing inflation. And to make things worse, debt is dollar-denominated, bankruptcies [can happen], and so on and so forth. You do not want to get there. Fortunately, we have the capacity, the forex reserves and, I hope, the confidence of the public to make sure we will succeed in achieving price stability. This chart shows that, relative to target, most countries are missing their inflation targets. The biggest miss is the UK, [which has a] target of 2.0 percent, but inflation is 9.4 percent. Europe [has a] target of 2.0 percent, but inflation is 8.9. Even USA… These countries have lower targets than us but [are experiencing] higher inflation than us. This is not misery loves company nor an excuse-making. All I am trying to show is that the tools of the central bank cannot move these things. It is like using a needle to push a cannonball. You will see that we have changed policy rates by [a cumulative] 125 basis points (bps), including the surprise 75 bps [rate hike]. The 75 bps [move] was a surprise in two ways: [first] it was not scheduled, and [second] it was 75 bps. The US Federal Reserve (US Fed) has actually done 225 bps [in cumulative rate adjustments]. From the picture I just showed previously, their actual inflation is much, much higher than target, so they are much more aggressive than us. [We may] expect the US Fed to be less aggressive than before, but we cannot rule out 50 bps [moves]—which, in central bank language, are [considered] large adjustments. [While] we do not discount what the US Fed is doing, the question becomes the extent to which we do have to respond, taking into account the exchange rate effect. If the exchange rate changes are already so large, you do not want that to add further to the already high inflation rate. In spite of the increases, what I would like to emphasize is that…while we increased the policy rate—it was at 200 bps for a long time—the yellow line is much lower than the red and blue lines. In other words, policy [settings] is still quite accommodative. The reason is that COVID forced us to be very aggressive in supporting the economy: We cut the policy rate to 2.0 percent, which we have never done before. We bought over a trillion pesos of government securities in the secondary market. We also lent ₱600 billion, interest-free, to the government. It is working and that is what we call a nascent, strong economic recovery. Even with the global problem, we already had an exit plan. Of course, the exit came sooner and faster than expected, given what is happening outside the Philippines. You can see there was a large contraction of the economy because of lockdowns; the economy contracted by almost 17.0 percent [in Q2 2020]. Fortunately, we were able to bounce back [with] vaccination and also because it seems that the current strain of COVID is not sending as many people to the hospital as the previous one. We can see that [trend] as well in our own statistics of our employees. Therefore, in that sense, I am optimistic that given the current [COVID] strain has not sent too many people to the hospital, we are less likely to lock [the economy] down. If we do have lockdowns, they will likely be limited. The other source of my optimism is that the services sector has already seen a quite strong recovery. What is lagging behind is agriculture. In particular, [agricultural] production could not keep up as demand for sugar increased, which is quite related to GDP growth because [it is found in] soft drinks, restaurants, ice cream—all of these things. For our second pillar, financial stability, you see a lot of strength. The pictures show that by and large, banks are strong, loans are recovering, and banks have sufficient provision in capital to take a hit from defaults due to COVID. This is the picture for loan demand, also quite encouraging. Of course, the downward revision by the IMF [International Monetary Fund] of global growth is the downside to growth, but it is also a downside to inflation. Indeed, what we are seeing now is oil prices are now going down because people are expecting demand to fall due to a slowdown in the US economy. Most people say, including Jamie Dimon of JPMorgan, that a [US] recession is inevitable. [They say that] the economy is not like a plane that you can land all the time; sometimes, it crashes, and sometimes, the landing is not very good. Exactly… To what extent to which all these policies by the US Fed, which are meant to control inflation, could now result in a hard landing is something we cannot guess. The best experts could be wrong, like Paul Krugman. Global commodity prices are easing. All these factors will contribute to growth, to a stronger Philippine economy because we are a net importer of all these things. But who can forecast what will happen? What I am saying is that forecasting things at this point is a very dicey thing. Amidst all of these, of course, the

Title: PH Economy: Harnessing the Bright Spots
Occasion: The Manila Times Mid-Year Economic Forum
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ The Forum’s theme, “Charting the Course at the Changing of the Guard,” is a very appropriate one, right after the State of the Nation Address (SONA) and the post-SONA. If I am allowed to make the title longer, I would write, “Charting the course at the changing of the guard at a very challenging time for both advanced and emerging economies.” I largely share the optimism of former Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) Governor and now Department of Finance Secretary Ben [Diokno]. Our own forecasts are quite consistent with theirs. I also agree with him that these [targets] are all possible and that it will take a whole-of-government [approach], great focus, and great implementation to achieve this. We owe it to the country to try our best. The good news is that we are starting quite well. After a very deep contraction of the economy that was caused by the pandemic, contracting by nearly 10.0 percent in 2020, we had a 5.7-percent growth in 2021 and 8.2-percent growth in Q1 2022. I expect that we should be able to get to 7.0 percent [full-year] growth this year, consistent with the DBCC [Development Budget Coordination Committee] number of 6.5–7.5 percent. I also agree that we could do a 6.5 or higher for the rest of the term of the current president and maybe even beyond. If one looks forward, one will also see very good numbers in FDIs [foreign direct investments]: US$10.5 billion in 2021, higher than any other year. The growth in the first four months of 2022 is 12.1 percent. Consumer confidence for the current quarter is still negative, but as you can see in the chart, it has been growing straight up, becoming less negative. When one asks about three months ahead and the next 12 months, the level of optimism is quite high. On the business side, we also see positive business sentiment in Q2 2022, and the expectations—the further down you go—become more and more optimistic. For the next 12 months, we are looking at more than 60.0 percent positive sentiment. Inflation, of course, has become a big problem. As we in the central bank say, a big part of it is [driven by] supply shocks, and not only that, [they were] external supply shocks. But we are hopeful that this will eventually subside—not that the prices will fall, but the rate of growth of prices will start tapering off in the near future. We expect inflation this year to average 5.0 percent, next year to be 4.2 percent, and in 2024, 3.3 percent. Our staff is reworking the numbers because we are getting new data. The 2023 forecasts are likely to be revised downward to below 4.0 percent. In short, barring large and unpleasant external shocks and domestic shocks arising from the weather in the Philippines, we are hoping that we will have a within-target inflation next year and very, very close to the midpoint of the target in 2024. The further you are away from the present, the bigger the standard deviation of the forecast. Given all the uncertainties that can happen, we, of course, cannot guarantee all of these, but these forecasts are the best we have, and we think there is a good chance that we will be able to [achieve] a target-consistent inflation path. All of these happened when we were already planning to normalize monetary policy. Let me summarize our actions during the pandemic: We cut the policy rate to 2.0 percent, which has never been done before. We lent money to the government so that despite the decline in revenue, they can continue spending enough to manage the pandemic. We bought a trillion pesos of government securities from the secondary market. Now that the economy is on its way to recovery, we were already planning to have an exit [strategy]. Initially, the plan was that the liftoff of interest rates will happen in the first month of the last quarter of this year. But what happened in the US changed our calendar. We are lucky… We have very adequate liquidity buffers. Adequate as they are, these are not enough to say we will resist responding to changes in US interest rate policy. The changes in US interest rates are just too large, and the dollar is such a large safe haven that those changes in monetary policy in the US will clearly affect the [peso-dollar] exchange rate. While we say that monetary policy does not target the exchange rate, it is also obvious that when you have very large changes in the exchange rate, that will add to inflation no matter how low the passthrough from exchange rate changes to inflation is. Our studies show that under normal times, the passthrough of a 1.0-percent change in the exchange rate is less than a 0.1-percent increase in the inflation rate. The problem is if you have a very large increase in exchange rate…then the passthrough will be much larger. Worst of all, when the changes in the exchange rate are very large, the relationship may no longer be linear. In other words, very large changes in the exchange rate could disanchor inflationary expectations. If you look at all the currencies in the chart, JPY depreciated by 16.0 percent, and SGD, which has a very strong peg, experienced [depreciation]. The Philippine peso depreciated by nearly 9.0 percent, but that number is a few days old, and the numbers now are much better. We say that the peso-dollar rate will continue to be flexible and market-determined. Changes in the exchange rate, from the point of view of an economist, when they are following fundamentals are actually a good thing. High economic growth will increase the demand for fuel. In turn, higher fuel prices will increase the amount of dollars you need to pay for the fuel. Those two together will affect the markets and increase demand for dollars, which is good because it signals the market to use less of these things. Consumers will become more careful in planning their trips. However, if changes in the exchange rate become so large, they may actually change not just relative prices, but they will also change inflationary expectations and price levels. When something like this happens, the central bank acts more aggressively. When there is too much appreciation, we end up accumulating foreign assets because we start buying. When I joined the Monetary Board, the international reserves was about US$45 billion; now, it is over a hundred billion dollars. Conversely, when there was intense pressure on the exchange rate, we either sell foreign currency or react if the pressure is too much. When the exchange rate changes are beginning to possibly disanchor inflationary expectations, we act more aggressively on the interest rate side also. Our gun has two barrels: one barrel is selling foreign exchange; the other is changing policy rates. Fortunately, this is a time when our banks are quite strong. All the numbers you look at [show] the banks are strong: non-performing loan (NPL) ratio remains manageable. Banks have also set up a lot of loan loss reserves for those loans. Secondly, banks are adequately capitalized—17.1 percent and 16.5 percent, depending on where you look at the balance sheet from a solo and consolidated bases. They remain high by both BSP standards and Basel. The BSP’s policy agenda on digitalization, on the other hand, will make the financial system more inclusive but also a lot more efficient. We have long recognized that digital platforms are needed. Listening to the talk of former Gov. Diokno, they will also improve tax collection and make payments of taxes a lot easier. We are proud to say that the impact [of our reforms] is quite high. The use of fast payment systems, like InstaPay, has skyrocketed. The share of digital transactions in total payments is now more than 30.0 percent. In addition, we have our goal of more and more adults owning transaction accounts. Technology has been very helpful here. E-money companies have also [helped] in increasing the number of people who have access to the financial system. As we increase the number of people with digital access, the number of people who use banking apps to pay and deposit, we have to increase consumer protection and improve the [defenses against] hacking. Going back to macroeconomic projections: The inflation forecast in 2023 is expected to shift downwards. The midpoint is less than 4.0 percent in the next round of numbers in the August meeting. On real GDP [gross domestic product] numbers, it is 8.3 percent for Q1. We are quite confident it will be between 6.5–7.0 percent this year and above 6.5 percent not just next year but also for the remaining years of this administration. The growing economy will mean a larger current account balance because capital goods are quite import-intensive. Quite a bit of production uses imported raw materials. The current account deficit stands at 5.0 percent of GDP, but it is currently very financeable. We actually have very large FDIs. On exports growth: 7.0 percent this year and 6.0 percent next year. On imports: Because of the high growth of the economy and the rise in oil and non-oil import prices, imports will rise by 18.0 percent [in 2022]. Because of the base effects and, hopefully, lower prices of imports next year, our forecast is a 6.0-percent growth in imports next year. Cash remittances continue to be robust; that is another very strong source of growth and stability in the forex market. I would like to add BPO [business process outsourcing] to that. Those are the things, together with our gross international reserves, that help ensure that you do not have changes in the exchange rate that can disanchor inflationary expectations. Now, I go to our policy direction. Some of our goals never change—or what we call our three pillars. What changes is the environment under which we operate. We are quite confident that our inflation rate will be moving in a manner that is consistent with our medium-term targets. My source of confidence is when I look at our history, every time we have years of high inflation, the following years have lower inflation. The [Philippine] central bank kn

Title: Effective Central Bank Independence: Essential Element to Economic Stability
Occasion: Management Association of the Philippines (MAP) 2nd Face-to-Face Economic Briefing
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Good afternoon, everyone. I would like to thank the Management Association of the Philippines (MAP) for the opportunity to share my insights. I have heard the presentation of my good friend [National Economic and Development Authority (NEDA) Secretary] Arci [Balisacan]. So, what I will share is the role of the central bank in all of these. These are the most important things to us: stable prices, as defined now as inflation between 2.0–4.0 percent; a stable and strong financial system; and a secure, safe, and efficient payment system. But why are these things important [to us]? The best example of this is our response to the pandemic: We bought a trillion pesos of bonds in the secondary market. By the way, one trillion is a lot because, at that time, our balance sheet was about ₱4 trillion. Of course, we also lent ₱600 billion to the government, interest-free. That stabilized the situation. In other words, the collapse of the economy by almost -9.0 percent [in 2020] could have been much worse. Of course, the question you ask is: where did we get the power to do that? The answer is our independence. During all those years, we never “printed” money to support the government. We bought a lot of foreign currency, mostly dollars. Because our independence was respected [by] government after government, as the economy needed more money, we produced the money by buying foreign assets. So, when the time came—when COVID came—we had more than enough resources to shore things up. In addition, because inflation was stable [then], the share of longer-term bonds in government debt was rising. This is the kind of environment we want: price stability so that fixed-rate loans are possible. We are very glad that the government and the Constitution itself created central bank independence and a law was written to make it independent. So far, in my entire 11 years on the Monetary Board, not once were we told by the government, “We like this interest rate. Keep this interest rate. Do this. Do that. Do not close that bank. Be nice to this bank.” This is the kind of environment we are in. That was, at the same time, accompanied by very good fiscal management. The man you are honoring, [former MAP member, the late] President [Fidel] Ramos said, “Oil price stabilization? Abolish that because it does not work in Philippine politics.” The entire idea of oil price stabilization is when international [oil] prices are rising, [the government would partially] subsidize it, and [it will] get the money to subsidize it when prices are falling. If you let politicians manage it [the fund], they will follow it only when the prices are rising. So, the subsidy will be unfunded. Ramos said, “The Philippines must be educated. Fuel prices are driven by international forces. The government has no business trying to stabilize it.” Then, [former President] Gloria Arroyo increased the value-added tax (VAT) from 10.0 to 12.0 percent and included fuel to VAT—a very unpopular decision. Later on, in the case of [former Department of Finance] Sec. [Carlos] Dominguez, he said “Let us cut personal income tax, raise fuel taxes, raise taxes on cigarettes, and raise taxes on sugary goods.” Our country, in other words, has a long history of good fiscal and monetary policy. The worst thing for a central banker is what they call fiscal dominance. You print more money than you should because you are saving the world. That is a very big dilemma. Your choice is letting it [the government] default or printing the money to save it. So, the second is the lesser of two evils. This is one [good] thing about the country. In spite of all the things we were quarreling about, we were able to agree. We were able to agree that the time called for a higher VAT and a VAT that would cover small things. We were quickly rewarded by capital markets [for this move]. When I was in NEDA, the spread between the 10-year dollar bond of the Philippine government and the 10-year dollar bond of the US government was 600 basis points (bps). The only difference was the borrower. Why were we paying 6.0 percent more? Because they thought we would default… After VAT, now, it is what? 100 bps? The [pain from] VAT alone is small compared to all the savings that came from [more affordable] loans. These [reforms] produced a fantastic period of [pre-pandemic] growth: 6.5-percent growth, 3.0 percent inflation, and falling poverty. We were also gifted by millions of Filipinos that attracted BPOs [business process outsourcing] and exported a lot of workers. The quality of the labor force and the excellent fiscal and monetary management produced a wonderful period. The question is: can we replicate this [after the pandemic]? Before I go to the topic, let me say that the banking system is strong: very well capitalized and they make provisions for losses. If a bank behaves badly, we tell them to raise capital. We have strong banks. You can see that in the recovery of lending growth. [Having] strong banks is good news. The rescue package we had for the government during the pandemic was so large that the IMF [International Monetary Fund] noticed and said, “Why is your balance sheet now ₱7 trillion from ₱4.5 trillion?” And they called it “unconventional.” We told them, “It is not unconventional because it is in our Charter.” But they said [in response], “It is unconventional because you have never done it before.” The key is: [if] the central bank, during good times, accumulates assets because the economy is growing, it can issue more money. Because of their faith in the currency, people want to hold the currency. These are the good things that are going for us. Of course, any rescue plan must have an exit. No central bank should ever rescue the economy and then ask, “Now that we have done this, how do we get out of this?” That is the big question in rich countries that have done a lot of QE [quantitative easing]. In our case, we said we will exit as the economy recovers. We will gradually lift the rates and then, we will let the securities that we bought mature gradually. But as they say, “The plans of mice and men often go awry.” The surprises came [in the form of the] US interest rate [liftoff]. The question is, what is next?
