When you have that, of course, the exit has to be more rapid. We are exiting much faster. The government already paid us back their advances. We are allowing the one trillion [in government securities] to run off our balance sheet. Maybe in the next two years, ₱200 billion of that will mature. And then, our interest rate increases were a lot harder [larger] than expected. Nonetheless, I am quite optimistic that the economy remains strong and can actually absorb the rate increases. Of course, whether the last one, yesterday’s 50 bps [hike] is the last one, nobody knows. The answer is: we will look at the data. This is our fan chart before the [August policy] meeting. Inflation will fall to 4.0 percent [in 2023]. The only reason August inflation is 5.4 percent is [because] we are averaging, and the first three months were low. So, 5.4 percent is the average of low numbers from January–March and the high numbers from the last three, four months. Some people are saying this will [still] rise because it has not peaked yet. My personal forecast is that the peak will come by September–October. And after that, as global prices start to fall and partly because people are expecting a US recession, then we will have lower inflation. In techno-speak, we are moving to a target-consistent inflation path. Most central banks would say, “Be as vague as you want.” But that was the old philosophy before [former US Federal Reserve (US Fed) Chair Ben] Bernanke. Then, when more and more academics went into central banking, he said, “We will tell you exactly what we think is going to happen and that is what we are going to do.” Of course, that is called forward guidance. It is no longer going to be 4.0 percent [average inflation in 2023] because we hiked by 50 bps [in our latest move]. The revisions downward are partly because we expect commodity prices to start easing but also because we expect our policy rate hikes to eventually bite. It takes 6–12 months before it [rate hike] takes effect. There is nothing we can do about the current high inflation from a monetary standpoint. The thing to do [to reduce inflation] is not to let bureaucrats and politicians know when and how much to import. Get the bureaucrats and the politicians out of import decisions. The best way to do that is if you want high protection, impose high tariffs. If you want low protection, impose low tariffs. The debate should be on how high the tariffs should be. It [the current setup] creates a lot of room for rent-seeking [because] he [a person or a small group of persons] gets permits to import instead of the government getting the difference between the Philippine price and foreign price. [What is] worse [is] if we give quotas to few people who connive. The best lesson on how to do it was when Sec. Dominguez tariff-ied rice. This is one of the reasons we raised rates. The red bar is the proportion of items below 2.0 percent [inflation]; you can see how quickly it is shrinking when it used to be the predominant item. Some items are resistant to demand pressures, but you notice that those items are getting fewer. On the other hand, the items in purple, which is above [the 4.0 percent] target, are growing. That means inflation is beginning to creep throughout the system and we had to act. The inflation you are seeing now compared to the inflation you were seeing before is the reaction of markets to the high imported inflation, or second-order effects, which we have to stop. We are beginning to see these as well [in the form of] minimum wage adjustments and transport fares. The other way to look at it is the comparison of the weighted median (grey line), which removes all the volatile items that are going up a lot, and the headline inflation

Title: Sustaining the Philippine Economic Growth Story: The Role of the Central Bank in a Challenging Global Environment
Occasion: Federation of Filipino-Chinese Chambers of Commerce and Industry, Inc. (FFCCCI) Forum
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Good afternoon. To all the members of the Federation, especially your president, Dr. Henry [Lim Bon Liong], I very much welcome this opportunity to be able to talk to you and make a short presentation. The Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) appreciates these opportunities that allow us: First, to share our perspectives on the economy and our outlook and the central bank’s responses to these developments; and Second, to listen to the concerns and issues of those most affected by our policies. We have always believed that the BSP will be able to do a better job if the business community knows that we appreciate your point of view and you also understand why we do what we do. Let me first take us back to pre-pandemic time. At that time, the economy was humming along very nicely. On average, GDP [gross domestic product] grew by 6.5 percent for a period of 10 years before the pandemic. Moreover, it uplifted the lives of many Filipinos: poverty was reduced not just as the economy was growing but [also because] the government was improving tax collection and spent some of the money on pro-poor and pro-growth platforms. As you can see on the chart, the pandemic put a very rapid end to this beautiful period of growth. [In] Q2 2020, [there] was actually a 16.9-percent contraction. In Q1 2020, it [the economy] only shrank 0.7 percent, but the reason [why the contraction was not larger] is obvious: the lockdowns only started in the first two weeks of Q1. Two bad weeks outweighing 10 good weeks—that means that the last two weeks were even worse. If you calculate GDP weekly, the last two weeks of Q1 2020 were the worst. Fortunately, the decline of the economy became less and less. The explanation was simple: we learned to live with the virus. The lockdowns become more sensible, and more people got vaccinated. Now, we know better. There is a very large recovery starting from 2021, but do not be deceived by the numbers. The 12.1 percent [growth in Q2 2021] may look large, but it is 12.1 percent of something that is 10.0 percent smaller. The 12.1 percent [growth] is much, much smaller than the 11.6-percent contraction in Q3 2020. Fortunately, the strength continued. Despite the -3.8 percent [contraction] in Q1 2021, the overall growth in 2021 was 5.7 percent. The signs are very good because Q1 2022 was an expansion of 8.2 percent. If this [pace of growth] proceeds, we should have 7.0 percent annually in 2022. Let me explain what the BSP has done that has partially fueled this bounce back: We brought the policy rate to a record low of 2.0 percent. Considering that target inflation is 2.0–4.0 percent, it is actually lower than the lower of the midpoint of our inflation target; We also bought one trillion pesos in government securities; and [We] extended ₱600 billion in provisional advances to the National Government, interest-free. We knew all along that it would work, but we also knew that we had to exit from these very supportive monetary policies. The [exit] program aims to reverse a lot of these things: raise the policy rate, the government pays back its advances, and as the securities we bought matured, we will not buy new ones. The one trillion pesos [in government securities that we purchased] will gradually decline. That was the initial picture—a very gradual exit from ultra-accommodative monetary policies. Unfortunately, as Robert Burns said, “The best laid plans of mice and men often go awry.” That is exactly what happened. What were the surprises? The biggest surprise is that America was initially saying that inflation was low and transitory. We know now exactly the opposite happened. Naturally, the US Federal Reserve (US Fed) reacted very strongly. In addition, we had the Ukraine war, which caused the prices of oil and non-oil imports to go up significantly. The most important non-oil imports are fertilizer and flour. Because of US monetary policy, the dollar became king again. Everyone wants to go back to the dollar because it is the safe haven. If the interest on peso assets is the same as interest rates on US assets, the choice is clear: go for the US assets. This has resulted in many currencies, not just the peso, to depreciate. To sum up: we had an initial exit plan that said maybe we will start exiting in the last quarter of this year. Instead, we had to exit sooner. The [initial] exit would be gradual, maybe ​25 basis points (bps). But, as you know, we did two 25s in an unscheduled move, then we increased it by 75 bps—which is very unusual and very high for most central banks, especially ours. The first thing that I would like to tell you is that it worked. The peso stabilized. The intended effect of the increase in interest rate is to slay inflation, and one of the ways to do that is to stop the deprecation of the peso. Higher interest rates also reduce inflation because it will reduce demand. People are willing to buy less if it is costly to borrow. The unintended effect that we are afraid of is that the recovery I just described will be nipped in the bud. We call it a nascent economic recovery. Fortunately, our numbers show that these [rate] increases that we have done so far and maybe even what we plan on doing later will not prevent the economy from recovering. This graph shows that the lowest line—policy rate—is rising but is lower than the two lines above—the 12-month moving average and actual month-on-month headline inflation. Therefore, interest rates may have risen but they are lower than the increase in inflation. In other words, what we are doing is we are not going to kill off the recovery. As the economy recovers, imports are also recovering. But on top of that, prices are recovering. The current account deficit has been adjusted significantly. The forecast was adjusted by more than US$3 billion for 2023. When you combine that with the increases in 2022, that is a total demand for US$6 billion. Normally, the central bank will not respond to changes in the exchange rate. After all, they do signify healthy adjustments to what is going on. The market works because it makes scarce things more expensive. Therefore, people will have the incentive to use it carefully and try to produce more of it. Similarly, the things that are in excess supply, their price will go down. So, people will want to consume more of it and produce less of it and move their resources to products that have become more expensive. It changes relative prices by encouraging the production of things that are becoming more expensive and discouraging consumption of such things. Unfortunately, foreign exchange is not the usual good. What we found in the BSP is if the exchange rate is moving very fast, if the changes are small and can go in either direction, the changes in the price of the dollar do not affect the way people price their goods. But if the changes in the exchange rate are large and sure to go in one direction, it becomes another story. They [producers] begin to relate that maybe if the exchange rate is moving at 10.0 percent, [then] maybe we should raise our price depending on the import content [of our products]. This table shows the policy rate response of the BSP and the US Fed. In our case, we had two 25 bps and 75 bps, or [a cumulative] 125 bps. The US, on the other hand, raised by 225 bps [cumulatively]. As you can see, the gap between our respective policy rates has become narrower, plus the expectation that demand for the dollar will rise because of the current account, means that people will be buying their dollar needs ahead of time. The pivot of the US Fed from being unconcerned about inflation to being very concerned has resulted in large changes in US monetary policy. We cannot ignore that, but we do not have to match them one for one. We did 125 bps, and they did 225 bps. We have increased it [the policy rate] much less than them. My point is: we have a balancing act. We have to respond to what is going on outside the Philippines, but at the same time, we have to be very concerned that what we do does not kill the recovery that is just going on. What are we worried about? We are worried that all these that are happening will change the dynamic of prices. The most difficult situation: prices are rising because prices are rising. That is what we want to avoid. For instance, there are signs of adjustment in the minimum wage and transport fares. In sum, the BSP had to move sooner, faster, and bolder to avoid disanchoring inflation expectations. In plain terms, we want the people to believe in our inflation target rather than [use] the news of the changes in the exchange rate [as their basis] for making inflation forecasts. Of course, we are ready to take more action, if necessary, to make sure our inflation targets are credible. We might miss it because global forces are just too strong, but as soon as these global forces taper off, it will [normalize.] and not create a wage-price spiral. I think our policies thus far, judging by the stabilization of the exchange rate after our 75-bps policy hike, seem to be working. But as I said, global forces are so strong that they could overwhelm the effects of our interest rate hikes. In 2022, we will probably get an average inflation [rate] of 5.0 percent. Inflation next year will be 4.2 percent, but we are quite confident that in 2024, it will be below 4.0 percent. This is another way to look at it. The chart shows several variables: which country sold more reserves, and which increased its policy rate the most? Then, on the first column, which currency depreciated the most? Excluding Euro and Japan, the Philippines is second to [South] Korea in depreciation, tied with Korea in the increase in policy rate, and second to Thailand in terms of selling reserves. In short, the BSP is coping with what is happening by: Saying “Imports should be more expensive so let the exchange rate move”; But at the same time, we do not want too much of that [depreciation] so we raise the p

Title: Toward Sustainable Banking
Occasion: Bankers Institute of the Philippines (BAIPHIL) Induction Ceremony
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ To the newly inducted officers and members of the Bankers Institute of the Philippines (BAIPHIL), all the past officers, advisers, and members, it is my pleasure to be here this afternoon. It is also my pleasure to be able to communicate to you what the central bank is doing. When I heard your hymn, there was a very good phrase, “Serve your bank and the country.” It has two possible meanings: you serve your bank nine-to-five, and the rest of the time, you serve the country. But that is probably not the meaning of the phrase. For me, a very important interpretation [of the lyric] is that by serving your bank and making your bank better, you make the country better. It is not always the case that that coincidence of objectives maps together so well. So, I ask myself, why is it that the first meaning is the more common one? I serve my company, and on the other side of my life, I serve the country, my family, and my town. Why is it so different from banks? Then, I said to myself, that is exactly how I felt about being a faculty member of the University of the Philippines (UP). When I looked at my students, later on, I realized that no matter how I screwed up, they turned out well. I did not damage them enough, therefore, I served the country. Back when I was a young faculty member, I was a killer. I would flunk one-third of my class because I was too idealistic, and I did not see life from the point of view of the student. I did not realize there are other things they enjoyed doing other than studying. So, when I had four kids who went to college and went to UP, I realized that kawawa pala yung mga estudyante sa ginagawa ko [the students are unfortunate because of what I am doing]. Over time, I became more and more mellow and began to see things from the point of view of my children. I see that when I was a professor at UP. I also see that when I serve the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP). As I do my best at my job in the BSP, I am serving the country. So, what is it that makes the work for banks not the same? The interest, of course, is public interest. Bankers, because about 80.0 percent of the money you lend does not belong to shareholders, that is why you are very important. When you fail, so many people suffer. Anybody that is in government should also think that way: the best way to serve the country is to do your best for your agency. Your theme “Innovate! Train! Certify!”—these are very important things: bringing the best out of any organization. If you do not innovate, train, and certify, many things will not improve. So, I hope that you will succeed in realizing the objectives that bring about innovation, training, and certification. Our partnership with BAIPHIL goes many years back. Three years ago, we had a partnership on the Clean Note and Coin Policy. You helped us connect with transport groups, retailers, vendors, local governments, and other stakeholders. With your help, we were able to reduce the amount of unfit currency that remains in circulation. Indeed, I would like feedback from you on how to make this even better. For instance, when I look at the distribution of notes or paper currency by volume, the 1000-Piso represents an amazing 34.8 percent. In terms of value, the share of 1000-Piso is even bigger. On the other hand, the share of the 100-Piso, in terms of volume, is 20.5 percent. And yet, when you look at the average transactions that I personally make, I am more likely to use ₱100 than ₱1000. That is because I have credit cards. If it is a relatively large transaction, I will not be using the 1000-Piso bill or pay using QR. The question that comes to mind is since it [₱100] is relatively small in volume compared to ₱1000, the only conclusion is that the ₱100 makes up for its small value by having a faster “velocity,” meaning it changes hands more frequently. Anong ibig sabihin noon? Madali siyang magiging unfit. [What does that mean? It will quickly become unfit.] But then, it usually circulates in [informal] sectors that do not [often] use the banking system and, therefore, is unlikely to come back to us [at the central bank] for replacement. So, mas pudpod yung ₱100 kaysa ₱1000. [So, ₱100 is more worn out than ₱1000.] So, we are thinking, what can we do about that? Perhaps we can encourage the malls to encourage consumers to surrender their unfit ₱100 and, for every so many pieces of unfit ₱100 that one returns, one could get a raffle ticket. The other solution is to maybe replace it with a coin, but that may increase the likelihood of the bill being kept away in a jar. Maybe we could also test the polymer on the 100-Piso bill. This is just an idea. At any rate, we appeal to you that if ever, in the future, we decide to introduce the 100-Piso [polymer] as a test circulation, we will get your support and your feedback. We hope that in all our future endeavors and activities that make the payment system better, you will be there for us and give us feedback. At this point, let me give you a brief overview on our banking system. If I were to summarize this entire graph in one sentence: banks are growing without sacrificing the stability and safety of depositors. All those statistics: growing deposits, growing loans and yet very high capital adequacy, and more than sufficient liquidity. Having said that, you do face risks, and some of the risks are new; some of the risks are old. The newest, of course, is cybersecurity risk. Of course, the changing natural environment may also make some loans risky. All of those things are the ones that we have to think about to the extent to which the changing climate and temperature of the earth can actually affect your default rates. In addition, you face very old risks. This is where my field [macroeconomics] is very much short. Many economic textbooks ignore default or assume default can easily be calculated and, therefore, can be easily priced in. The typical macroeconomic textbook does not even have a section on the banks. The entire banking system became a little black box. That is what I learned when I went out to the real world: madaming hindi nagbabayad ng utang [a lot do not pay their debts]. That is the problem that the typical bank has to solve, plus interest rates, bond prices, and asset prices can fluctuate and hurt you. So, our message is: we hope that you are able to manage those risks. Of course, we will continue to monitor you. Thus far, we like what we see. Non-performing loans, loans that are behind, [are sufficiently covered] and to that, we say, “Well, maybe we learned a lot from the Asian Financial Crisis.” In addition, to address cybersecurity risk, the BSP has intensified cyber-surveillance efforts. Related to this, of course, is what happens when a depositor is complaining that his loss is due to the fault of the bank. He probably would need consumer protection and adjudication [services] as well, and these are the things that we are looking to improve. As I already stated, we recognize the importance of factoring in environment into your [risk] analysis because, in the future, it could become a major source of risk. For instance, the reason we will have to move to renewable energy is if you build a coal plant now, nobody will insure you because of the global action against coal-fired plants. The BSP will continue to build on its existing regulatory and supervisory frameworks. We will nurture the growth of digital innovations. In that sense, the pandemic was a major factor in speeding it [payments digitalization] up. We realize it is a very, very important path, especially since we found that electronic wallets (e-wallets) helped in [expanding] financial inclusion. The banks are just too far; [maintaining] branches are just too expensive. Digital will be very important for both cost-cutting and extending the reach of banks. If you will allow me, our kasambahay [house helper] who helped raise the children got married, but my wife keeps in touch. Because of the lockdowns, gutom na gutom na sila at hindi makapag-trabaho, pero ‘di pa dumadating ‘yung ayuda. Tinanong niya [Mrs. Medalla], “Paano kami makakapagpadala ng pera sa iyo?” Wala kasi siyang Gcash, Paymaya, o bank account. You know what saved the day? ‘Yung sari-sari store sa tabi niya, merong [e-wallet account]. Doon namin pinadala yung pera. [Because of the lockdowns, they were very hungry and unable to work, but the financial support from the government has not yet arrived. Mrs. Medalla asked, “How are we going to send you money?” Our helper does not have a Gcash, Paymaya, or bank account. You know what saved the day? The neighborhood sundry store beside their house; It has an e-wallet account. That is where we sent the money.] These are little things that get lost when you are too focused on macro. The BSP cares about [individual] welfare—whether they are represented in the largest of numbers or the smallest of numbers. We are not just a macro central bank; we are interested in the lives of the people. And what we have seen, thus far, is that these new ways of transferring money are actually, in some cases, life-saving, as with the case I gave you. We want you to be our partner in improving financial literacy. For some reason, we score lower than our neighbors [in knowledge and application of economic and financial concepts]. I have a few theories about that: the problem in the Philippines, as you learn concepts, you learn them with the language that it is used to teach in. So, if you are not good at English, you will not learn the thing that is being taught. This is an untested hypothesis of mine; I could be very well wrong. Before I end, let me describe what has happened [on the macroeconomic front]: we have the biggest shock to the economy after WWII [World War II]. In no time did the economy shrink by more than 10.0 percent in one year. Fortunately, that [contraction] did not continue. As we learned [how to live with the virus] and became vaccinated, the cost to th

Title: Towards the Path to Recovery
Occasion: Rural Bankers Association of the Philippines (RBAP) Meeting
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ When I was growing up in Lipa City, there was only one bank in Lipa and that was a rural bank—that is the Malarayat Rural Bank. They are still around. Eventually, PNB [Philippine National Bank] came. Only much, much later did we see commercial banks. So, my point is, you have PNB but that was there upon government orders. No particular individual really chose Lipa. On the other hand, the commercial banks came when Lipa was already a large and successful city—when it was possible to send mail and just give the street without the number, and the mail will still reach you. My point is: rural banks were there long before the city was progressing. That is why I have always said to myself: I may have studied macroeconomics. I may have studied what people call price theory, which is how the price system, by equating demand and supply, is actually allocating resources efficiently. Those are very abstract concepts. The tendency is to look at large numbers or summaries: total assets, total loans, or some not-very-good statistics, like non-performing loans, but never looking at the detail. I am glad to tell you all my friends here that, in the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP), we may be good at the macro, but we will never forget the micro. We will never say, “Oh, this sector accounts only for 3.0 percent of total assets,” and a very simple reason for that, [which] I already described, [is] the days when I was growing up. These numbers that are aggregated hide a lot of important details. For instance, if you look closely at commercial banks, the branches are more deposit-gatherers than loan-granters and decision-makers. If a loan is big, it has to be decided at the head office. When you look at the fund flows, literally, the commercial banking system is gathering savings from all over the country to finance the government—[via their purchases of Treasury bills]—and, of course, to finance really big undertakings. This is not bad. What we learned from Japan and Korea is that largeness, if it is dynamic, can be an economic miracle. So, when you see all those big stories of success, conglomerates, of course, that is very important for global competitiveness and that is one reason, I guess, that Japan and Korea are very successful: their zaibatsus and chaebols conquered the world. But there is another side to human progress which is what is happening at the individual level, what is happening to individual places. My hometown of Lipa, at that time, was a small place but, clearly, towns like that did matter. So, for that reason, the rural banks are very important to us. Of course, you also have to have some form of standards and goals. Sometimes, certain things do not pass, and certain things pass. I am glad to hear from your presentation that you have a PCA [Prompt Corrective Action] Academy. [I am] glad to know that you are actually saying, “Maybe we can learn from each other.” There has to be a way of going around so that banks can learn from each other. Maybe a way of doing [that is] case writing, maybe changing the numbers a little bit. The success stories are being documented and the failures, too, are being documented. So, I think of the role of doing something about the third generation because it is a very common failure. A very well-run bank that has been around for 30, 40 years is in trouble because the grandfather was passionate about the bank and so was the son, and the son actually learned from the father. However, the third generation is a bit spoiled and no longer interested in [running] the bank. One solution is learning how to transition from being a family-run bank to being a professionally-run bank. But I suppose that is not easy, because we see that transition from the family-run, taipan-centered [organizations], and you can see that the transition is very disorderly. The Macroeconomy & BSP’s Policy Actions to Maintain Price Stability Let me start with the macroeconomy. If you make this graph longer in the left direction, you will see very tall positive bars—more than 10 years of very high growth averaging 6.5 percent [before the pandemic]. Then, a big crash [in 2020]. The pandemic ended more than 10 years of very high growth. The economy shrank by almost 10.0 percent [in 2020]. The economy has started to recover, and we take a little bit of credit for that. We saw how bad the pandemic was to the economy and decided to take very aggressive action: We cut the policy rate to a record low of 2.0 percent. Not only that, we also noticed that people were panicking. People were fleeing to cash. This is one lesson from history: when people do not know what is going on, they flee to cash. Economic theory says that if the people are panicking, the pandemic will actually make interest rates fall because it will reduce demand for CaPex [capital expenditure]. The motive to save is even stronger. More savings, less CaPex = low interest rates. But exactly the opposite happened. The lesson is that markets can go crazy. The central bank is not just the lender of last resort; it is the market-maker of last resort. The central bank starts buying government securities; we bought a trillion pesos worth. On top of that, we lent ₱600 billion, interest-free, to the government. That calmed the market and interest rates went in the right direction. Low CaPex should mean low interest rates; that is exactly what happened. If we had not intervened, then markets would have gone crazy, and the exact opposite should have happened. ​ But we know that what we did is something not normally done—things that you do only under abnormal times. The IMF [International Monetary Fund] called it an “unconventional” monetary policy. If this continues for too long, this could sow the seeds of a bubble or may threaten financial stability. So, we must have an exit plan. When you have a speeding car, you do not suddenly step on the brakes; you decline gradually to a nice stop. The passenger who is sleeping should not be woken up. But of course, that is not what happened—big, big surprises. Even Nobel Prize economists were totally wrong about US inflation. They were saying, US inflation is low and transitory. What you are seeing instead is high and somewhat persistent. What does the US Federal Reserve do when that happens? It tries to regain credibility by having unusually large and frequent interest rate changes. What happens when the global currency has higher interest rates? That global currency will actually be the safe haven because these are disturbed times. By itself, money will be moving to the safe haven. Tapos, mataas pa ‘yung interest ng safe haven. [Then, the interest rate of the safe haven is high.] So, you have falling currencies. That is why I keep saying it is a strong dollar, not a weak peso. The problem is mahal pa rin ang imports. [The problem is that imports are still expensive.] On top of that, even if the exchange rate did not change, mahal pa rin [it is still expensive]. We are calculating that not only did imports go up by almost 50.0 percent, of course, oil went up even more. If you add 50.0 percent plus a 10.0-percent deprecation of currency, that is quite a shock for many products. Fortunately, many other prices are not responding. Any central banker is afraid that as the other products respond too, you will have a spiral. Prices are high because prices are high. Wages are high. Prices are high. Before you know it, you have inflation out of control. Even if it is not popular, we will do what we have to do so that higher inflation does not beget higher inflation. The last thing you want is prices are high because prices are high. That will be a never-ending spiral. From the beginning, we were thinking of exiting [from these policies]. But because of high prices, the Ukraine war, and the sudden and unpredicted change in US monetary policy, we were afraid of what we called second-order effects. Of course, you do not see it everywhere. My favorite example of how rising imported prices does not affect local prices is that the cost of a haircut remains the same. The point is you still see a lot of products that are not responding. We look at that by taking out the outliers and using statistical methods, we take out the things that are rising or falling too much [to arrive at] core inflation. There are two measures of inflation: this month vs. 12 months ago. July 2022 vs. July 2021 is 6.4 percent, whereas June 2022 vs. June 2021 is 6.1 [percent], whereas January 2022 vs. January 2021 is below 4.0 percent. You can see the acceleration. So, our response, therefore, is, “This situation is different from what we initially expected. We did not anticipate the Ukraine war, that the US will experience this kind of inflation.” Therefore, our response was two 25 bps. Initially, we were sticking to the gradualist story. But then, the peso was falling rapidly. Normally, we do not react to the exchange rate because it is [small exchange rate fluctuations] actually a healthy thing. What that means is that high import prices are causing a dollar shortage. And what is the best way to reduce a shortage? Make the things scarcer and pricier, therefore less people will buy. The market works because it changes what we call relative prices. The imported goods become more expensive compared to domestic goods. Hopefully, the reaction of people is to shift away from imported goods, which is healthy. The problem is the moment inflation has second-order effects; it affects not just the ratio, but all the levels. In other words, changing prices that do not include the efficiency of the economy for allocation only causes inflation. That is why we had to act. You can see here that the Philippine peso depreciated, but there are currencies that depreciated even more. You can now look at the summary and see that in terms of depreciation, we are second to South Korea, taking out Europe and Japan. We are also the same as South Korea in terms of increase in policy ra

Title: Onto a Sustainable Growth Path: The BSP’s Role in Helping the Philippine Economy Navigate a Challenging Environment
Occasion: HSBC Global Emerging Markets Forum
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Good morning, everyone. Let me start by thanking HSBC for inviting me to speak at today’s Global Emerging Markets Forum. We, at the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP), have always been ready and glad that we can have this opportunity to converse with you so that you can understand us better and understand why we are doing what we are doing. I always say that our three pillars are constant: Price stability: inflation that is both low and predictable. It is hard to overemphasize [the importance of] that. There are so many [difficult] possibilities when there is a long-run increase in prices. For instance, it would be very hard to issue fixed-rate bonds. If the bond buyer thinks that inflation will be 20.0 percent, it is very hard to sell him a bond that yields only 6.0 percent. I remember a time when the T-bills [Treasury bills] accounted for more than 60.0 percent of government bond issuances. Now, fast forward after inflation-targeting [regime of the central bank], the share of T-bills in total bond issuance is, I think, down to less than 10.0 percent. In other words, the government is able to borrow long-term and fixed-rate because people think that the inflation target of the central bank is likely to be achieved or if it is not achieved—there will be years where we exceed or [fall] below [target]—the midpoint of the inflation target is a good average over the long run. Financial stability is also important. We know from past history that when banks become weak, they become shy about lending, and when that happens, you get years…of low growth. In addition, we like to look at environmental sustainability, not necessarily just for the country but for the banks to be very conscious of the risks involved that they must factor in. Our last pillar is an efficient payments and settlements system. Now, because of fintech [financial technology], it is now quite an important topic as well. So, our three pillars are constant; what changes are the global conditions and, in the case of the payment and settlements system, the technology. If you use a ship as an analogy, the ship and what makes the ship strong remain the same. But, sometimes, the currents, waves, and winds change. As they change, piloting the ship will, of course, change. Of course, we were hard-hit by the pandemic, but we are now recovering very strongly—7.8-percent growth for H1 2022. What got us there, in part, was the very accommodative monetary policy by the central bank: For instance, we cut the policy rate to a record low of 2.0 percent. We bought one trillion pesos worth—this is unprecedented—of government bonds in the secondary market. In addition, we lent more than ₱600 billion to the government, interest-free. ​​ To give you a sense of proportion, at that time, the balance sheet of the central bank was ₱4.5 trillion. In other words, the central bank expanded its balance sheet by a third to help the economy. Now, like any measure to get the economy out of a deep slump like the pandemic, we, of course—from the very beginning—had an exit plan. We thought we would be exiting gradually; as the economy grew, we would make our exit plan. The plan was a gradual liftoff of rates and a gradual runoff of assets as the government securities that we held matured. But to be very frank, the exit plan became obsolete very quickly. As they say, “The plans of mice and men often go awry.” What were the unexpected developments? High commodity prices, slow global growth from the Ukraine-Russia conflict, and, of course, the pivot of the US Fed [US Federal Reserve]—from an attitude that viewed inflation as low and transitory to [viewing it as] high and persistent, which resulted in a much more aggressive tightening by the US Fed. As a result, the BSP had to take bigger and bolder actions: From two 25 basis points (bps) each [hike in the policy rate] in May and June when we still thought that the exit should be a gradual one, we followed it up with a surprise off-cycle 75 bps [hike], a follow-through 50 bps [hike], and then another 50 bps [hike] just last week. So, we raised our policy rate from 2.0 percent to 4.25 percent in just five months. The National Government also paid back its debt. And we scaled down our securities holdings. Now, let me go back to our most important pillar: price stability. As you know, we will probably exceed the 2.0–4.0 percent [inflation] target by quite a bit this year. The average [inflation] for this year will be 5.6 percent, way above [the upper band of our target] 4.0 percent. The question there is: are our policy responses working? We think they are. For instance, although by H1 2023, it [inflation] will still be quite high; by Q3 or Q4 2023, inflation will be closer to 3.0 percent than to 4.0 percent. As you can see in our forecasts, we expect inflation to be 4.1 percent in 2023 and 3.0 percent in 2024. This is what we call a target-consistent inflation path. The other way to look at it is that much of the inflation is due to supply-side [pressures], and these supply-side [pressures] are largely in turn due to external shocks. The impetus to inflation about which policy rates do not have very much effect. To begin with, changes in policy rates take 6–18 months before they work. Second, they are meant to reduce [or stimulate] demand and cannot address supply shocks. So, why did we raise rates? What we are trying to prevent is these supply shocks will create so much momentum that the following years will also be high-inflation years. Of course, our forecasts can be wrong, but if our forecasts are right, then what we have done is to prevent this high momentum of inflation—of 5.6 percent in 2022—from being carried over to the next two years. In other words, what we do not want is that inflation is high because inflation is high. What we do not want is a wage-price spiral. The worst case is prices are high and changes in the exchange rate are large, [so] prices are high. These are the things we are trying to prevent. So far, we think we have done what is necessary. The reason I say “so far” is there may be more coming from the US Fed. The other way to look at this is to look at core inflation. Core inflation used to be below 4.0 percent; now, it is higher than 4.0 percent. The right side of the chart will give the same picture. The share of core inflation to total inflation is rising. Therefore, we had to act. I think we acted on time. Although core inflation has now exceeded 4.0 percent, this has started to go down, and this is why we are forecasting a target-consistent path where much of the effect [of our rate hikes] will be felt in Q3 or Q4 2023 and 2024. Inflation, if left unchecked, could result in inflation gaining even more momentum. Higher prices beget higher prices, or what we call second-order effects. Our policy rate changes are not meant to address supply shocks, because supply shocks clearly cannot be addressed by reducing demand but [are aimed at] addressing second-order effects. We already see wage and transport hike petitions happening. The good sign is that they are not in excess of what we think will happen. You will see that in comparison to the United States, which has done 300 bps [in cumulative rate adjustments] since March, we at the BSP, since May, did 225 bps. If you were to compare what we have done with other Asian countries, we did the biggest policy rate changes, [followed by] Korea at 150 bps and India at 140 bps. The adjustment that we are doing has three components: Allow the currency to adjust or respond to the exchange rate and be the natural shock absorber. If import prices are rising, dollars will be scarce. If dollars are scarce, the exchange rate will go up, and the correct response is… Imported goods will become more expensive so people will buy less. But we cannot rely completely on this response because too much of that [depreciation] could disanchor inflationary expectations. We are also doing changes in the policy rate largely because the smaller [interest] difference with the US Fed is; the more attractive the US dollar becomes against the peso. Normally, we do not pay much attention to the exchange rate, but when you have very large changes in the exchange rate, even if the pass-through is only 10.0 percent, if the currency has depreciated 12.0 percent, the pass-through will be larger. The other thing that we did was sell some reserves and participated in the forex [foreign exchange] market. As you can see, only Thailand has a percentage of reserves [sold] more than us. So, clearly, we now have to deploy all these three measures almost to the fullest. I say “almost” because we still have room to absorb and use these tools depending on what happens and what the US Fed does. You may ask: why is Indonesia not doing as much? The price changes are actually favorable [to them] since they export coal, which we [the Philippines] import. On the other hand, why is Thailand only doing 25 bps, whereas the Philippines did 225 bps [in terms of cumulative policy rate adjustments]? First, the Philippine economy has recovered more than Thailand. Second, Thailand has much bigger forex reserves than we do. But relying completely on these three tools is not enough. When much of the inflation is due to expensive food, the easiest thing to do…is reduce trade restrictions. The best example is sugar. When the sugar shortage threatens the food processing industries, the correct response, in the long run, is to improve [agricultural] productivity, but in the short run, import more. This is what we are hoping the government can do to address supply-side pressures, which, in effect, reduce the momentum of current inflation dynamics. As I already said, as an inflation-targeting central bank, we view adjustments in the exchange rate as healthy since they send the right signals. However, during times like these when there is a very strong dollar and most of our imports are dollar-denominated, the exchange rate becomes a

Title: Enabling Sustainable Growth: The BSP’s Blueprint Toward a Post-COVID Economy
Occasion: Citi “Perspectives” Forum
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Good afternoon to everyone. Thank you, Citi, for inviting me to your event. Let me begin by giving you the macroeconomic picture and close with some key messages. I will start by saying that we take our three pillars very seriously. Those three pillars are price stability, which is operationalized in our inflation targeting [framework]; financial stability, a big part of which is we closely watch the capitalization of banks. They must have sufficient capital relative to the risks that they take on—whether they took it on voluntarily or accidentally. Of course, [our third pillar that has become] very important recently is the efficient payment and settlements system. The key to this is digitalization and more and more cashless, cash-lite payment systems. We do care about economic growth although it is not explicitly a part of any one of the three pillars. We believe that when we deliver on these three pillars, that is the best way we can contribute to economic growth if one takes a longer view. Over the past 10 years, we did very well until COVID came. We had both high and inclusive growth. Growth was more than 6.0 percent, and poverty incidence was falling. COVID, however, put an end to this period of strong and inclusive growth. Fortunately, in our view, I think due partially to the extraordinary measures we [at the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP)] have taken, we are seeing a nascent and, most likely, durable recovery. The economy grew by 5.7 percent last year, and we expect it to grow by at least 6.5 [percent in 2022] as shown in [projections of the] DBCC [Development Budget Coordination Committee], where the BSP is a resource institution. What brought about this nascent and strong recovery? There were hardly any lockdowns, unlike at the start of the pandemic when the lockdowns were so crippling that the economy contracted by nearly 17.0 percent in Q2 2020. Our reaction to this was: To bring down the policy rate to a record low of 2.0 percent. Knowing full well that inflation will be between 2.0–4.0 percent, we had a policy rate of 2.0 percent. We also lent ₱600 billion, interest-free, to the National Government. To their credit, they have paid this back already. Then, we purchased one trillion pesos of government securities in the secondary market. To give you a sense of proportion, at that time, the balance sheet of the BSP was ₱4.5 trillion. So, effectively lending ₱600 billion and [buying] one trillion pesos in government securities are increasing the balance sheet by more than a third. Naturally, in the absence of lockdowns, this should stimulate the economy, especially in light of the fact that there is so much pent-up demand. From the very beginning, we were already preparing for an exit. The exit that we planned was going to be a gradual increase in policy rates—no 50s, no 75s—and that it would start [by] Q3. Unfortunately, as a Scottish poet once said, “The best laid plans of mice and men often go awry.” What happened? First, there was the Ukraine-Russia conflict, which resulted in very high oil and non-oil imports. In addition, there was what was largely an unanticipated pivot in US monetary policy. With very large changes in US interest rates and the dollar as a safe haven, naturally, a smaller differential between peso policy rates and US policy rates will make the dollar much more attractive. ​ There was a sort of double whammy: the higher growth of the economy relative to trading partners means our imports will grow faster than exports; second, higher prices of imports would increase demand for dollars. Our estimate is that two-thirds of the increase in the current account deficit was due to higher [import] prices and only a third is due to growth. The large increases [in the US Federal Reserve’s (US Fed) rate hikes] meant there was quite a bit of pressure on the peso to depreciate compared to other countries that were having fewer problems with the increase in import prices. We import nearly all of our oil and import quite a bit of our food. The impact on the demand for dollars increases [is] naturally resulting in a weaker peso. On top of that, the narrowing differential between the Philippine and US policy rates added further to the weaker peso. To summarize, these caused us to increase our policy rate sooner and by a larger amount compared to the initial plan. Now our policy rate is 3.75 percent compared to 2.0 percent in just four months. With regard to inflation, when we took these measures, we thought that whatever we do, we will miss our inflation targets in 2022 for the simple fact that our policy instru​​​ments are not very effective against this kind of inflation. To begin with, inflation is largely a supply shock, and to add to that, our policy instruments take 6–18 months before they take full effect. The outturn of new information meant we had to revise the inflation forecast even further. For instance, we revised our forecast from 5.0 percent to 5.4 percent between the June and our last August meeting. For 2023, partly because of the policy rate increases, we revised our average inflation forecast from 4.2 percent to 4.0 percent, but this does not yet take into account the 50-basis points (bps) [increase] we just did. We are quite confident that, absent new shocks, inflation will be very close to the midpoint of our targets to 3.2 percent. In other words, we are moving toward a target-consistent path. It is above target [now], but the path is target consistent over the next year. There is still some chance that we may exceed 4.0 percent next year. Then [in 2024], it will be closer to 3.0 [percent] than 4.0 [percent]. The good news is private sector economists seem to take our forecasts very seriously. Our studies show that when we revise our forecasts, private forecasts tend to follow. The other thing that is noteworthy is despite high inflation in 2022, inflationary expectations are closer to 4.0 percent than 5.0 percent. In 2024, it is [private sector forecasts that are] below 4.0 percent, although it is higher than our own forecast. We think that private sector economists will revise their forecast downwards as we revise ours and take into account the effects of our policy rate changes, and we expect that the commodity prices will be kinder to Philippine inflation in the next year. One of the reasons we acted aggressively is we were afraid of what is called second-order effects. It is not very strong: wages are increasing but not [to the extent that we are] worried about a wage-price spiral; we are also seeing an increase in transport fares. In our base forecast, the second-order effects should not prevent us from hitting near the mid-point of our target in 2024. Although the inflation rate next year will be closer to 4.0 percent than 3.0 percent, it is largely because of [lower figures in] the first half [of the year]. In the second half of the year, we should already see headline inflation closer to 3.0 percent than 4.0 percent. Of course, knowing that these are forecasts and we have been wrong before. One of the things that we now worry about is the exchange rate. In the past, we largely ignored the exchange rate because we saw it as a healthy reaction of the market to fundamentals. Naturally, if imported goods are expensive, demand for dollars will rise and the peso depreciates. The markets are signaling that people should buy less imported goods, and domestic suppliers should be more competitive with imports in the local markets. However, there are times when we worry that the changes in the exchange rate do not just change relative prices between tradables and non-tradables but may also potentially become the anchor of new inflationary expectations rather than our forecasts. That is why we had to move quite aggressively. The point is we are in a global period of very high inflation. The situation has been reversed: it is the rich countries that have very high inflation. The most recent inflation prints are much higher than the average for the last 10 years and, for most countries, higher than target. The other way to look at it is to look at the ratio of the most recent inflation prints to the average of the last 10 years, and you could see that the Philippine ratio is second lowest, after China. So, inflation is high, but relative to other countries, the increase in our inflation is relatively low. In that sense, we think that our credibility as an inflation-targeting central bank has helped, and it will help again in the coming years. That is why we take our first pillar very seriously. If you compare the Philippines with the US Fed, you can see that they had done 225 bps [in cumulative rate adjustments], and we have done 175 bps. The reason is that our inflation problem is not as big as the US, but at the same time, we are worried that if we did not act aggressively, it may be harder to control inflation if we act too late. The question we always hear is: does this [policy normalization] take away or kill the nascent recovery that we are now observing? We believe that the 175-bps [increase]—or even 25 bps or 50 bps more—will not be a major factor in reducing growth. Before the pandemic, the typical policy rate was around 4.0 percent, which was the case as of January 2020. If you compare policy rates to inflation, the real rate is, in fact, still negative. Another way to look at it is to see the Philippines through three metrics: how much we [the peso] depreciated relative to other countries; how much we increased the policy rate; and how much our [gross] international reserves were reduced. There are three responses to the current situation: (i) let the currency depreciate as a response to fundamentals, (ii) raise the policy rates, and (iii) intervene in the forex [foreign exchange] markets. In terms of policy rate, we had the largest increase, the second being South Korea. In terms of depreciation, we are very close to Thailand but lower than Korea. In terms of

Title: Transforming Transport by Digitalizing Payments
Occasion: Automated Fare Collection System (AFCS) EMV Contactless Pilot Launch
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Fellow workers in government, especially officials from the Department of Transportation (DOTr), good day. By the way, I was expecting to see [Transport] Secretary [Jaime] Bautista here. The reason is, when I took my oath, katabi ko siya. Ang tagal naming nagkwentuhan. It turns out, kaibigan niya ‘yung kapatid ko. Napakaliit talaga ng mundo. [The reason is, when I took my oath, I was seated next to him. We talked for a long time. It turns out, he was a friend of my sibling. The world is small indeed.] And I was impressed by how much he is thinking about how to improve transportation, especially for people who cannot afford private transportation. Let me also greet fellow bankers, President and CEO of the Land Bank of the Philippines Cecile Borromeo and Vice President of Digital Banking Randolph Montesa. Ang bansag ng mga ekonomista sa Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) ay “macro,” hindi micro. Puro macro ang tinitingnan. Ang major counterparty namin, o ang ka-deal namin, ay mga bangko. [Economists refer to the BSP as “macro,” not micro—solely looking at the macro perspective. Our major counterparty, or those we deal with, are banks.] Nakakalula ‘yung dami ng zero. Halimbawa, nangungutang kami sa bangko para ma-regulate ang money supply. Kunyari, ang utang namin sa inyo, Cecile [Land Bank], maari nating sabihing ₱2.3 trillion. In other words, number “2” na may kasunod na 12 na zeroes. [The number of zeroes is overwhelming. For example, we borrow from the bank to regulate the money supply. For instance, our debt from Land Bank, we can say that it is at ₱2.3 trillion. In other words, it is a number "2,” followed by 12 zeroes.] Ngayon, ang mahirap sa isang macro na organisasyon na may napakalaking pananaw, dahil sa napakalaki ng ekonomiya, baka makalimutan nila ang napakaraming tao [na bumubuo sa pananaw na iyon]. In other words, ‘wag kakalimutan ang mga tao dahil lang sa statistics. [Now, the difficult thing about a macro-organization that has a vast perspective, because of the expanse of the economy, is that they may tend to forget the many people that form that perspective. In other words, let us not forget the people just because of statistics.] This will show my age. Noong ako ay estudyante, later a junior instructor sa UP [University of the Philippines]—noong araw kasi may junior instructor sa UP—sumasakay ako ng bus galing Batangas. Ang bus noong araw, walang aisle kasi ‘yung side niya sa sidewalk, doon lahat lalabas. [‘Yung mga pasahero sa] bawat silya, doon ang kanilang labas. Ang advantage ng walang aisle, may dagdag na isang pasahero [bawat hilera]. [This will show my age. When I was still a student, later a junior instructor in UP—there was a junior instructor in UP before—I would ride a bus from Batangas. Buses back then did not have an aisle because its side, the one facing the sidewalk, served as the entryway. Passengers from each row pass through that side. The advantage of not having an aisle is an extra passenger for every row.] Pero napakahirap noon para sa mga kundoktor kasi ang kundoktor nakabitin sa labas. At saka, bibilib ka sa kundoktor kasi kahit nakabitin siya sa labas, tatanungin niya isa-isa ang pasahero, “Saan ka pupunta?” Ibig sabihin, mine-memorize niya [kung] saan nanggaling yung pasahero at saan bababa. At papaano kung wala siyang panukli? Ang gagawin niya, kokolektahin niya lahat ng pera, mauupo siya sa likod, tapos bubutasin niya yung ticket, isa-isa. Tapos memorize niya kung sino pa ang kanyang susuklian. Babalikan niya ‘yan [nang isahan] kasi alangang balikan pa niya pa-isa isa—medyo delikado yung paganun-ganun ka sa bus. [But it was hard for conductors back then, as they must hang from the outside. You would be amazed at the conductors because, despite their position, they would ask the passengers, one by one, “Where are you going?” It means they memorize where the passengers came from and where they are headed. And what happens when conductors do not have loose change? They would collect all the payments of the passengers, sit at the back, and mark each ticket. Then, they memorize who among the passengers do they still need to provide change to. The conductors would return to them all at once—as it would be dangerous to move from one aisle to another while hanging from the outside.] Sabi ko sa sarili ko, kapag naging konduktor ka ng bus, baka pwede ka nang mag-PhD [Doctor of Philosophy] sa economics. Biruin mo, tatandaan mo lahat ‘yun. Pero the point is ‘yung sistema, masyadong nagre-require ng full attention and focus. [I told myself, once you become a conductor, you might already be able to gain a PhD in economics. Come to think of it, you have to memorize all of those things. But the point is that the system requires too much, even full, attention and focus.] Of course, gusto nating umunlad tayo. Makakalagpas tayo doon. At yung mga bansa na nauna na sa atin…sometimes, mas mahirap ‘yung nauna ka… Wala ‘yung mga features na dinescribe ninyo. Of course, cashless na sila, pero wala ‘yung open, interoperable [system]. [Of course, we want to develop. We will get through this. And to the countries that are ahead of us...sometimes, it is more difficult when you came first... They do not have the features that you described. Of course, they are cashless, but they do not have an open, interoperable system.] At saka, ‘yung dinescribe niyo kaninang napakagandang [palagay] na: “Paano magme-maintain ng balance para sa aking [pambayad sa] transportation, eh hirap na hirap na nga ako sa isang kahig, isang tuka? Wala na nga akong pera, ilalagay ko pa sa card? Paano ang pambili ko ng pagkain?” Kaya talagang, noong araw, lahat ng bagay ay hiwa-hiwalay. [Also, you raised a good sentiment earlier on: “How will I maintain a balance in my payment for transportation when I am already having a hard time earning enough money for food? I already lack funds. Why should I place what I have on a card? How about my budget for food?” That is why, back then, everything was dispersed.] Fortunately, ang teknolohiya ay nag-improve, at pwede nang paghaluhin ‘yung pera na pambili ng sardinas at saka pamasahe. At ito ay isang napaka-importanteng punto—multi-modal siya. [Fortunately, technology improved, and now, we can put our money for food and transportation in one place. And a very important point is—it is multi-modal.] In other words, malaking increase in efficiency at ginhawa. Na-solve ‘yung problema na kawawa naman ang mahirap na tao—na kakaunti na nga ang pera niya, ‘yung balanse pa ay natutulog sa kanyang card. [In other words, there was a huge increase in efficiency and ease. The problem of poor people—that they only have a small amount of money, yet the balance had to remain on their card—was solved.] Kami, ang lalaki ng mga tinitingnan namin [sa BSP]. Meron kaming three pillars: Ang first pillar namin ay price stability. Napakalaki noon kasi ‘yun ay kung ano ang average na price ng binibili ng lahat ng mga Filipino. Ang pangalawa ay banking stability. ‘Yung mga bangko nga na aming counterparty at mga nire-regulate, ‘yung iba [sa kanila] sobrang pagka-laki-laki, katulad niyang bangko ni Cecile [Land Bank]. Ang pangatlo naming pillar ay payments [and settlements] system. ‘Yung payments system na ‘yan ay mayroong [kakayahang mag-proseso ng] malalaking payments. Halimbawa, noong araw ay [gumagamit ng] tseke; ngayon, PESONet na. Of course, maghihintay ka lang nang kaunti. Kung nag-transfer ka ng pera ng alas otso ng umaga, mare-reflect lang sa kabila ng mga alas dose. Kung trinansfer mo after 12 o’clock, mare-reflect lang…pero hindi mo na kailangan ng tseke. Of course, dumami nang dumami yung cashless payments sa pamamagitan ng InstaPay. Pero kulang ‘yan kung hindi nararamdaman ng mga tao araw-araw. [For us, we look at big things at the BSP. We have three pillars: Our first pillar is price stability. This one is vast because it covers the average price of the goods that all Filipinos purchase. Our second pillar is banking stability. The banks that are our counterparty and those that we regulate, some of them are really massive, like Land Bank. Our third pillar is payments and settlements system. The payments system has the capacity to process huge payments. For example, back then, people used cheques; now, they use PESONet. Of course, one would have to wait a little while. If you transferred money at 8 o’clock in the morning, it would be reflected on the receiver’s end by around 12 o’clock. If you transferred after 12 o’clock, it will be reflected by… But you no longer need a cheque. Of course, the number of cashless payments kept on increasing due to InstaPay. But that is not enough if the people would not feel its impact everyday.] Kaya I am really very grateful to all the people who worked together to get this pilot going: ‘yung mga samahan ng jeepney drivers, ang ating LTFRB [Land Transportation Franchising and Regulatory Board], ang ating mga kapwa empleyado ng gobyerno sa DOTr, at ang ating mga kasama sa Land Bank. Nangyari ito dahil lahat ay nagtulungan. [That is why I am really very grateful to all the people who worked together to get this pilot going: the association of jeepney drivers, our LTFRB, our fellow government employees from DOTr, and our partners from Land Bank. This became possible because everyone worked hand-in-hand.] At ang aking tingin…sana lumaki ito at dumami nang dumami ang [ating] kasama. Ang ating bisyon ay ang Land Bank card mo—of course, pwede namang ibang bangko pero baka magsimula sa Land Bank—‘yung Land Bank card mo ay pwede [ng gamitin] sa lahat. ‘Yun ang maganda dito—ang pag-expand nito sa transportation. Hindi lang transportation ang ma-i-improve, lahat ng aspeto ng buhay, tulad na nga ‘yung kung pupunta ka sa McDonald’s...at the same time. So, ‘yun ang ating bisyon. [And in my view...I hope that this program expands and that our partners increase. Our vision is that your Land Bank card—of course, it could be other banks, but we may start with Land Bank—your Land Ba

Title: Philippine Economic and Financial Updates
Occasion: Financial Executives Institute of the Philippines (FINEX) General Membership Meeting
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: Good day, everyone, mga kaibigan [friends]. First of all, my last non-virtual public speech was with FINEX [Financial Executives Insitute of the Philippines] also. Then, my first public [on-site] speech [as the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) Governor] is again with FINEX. I thank you for inviting me to share my view on the latest developments in the Philippine economy and, of course, from the point of view of a central banker. Let me start by saying that these are truly difficult times and quite unique for two reasons. We were starting with monetary policy that was meant to speed up the recovery of the economy from a nearly 10.0-percent contraction. If I can compute GDP [gross domestic product] per week, what would the contraction look like? The worst two weeks of the Philippine economy were the last two weeks of Q1 2020—because an economy that was initially growing at 6.0 percent contracted by 1.0 percent, when, in fact, there were only two weeks of lockdown. When you compute backward, the rate of contraction was actually 20.0 percent. If that had continued, the economy would have been 20.0 percent smaller. Fortunately, we learned [how to live with COVID] along the way. We found out that certain things hurt us more than the virus. We learned along the way and coped with it better and better. Then, we got vaccinated. You saw the contraction and are now seeing recovery. In Q1 2022, we grew by 8.2 percent. I think the Q2 2022 [GDP figures] will be great, too. If things go well, we will be returning to pre-pandemic growth. Those 10 years before the pandemic were quite exceptional: growth was both high and inclusive. The rate of growth of wage and salaried employment was strong. Meanwhile, [the share of] unpaid family workers was declining—a true sign of becoming a more market-oriented, formal sector economy. Here is what we did during the pandemic: [We reduced to] 2.0 percent policy rate. Never in the history of the Philippines, since we became an independent central bank, did we have a 2.0 percent policy rate. That is understandable, of course, since the mid-point of the inflation target is 3.0 percent. We bought one trillion pesos worth of government securities. Our balance sheet [before that] was ₱4.5 trillion. And we lent ₱600 billion to the National Government. But while we were doing all these [accommodative measures], we were already thinking of an exit plan. But as the Scottish poet Robert Burns said, “The best laid plans of mice and men often go awry.” They are not awry; they just have to be massively adjusted. The plan is still the same except it will be way ahead. The liftoff of the policy interest rate will be way ahead and the size—as shown by our 75-basis points (bps) off-cycle increase—is bigger. I thought that [our previous hike of] 25 bps and a surprise 75 bps is more dramatic than two 50 bps. Two 50 bps at our regular meetings is inferior to one 25 bps and a 75 bps that you did not expect. The market had to be jolted, too. This central bank is not staying idly by. Now, we had to exit from that. The plan was to exit maybe in the first month of Q4 [and do it] gradually: 25 bps. Then, we will gradually wean the government off this cheap money. Of course, the ongoing debate in the US about inflation: you had a team ‘Low and Transitory’ and a team ‘Large and not Transitory.’ By the way, Nobel Prize winner Paul Krugman was wrong [about US inflation], and he wrote a column trying to explain why he was wrong. Now, we had to speed up everything. Why did we do that? The textbook inflation-targeting central bank cares about the exchange rate only as inputs in the model to the inflation rate. Whatever interest you have on the exchange rate, it is because it affects inflation. The normal situation is when changes in the exchange rate are small and transitory, they hardly translate to inflation. However, if it is large and [it goes] in one direction, we have to compute longer-run elasticities. In the first case [where changes in the exchange rate are small], elasticity [to inflation] was 0.06 to 0.1. But we are not seeing small changes and, possibly, our elasticities are much higher. Unlike the textbook inflation-targeting central bank, we are an Asian inflation-targeting central bank. What does that mean? We do care about exchange rate volatility. We think we should have some because having none will [make the central bank] look like an insurance company. We like volatility but not excess volatility. Not only that, it gets to a point where we care about the [absolute] level: if the exchange rate moves to ₱35:$ [at the extreme level], that is going to be disastrous. Philippine products measured in dollars become more expensive. That is why we tend to accumulate reserves when the currency is appreciating. When I first entered the central bank, it took courage on the part of [former BSP Governor Amando] Tetangco to borrow ₱2.5 trillion to grow the reserves from US$45 billion. We lost money because our reserves were earning much less, but that was a fantastic decision from a longer-term standpoint. We also care a lot about the exchange rate when it is moving up too fast because who will believe our inflation targets? To use econ-speak, it will disanchor inflation expectations. These are all the reasons we have to consider. We have other things: we want to develop the relationship between the GS [government securities] markets and our policy rates. Fortunately, we were able to get amendments on our charter so we can borrow directly from the public. Historically, why was that removed from the powers of the central bank? It was the reaction to the “Jobo” Bills. For technical reasons, borrowing from the public is better than borrowing from the banks…because the people who are there to save the money and put it in securities are most likely going to roll it over; they have no intention of lending it to anybody. In other words, we have more permanent ways of sterilization. We have powers we did not have before. We are also asking for more powers: BSP supervisors should be able to look at the bank accounts of [officials] of banks. Bank supervisors should not be covered by deposit secrecy. We need your support there. Do not [let bank deposit secrecy] paralyze bank supervisors. The other way to look at things is to contrast us with Thailand. Thailand hardly moved its policy rates; we have already raised by 125 bps. Why? They have more dollars to spare. When you sell dollars, you are actually tightening monetary policy. Pesos are going back to the central bank. Our GIR [gross international reserves] level was about US$108 billion; now, it is about US$100 billion. If you look at Thailand, their dollar sales are much larger, [since] they have the luxury of bigger reserves. We, on the other hand, think our reserves are comfortable, not excessive, and this is not the time to waste our bullets. When you look at the yellow line, the policy rate, even after the increase, is still significantly lower than it used to be. It [policy rate increase] will not kill the economy. The policy rate is higher now, but the inflation rate is even higher. And, in fact, the midpoint of our forecast for next year is 4.2 percent. Relative to our own year-ahead forecast of what the inflation will be next year, the policy rate is still negative in real terms. So, we can afford to step on the brakes without killing the nascent economic growth. A lot of the inflation is [driven by] supply shocks. If you take out the extreme values and get the averages, that tells you what the more regular inflation is. The problem is oil and non-oil [items], which became very expensive. The hope is that these prices will normalize. We revised our current account deficit and you will notice that the revision was huge—from about US$5 billion to about US$20 billion. This is a question we are often asked: how do you finance that? My answer is: this is why some depreciation of the peso is healthy. People with Foreign Currency Deposit Unit (FCDU) accounts will change their minds and convert some of that to pesos. In a sense, we are lucky that we have the FCDU system. You can consider that our second-level reserves. If you look at our inflation forecast—and this will have to be revised [at the next meeting]—it is 4.2 percent for 2023. We will revise this with the new numbers. We have a better than 50/50 chance to do lower than 4.0 percent. That is why we may do 25 bps or 50 bps at the next meeting to make sure that there is enough follow-through for that to happen. We are quite confident that there is still sufficient momentum for growth. What if I am wrong? I still will not regret it because GDP growth is not one of our three pillars, and we believe that, in the long run, credible inflation targets result in higher growth. The other way to look at it is the moment inflation is out of control, unavoidably, large output has to be lost to kill it. The best memory of that is [former US Federal Reserve Chair Paul] Volcker. Unless you are willing to live with high inflation forever, high inflation will eventually result in lower output. The larger inflation is, the chances of bringing it down without bringing down growth are near zero. You can fly the plane, but you cannot land. This is the reason we are very firm about our three pillars, the first of which is price stability. You all know that we will definitely exceed 4.0 percent this year, and there is very little we could do about it. You have these powerful global forces, about which you can do little. It has also been said in the US that, “The dollar is our currency, but it is your problem.” If you look at the numbers, you will see that our depreciation is not exactly out of line. There are countries that are more depreciated and less depreciated than us. Before we did the 75 bps [off-cycle rate hike], we were neck and neck with the South Korean won. So, the 75 bps worked. But if a central banker does too many surprises

Title: Beyond the Numbers: The Press as a Reliable Partner in Economic Development
Occasion: Economic Journalists Association of the Philippines (EJAP) 31st Business Journalism Awards
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ The Press as a Partner It is my pleasure to speak to you on this important occasion. Today, we honor excellence in economic news reporting and the valuable contributions of business journalists to the nation. I have always said that the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) becomes more effective when the nation understands what we do and why we do what we do. And in this regard, you are an important partner. We cannot overlook the value of your work, especially during times like these when our country must sail through stormy seas in order to reach the shores of sustainable and inclusive economic growth. Through your coverage and analysis, you help the people understand and, when possible, appreciate the efforts of government institutions in navigating this very difficult time. There are many different—sometimes polarizing—takes on economic issues that may not necessarily capture the complexity of the challenges that we face, so we appreciate your attempts to promote greater public awareness and foster more meaningful conversations. Beyond demystifying central banking, you help us communicate the impact of our actions and decisions on everyday life. You give the ordinary Filipino the tools to answer daily questions, like, should I have my gas tank filled today? Is it a good time to take out a loan from the bank? How do we allocate our household budget? In this way, your timely delivery of the news empowers the Filipino people to make sound economic choices daily. That said, we are facing more challenges than ever on the economic front. In fact, I have said before that this may be the most difficult time since I joined the Monetary Board more than a decade ago. We are facing very difficult challenges. There is the very aggressive response of the US Fed [US Federal Reserve]. Then, we also have the Ukraine-Russia conflict, which along with domestic supply issues, pushed up the prices of oil and non-oil commodities. This has pushed inflation to the center of mainstream consciousness. Last week, it was reported that October inflation rose to 7.7 percent. But while these challenges are indeed tough, the Philippines will pull through. We have done so in past crises, and we will do it again. Anchored on Three Pillars Our actions are anchored on what we call our three pillars: price stability, financial stability, and a safe and efficient payments and settlements system—these are the three pillars of central banking. Allow me to provide updates on each. On price stability, as an inflation-targeting central bank, we aim to bring inflation back to the government’s target range over the medium term. Based on our latest estimates, we expect inflation to ease from the projected 5.6 percent this year to 4.1 percent next year and further to 3.0 percent in 2024. As you know, next Thursday, we will be holding our policy meeting. I have already communicated that we are prepared to match the US Fed. Since they hiked by another 75 basis points, you can expect that I will be voting to raise the policy rate by a similar magnitude. The reason we do this is to increase the likelihood that headline inflation will be within target by the second half of next year and, hopefully, for the rest of 2024. The BSP’s policy rate hikes will also prevent a significant narrowing of the interest rate differential between the US and the Philippines. Keeping a comfortable differential between our policy rate and that of the US lends support to the peso. As you know, the BSP observes a flexible exchange rate policy. As such, we do not target a specific exchange rate nor set a specific line in the sand. Nevertheless, we recognize that significant and persistent depreciation of the peso can dislodge inflation expectations. We, therefore, intervene in the market as needed, consistent with our price stability mandate. In addition, the BSP also exercises flexibility in selling dollars. The Philippines has ample gross international reserves—standing at 94.1 billion US dollars as of the end of October 2022—allowing us to sell dollars and help smoothen foreign exchange market volatility. In summary, the BSP uses three tools to cushion the economy against disruptions—interest rate adjustment, a flexible exchange rate, and foreign exchange market participation. We use a combination of these tools in a well-calibrated manner to keep the impact of external shocks manageable. Meanwhile, there is a fourth tool, which are non-monetary measures implemented by the National Government to help boost the supply and moderate the prices of key food commodities.
