So, we [in the government] will eventually get consensus [on how to balance trade-offs between cutting spending or raising taxes in response to lower-than-expected growth]. I think, over time, even as we debate among ourselves, we will gravitate toward common sense. ###

Title: The Philippine Thrift Banking Industry: A Picture of Resilience
Occasion: Chamber of Thrift Banks General Membership Meeting
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: Magandang tanghali sa inyong lahat. By the way, in the details [of my introduction, it said] I started teaching in 1991, but, in reality, I started much earlier. I think I joined the UP [University of the Philippines] faculty in 1976, but, then, I was not really considered a full-fledged member of the faculty because I was an instructor. I went to Northwestern in 1978 and came home in 1983. So, there is no other place where I have stayed longer than UP, but there is no other place outside UP where I have stayed longer than in the BSP [Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas]. And as they say, sometimes, you love your latest girlfriend more than the previous ones. Of course, another institution—I was there for a long time—was La Salle, because I came from La Salle Lipa and eventually moved to La Salle Taft. So, I guess, in terms of length of residence: UP, BSP, and La Salle. But if you weigh it where recent years have twice the weight of older [previous] years, then the longest place where I have stayed is BSP. So let me thank the Chamber for inviting me to be your guest speaker. Last October you invited me to your annual convention and I think I gave a video message. I’m glad that my schedule allowed me to come [in person] today. As I always say, we in the BSP always welcome the opportunity to engage the public and engage the very institutions that we regulate, which we proudly say are a source of strength of our economy, a source of strength and stability, as you will see later. Three pillars of central banking Of course, I always start with our three pillars: price stability, financial stability, and a safe, secure, and efficient payments and settlements system. The last one [pillar] used to be the most boring because it’s largely—a big part of it—is the physical payment system. Because of digital technologies, it’s now the most dynamic [among the three pillars] where the changes are happening very fast. I’m glad, listening to the previous speaker, that you, the thrift banks, are really very open to new ideas and new technology. Because, I think, that’s very important for the thrift bank industry to prosper. Taking decisive action to preserve price stability Going back to the first pillar, we have just increased the policy rate by 25 basis points (bps). Remember as late as November 2020 [at the height of the pandemic], the policy rate was just 200 ppts. Now, it’s 625 ppts. Of course, I always say, do not look at that as [moving from] 400 ppts to 625 ppts because, in reality, the policy rate of 200 ppts was really a response to something so unusual, which was the pandemic. In fact, from the beginning, when we made [brought down] the policy rate to 200 ppts, we already had an exit plan—a gentle one – [where we intended to slowly raise rates by] 25 bps, 25 bps, 25 bps. The other thing we did—because the pandemic was causing people to flee to cash—is we had to buy a trillion pesos worth of government securities. Of course, we lost a little bit of money there. But in the long run, it’s a very important part of our balance sheet because our plan is a reverse repo, which is now a fixed rate option, will be a true market rate because we now have sufficient security for the reverse repo. We did not plan it that way but when we decided to do something to save the bond market from panic, we ended up with securities that can now be used for this reform. Do watch out for the time when we no longer ration [central bank securities]. By the way, rationing creates all sorts of problems. Say a bank that wants ten will bid twenty, hoping that if they allocate proportionately, he has a bigger share in the program. Of course, there are banks who outsmart themselves: bid too much and then regrettably win what they bid for. And they end up, of course, being penalized or end up borrowing at a higher cost just to make sure they will not be penalized. Follow-through monetary action aimed at nipping inflation in the bud and ensuring credibility as an inflation-targeter Now, going back, what was the consideration for doing [raising the policy rate by] 25 bps [at our latest policy meeting] when we’ve already done 400 bps [in cumulative policy adjustments]? In fact, the 400 bps already puts us at the top of the list [of central banks that have raised their policy rates]. The reason is we really want to make sure that our credibility as an inflation-targeting central bank, after all of this, will remain solid and widely respected. In the past, I always say the worst case of inflation we have had is 15 straight months of inflation being above target. Now, this one [current round of elevated inflation] is really special; we may have 19 [months] already. Let me explain why. The typical [drivers of] Philippine inflation is never because we [at the central bank] created too much credit; it’s because of supply shocks. The typical supply shock lasts about four or five months. So we say, the system takes 12 months to digest all of this, then we’ll [inflation] be back to normal in 15 months. The last shock was terrible. Remember, [the mid-point of] our [inflation] target is 3 percent divided by 12 [months] so that’s 0.25 month-on-month (MoM). There was only one month during those past 13 months where we [MoM inflation] were at 0.2 percent or 0.3 percent. In other words, for 12 out of 13 months, the monthly inflation, the month-on-month suggested inflation was much higher. The worst one [MoM inflation print] was in January 2023 [in comparison to December 2022 where it was] 1.7 percent in one month. Of course, if you adjust for seasonality, because January is usually a price adjustment month, it’s something like 1.2 percent. But if that had continued for 12 months, that would have been 15 percent [annualized] inflation. That’s why, as you can see, we are not taking any chances. We can make two mistakes: [the first is] act when we shouldn’t have. The other is not act when we should have. This first mistake is easy; all you have to do is reverse your policy two, three, four months later. The second mistake is harder now that you have fallen behind and maybe lose your credibility. Above-target CPI items on the rise as price pressures broaden If you look at the next chart, you will again appreciate this even more. Of the more than 300 [total CPI items], 206 were above the [upper end of our inflation] target, above 4 percent. Only 109 [CPI items] were within target [2-4 percent] or below target [less than 2 percent]. In other words, inflation is now spreading from the sources of inflation to other sectors [in the economy]. And you can see this in two charts. The first one is the number of items and then distribution. The next one is when you look at inflation by breaking it down by food [inflation] versus non-food [inflation]. For instance, in January and February 2023, [MoM] inflation numbers were fairly quite good at 0 percent. In other words, the price index in February is exactly equal to the price index of January. However, it has really two parts: food [inflation] being negative because it was too high already at the time. Mahirap nang pamahalin yung sibuyas, napakamahal na eh, ‘di ba? Sugar, mahirap nang pababain, napakamahal na, sobra. And non-food [inflation] was actually the 0.5 percent [on an MoM basis], annualized, it’s 6.0 percent. That’s the problem with the last [inflation] print [in February 2023]. It’s [headline inflation] low but if you break it down into two components, the non-food part is now beginning to follow [the food part] with a lag. We got to do something about it. Private sector inflation expectations approach upper end of target band The other thing that worried us was, although inflation expectations of private analysts are now below 4 percent [the upper end of the inflation target], they are still high at 3.7 percent for 2024 and 3.6 percent for 2025. Their [inflation] forecast for 2023 is very close to ours [at the central bank]. So they’re still believing, more or less, our forecasts. Nonetheless, forecasts are forecasts. And the question is what is the risk to the forecast? Right now, our assessment is there is a greater probability that it’s [the forecast] wrong on the high side than it’s wrong on the low side. There are many elements in the forecast that we think are more likely if it doesn’t happen, the higher side is more likely to happen than the lower side. Forecasts point to signs of normalization Still, we have a relatively encouraging picture. We have revised our [inflation] forecast downwards. The average inflation as forecasted for this year is [down] from 6.1 to 6.0 percent. For next year, [the forecast is down] from 3.1 to 2.9 percent. If you look at the chart more closely, we will have below 4 percent inflation, if our forecasts are correct, by October [2023]. Then, we’ll be back to normal inflation for the rest of the year next year. But of course, I think it was Yogi Berra who said, “It’s very hard to forecast, especially the future.” Non-monetary interventions key to address demand-side pressures Now, of course, the non-monetary interventions remain key. This is where I have a lot of confidence. I think the President and the Cabinet are now really laser-focused on making sure that timely imports of food [come in]. They’ll make sure that you no longer have a repeat [where] very, very expensive [CPI] items with a very low share of the budget accounted for such a big part of the inflation [drivers] because of humungous [price] changes. I already mentioned, sugar [prices] doubling year-on-year, and of course, sibuyas. If you get one that’s small enough, it’s expensive enough to be your earring. Fed actions: still relevant but less of a factor in decision-making as exchange rate stabilizes Of course, what the Fed does, though it’s not very central to our inflation forecasting [at this time], is also quite important because the interest rate differential – the differential between the policy rates of the Fed – and the B

Title: View from Manila: Outlook for 2023 and 2024
Occasion: Philippine Economic Briefing in Washington, D.C., United States
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: Friends of the Philippines, ladies, and gentlemen, as they say in the Philippines, magandang umaga. I will start by saying that the Philippine Constitution required the central bank to be an independent central bank. Of course, it took three or four years to pass a law after the Constitution was written to put that law down more precisely. The most important feature of that [law] is only one member of the Cabinet is a member of the Monetary Board (MB). The rest are people, like me, who have a fixed tenure, and the others are drawn from the private sector. Sometimes, the [representatives from the] private sector are former bankers—like my friend here, [MB Member] Tony Abacan, who was the former president of Metrobank. And, sometimes, they get academics, like me, who do not understand banking but eventually learn after being there for nearly 12 years. BSP's three pillars: guiding principles of central banking Our mandates, or our so-called three pillars, are price stability; financial stability; and a safe, secure, and efficient payments and settlements system. On the payments and settlements system: transforming the way governments, businesses, and households pay By the way, the third one [pillar] used to be boring. We print money and [provide] clearing [and settlement services]. Now, it is more exciting because—together with the government—we are promoting digital payments. Of course, the goal is that tax payments will be done digitally. And one vision that we have—that I share with [Finance] Secretary [Benjamin] Diokno—is when the Philippines imports goods, the customs duties are already computed properly while the ship is halfway to the Philippines. So, no more negotiations. Things become faster out of the port, right? So, all the payments to the government will also be easily tracked, including payments to local governments. This is getting very exciting. In addition, of course, we want more and more people to have formal bank accounts, and that a lot of payments will be done digitally. That is exactly what is happening right now. We have [automated clearing houses], one is called InstaPay, and a bigger and bigger part of the payments are now digital. And then, we have PESONet, which is the equivalent of checks but is also electronic. So, we are moving fast on the digital side. On financial stability: regulators work in tandem Now, let me go back to the second one—financial stability. Clearly, we do not regulate the stock market. We do not regulate the issuance of private bonds. We do not regulate insurance. Financial stability is done by a committee chaired by me, and the other members of the committee are the chairman of the Securities and Exchange Commission and the chairman of the Insurance Commission. On price stability: month-on-month inflation figures paint an encouraging picture [Let us go] back to price stability. Well, if you look at that next picture, it seems alarming, right? Headline inflation is 8.6 percent [in February 2023], which eventually fell to 7.6 [percent in March]. If you take out the extreme price changes—what you would call core inflation—it is also quite high [at 8.0 percent in March]. But to me, the numbers that mean a lot more are the little bars below [month-on-month (m-o-m) inflation]. If you multiply that by 12, that is the implied inflation rate for the year if everything is the same. For instance, that first figure there, 0.9 percent [m-o-m inflation] in March 2022, that is terrible. That is 0.9 percent times 12 [months]. If that had continued for 12 months, the implied inflation is more than 10 percent. On the other hand, if you look at the other end, there are two 0.3 percent [m-o-m inflation prints] there [in December 2022 and February 2023]. The first 0.3 percent [in December 2022], we celebrated too early and said, “Oh, we are there already!" 0.3 percent times 12 [months] is 3.6 percent [in annualized inflation, which is well within the inflation target band]. And then, we got hit by 1.0 percent [in January 2023], which is 12 percent [once annualized], right? Of course, we are now seeing what, we think, will really be the trend next time, which is 0.3 percent from January to February [2023] and zero inflation from February to March. We think we are on our way toward our [inflation] target of 2.0 percent to 4.0 percent. And that is what is shown in the next chart. Inflation forecasts may be revised downwards but subject to global forces For the [current] year, of course, it is impossible to hit 4.0 percent [inflation target]. We will need negative inflation [during] the remaining part of the year to hit the 4.0 percent. By the way, we are revising this [forecast] with the newer numbers. [2023 full-year] inflation will probably be around below 6.0 percent in the revised numbers. But, as you can see, for 2024, we are confident that we are going to be very close to the midpoint of our target [at 3.0 percent]. Of course, a lot of things can happen. Historically, when there was global deflation, inflation was actually below 2.0 percent for two years. Of course, nobody complained [then]. The point is, global forces can actually be the main driver of [domestic] inflation, and we have already experienced importing globally low prices. Now, of course, we are seeing the other side. We are probably importing high inflation globally. Private sector inflation forecasts converging with the central bank's, reflecting inflation-targeting credibility At any rate, the private sector forecasts [of inflation] are, more or less, very close to ours, except that [these] are slightly higher than ours for 2024 and 2025. [For 2023], the private sector says [that inflation will be] 3.7 percent; we [at the central bank] say around 3.0 percent. By the way, this usually happens. The private sector eventually adjusts its forecast with a one or two-month lag from ours. I think this shows you how credible the central bank is [at inflation targeting]. It can make mistakes, just like any other central bank, but, clearly, it has been given the mandate and the powers to make sure that when there is inflation, it is not due to excess demand. It is not due to the fact that the central bank is printing money to finance the government. In fact, when you look at the rise of our balance sheet, it is largely because we purchased foreign exchange. The only time we purchased Philippine government bonds was because of COVID. ​​Fed actions: always relevant but less of a concern in policy-making Of course, we are affected by what happens with the Fed [United States (US) Federal Reserve]. But, in general, our inflation policy is largely driven by Philippine inflation. What was unique about this year is that the US policy rate changes were so large—four 75 basis points (bps) [hikes] in a row—to the point where the Philippine policy rate became too close to the US policy rate. And, as you know, since the dollar is the global currency, people would prefer the dollar to the peso if the policy rates are the same. We are now at that stage where we are watching that [differential]. We want to make sure that our policy rate is not lower and should be around 1.0 to 1.25 percentage points higher than the Fed's. That is what the markets are saying. And, as shown in the next slide, when you have very, very narrow differentials between the Fed policy rate and ours, you have a weak peso. As you can see, it [the peso] has appreciated lately. Taking decisive monetary action And one of the reasons [behind the appreciation] is we have acted very aggressively. We increased the policy rate from 200 bps to 425 bps, more than any central bank in Asia. The only central bank that comes close is South Korea. Now, we are probably near the end [of our tightening cycle]. We are probably [considering] pausing at the next meeting because the [m-o-m] inflation prints are very good. If April turns out to be another very low inflation month—so, that is three good points in a row—then we are in a position to pause. How the central bank creates the conditions conducive to growth and supports economic development ​Let me wrap up and say, we [at the central bank] use all our powers, and we have enough of them to make sure that we support our three pillars. This has been very good for us but also quite good for the Philippine government itself. And we are very, very fortunate that every Finance Secretary that the Philippines has ever had—that I can remember—has always been very fiscally responsible because they know that they can finance a big part of the debt of the government by issuing peso-denominated bonds. Indeed, around 80 percent of our debt is domestic debt, and much of that is longer than two years. In summary, what you have is the financial and banking conditions of the Philippines that are consistent with long-term growth. No shortcuts. No boom and bust. We try to keep growth as steady as possible and to make sure that the banks are strong so that the economy will be supported by price stability, financial stability, and the payment system that we support. Thank you very much. ​###

Title: Within Reach: How Digitalization Boosts Financial Inclusion in the Philippines
Occasion: ASEAN High-Level Seminar on Innovative Strategy to Further Enhance Financial Inclusion
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​To guests, financial inclusion champions, ladies, and gentlemen, selamat siang. The first thing I would like to do is thank [Bank Indonesia] Governor Perry [Warjiyo], our Indonesian host, for hosting such a wonderful event. On behalf of the Philippines’ central bank, thank you for inviting me to share the Philippine experience in digital technologies to reinforce financial inclusion—a topic very important to us at the central bank, especially the Monetary Board. In a sense, we are a very traditional central bank that is very concerned about [our three pillars of] price stability—of course, right now, it is always putting us in the headlines because we are missing the [inflation] targets; financial stability; and, of course, a safe and efficient payments system. But with all of these strong three pillars, it will be a pity if it is not inclusive. So, the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) has made financial inclusion a very important part of its strategic objectives for the next so many years. Expanding the mantle of financial inclusion through digital tools As you might have already heard, our target is to increase the proportion of people who are included in the financial system—that is, they have a formal bank account or electronic wallet—to 70 percent this year. The other way to put it is, we want to reduce the number of [financially] excluded people to below 30 percent [at the ASEAN level]. By the way, it [the financial exclusion level in the Philippines] was 71 percent in 2019. The 2021 number is now at 44 percent. We are certain that the 2023 numbers are much lower, and we are on our way toward 30 percent or lower. We are actively working to bring the figure down, and COVID sped up the process [of harnessing digitalization to expand financial inclusion]—but, I think, there is no stopping us. Of course, there are limits because of the infrastructure itself. A big part of the population has no access to even a cellphone signal in some cases. So, we have to find other ways of reaching them. E-money [electronic money] has played a big role [in onboarding Filipinos to the formal financial system]. E-money accounts grew to 36 percent in 2021 [from 8.0 percent in 2019], much higher now. Promoting strong consumer protection mechanisms In the aspect of consumer protection and fraud, fortunately, we were finally able to pass a law that requires every prepaid SIM [subscriber identity module] card to have a verified ID [identification]. By the way, I tried it myself. I bought a SIM card, uploaded my ID, which was a National ID, and took a selfie. It took them only three days to send me a text [message] saying, “You have been verified.” Maybe they found out that I was the central bank governor; that is why they were fast. I do not know, but I do not think so. Then, I opened [an e-wallet account]—I will not say what kind, whether it is GCash or it is Maya—and was able to do so very quickly. By the way, I am also the Chairman of the Anti-Money Laundering Council, and what we found was that before we did this—making sure that every SIM card has a unique identity of the individual with the right name, with the retina, and the fingerprint—we actually found out that there was one person owning 1,000 SIM cards [that was] actually operating illegal gambling. Finally, because of all these problems, Congress moved and said, “From now on, no verification of identity, your SIM card will expire in two months.” So, it is working. Since the number of people who use their mobile phones [for digital payments] has increased significantly, that is why we are actually quite confident that we will be able to succeed if we can improve the physical infrastructure—maybe we can even bring it [the proportion of Filipino adults that are financially excluded] below 30 percent. Taking a whole-of-government approach to financial inclusion From the point of view of government, it [financial inclusion] is a whole-of-government approach. The National Strategy for Financial Inclusion is not just the central bank’s [initiative]; it is the [initiative of the] entire government. Addressing the infrastructure problem As I already alluded to, there are issues, of course. You have the digital telecoms [telecommunications] divide, and that has to be addressed. [With] what we are doing now, satellite services will no longer be needed to be part of telecoms franchises. Some areas are underserved. Any entrepreneur would come up and put up a VSAT [very small aperture terminal] so that they can increase access to the internet in those places that are underserved. The birth of the BSP’s Consumer Protection and Market Conduct Office (CPMCO) In addition, we are creating an enabling regulatory environment, and consumer protection is one of them. Fortunately, we were able to pass a law that literally turned an office [the CPMCO] in the central bank into a court—the decisions of which can be challenged only at the Court of Appeals, just one level below the Supreme Court. Of course, because of these powers, it [the office] can force very fast mediation, but we hope we never get there, [to the point] where somebody from the central bank acts like a judge and makes a ruling, whether who is at fault and the extent to which the consumer indicates the bank is wrong. We also have a chatbot where we can deal with consumer concerns in real-time via a web chat on the BSP website, via messenger or Facebook, and via SMS [short message service] or text. Strengthening digital financial literacy Of course, we have programs that are trying to improve financial literacy. My own experience with it is, sometimes, financial literacy problems are also arithmetic problems. Sometimes, some people could not solve very simple arithmetic problems, like computing interest. So, I suppose, a big part of financial literacy has to start from grade school. Boosting responsible innovation We are also into using a framework of digital banks, where digital banks are not allowed to have more than one branch so they will be forced to be really digital. We opened up six [digital banks]. The reason we capped it at six is that we are actually quite afraid that if there are too many, some of them might actually fall by the wayside, and consumers may lose their trust [in the financial system], and our ability to regulate them may actually be reduced. Finally, we are also moving toward an Open Finance Framework. That might take some time, but the entire point is that the consumer owns the data, and it is up to him whether somebody could see it or not. By the way, if you have a very good borrower, the tendency of the bank is to keep that a secret because it prevents you from going to another bank. Now, [open finance promises that the] consumer can actually force the bank to share the data with a competing bank. Paleng-QR: a high-impact use case of digital finance We are doing QR [quick response code payments], where even wet markets, drivers, or pedicabs can receive payments through the QR system. We call it “Paleng-QR.” “Palengke” is the Mexican term for “market.” Most people thought that “palengke” was Spanish, but it is really Mexican because of our history with Mexico. We have already done work with 11 local governments and counting [that issued local ordinances in support of the program]. I physically visited four of the [Paleng-QR Ph] launches. The feedback is very good, but one thing we noticed is that the people in the wet market say, “It is very hard because we are buying vegetables from farmers who do not have access to QR codes.” So, they still have to go back to their banks to withdraw cash so they can pay the farmers. As you can see, these are the things that have to be addressed because the system becomes less efficient if there are excluded people. And the last mile, like paying the farmer, still has to be [paid via] cash. So, there is so much progress that still has to be done. Moving forward as a region In closing, we will continue to work closely with our ASEAN counterparts to achieve our financial inclusion goals. As I already mentioned, at the regional level, our cooperative arrangements in ASEAN place us in a unique position to engage in knowledge and experience-sharing in digital financial inclusion. Let us capitalize on each other's knowledge and cooperate so that we can have a financially inclusive ASEAN for the benefit of all our people. Thank you very much for your attention. Maraming salamat. ###

Title: Preserving Price and Financial Stability in the Philippines
Occasion: Meeting with Fitch Ratings at the sidelines of the 2023 International Monetary Fund–World Bank Spring Meetings
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ The headline [inflation] numbers are high. Even the core [inflation] numbers are high. But what must be seen is [inflation] in relation to month-on-month (m-o-m) changes. Unless there are new shocks, we are already moving toward an inflation path of below 4.0 percent by October or November [2023]. The reason for this is that the price index in March [2023] is actually slightly lower than the price index in January. One way to look at it is: Year-on-year [inflation] is the average of 12 m-o-m [inflation prints]. And if you have 10 very high [months of elevated inflation] and two [months of] very low [inflation], as soon as the higher months are bumped off by two months [of within-target inflation], then the inflation number is going back very quickly to below 4.0 percent [which is the upper end of the inflation target]. That is why we are confident that, in the absence of new shocks, average inflation will be within-target, possibly below 3.0 percent. Supply-side pressures ease with the relaxation of import restrictions; looking to rice as a lesson Because of base effects, because of very high inflation in January 2023, [m-o-m inflation for] last January was terrible. Without adjusting for seasonality, the January 2023 price index was 1.7 percent higher than December 2022. Anything terrible can happen [that can drive inflation, such as] Swine flu, meat shortage, [as well as increases on the prices of] vegetables, fruits, and sugar—all those little things. What is notable is that rice is not there [in the items driving inflation]. The reason rice is not there is that the restrictions on imports were replaced with tariffs. In a sense, what caused the [elevated] inflation was that the government was restraining imports. But that is now being addressed. There was a survey [which revealed] that the issue that most people are concerned about is inflation. In other words, all of these are working to relax the speed of the importation process. The President is already on this. That is why I am quite confident that we will be back to normal inflation next year. No tradeoffs between price and financial stability The other reason that this is the case is that we [at the central bank] essentially prevented knock-on effects from supply shocks because we were aggressive. We increased the policy rate from 200 basis points (bps) to 625 bps in just nine months. We were able to do that because we knew that bank balance sheets could take it [the impact of the rate hikes]. With high policy rates—while they [the banks] could have [incur] losses on their holdings of government securities—but with the higher interest rates, they earn more money. On the whole, because the banks have very strong balance sheets, financial stability was never a constraint in our use of the policy rate to quell inflation. On the narrowing interest rate differential and the weakening exchange rate The other reason we did that [raise the policy rate] is, as the US [United States] increased its policy rate, its policy rate became very close to ours. If you asked people a hypothetical question, “If the interest rate on the peso instrument is the same as the interest rate on a dollar instrument, which instrument would you choose?” Even Filipinos would choose the dollar because of all the advantages of the king dollar. That is the other reason. The differential between our policy rate and the US policy rate, if it narrowed too much, could cause a very large fall in the peso. However, this [depreciation] is also partly reflecting fundamentals since the current account deficit increased, and the deprecation of the peso last year was clearly not good for trade numbers. As foreign exchange pressures wane, the central bank can dedicate full resources and focus on fighting inflation It [the depreciation] was due to the differential and the fact that people look at the exchange rate and say, “All the corporations that borrow abroad wanted to buy their dollar needs for debt servicing.” So, the moment we give [delivered] the 425 bps [in cumulative] policy rate increase, the peso appreciated. After a long series of policy rate adjustments, year-to-date [from 1 January to 4 April 2023], the peso actually appreciated by 2.0 percent. In other words, the exchange rate is no longer adding to the inflation problem. That is the situation. The policy rate [adjustment] addressed two issues: First, it prevented the knock-on effects of supply shocks from spreading too much. Second, it made the peso stronger, which also significantly reduced inflationary expectations. And, as I said, we did it without any risk that financial stability is reduced.

Title: From ‘Nowhere’ to ‘Now Here’: The CRD as a Step Toward a Vibrant Financing Ecosystem for SMEs
Occasion: Credit Risk Database Scoring Model Launch
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Ambassador Koshikawa Kazuhiko; JICA [Japan International Cooperation Agency] Chief Representative Sakamoto Takema; the Credit Risk Database (CRD) Japan expert team; representatives of participating banks, especially my friend, [Rizal Commercial Banking Corporation Chief Executive Officer] Eugene [Acevedo]; and, of course, the three [industry] associations that we are all familiar with—the association of banks [Bankers Association of the Philippines], thrift banks [Chamber of Thrift Banks], and rural banks [Rural Bankers Association of the Philippines]—to whom I have given two or three speeches in the course of the past year. So, they are probably bored seeing me. Of course, to all our friends and to all BSPers, konnichiwa! It is my honor to join you all today for the launch of the CRD scoring model. We have heard from our esteemed guests, the previous speakers, about the promise of this approach and look forward to the many exciting possibilities. How myopic policies sometimes start with good intentions Now, I am going extemporaneous. One of the reasons that I strongly believe in this [program] is that from the premise that MSMEs [micro, small, and medium enterprises] are important come very bad policies [to support them], like “Banks must lend X percent of their loans to SMEs [small and medium enterprises].” The problem is that it becomes a one-size-fits-all policy. One example of that, for instance, is a highly specialized Japanese bank—with less than a hundred employees and focused on lending to Japanese firms in the Philippines—is being told to lend to MSMEs and agriculture. Most of them end up saying, “We must just pay the fines [for non-compliance].” It is a terrible thing. It is a tax on foreign investors who want to help the Philippine economy—this one-size-fits-all policy. Another terrible example is, rural banks are being penalized for not lending enough to MSMEs when, in fact, some medium-scale industries are bigger than some rural banks. You can see that, sometimes, good intentions lead to bad policies. The last time that I went to Malacañang to join a meeting with the President and his Cabinet, they realized that 70 percent of bank lending is mandated by one law or the other. For that reason, my friend, Eugene, will become a bureaucrat. He will just be following those [loan] allocations. In other words, a better way of doing things must be done. And as with many things, sometimes, little changes could mean a lot. That is why I like [JICA Chief Representative] Sakamoto-san’s way of putting it: “Nowhere” can become “now here” just by putting the letter ‘w’ from the second word to the first. Of course, many changes are not like that where one change changes everything, from “nowhere” to “now here.” I wish many things can become better just by moving one letter, but this is about as close to it as we can get. In the general order of expenditures, it is not a lot but a very big beginning. SMEs turn to families as a way to raise funds The other side of it is my personal experience. I look at my friends who are successful businessmen. Many of them started by borrowing from relatives. If your relatives cannot trust you [to lend you money], who will, right? The worst part is when you have to borrow from your father-in-law. Some of my friends did that; they must have really wanted to go into business to do that. Unfortunately, that is probably worse than collateral-based lending. Not all of us have collateral; not all of us have fathers-in-law who are willing to lend us money. We really have to move away from that. Although there is such a thing as graduation, I noticed that some of these friends of mine [who started by borrowing from relatives] got a good start and have very good financial statements now. After 10 to 20 years [of being in business], they have become very bankable. Banks are fighting over each other to lend them money. But, precisely, the reliance on these things [family relationships] to develop credit will, of course, bypass many people in the economy. What happens if you do not have a father-in-law to lend you money? What happens if you do not have collateral? Shifting to data-driven lending through an analysis of anonymized firm data Clearly, an approach [to lending] that uses [anonymized] data [is key]. A very important word is “anonymous.” There is no such thing as an “anonymous father-in-law,” for instance. As a graduate student of economics, that has always been a holy grail: Knowing the characteristics of a firm, hopefully, predicts where it will locate and so on and so forth. I think, not only will the CRD scoring model contribute to bridging the funding gap for our SMEs, but it will also enhance the credit risk management of financial institutions. From ‘nowhere’ to ‘now here’ If I may, we have a similar related program called the Paleng-QR Ph Plus initiative, which is also driven by the same motivation. That is, if a lot of transactions are done in QR [quick response] payments, this will generate a trail of financial information, which may also improve the quality of lending. Many of these things put together will reduce the temptation of legislators to follow one-size-fits-all policies that are very well-intentioned but have a lot of unintended side effects, as I mentioned earlier. I am very optimistic that “nowhere” will become “now here.” With the help of all our partners, I have no reason to think otherwise. Japan as a reliable partner Before I end, let me thank the government of Japan and JICA for their contributions to the CRD project and their unwavering support of our development efforts. In my previous life, I was NEDA [National Economic and Development Authority] director-general. During this time, when the credit risk [between United States and Philippine sovereign debt] was 600 basis points, no other institution was more important to us and no other country was more important to us than JICA and Japan. They have always been with us when we needed them the most. We thank JICA for extending its technical assistance to the Bangko Sentral for another year, until 31 March 2024. Of course, I also thank our 32 CRD participating banks as well as our partner industry associations, government agencies, and academic and research institutions for making this project possible. Closing message We look forward to continued partnership with all of you to keep the CRD project ever-relevant and responsive to the evolving needs of the financial community. As we move forward, let us keep aflame this shared commitment for the benefit of the SME sector in particular and the Filipino people in general. Arigatou gozaimasu! Maraming salamat po, at mabuhay tayong lahat! ###

Title: Carrying the Digital Financial Inclusion Mission Forward
Occasion: 2023 Digital Financial Inclusion Awards Media Launch
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Mr. Paul Favila of Citi Philippines; Mr. Gilbert Maramba of the Microfinance Council of the Philippines (MCPI); Mr. Paolo Honrado, president of the Rural Bank of Guinobatan; Ms. Lorie Almazora, our Digital Champions–Microentrepreneur Awardee from the first DFIA [Digital Financial Inclusion Awards]; and, of course, to my fellow advocates for financial inclusion, magandang hapon. It is an honor to celebrate the second year of the DFIA with you. The DFIA program is very much aligned with our National Strategy for Financial Inclusion (NSFI), which aims to promote inclusive digital finance. Nurturing digital-ready entrepreneurs As the chair of the interagency Financial Inclusion Steering Committee that implements the NSFI 2022–2028, the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) remains steadfast in leading digitalization efforts and programs to support MSMEs [micro, small, and medium enterprises] and the microfinance sector. Let me cite some of the BSP’s initiatives in this area. First, our Paleng-QR Ph Plus program maximizes the QR [quick response] Ph initiative and its use in wet markets, local transportation, and business establishments. To date, 13 local government units (LGUs) have issued ordinances in support of this program, and we have launched it in seven cities—Baguio, Davao, Tagbilaran, Naga, Lapu-Lapu, Pasig, and Bacolod—and I am glad to share that several other LGUs are considering respective rollouts. We also actively support the implementation of our national ID [identification] system, PhilSys. By the way, the importance of this ID is very clear: Without e-money [electronic money], the number of people who have formal banking accounts will be less than half of what it is now. In addition, we continue to promote technical assistance for the digitalization initiatives of the microfinance sector. With the NSFI 2022–2028 as our guide, we hope to cover more ground with our partners and stakeholders. Call to join DFIA 2023 This awards program gives us the venue to highlight our inclusive digital finance agenda and microfinance advocacy—not to mention all the inspiring stories that we saw and heard when the awards were given. We encourage everyone to submit nominations for this year’s DFIA. Last year, we received a total of 53 nominees—47 of which are microentrepreneurs and six are micro-finance institutions. With your help, we hope to receive more this year. In closing, let me express our sincere gratitude and appreciation to Citi Philippines and MCPI. I hope that we can continue to build on this fruitful collaboration as we carry our digital financial inclusion mission forward. Thank you very much, and good afternoon.

Title: What Sticky Inflation Means for Monetary Policy in Emerging Asia
Occasion: Governors Talks at the International Monetary Fund–World Bank 2023 Spring Meetings
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ First of all, thank you for inviting me. It is a privilege to be here as the representative of Asia’s [central bank] governors, although, as you said, [inflation in] the Philippines is quite different from the rest of Asia. Defining ‘sticky’ inflation I would first like to qualify what is meant by “sticky.” Sticky inflation really is a lot of shocks. It is like the old wrestling days with a tag team. When one guy gets tired of beating the other guy, his partner comes in and replaces him. In this case, it is really a tag team [of supply shocks]. You had the Ukraine-Russia war. Then, later on, a lot of agricultural shocks. It started with sugar, later on, vegetables, meat, and fruits, partly because of very protectionist [agricultural] policies. Of course, farmers are poor; therefore, there were a lot of restrictions on imports, and there was a delay in issuing import permits. What happened is when you look at the 12 months, starting from March last year to February [this year], only one month1 was the month-on-month (m-o-m) inflation [within or] lower than 4.0 percent, divided by 12 [months in a year]. 4.0 percent is the high end of our [inflation] target because of all the shocks coming together. Clearly, when you have high inflation because of all the supply shocks, this will eventually travel to the services sector. If food is expensive, you have to pay higher wages. If fuel is expensive, you have to pay higher wages. So, with a six-month lag, service sector inflation went up as well. Deploying all weapons in expanded toolkit We [the central bank] decided to use all our possible weapons. First, we let the market move the exchange rate, which is still [a] textbook [response]. The other one is we raised the policy rate, which is also textbook. The other one is we intervened [in the foreign exchange (forex) markets] because the exchange rate was moving too much because of United States (US) monetary policy. What happened was, we ended up increasing policy rates from 200 basis points (bps) to 625 bps. This exaggerates the movement because the 200 bps [policy rate during the pandemic] was quite abnormal, to begin with. The normal policy rate in the Philippines is more like 3.5 percent, but because of COVID, we brought it down to 200 bps. In addition, our balance sheet expanded by nearly 20 percent because we purchased a lot of government securities. Because of COVID, a lot of people were fleeing to cash, selling bonds. We did not like bond prices to fall too much. So, we bought bonds. This is the context. We ended up intervening [in the forex market] quite a bit. I will not say when and how much. We used to call it “foreign exchange participation,” but after a time, we already began to use “intervention” to more appropriately characterize our actions. Results of decisive monetary action seen in easing m-o-m inflation and strengthening currency It [the mix of policy actions] worked. With the high policy rates, from January to October last year, the peso depreciated by about 13 percent against the US dollar. By October, because of the policy rate increases, it appreciated [on a year-to-date basis]. The last two prints of [m-o-m] inflation are quite encouraging. The March price index is actually slightly lower than the January price index. With seasonal adjustments, they are just about the same. Technically, we had zero inflation for two months. As you know, year-on-year is the sum of 12 m-o-m [figures], divided by 12 [months]. The last two [months] were good, but the first ten [months] were terrible. If we have more of this [within or below 0.3 percent m-o-m inflation], we should be back to below 4.0 percent by November or December [2023]. Strong prudential regulation, good governance cast the Philippines in a different boat from the US in navigating tradeoff between price and financial stability The question that I am often asked is: “Why have the high policy rates not been a cause of worry for financial stability?” Because, as you know, if interest rates rise, bond prices fall, and the holders of the bond will take a loss. In our case, because to begin with, we were starting [with a policy rate] from 2.0 percent—or even 3.5 percent if you take a normal view—unlike the US, Europe, and Japan, who were starting with zero [percent policy rate]. Therefore, the loss in bond prices is much higher. Also, our more typical [bond] issue is five years and ten years. So, the loss in [bond] value due to changing interest rates is smaller. In the US, they have 30-year bonds. The other thing is our regulation of banks. We have capital adequacy ratios. We have liquidity coverage ratios, net stable funding ratios. The banks, by and large, have very strong balance sheets. With me is Monetary Board Member Tony Abacan, who used to be the president of [one of] the biggest bank[s] in the Philippines, Metrobank. Our banks are run by people who have a very long memory of [the] 1997 [financial crisis]. They [have learned to] hate mismatches, even without us [at the central bank] telling them. We were able to raise policy rates. The banks took a loss in their holdings of government securities, but their profits [as a whole] increased because of the higher lending rate. By and large, the banks are very stable [despite tightening financial conditions]. In summary, if you use all your weapons, your primary weapon for inflation can be used to its full potential. The primary weapon is still interest rates, but this must be supported by good prudential regulation and buffers because foreign exchange markets can go crazy, and the currency issuer of the world [the US] is driven by very domestic concerns, [like] what happens to Iowa. Clearly, the policy rates in the US, they do not care what happens to the world in general. They are very targeted to what is happening in the US. You have to be ready. Therefore, having buffers is very important. International Monetary Fund (IMF) Asia-Pacific Department Director Krishna Srinivasan: You are saying that financial stability risks are less of a concern right now, compared to other countries in the region. You said that banks have already taken the loss on this. So, you are less concerned about the financial stability risk? In that sense, do you not see a tradeoff between financial stability and inflation in the Philippines? Yes. I am old enough to remember LIBOR [London interbank offered rate] going 20 percent. We are nowhere near that. With [former US Federal Reserve Chair Paul] Volcker, inflation was so high. He raised interest rates, and effectively, many countries started changing [their] monetary policy. The impact on the world was so huge. That is the other difference: Central bankers in Asia do remember 1997. The regulators do remember 1997, and our regulations, therefore, [reflect that]. IMF: Assuming that the tightening cycle is coming to an end, given the fact that [m-o-m] inflation in the last two months is coming down, what role does communication play here? It is very important that the public knows why the central bank is doing what it is doing. Therefore, people say, “Of course, interest rates have to be high because the [elevated] inflation has to be controlled.” People have to also understand that some degree of exchange rate volatility is actually quite useful, and they trust the central bank enough to know when it is too much. They realize that the fiscal authorities themselves must be doing the[ir part of the] job. The perception of the risk that the Philippines will default is quite low because, again, I am old enough to remember when the spread [between the Philippine and the US sovereign debt] was 600 [bps]. Imagine, the rate of the US Treasury is 600, and when we borrow abroad, we pay 600 plus 600 [in premium]. These are the other things that people must remember. It is very important to have your macroeconomic and monetary policy be very prudent and actually follow time-tested policies. IMF: In the context of inflation being very high for the last 8–10 months, what role has fiscal policy played in trying to protect the poor from the impact of inflation? Two things: First, we actually have a conditional cash transfer [system] for families with young children to make sure that they will be kept in school. We call it “4Ps: Pantawid Pamilyang Pilipino Program,” which [translated] is “bridges for families.” [Second], during the pandemic, we had a registry of people in need who were, [in turn], given cash after the lockdowns prevented them from working. The problem was cash collections were down and expenses were up. Fortunately, the perception of risk that the Philippines will default seems not to have changed despite the fact that our deficit-to-GDP [gross domestic product] ratio had gone from 3.0 percent to about 7.0 percent. I give credit to our Ministry of Finance. They actually publicly announced that the goal is to bring it [the deficit-to-GDP ratio] down to 3.0 percent by the end of the [term of the] current president. So, the government must actually show that it is behaving responsibly [when it comes to fiscal matters] and that the central bank is regulating banks without any political intervention, and that it is a truly committed inflation-targeting central bank. IMF: In some sense, the fiscal support provided is very targeted to people who are hit hardest by inflation? And by the virus. Remember, a lot of people could not work because they were not allowed to leave their homes or, at least, their neighborhoods. Many of them have very little savings. It [the cash assistance] may look small. It could be, let us say, ₱5,000, which is around a hundred dollars, but that is the difference between them going hungry and not going hungry. IMF: [In] the need for coordination between central banks and the fiscal authorities in the context of the pandemic and the latest shocks we have had, was there any explicit framework that you had? That is a good question. The first thing we did

Title: The ADB as the Philippines’ Trusted Partner in Nation-Building
Occasion: Opening remarks for the Business Session at the 56th ADB Annual Meeting in Incheon
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Let me start by expressing our appreciation to the ADB (Asian Development Bank) and the Republic of Korea for hosting this meeting. By the way, I walked around a lot since I arrived, and I am very impressed by Incheon’s parks and buffers between vehicular noise in the highways and residential areas. I would like to thank the officials of Incheon and its people for their hospitality. Bright growth prospects for the Philippines and developing economies The theme, “Rebounding Asia: Recover, Reconnect, and Reform” is the best way to summarize what Asian economies must do in the coming years. Recent forecasts of various international and multilateral institutions, including the ADB, highlight the strong recovery of developing Asian economies. The Philippines, for instance, grew by 7.6 percent last year. We expect GDP (gross domestic product) to grow by 6.0-7.0 percent this year and 6.5-8.0 percent in 2024 to 2028. With regards to inflation, the latest outturns are still high, but we are quite confident that inflation – although it remains elevated – will revert towards the mid-point of our target by next year. Nevertheless, the risk to inflation, especially because of possible global shocks and tensions, maybe even the El Niño, continue to lean toward the upside for 2023 and 2024. Therefore, going forward, the central bank remains vigilant against inflation risk over the policy horizon. Policy priorities for sustained economic development In line with the Philippine Development Plan (PDP), the strategies of the Philippine government will focus on modernizing agriculture, expanding agribusiness, encouraging private sector participation in infrastructure development, promoting digital transformation, and enhancing the competitiveness of local industries, among others. The ADB as a partner in nation-building We acknowledge that the ADB has been relentless in its efforts to alleviate poverty and social inequality, tackle climate change, enhance resilience and sustainability, and foster growth across the Asia-Pacific region. For that, we thank the ADB for its continuous support to the Philippines. In particular, the ADB has been a strong partner in the pursuit of the Philippines development agenda. Recently, the ADB held a consultation with our national government to present the Bank’s proposed themes for new country partnerships focusing on intensifying climate action, which the Philippines needs, because we will no longer build new [coal-fired] power plants. In this regard, the ADB’s energy transition mechanism is very important. Second, it is investing in climate-smart transportation and digital transformation. Third, of course is investing in Filipinos. These clearly are well-within and quite consistent with ADB’s policies and agenda. Prudent fiscal management While the contributions of the ADB provide tremendous financing support, for our part, it is imperative that strong fiscal consolidation strategies be undertaken. This fiscal consolidation strategy will bring down our debt-to-GDP ratio from the current 63.5 percent to less than 60 percent by 2025 and cut the deficit-to-GDP ratio from the current 6.4 percent to 3.0 percent by 2028. At the same time, the government will Build Better More as it remains committed to the goal of ramping up spending on public infrastructure and completing on schedule especially ongoing projects in all parts of the country within the next six years. With target infrastructure disbursements carefully recalibrated in consideratio​​n of the available fiscal space, government spending in the [infrastructure] sector is expected to reach pre-pandemic levels by the end of the term of the current president. Coupled with the tighter fiscal program, this is expected to signal stronger economic growth in the medium-term. Multilateral cooperation and concessional funding and assistance given to developing member countries will remain to be the most effective tools in ensuring a sustainable and inclusive recovery. We commend the ADB efforts in exploring initiatives that will increase its lending capacity. Taking a whole-of-government and whole-of-society approach to public issues Climate action and navigating policy issues surrounding it require a whole-of-society approach. The ADB needs to find ways to seamlessly restructure finance in such a way that it will be a greater enabler for greener and more resilient growth for its member countries. As we continue to see developments in this dynamic region, we will continue to see member countries [meet their] evolving needs. Let us continue to work together in envisioning our own path of development and ushering improved social and economic well-being for our people. ​ Thank you very much.

Title: Enhancing Policy Calibration for Macro-Financial Resilience
Occasion: High-level seminar organized by the 2023 Association of Southeast Asian Nations Chair–Bank Indonesia in Bali, Indonesia
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: First of all, I would like to thank our host, especially [Bank Indonesia] Governor Perry [Warjiyo] and [Finance] Minister Sri Mulyani. You have a wonderful place here. And, of course, Bali is a very easy city. Love the beaches, love the culture, love the dancers, the gongs, and so on and so forth. I will avoid using jargon, which is the title. What I will do instead is talk about the Philippines, and I have only two charts. For those of you who are students of economics, I will start with the so-called “Tinbergen principle,” which is: If you have five objectives, you must have, at least, five tools. The other way to put it is: It is very hard to shoot two birds with one stone. The other reason [why] you need a lot of tools is that sometimes, the medicine that is good for an illness could have [unintended] side effects. Therefore, you try to find another medicine that will not have side effects. Sometimes, you may actually settle for the weaker medicine, which has a weaker side effect. In this sense, we economists are people who wanted to be doctors of medicine but ended up being doctors of economics. Priorities of an inflation-targeting central bank Now, the other thing that I would say is that for an inflation-targeting central bank, in general, the exchange rate is not important until it is important. So, that is a bit of a contradiction, but what it means is that study after study shows that when the exchange rate is moving up and down—little changes [in the exchange rate]—when you look at its effects on inflation, it is nearly zero. And it is to the credibility of central banks, like the Bank Indonesia, Bank of Thailand, and the central bank of the Philippines, that that is the case. I remember when exchange rate changes had such large effects on inflation. And as we adopted inflation-targeting, that [exchange rate pass-through] became less and less so. Also, we believe that reserves are important because we know that money from abroad can be very fickle. Much of that money is with central banks when these are not needed [during times of economic stability]. So, it would be better to have these reserves when those times [of difficulty] come. Maximizing use of expanded toolkit Now, to talk about these choices. As you can see, there are three indicators there. The first one is the change in the policy rate. Sad to say or good to say, the Philippines is the topnotcher there. We increased our policy rate from 2.0 percent or 200 basis points (bps) to 6.25 percent or 625 bps. The next country, South Korea, [to raise their policy rate] is much lower [at 275 bps]. On the other hand, the Philippines, like Indonesia, had appreciating currencies since January 2023, but we had a tremendous drop in the currency from January last year to August last year when the US [United States Federal Reserve] started their jumbo increases in the policy rate. To keep it simple, the explanation is if a peso instrument is being compared to a US dollar instrument, other things being equal, one would prefer the dollar instrument if the interest rates are the same. Like it or not, the dollar is a safe haven. In the case of the Philippines, the markets tend to doubt the peso when the difference between the policy rate of the BSP, the Bangko Sentral [ng Pilipinas], is less than 1.0 to 1.25 percent higher than the Fed [funds rate]. So, when the Fed started raising its rates, the peso started to fall, and in just nine months, the peso fell by 13 percent from January last year to September. Of course, this necessitated a response from the BSP. Grappling with domestic and international supply-side pressures on inflation To make matters worse, the Philippines experienced a number of shortages. Of course, the Ukraine-Russia war also caused some shortages, [such as] shortage of fertilizer, but, later on, we were quite unlucky. I call the inflation that we had “shortage of many little things.” Sugar [had a] 100 percent increase in prices. Onions—by the way, the onions, as you can see, the small ones look like earrings. They are so expensive that you can use them as earrings. And then, Asian swine flu, [which resulted in] shortage of meat. And then, if pork becomes expensive, chicken will be expensive, too, [because] people will be shifting. So, all those things [are] relatively small part of the basket but [their prices are] increasing by lots. These were terrible, terrible supply shocks. In the last 13 months, ending last January [2023], every month [except December 2022], prices were increasing by at least 0.4 percent [on a seasonally adjusted basis]. Of course, 0.4 times 12 [months] is almost 5.0 percent, whereas [the upper band of] our inflation target is just 4.0 percent. Taking decisive monetary action to re-anchor inflationary expectations In this sense, the BSP needed to act quite aggressively. We had two meetings where we had [raised the policy rate by] 75 bps [in July 2022 and November 2022]; meetings where we had [raised the policy rate by] 50 bps [in August 2022, September 2022, December 2022, and February 2023]; and several meetings where we had [raised the policy rate by] 25 bps [in May 2022, June 2022, and March 2023]. By the way, I became [the central bank] governor because the [former] governor became the secretary of finance. It is like the two of you traded places [referring to Governor Perry and Minister Sri]. I have just been in the central bank [as governor] for six months, and I increased policy rates more than the two previous governors. But [these actions] really re-anchored [inflationary] expectations and stabilized the peso. By January to December last year, from 13.0 percent [in January to September 2022], it [the depreciation] became [narrowed to] 9.0 percent. Just like Indonesia [whose currency appreciated by 2.8 percent as of 27 March 2023], from January this year to the current date, we [the peso] appreciated by about 2.7 percent [over the same period]. We are literally twins there, right? The only difference is that we [the Philippines] did more policy rate increases [compared to Indonesia]. Well-capitalized, well-run banks as a source of strength; robust inflows provide cushion against external shocks Now, a question I am always asked is: “The US [Fed] increased policy rate and caused the failure of Silicon Valley Bank, [is there a risk that situation may also happen in the Philippines?]” Because, then, as interest rates increased, the value of securities fall. And my classic answer to that is: This time, the Philippine banks are strong. Remember, when we were hit by COVID because we have one of the longest and stringent quarantines, our economy contracted nearly 17 percent in the second quarter [of 2020] and 9.5 percent for the full year in 2020. By the way, since we were expecting the economy to grow by 6.0 percent [that year], it was really a 16-percent contraction. And yet, [our banks remain strong] because the banks were well capitalized, because we adopted Basel standards very, very quickly, and because the people who are running the banks were probably the vice presidents or the managing directors in 1997. So, they all knew the memory of the Asian financial crisis. To make things even better, we had a boom in BPO [business process outsourcing] plus the remittances. So, we had recurring account surpluses. With the inflows coming as a result of very expansive monetary policies in the US, our reserves increased from US$45 billion to US$90 billion. The central bank bought a lot of dollars. Of course, we will end up increasing money supply. So, we increased reserve requirements to 20 percent, which was terrible in the long run but good in the short run. Now, when we decided to cut the reserve requirements—because it is not a good idea to overtax the sector that you are regulating—when we did that, we also introduced new regulations, which we call liquidity regulations. So, the banks were also quite liquid because of our regulations and also because of the timing of the reduction of reserve requirements. So, the banks were well-capitalized and quite liquid. Therefore, we are confident that 425 bps [in cumulative policy rate adjustments], probably even more, can be done without risking financial stability. In short, we have the policy rate [as our main tool] for inflation, and we have the banking regulations for stability, and we were able to achieve it. And because we believed that reserves were important, we strategically built up our reserves. Month-on-month inflation figures suggest signs of normalization Let me now go to my last chart. Inflation remains a top concern and, in fact, from the point of view of policy rates, the only concern. We are quite confident that the medicine [policy rate adjustments] will not have negative side effects on financial stability because the banks are strong and liquid—that is the second bullet. Therefore, we have the luxury of aiming for inclusiveness and digitalization. By the way, our last two prints of [headline] inflation were 8.7 percent in January 2023 and 8.6 percent in February 2023, but it looks much better month-on-month. [From] January to February, [it was] zero percent. This means [inflation for] January this year is much higher than January last year, and [inflation for] February this year is much higher than February last year—but [for] January to February [in 2023], it seems to be stabilizing already. One of the main reasons for the shortages [driving inflation] is we protect farmers too much from imports, and the President has decided to let those imports in. So, more onions from Indonesia, I suppose? Building in resilience while keeping an eye on the horizon In other words, we are resilient. That is not the same as saying we never lose sleep at night because anything can hit you. We, for example, have these regular [stress test] exercises. Imagine if conglomerate X fails. Then let’s say X is a big bank. X is big in real estate, etc. And then another one, X is

Title: Growth amid Headwinds: Balancing Inflation and Economic Growth in the Philippines
Occasion: First General Membership Meeting of the Money Market Association of the Philippines
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ To the BSP’s [Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas] partners from the Money Market Association of the Philippines (MART), ladies and gentlemen, magandang gabi sa inyong lahat. Before I start, let me share with you a conversation I was having on my table. Where is the letter ‘T’ in [the acronym of the] Money Market Association of the Philippines? There is one at the end of the word “market.” So, we were wondering why your acronym is “MART.” The important point, though, is that it works. You may not know the exact meaning of the acronym, but you know exactly what it represents. In a sense, the Philippine economy is like that. It is not the most conventional economy. Indeed, it is an economy where the services sector became very large before there was any full-blown industrialization—one case of many unique things about the country. Thank you for inviting me to be your guest of honor and speaker at your First General Membership Meeting. As always, we value these conversations with you—various associations—where we are interested in the same things but not necessarily seeing them in the same way or from the same point of view or mindset. Putting these together, these different mindsets give us a better grasp of policy. Navigating the delicate balance between controlling inflation and keeping output loss to a minimum The theme for this presentation is balancing inflation and economic growth. I should say that there are times when there is nothing to balance. For instance, if the cause of inflation is excess demand, then [the act of postponing addressing inflation [through policy rate adjustments] does not have any sacrifice [in terms of output loss] and does not result in any gain in output. All you do is postpone the pain of adjustment [later on]. There is no tradeoff. It is either you take the pain now [or later]. The tradeoff is when you take the pain of output loss now to control inflation or take the pain of output loss later to contain inflation. As in most businesses, postponing treatment makes [administering] the cure more painful. Worse, it makes the probability of a successful cure lower. Fortunately, in the case of the Philippines, the cause of [above-target] inflation is not excess demand. That is the advantage of having an independent central bank, which I will discuss later. When supply-side pressures warrant a decisive monetary response In this case, there has to be some tradeoffs, which is—to the extent that the inflation is [driven by] supply shocks—the fear that supply shocks can morph into self-fulfilling higher inflation. Supply shocks cause prices to rise. Rising prices increase inflationary expectations, which, in turn, can cause higher inflation. Therefore, we are afraid that second-order effects make a very hard choice of using higher interest rates to address supply shocks. That is, if you do not raise interest rates to respond to supply shocks, then you may have a bigger problem later. On the other hand, if it turns out that the supply shocks are like viruses that could cure themselves, then, maybe, [we can] just try staying at home, wrapping ourselves in a very thick kumot (blanket) and wait for the fever to go down by itself. That is the problem. There is a possibility that it is self-curing, and you do not have to do anything. Of course, you do not know. So, you can make two mistakes: One is that it [supply-side driven inflation] is self-curing, and you tried to cure it, then there is output loss. The other mistake is that it is not self-curing, and it can become a spiral. If you have postponed addressing it, inflation expectations become so deeply embedded that getting rid of it becomes harder. It is like if you did not treat the disease, it is now [spreading] all over your body; therefore, the medicine that has to be given will [have to] be more powerful. In that sense, there is a balancing act. The balancing act is really the result of economics—like a medicine [from] 200, 300 years ago where your doctor is more likely to kill you than cure you. Precisely, the balancing is the result of great uncertainty on what is really needed. On top of that, [there is] the great uncertainty about how [inflationary] expectations are formed and the extent to which the central bank is capable of changing how people form expectations when it really matters. Clearly, then—and this is how I look at it as an economist, even predominantly supply shock-caused inflation must require some form of monetary response. That is where I will start. Faced with a challenging macroeconomic environment, I hope that this presentation will shed light on the BSP’s policy actions, which are anchored on three pillars and, in turn, contribute to sustaining economic recovery. Central bank independence and inflation-targeting: The Philippine experience As I already said, the Philippine central bank has the benefit of being an independent central bank. It has used this period of independence to establish its [inflation-targeting] credibility, and it also has used this period of independence not to follow the textbook [definition]. The textbook says: An inflation-targeting central bank should not care too much about the exchange rate. If we had followed that, we would have let the peso appreciate to ₱35 [against the dollar] when the US Fed [United States Federal Reserve] started quantitative easing and negative interest rates. Then [BSP] Governor [Amando] Tetangco [Jr.] thought that that was not the case. If you allowed excessive appreciation, the result later will be excessive depreciation. Excessive appreciation will cause an output loss because wages will not go down when the currency is appreciating. That is when wages are “downward rigid.” So, excessive appreciation is harmful. To his credit, he [former Governor Tetangco] used that period to prevent the peso from appreciating too much [by building up the BSP’s foreign exchange (forex) reserves]. International reserves went from US$45 billion to over US$90 billion in a very brief period of less than four years, but that resulted in another problem. As we buy dollars, we either borrow or print pesos to buy them. To make matters worse, the law prevented us from buying from the public. Every time we buy dollars, we either print pesos or borrow money from banks. The problem is that the banks can always change their mind and lend the money. The liquidity that we created is [what] we owe the banks, but the banks can change their minds and say, “Wait a minute. I no longer want to deposit my money with the BSP.” When I was on the Monetary Board, we had special deposit accounts that reached ₱2.3 trillion. It is not surprising because forex reserves went from US$45 billion to US$90 billion. We were thinking that if the banks changed their minds and started withdrawing and lending that money, we will have problems on our hands. Fortunately, we now have the power to borrow from the public. That is one thing we learned. We owe money from the public that will not become loans. If we owed money from banks and the banks decide to withdraw the money and lend, you will now have what is called the “money multiplier process gone wild.” Significant and one-way changes in the exchange rate posed risks to price stability The other side of it is when the peso depreciates too much, our fear is that the translation of peso depreciation [exchange rate pass-through] to inflation, which is [usually] very low, will become high. Our measurement shows that during the inflation-targeting period, a ₱1 change [depreciation] in the exchange rate will increase inflation by 0.076 ppt [percentage point] in the short run.1 This means that when the exchange rate goes up and down—[or] is not going in any particular direction—and all the changes are very small, people do not bother to change prices because changing the price is also “expensive,” especially if after you raise it, you have to bring it down again. If you raise prices and by the next month, you have to reduce them again, you will just confuse your buyers. Now, what happened was quite different. In just nine months, from January 2022 to October 2022, the peso-dollar rate moved [depreciated] by [a peak of] 13 percent. As you can see in the chart, our first mandate is price stability, and we were not sure that the translation of the changes in exchange rate to inflation will remain small. In the words of macroeconomists, the translation of changes in the exchange rate to inflationary expectations has changed. If you look at countries in Latin America, that is a problem. [A significant] change in the exchange rate [results in] higher prices. Higher prices [beget] changes in the exchange rates with very little gain—because what really matters is the exchange rate, divided by the price, right? If the exchange rate has doubled but the price has [also] doubled, the real terms of the exchange rate did not make it more competitive. These are the concerns we have. My main topic would be the first pillar, but I will not be giving a good picture without discussing the two other pillars. The third pillar: Bringing the benefits of digital payments within and among countries The third pillar—a safe, secure, and efficient payments and settlements system—used to be a boring topic. You have the PhilPaSS [Philippine Payment and Settlement System], then you have the oldest technology, which is printing coins. You actually see that in the Bible when Christ was asked who should be [featured] in the coin. Christ said, “It should be Ceasar.” Not him, not God. After all, the king will hang you if you print money, which is his sole monopoly. It [payments and settlements] is becoming exciting because of the rise in digital payments. You can see this in the two forms of payments [systems] that we have: [First is] InstaPay, which is almost instantaneous. InstaPay is what we call our fast payment system. It has different names in different countries. In another country [Singapore], it is

Title: The New Frontier of Financial Stability: Global Problems, Global Solutions, Local Challenges
Occasion: Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas-International Monetary Fund Conference on Financial Stability
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: To our colleagues from the IMF [International Monetary Fund], thank you for [co-hosting] this event; fellow central bankers from all over the world—some of them guests from multilateral institutions; friends from the banking industry; ladies and gentlemen, good morning. We say “good morning” differently around here. Where I came from, it is magandang umaga. Here [in Cebu]—it means exactly the same thing—it is maayong buntag. It is my pleasure to welcome all of you to the second BSP [Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas]-IMF Conference on Financial Stability. Today, we have gathered over 140 participants from 14 central banks and financial authorities, six regional and global organizations, and 31 private sector organizations. Let me also take this time to thank the 22 regional and market leaders who will lead the discussions over five sessions.
