Four years ago, in 2019, we held the first BSP-IMF Financial Stability Conference in Manila. That is why we are holding it here now [in Cebu]; it is nicer here. During that time, we talked about potential systemic risk issues, practical approaches to policy-making, and financial stability frameworks. Little did we know that barely a year after, we would have a pandemic and very long lockdowns that happened in the Philippines because we had no vaccines [at that time]. Lessons learned from past crises Fast forward to today, the environment has changed—improved somewhat—but the pressures and risks remain. As much as things have changed, there is no greater lesson than the experience itself. What lessons have we learned? First, we Asians will always look at [the Asian Financial Crisis of] 1997. Much of our attitude toward many things was shaped by that. If you look at it, we cannot have enough foreign exchange [forex] reserves. Many things have happened since then. So, we built up our forex reserves. We refined the rules on bank capitalization. Of course, since banking by nature is [a business of] maturity mismatch, which does not matter during good times but matters a lot during bad times, we [at the central bank] become the liquidity provider of last resort. Not only do we regulate capitalization, but we also regulate liquidity. Of course, going the other extreme is [if the central bank goes around] telling the banks: “[Achieve] perfect match [by changing] interest structure and asset structure.” But [if we do that], they will not be called “banks”; they will be called “bureaucracies.” That is what I heard from my banker friends. So, it is finding the middle—[where] there is enough creativity [encouraged] but without thinking in terms of the public cost of mistakes. Sad to say, the people who pay for mistakes are often not those that made those mistakes. Examining how crises have impacted the economy and banking sector: 1997 vs. 2020 The fruits of our labor can be seen in the [varying degree of] impact of crises on the economy and banking sector. In [the Asian Financial Crisis of] 1997, GDP [gross domestic product] contracted by just 0.5 percent, but, of course, it was a big [banking] crisis. Of course, estimates vary, but growth was slower by 1.0–2.0 percent for at least seven years. That is why you could see a spike in Philippine economic growth sometime in 2005 to 2006. Of course, it is very hard to attribute it to that because we also did a lot of good things at that time, like we expanded VAT [value-added tax]. We had a lot of fiscal reforms. But although the collapse of GDP was only 0.5 percent [in 1997] compared to almost 10-percent [GDP contraction in 2020] because of COVID, the period of balance sheet repair was a long period of sub-par economic growth. When you look at the cost that way, the Asian crisis was quite costly. In 2020, GDP contracted by 9.5 percent for the full-year period, but in one quarter [Q2 2020] when we had lockdowns, it actually contracted by 17 percent. It should have grown. That means, it contracted by a fifth. Imagine, the economy contracting by a fifth! But banks remained strong and ready to lend once mobility restrictions were lifted. We had a 5.6-percent growth [in 2021] with the partial lifting of restrictions. And then, with the almost full lifting of restrictions, [we experienced growth of] 7.6 percent [in 2022]. So, you can see: The big difference, I think, is the banks were healthy in spite of the [almost] 10-percent [GDP contraction in 2020.] Well, if you factor in what could have been, maybe, [it was] a 15-percent hit from the crisis. When cracks in the system form One has to say, then, that financial resilience and financial stability are related but different things. Indeed, in the case of the latter, it is better to say it in the negative—financial instability. We all know that the roots of that [instability] form during long periods of stability. People become complacent. People forget what happened in the past. As the old bankers retire, the new ones do not have their memory anymore. The best example of that is the United States. Who remembers 1932? The importance of defined government structures and getting the public on board The lesson is: We must understand this, and we should not be limited to bankers and regulators. The general public matters as well. The advantage of the general public understanding this is that they would be more supportive of the regulations. Sometimes, during good times, you hear a complaint, “Oh, you central banks! You regulate too much. You are turning us into a bureaucracy.” But part and parcel of everything is the structure of society. We were just discussing this among our central bankers. I heard that some of our colleagues in the central bank are also the treasurer of the government and the manager of the funds. Clearly, they have a harder job. Clearly, countries having the right structures [is also important] so that the probability of terrible events happening [are reduced]. And when terrible events do happen, we have the resilience to respond to them. Conference objectives What are the conference objectives? Can Asia take a collective view on systemic risk issues? Is there a way forward? What do we do with spillovers across borders and within the region? But we all share the desire to find and work toward a common solution and learn from each other. That is why this conference gives us the valuable opportunity to talk and listen to one another. We will take stock of the state of play on systemic risk management, how stakeholders have coped with changing market conditions, and the latest tools available. Of course, we want to have the tools ready before it happens. The worst thing is, you are designing the tools after it happens. [Then, we have] the tricky issue of communication and messaging. At the end of all of these, the issues will be about the execution of the things that you prepared for during good times so that when the bad times happen, you are able to minimize the damage and return to a normal state of growth more quickly. As we learned from COVID, how quickly we bounce back from the 10-percent contraction of the economy [in 2020 was] because the banks were strong when it happened. I look forward to having rich and free-flowing discussions, and I hope that our breaks will be great opportunities to make friends and for old friends to catch up. Welcome to the beautiful island of Mactan. Maraming salamat, at mabuhay po tayong lahat! ###

Title: Beyond the Numbers: The Central Bank’s Policy Actions During and Beyond the Pandemic
Occasion: JPMorgan Investor Seminar at the sidelines of the International Monetary Fund-World Bank Spring Meetings
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Thanks very much, Mr. Aziz. Should I call you Jahangir? To your team and clients, thank you for the opportunity to present to you, and magandang umaga, which is “good morning” in our language. You are right. We were badly hit by the pandemic. We probably had the longest and strictest lockdown in the world. The worst quarter was [in Q2 2020 when there was] a nearly 17-percent [economic] contraction, relative to the same quarter last year. That was where we are coming from, and both the central bank and fiscal authorities, of course, did everything in their power to reduce the impact [of the pandemic] on the economy. Imagine, if we had not done what we did, maybe, it would have been a 25-percent contraction. The problem was, of course, if the vaccine came late. The reaction was to lock everybody down. Taking decisive action on the monetary front So, our response [at the central bank] was extraordinary. For the first time, the central bank lent money directly to the government. Under our system, this requires Congressional approval. Of course, the loan has been paid back already. In addition, because people were panicking and moving to cash, [in order] to prevent bond prices from falling too much, we also bought government securities. Because normally as the Philippine economy needs more money, we purchase foreign exchange (forex), which supplies more money to the economy. In this particular time and space, the central bank not only lent money directly to the government, but it also bought government securities and cut the policy rates to 200 basis points (bps). Growth surprised on the upside When the vaccine came and hospitalization rates fell drastically, there was normalization, which just happened last year. Because of all the things that we have done to prevent the economy from going down and all the stimulus that came in, the economy turned out very, very strong last year, surprising everyone. I think, the World Bank [2022 gross domestic product (GDP)] forecast was 6.0 percent, and the [forecast of the] IMF [International Monetary Fund] was even lower, but the actual growth [for 2022] was 7.6 percent. That [recovery] was largely due to revenge spending and pent-up demand. Exit strategy to unwind extraordinary support measures that were already in place When we cut the policy rates by 400 bps, we already had an exit plan. As soon as the economy recovers—no matter what the inflation is—we will go to a more normal policy rate. The more normal policy rate is higher than the midpoint of our [2.0–4.0 percent] inflation target. If our [midpoint of the] target inflation is 3.0 percent, the policy rate should be higher than 3.0 percent. In other words, we were starting from a policy rate that was quite abnormal, to begin with. We would have raised it [the policy rate] from 2.0 percent to 3.5 percent even if there was no problem because that was our plan from the very beginning. Once the pandemic is no longer a problem for the economy, there is no need for all these things [accommodative measures]. That was the plan—a gradual exit. Global developments pushed the carefully crafted exit plan ahead of schedule Of course, Uncle Sam changed all of that. We never thought that the US Fed [United States Federal Reserve] will do four 75 bps [hikes] in a row at that time. The exit was a lot faster and sooner than we thought. The other thing is the [Ukraine-Russia] war, which made the [prices of] gasoline and flour very expensive. This was the first supply shock. And these two together forced the BSP [Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas] to be very aggressive. As you can see, inflation in the Philippines hit 8.7 percent at the peak [in January 2023]. Month-on-month inflation rose as supply shocks bite To me, the more telling numbers are the green bars [referring to month-on-month (m-o-m) inflation]. You can see, by March [2022], there is the Ukraine war. [So, m-o-m inflation was] 0.9 percent. Annualized, that is more than 10 percent. By April [2022], [we experienced] another 0.9 percent [in m-o-m inflation]. We have not seen this number of shocks in a 10-month period. I looked back at our record as an inflation-targeting central bank. The typical [supply] shock is half as big and half as long as this. That is why in the past, when we have supply shocks, inflation will exceed the inflation target. And, maybe, the longest [streak] is 15 months in a row. This time, we think, it [inflation] will exceed it [the target for] 19–20 months. In fact, we thought that in December 2022, it [the peak] was already happening because the m-o-m [inflation] normalized. But then, we were hit by another shock. After the fuel shock, of course, in the Philippines, the cause was onions. We had [one of] the most expensive onions in the world. In other words, it looks like inflation is sticky. In reality, we were just unlucky. [There was] a series of punches coming. The other one was the African Swine Flu. [Since] Asians do not like frozen meat, importing meat is not an option. Then, the supply of vegetables and fruits was destroyed by the very bad weather. All of these gave us a 1.0-percent increase in m-o-m [inflation] in January [2023]. Annualized, that is 12 percent. Actually, the number is even more shocking if you do not remove seasonal effects. The m-o-m [inflation in January 2023] was 1.7 percent. Above-target consumer price index items increase, crowding out within and below-target items, and demand-side pressures emerge Then, you can see this. The proportion of [consumer price index (CPI)] items above 4.0 percent [upper end of the inflation target] became very, very high. Inflation in the services sector that does not directly use the products that are “under shock” also increased because, of course, they [employees] will ask for higher wages and so on and so forth. Thankfully, recent inflation prints show signs of e​asing as monetary and non-monetary measures kick in Now, we are beginning to see, finally, that it [the path of inflation] is normalizing. In fact, the price index in March [2023] is actually slightly lower than the January [2023] price index. So, we have two good m-o-m [inflation prints for] February and March. What is happening, of course, is that the policies that we undertook, which I will explain later, plus the imports [worked]. What is holding back inflation is the supply shocks that began to have second-order effects. That is why you can see core inflation, the red line, is actually quite high. Of course, we revised our inflation forecast, and this was much lower before. Our inflation forecast [for 2023] now is 6.0 percent, but the private sector’s forecast is 6.1 percent. By the way, the way we calculate it [full-year inflation forecast] is the average of the 12 [months of] year-on-year headline inflation. Note that the [inflation] expectations for next year [by the] private sector is that it [2024 inflation] is now going to be below 4.0 percent, although it is still higher than our forecast. Our own forecast is that it [2024 inflation] is going to be slightly below 3.0 percent next year because we revised downward from the previous policy meeting [in February 2022], when we thought it was going to be 3.1 percent. In other words, unless new shocks come in, the direction of the m-o-m changes, and our models say that inflation will be below 4.0 percent starting November or December this year, and [it] will average close to 3.0 percent next year. A part of the actions [to stem inflation] are not just monetary. [We have] the importation of key commodities, safety nets for those who are poor, and medium-term strategies to enhance farm productivity. JPMorgan Emerging Market Economics Head Jahangir Aziz: The Rice Institute was put together in the 1950s, and we have been waiting for a miracle rice variety since then. Yeah, [we have th​e] same story about our two countries there. But, I think, the importation helped very much, and now, we are seeing negative [growth in] food prices. Because the [elevated] inflation was largely [driven by] supply shocks, the [tightening] monetary policy settings prevented the supply shocks from being overly magnified. That is the story. The other thing that complicated the story was that even at 2.0 percent, our [former] policy rate [during the pandemic] was still significantly higher than the US policy rate. But when the US raised the policy rate and the difference [between the two policy rates] became around 1.0 percent, the peso weakened significantly, especially on the back of rising current account deficits. The current account deficit was about US$5.0 billion in 2021 and became US$17.0 billion in 2022. JPMorgan: Governor, before we move on to the Fed, can I steer it back to core inflation? Core inflation, in our case, generally—with a lag—starts following the headline [figures]. By the way, we are also seeing the m-o-m core going down. For most of the [CPI] items, with a few exceptions, it [inflation] is below 0.3 percent. So, even core [inflation] is going down from the m-o-m [perspective]. JPMorgan: And with China normalizing supply [chains], are import prices also coming off that? Yes. In addition, when Chinese demand is rising, it is very good for the Philippine economy. The first beneficiary will be tourism because as you can see [in] the size of the two countries, one is 10 times the other. If a small percentage of a billion [people] goes to you, that is a lot of tourists. My other joke there is if every Chinese ate half a banana, that is a big boost to the Philippine banana industry. The other good news is that the Fed—this is your market saying so—seems to be no longer going to do a big rate adjustment. And, probably, [there will be] just a few more [hikes], right? JPMorgan: The market has [priced in] this one more rate hike for May, and then, it is a pause. Then, the market’s pricing in the second half [of the year], a significant cut. We [at JPMorgan] do not buy the cut, but we do buy th

Title: Approaching and Taking Stock of Systemic Risk Issues from a Regional Perspective
Occasion: 23rd Financial Stability Board – Regional Consultative Group for Asia Meeting
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ A very pleasant morning to all of you. I warmly welcome you here in Mactan, Cebu to the 23rd meeting of the RCGA [Regional Consultative Group for Asia]. Today’s RCGA meeting comes at a very opportune time. Over the last three years, we have experienced shocks that are unprecedented in many respects. These have been trying times for financial authorities. In addition, market stakeholders have had to adapt to the same changing conditions. We often describe this in the context of a risk-on, risk-off [stance] or money is coming in or money is leaving. Here, perception and herd behavior matter, which is yet another layer of challenge to our jobs. But as we all try to navigate a path forward, the changing times also highlight the importance of us collaborating. Very few jurisdictions enjoy the status of driving global markets. Instead, many adjust to the spillovers that emanate from advanced economies and, of course, from shocks, like an epidemic and global shortages along the supply chains. The RCGA in a VUCA world This is where, I believe, the RCGA can have a role. It is always useful to have a central point from which emerging best practices can be discussed. This is exactly what the RCGs [regional consultative groups] can do. But beyond raising awareness, we also know that the world is volatile, uncertain, complex, and ambiguous (VUCA). This VUCA world, coupled with heightened interconnectedness, will be a perfect storm for shocks, spillovers, and systemic risks. The promise and limitations of regional decision making For Asia, collectively, we are poised to continue [to account for] the bulk of economic growth for the entire world. From my perspective, the key is [to make] the reference to Asia—although it is very, very hard to refer to it—as a single entity. But we, I think, can work together, and this diversity actually presents a lot of opportunities, just as it is fraught with challenges. Where all these put us is that we face a lot of interlinked uncertainties at both regional and global levels. These affect all of us. One can easily make the case that global disruptions require global solutions. But as any economist knows, the bigger the group, the harder it is for collective action to be rational. [We can refer to] the famous [Kenneth] Arrow impossibility theorem: “Individually rational people are not necessarily collectively rational.” They prefer A to B, or B to C, and C to A. Welcome, all of you. I look forward to a very fruitful conference. Thank you for coming, and I hope you enjoy your stay here in Mactan. Let me turn the floor over to [Reserve Bank of India] Deputy Governor [Rajeshwar] Rao.

Title: Responding to a “Tag Team” of Inflationary Pressures
Occasion: Barclays’ Emerging Markets Roundtable at the sidelines of the International Monetary Fund-World Bank Spring Meetings
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Good morning and thank you, [Barclays Managing Director and Head of Emerging Asia Economics Research] Rahul Bajoria, the Barclays team, and your clients. As they say in the Philippines, magandang umaga. Central bank independence and the three pillars I will start by saying that we are an independent central bank, and our independence is enshrined in our Constitution. It was rewritten in 1987, and the law that implemented the requirements of the Constitution for our independence was done [passed] three or four years later. Later on, 10 or 12 years later, we decided to be an inflation-targeting central bank, after New Zealand and Australia. That turned out to be very consistent with our mandate, which you can see in the next slide—price stability. We define price stability as [an inflation target of] 2.0–4.0 percent, but we always say [that our target is] 3.0 percent ± 1.0 [percentage point (ppt)]. Financial stability is our second pillar, although we [at the central bank] only regulate banks. Other agencies regulate the private bonds and the stock market—that is the [purview of the] Securities and Exchange Commission. Then, longer-term capital insurance is regulated by another agency called the Insurance Commission. The three of us [together with the Department of Finance], we are what is called the Financial Stability Coordination Council. But the Philippines is very bank-centric because our capital markets are less developed. In that sense, much of the action that comes with the Fed’s [United States Federal Reserve] response to financial stability is undertaken by the central bank. Finally, we have a third objective, which is a safe, secure, and efficient payments system. This used to be boring until it became exciting because of digital payments. I will talk mostly very little about the two [pillars] and spend more time on the first [pillar] because all the problems are in the first. Headline and core inflation remain elevated but month-on-month figures point to signs of easing The next slide shows you the problem. Inflation is high whenever you look at the headline or core [figures]. The good news is that it seems to have peaked in January [2023]. We thought, initially, that it will peak in November [2022], but there are several supply shocks that postponed the peak. The easier way to understand inflation and its momentum is the little bars below: These are the month-on-month (m-o-m) inflation [numbers]. The reason we do not explain that too much to the public is that we have to adjust it for seasonality. For instance, 1.7 percent m-o-m inflation in January translates to what? 18 or 19 percent [in annualized inflation] if that had continued for 12 months. It turns out that since January is price adjustment month, if you adjust for seasonality, that 1.7 percent [on a non-seasonally adjusted basis] is just 1.0 percent [on a seasonally adjusted basis]. But it is still large, right? Last January, we had the biggest [m-o-m] inflation in just one month. Adjusted for the seasonality, the January price index is 1.0 percent higher. If that [trend in m-o-m inflation] were to continue, we will have 12 percent [annualized] inflation. Fortunately, our view is that [it is] not the case. We initially thought that it already peaked because it [m-o-m inflation] fell to 0.3 percent [in November 2022]; but then, it rose to 1.0 percent [in January 2023]. So, now, we are seeing a more consistent [downward] pattern, and the new price index in March [2023] is actually slightly lower than the January price index. Of course, that [downward trend] cannot continue. [Otherwise], we will have a period of negative inflation. Of course, we will adjust for seasonality. It is just that—the two-month period of zero m-o-m inflation. That is the reason we are confident. [That is] because the factors that cause much of the inflation are largely supply shocks. Unless there are new supply shocks, we are on our way toward our target of 2.0–4.0 percent. Identifying the “tag team” of back-to-back supply shocks Historically, the supply shocks [driving inflation] will go on for many months. The longest period where we have about 4.0 percent [above-target] inflation was 15 months. As you can see, though, in this particular period, nearly every month is a “shock” month. The way I see it—for those of you who watched wrestling when you were young—it is like having a tag team. When the bad guys are beating up the good guys and he gets tired, he calls the other guy to beat him up. Then, they take turns beating him. Eventually, the good guys win, but it does go on for quite a long time. So, what was our tag team? Nearly everything! Initially, the Ukraine-Russia war made fuel expensive. Our price watcher watches the cost of operating a private vehicle. Many people go to work either on a motorcycle or [a] car, and those [prices] spiked a lot. On top of that, food and flour prices went up—that is the first spike. Then, just as we thought it was beginning to wane, another set [of supply shocks hit]. We had a sugar shortage. The price of sugar nearly doubled. Between July and December last year, it increased by nearly 50 percent in six months. Later on, we had a meat problem because of the African Swine Fever. We had to slaughter nearly all our pigs because we did not want it [the disease] to spread. Unfortunately, in this case, there is a very strong preference for fresh meat so importing does not help very much. Later on, we had bad weather that hit [the supply of] vegetables. We call it—if we are using a Spanish word—the sibuyas problem, which is onions [once translated]. We probably had the most expensive onions in the world because we have a very protectionist policy for farmers. “Oh, our farmers are poor. We should not import.” In this case, we should import because it [the price] was really very high. Unfortunately, the import was delayed, precisely, because of this fear of hurting farmers. Then, later on, you had [supply shocks from] fruits and vegetables. Demand-side pressures on inflation starting to manifest Because the economy was very strong, we were expecting it to grow 6.5 percent last year, but it ended up growing 7.6 percent [in 2023]. In the latter part of the year, we had a little bit of demand [pressures] in inflation [in addition to supply-side pressures]. We are seeing that in higher rentals. Path of inflation already target-consistent Our forecast, essentially, is that the average year-on-year inflation over the last 12 months of this year will be around 6.0 percent, although we will most likely revise this downward. As you can see, the path of inflation that we see is that by November or December this year and by next year, inflation will already be below 4.0 percent. Non-monetary interventions already coming online to ease supply-side pressures I already talked about the commodities that we started to import. It was the first time that politicians have said, “Maybe this time, the farmers are not going to complain too much if we import.” This is what is bringing down a lot of the prices. This time, politics swung in favor of the consumer. But generally, the politics is pro-farmer. “Oh, these poor farmers.” “Oh, we should not import rice because people who supply rice, when they have shortages [in their home countries], they ban exports.” By the way, a lot of other countries have done that. But if the price is too high, then clearly, that does not make sense anymore. So, the government responded this time and said, “Under extraordinary times, we will be importing.” That is why food inflation actually became negative in the last three months [of 2023]—January, February, and March. On the Fed and the exchange rate Of course, what the Fed does also affects us. In the past, our policy rate was comfortably higher than the Fed policy rate. The problem was, after the last increases in the Fed policy rates, the differential between our policy rate and the Fed actually became about 1.0 percent. We found out that at that range, people put a premium on the dollar [since] the dollar is supposed to be a safe haven. The moment you have a differential smaller than that [1.0–1.25 ppts], the peso starts to depreciate. Normally, we do not react to the exchange rate, [except to smoothen excessive volatility], but [it is different], given that inflation is very high and given that the depreciation of the peso was large and continuous. From January to October last year, the peso depreciated by 14 percent. With these two factors together, we [at the central bank] had two responses: We significantly raised the policy rate, and the other one was we sold foreign exchange (forex). And you can see, it starts to work. The moment we started doing that [tightening policy], the peso started to appreciate. In fact, since January [to 4 April 2023], it actually appreciated by about 2.0 percent. The exchange rate is no longer a driver of inflation. In addition, our current account improved because remittances and BPO [business process outsourcing] earnings started to support the peso. As a result, after our forex reserves declined because we sold [forex reserves] to support the peso, our reserves started to recover. It went as low as US$90 billion. Now, it is back to a hundred billion dollars [as of end-March 2023]. Maximizing expanded use of the inflation-fighting toolkit This is what we did. You note that the Philippines increased the policy rate much more than any country in that table. We are the most aggressive central bank in responding to both the weak dollar and the high inflation. Some have criticized me and said, “It is a supply shock. Why are you responding with interest rates?” The answer is simple. If we do not do anything, inflation will start migrating to other products, which we were already seeing. In addition, as I have already said, we sold forex. As you can see in the chart, we are not very different from South Korea, Thailand, and India. The outlier

Title: Navigating Global Uncertainties through a Whole-of-System Perspective
Occasion: Special Financial Stability Coordination Council Briefing on Emerging Systemic Risk Scenarios and its Implications to the Philippines
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ First of all, I am so glad to see many friends that I have not seen physically for a long time. Thank you for coming. Our topic for today is “Emerging systemic risk scenarios and its implications to the Philippines.” When stability belies brewing instability As an economist, our background is, sometimes, too much of many good things [that] may actually result in stressful situations. A very good example is the economist Hyman Minsky who was known for saying, “The root of instability is stability.” For instance, during a period of very stable growth in real estate prices, people begin to have a feeling that real estate is [an] extremely safe [investment]. Indeed, this [view] is confirmed by the fact that there are no defaults [around that time]. But when we look back, the reason there were no defaults, then, was that real estate prices [were] steadily rising. If the buyer, for some reason, suffers a financial problem, all he had to do was sell the property to pay. Therefore, [we saw] no defaults [at the beginning]. So, you can have a period of long stability that can actually cause the buildup of stress [over time]. Eventually, when a real shock comes and real estate prices start falling, then it is likely too late. The buildup of stress has become too large that a crash ensues. Now, you have the opposite [situation]. With real estate prices falling, people cannot pay their debt. Since they cannot pay their debt, then the banks are in trouble. And since the banks are in trouble, the economy is in trouble. When the economy is in trouble, government collections go down. All sorts of terrible things could happen when there is seeming strength and stability [on the surface]. When regulatory actions aimed at stability become the very cause of instability One of the lessons learned from the Asian financial crisis was that it seemed that central banks were guaranteeing the exchange rate because they intervened to keep it stable, and they were capable of doing so because there were so much [foreign exchange (forex)] inflows. Then, people, seeing that the central bank has high forex reserves, become overconfident. So, they borrow in their own currency. Without intending to, the central bank had an implied put on the exchange rate and encouraged behavior that, later on, was not sustainable. When the bubble pops and the music stops: what now? Of course, the other question is, given this buildup of stress, what would later be the trigger? In this case [Asian financial crisis], the trigger was something that happened in Thailand, which eventually affected Indonesia. For those of us who are old enough to remember, if you look at that period, it was clearly a very stressful period [for the banks]. But when you do look back, the contraction of the economy from 1997 to 1998 was just 0.5 percent. On the other hand, COVID caused the economy to contract by almost 17 percent in Q2 2020, relative to Q2 2019. For the whole year [of 2020], there was almost a 10-percent contraction of the economy, yet there was no sense of buildup of stress [in the financial system]. The question is why? Of course, one answer is that the banks were better-capitalized [now than in 1997]. In addition, in my own view, COVID might have actually prevented the next real estate crisis. It brought everything back to “normal.” The misalignment of [property] prices became less serious. The point is, the exercises that we have to do, the other part [of it] is looking at what could possibly trigger these emerging systemic risk scenarios. In particular, a very interesting question is: How could monetary, fiscal, and banking policies in these countries [in the region] affect us [in the Philippines]? Financial stability as a shared responsibility Three government agencies are largely mandated to promote financial stability. By the way, the central bank’s original mandate was banking stability. Now, the [expanded] mandate is financial stability. Clearly, we have to work together with regulators from other parts of the capital markets, namely the Securities and Exchange Commission and the Insurance Commission. But as you can see, [under the current market structure], what happens in the banking sector is clearly more important for two reasons: One, it is a very big part of the economy. The last time I looked, total bank assets is about equal to GDP [gross domestic product]. Of course, you can say that the fixed assets of the economy are four, five times its production. Therefore, banks are financing only one-fourth of all the fixed assets because, remember, these fixed assets do not count in one year. In that sense, the share of banks in what is being financed is quite large. The other problem, of course, is that the banks are more interconnected. The banks themselves are a central part of the payment system. Scenario generation and planning as useful exercises in systemic risk management That is why I am so glad you are all here so that we can discuss these issues in a less formal way, [engage in] the exercise of developing systemic risk scenarios, and ask questions on its implications on the Philippines. In addition to that, we do other scenario-generating activities, like: What would happen if one big conglomerate fails? We do not know what the cause could be. For instance, [suppose] there is a mistake in contracting. A power supplier guarantees the price of power regardless of the price of fuel. In return, he gets a premium. What happens if the price of fuel goes way beyond any imagined scenario? What happens to the firm? What happens to the power supply and so on and so forth? In this particular scenario, the worst thing that could happen, in a sense, [is that] the courts could say, “The company is no longer responsible for [maintaining] the fixed price because of the unusually high increase in world prices.” The sad thing is that if a crisis like that happens, the impact of the stress is distributed to consumers [through higher energy bills]. The importance of clear and careful communication for policymakers But there is a problem with these discussions. When we are talking about these scenarios, merely discussing them could be misinterpreted by the media. It could have unintended effects. “Dini-discuss pala ng Bangko Sentral kung paano [ang] mangyayari kung babagsak si ganito.” Then, people, all of a sudden, will start thinking, “Aba, babagsak si ganito!” Clearly, the discussion has to be open, but at the same time, we have to make sure that we know [and communicate to the public that] these are scenario-generating exercises. We have to be very careful what we say to the public because what we do could be misinterpreted. At any rate, I am so glad you are all here. The only way to learn, as a society, to prepare for these scenarios and actually make [generate] these scenarios so that we can better plan [for them] is to have these discussions and do it in an open way—probably, with quite a bit of carefulness about what we say and what we communicate to the media and the public. With that, I open this briefing on emerging systemic risk scenarios and their implications for the Philippines. I thank you in advance for your contributions to improving our knowledge about how these scenario-generating exercises can be useful. Thank you very much. Maraming salamat.​ ###

Title: Financial Stability: Steering the Boat through Strong Waves
Occasion: Opening of the Financial Stability Exhibit
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Guests, ladies and gentlemen, magandang umaga. It is my pleasure to welcome all of you to the opening of the exhibit that has to do with financial stability, themed “Executive Insights on Systemic Risks: Its Nuances and Challenges.” The title alone is very nuanced and challenging. This exhibit has been put together by our Office of Systemic Risk Management (OSRM) to better understand systemic risks. Economic stability and growth as a shared mission I will start by citing the mandates of the BSP [Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas]: to maintain and promote price stability, financial stability, and a stable and efficient payments system. If I were to complete it [the central bank’s mission statement], [it is] “in pursuit of sustainable and inclusive growth.” When you include everything, especially inclusivity and sustainability, it is [involved in] the business of the entire society because it is not just [working toward] a stable or growing economy, it must be inclusive and sustainable as well. Clearly, the BSP is an important player, but it has to deal with many other parts of society—let us say, in the case of banking stability, the banks and, of course, the rest of the government. For instance, the current [elevated] inflation is not just due to excess money but due to shortages of many agricultural and food products and, of course, the Ukraine war that caused fuel [prices] to rise. It is very hard to define stability and, in fact, sometimes, it is easier to define its absence. How crises impact the Philippine economy and banking sector How many of you were already born in 1997? If you recall, there was the Asian financial crisis, which—from the point of view of many bankers in the Philippines—was also a banking crisis. And yet, the economy contracted by only 0.5 percent in 1998 [despite the crisis]. On the other hand, with COVID, the economy actually contracted nearly 10 percent [in 2020]. And yet, the impact on the banking system has not been the same [as that of the Asian Financial Crisis of 1997]. Not just the size of the waves, but the strength of the boat and how well it is managed So, if I were to use that analogy, what matters is not just the size of the waves, but the strength of the boat. I can personally speak about this because when I was a college student at a university nearby, I spent two months in a Navy boat because ‘yun ang ROTC [Reserve Officers' Training Corps] namin. Ang dami dami kong demerits. So, I chose [to spend] two months in a Navy boat rather than repeating ROTC. There were waves that were so strong that people who were not used to it were vomiting. And yet, the boat remained stable. The key, then, is not just the size of the waves, but how strong the boat is and, I suppose, how well-steered the boat is. Maybe, a large wave could sink the boat if it strikes the boat from the side and could, therefore, upend it. So, the way the boat is navigated [also matters]. Financial stability as a shared responsibility, but bank regulation remains key in “bank-centric” financial system Therefore, we must work together with all our partners in government. In particular, the most important regulatory agencies are the Securities and Exchange Commission, the Insurance Commission, the Philippine Deposit Insurance Corporation, and the Department of Finance—who, collectively, form the Financial Stability Coordination Council or the FSCC. Of course, the central bank regulates banks and not the entire capital market, but we play a very important role because the Philippine economy is so-called “bank-centric.” I was looking at the recent balance sheet of banks. All the assets of all the banks that we regulate, it is about ₱23 trillion. And then, I look at GDP [gross domestic product], it was also roughly ₱22 trillion. By the way, this overstates the importance of the BSP because the assets of an economy are greater than its annual GDP. If the usual ratio is fixed capital is four times the output, then you can say that the banking system has financed one-fourth of the fixed capital of the economy. That is a lot! Therefore, making sure that the banks are well-capitalized, highly liquid, well-managed, and well-governed is extremely important. Mismatches sowing instability If you go back to [the] 1997 [crisis], there were plenty of mismatches. Again, you could attribute instability to mismatches. There were a lot of businesses that borrowed in dollars, but their revenues were in pesos. So, when the peso depreciated because of the crisis that started in Thailand, clearly, the debt has grown very much and very high. Then, if your assets are real estate, they are falling [in value]. Clearly, a lot of people are in trouble. That is the first thing. The other problem, of course, is banks are engaged in maturity transformation. They finance loans that take time to be paid [with deposits], but the depositor can change his mind and withdraw his money quickly or in a few days. Clearly, the issues have to do with the governance of banks: how well-capitalized they are, how liquid they are, and how exposed they are. Philippine banks remain strong and stable amid global banking developments Of course, Philippine banks are not as badly affected by the [recent banking] crisis in the United States (US) [where the proximate cause was the interest rate increases of the US Federal Reserve (US Fed) in response to domestic inflation]. As it [the US Fed] increased interest rates, bond prices fell, and some banks were in trouble. In the Philippines, that does not seem to be the case because of two reasons: First, we were not starting from a zero interest rate; we were starting from [a policy rate of] 2.0 percent [in November 2020]. The second reason, our [policy rate of] 2.0 percent was there for less than a year [from November 2020 to May 2022], whereas it was zero in the US for a long time. And, as I already said, our banks are well-capitalized. The task ahead The point is, spotting financial instability and when it could happen is not easy because it is a combination of many things: What is the source of the crisis? Is it a crisis from abroad that is spreading contagion? How many imbalances and mismatches are there in the major economic players? What to expect from the financial stability exhibit I guess, this is what we have to keep understanding because it is so complex. Many factors have to come together—some domestic, some foreign. We must watch out for anything that we can imagine is relevant. Therefore, this exhibit [is important]. In this exhibit, you will hear from our Monetary Board Members about why a failed bank is not necessarily going to create a crisis for the system. Of course, we have our own terminologies. Some banks are called “domestically systemically important banks.” If they fail, the central bank is under even greater pressure to help it rather than just close it. In closing, let me thank OSRM for mounting this exhibit and our colleagues who made this possible. This exhibit is a collective step forward on the new frontier of developing and increasing our ability in spotting potential crises, potential instability, and therefore, prevent them from happening. I urge everyone to take advantage of the available information, listen to the videos, read the boards, and join us in managing the challenges ahead. By the way, when I was going through the exhibit, my first impression is how photogenic most of my colleagues are on the Monetary Board. Maraming salamat po, and I hope you will enjoy watching the exhibit. ###

Title: Monetary Policy in an Abstract World: The BSP’s Pursuit of Price Stability
Occasion: Young Presidents’ Organization Philippines’ Gala at the Metropolitan Museum of Manila
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ To our friends from the YPO [Young Presidents’ Organization] Philippines Gold Chapter—I was told that “gold” means something, which means it is above the age limits—ladies and gentlemen, magandang gabi po sa inyong lahat. Thank you for inviting me to tonight’s gala at The M [Metropolitan Museum of Manila]. In fact, this is my first time at The M. I really enjoyed looking at the paintings. I have been given a very good guided tour by [The M] ​President Tina Colayco through the “Elusive Edge” exhibit, which attempts to articulate Philippine abstraction. I would like to start by saying that, in a sense, monetary policy is also a reaction to an abstraction. I noticed, for instance, how an old piece of tire is one of the things being displayed here. You know that it is a piece of tire [on the surface], but somehow, there are features that after the artist is done with it, you know it is [depicting] a human being. So, how a piece of tire starts looking like a human being is that some of the key features are there that [let] you know it is a human being depicted. In a way, economics is the same thing. The economy is so complex that typical economic policies are reacting to an abstract version of reality. You will go crazy if you try to understand every piece of information about it. Indeed, the key is to understand what to take out and what to include so that better policies are made. The case for central bank independence: specialization in three pillars as the best way to contribute to economic progress The same thing applies to central banking. In fact, there are debates on what it [the central bank’s mandates] should be. One extreme is perfect coordination between the central bank and the national government, which was the case with the Central Bank of the Philippines (CBP). Literally, the Monetary Board [of the then-CBP] was just a subset of the Cabinet. The good part of that is better [fiscal-monetary] coordination. So, the argument is: Why make it [the central bank] independent? The reason is that—it could be that—any administration will have a shorter horizon that is good from the point of view of monetary policy. If your goals are price stability; financial stability; and a safe, secure, and efficient payments and settlements system and you abstract from everything else [remove the central bank from performing functions outside of those three pillars], you may actually end up doing a better job at central banking [rather] than taking up too many things. In other words, the argument is, there is good reason for specialization, and there is good reason for giving the central bank independence because if these three things are done well—price stability, financial stability, and payment system—that is the best way that the central bank could contribute to economic progress. Giving it independence will actually be very good for the very government that gave it independence. When people believe that the central bank will achieve its inflation targets is actually good for the government because the government is trying to sell long-term bonds. And if people do not believe [in] price stability, then banks will think that will be too risky. Of course, the problem is, if I am a politician, well, that [price stability] is good in the long run. But if I can get cheap money in the short run, it is good for myself. But as [John Maynard] Keynes said, “In the long run, we will be all dead anyway.” In a sense, the belief is that a more independent central bank—that has a very focused mandate—is good for the economy because the other parts of the economy are hopefully run well by two very important parts: a good private sector and, hopefully, a government that is able to deliver basic public goods, peace and order, and all the things that are necessary to create the environment for progress. That is essentially where I am coming from, that in that setting, a highly specialized central bank—independent and focused on a few things—could be good for the economy. By the way, this idea is quite simple, but that was not the belief in the old days. I will not tell you about the debacle that happened, the crisis [when] the central bank literally [faced the risk of] running out of forex [foreign exchange] reserves. By the way, the central bank lost ₱400 billion in the 1980s because it was, essentially, pursuing goals that were not sustainable, like [handling] more fiscal matters and [maintaining] a fixed exchange rate, which are essentially conflicting goals. So, I guess, we learned our lesson. Our Constitution put [enshrined] an independent central bank. The three pillars of an independent central bank The independent central bank, as I already said, is enshrined in the Constitution and became law. Eventually, that central bank chose to be an inflation-targeting central bank. I already talked about the three mandates: price stability; financial stability; and a safe, secure, and efficient payments and settlements system. The third pillar: the “oldest” technology transformed and visions for connectivity By the way, I initially thought—when I was a student—that the third one [pillar] is the most boring. It turns out that it is now the most exciting because of fintech [financial technology]. See? The technology changed so much what used to be the oldest part of central banking. Of course, you can see it in the Bible, right? Christ being asked, “Whose face is it in the coin?” Christ said, “Caesar’s.” Money is for the sovereign; the soul is for God. Old as it [payments] is, it is now one of the most exciting things. One of the good things that is happening is that it is really moving very fast, with the pandemic forcing us to do more digital payments. Of course, it [digitalization] comes with a lot of scammers, a lot of issues. But at the same time, if you look at the numbers, they are fantastic. The rise in digital payments relative to the share of total payments has been very fast. Now, you have the rise, as well, of digital banks. By the way, we used to think that the biggest innovation in the financial system from a technological standpoint was the ATM [automated teller machine]. Before, you had to go to your bank to withdraw money. Then, all of a sudden, regardless of your bank, you can share the same ATM. Of course, the technology is doing much, much better now. So, [transactions through] InstaPay has already overtaken the ATM in terms of [volume and] value, and PESOnet could overtake checks. Our vision is that we will have a national QR [quick response] system where your phone can be used for making payments. Of course, our goal is, using your phone, you can pay a tricycle driver, pay a sidewalk vendor, or even give money to a beggar. Of course, why the beggar has a cell phone, I do not know why. An even bigger vision is, an ASEAN [Association of Southeast Asian Nations] citizen can use his phone anywhere in Southeast Asia. I think, we are less than three years away from that vision. Indeed—I will not mention whether it is GCash or Maya—one of them already has agreements with several governments that [allow] Filipinos abroad to use their local phone [numbers] to remit money to their relatives here. This will make the remittance much, much cheaper and faster. If the wife is working abroad, she has more control over the husband. [She can now] directly send money to the kids [if she says], “I do not trust my husband.” The beauty of the technology with these payment reforms—this will be exciting times for payments, that is what I am saying. Another thing that has happened is that the number of formal accounts has nearly doubled [from 29 percent in 2019 to 56 percent in 2021] because opening a formal account with e-wallets [electronic wallets] got a lot easier. It turns out that they can graduate the wallet into a savings account and so on and so forth. These, again, are exciting times. Navigating the risks that come with financial digitalization: the BSP way By the way, when you become central bank governor, friends will call you all of a sudden and say, “My son was scammed. What can I do about it?” The scam is: You play a game with the scammer, and he asked you to do things and certain tasks. You deposit money into his account, and then, he deposits money into your account, in return, as you perform certain tasks. New as it sounds, it is a very old thing. The pyramid is the same thing: keep rewarding you and if the money is large, you run away, right? But I would rather emphasize all the good things that can happen because we think that we can actually have a regulation that prevents bad things from happening, especially if people are better educated. Please do not share your OTP [one-time password]. I am surprised that, sometimes, very intelligent people are fooled into sharing it. A friend of mine, a thoracic surgeon, very, very smart—somebody pretended to be working for a bank and knew a lot about him and gave him all sorts of instructions. He ended up getting an OTP and ended up sharing it. Before he knew it, he lost ₱280,000. But rather than be afraid of all these problems, let us look at what the future can be. That is the BSP [Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas] attitude. We must improve our regulation. We must have more regulators that understand it. Then, we have to find a way of punishing those guys who abuse the system. The first pillar: when supply shocks warrant a monetary response Now, back to inflation targeting. As you can see, the last two years were really bad for inflation. Last year, average inflation was 5.8 percent when the target was only 4.0 percent. This year, we say, average inflation would be 6.0 percent, although the last three [month-on-month inflation] prints are quite good [within target]. So, I think, we will revise our estimate downward. But still, this could be quite a big miss. The interesting question is: Having missed [the target], why are we still credible [as an inflation-targeting central bank]? I h

Title: Chronicling the Evolution of Philippine Banking Laws
Occasion: Launch of the legal book Banking Laws of the Philippines – Annotated
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: Chief Justice Alexander Gesmundo; Senator Mark Villar, who is a great supporter of the economic team and really patient in answering all the questions from his fellow senators; representatives from the academe; all the important people from the judiciary, both from the past and present; our friends from the banking community; guests; and BSP [Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas] colleagues, magandang hapon po. Thank you for taking the time to join us today. I would like to start by saying that, as an economist, the speech of Chief Justice [Alexander Gesmundo] should be required reading for anyone who eventually gets a degree in economics—even at the bachelor’s level. Sometimes, people misinterpret Adam Smith when they say “invisible hand.” It is as though self-interest, by itself, is guided by an invisible hand so that self-interest and efficiency in the economy are automatically reconciled. But as the speech of the Chief Justice shows, it is a far, far much more complex relationship than that. Indeed, a relatively new field in economics is law and economics. Of course, one way to understand law and economics is to read opposite books. One of my favorite books is Lawlessness and Economics, and it shows exactly what happens when you have lawlessness. [For example,] suppose that instead of the entire [global] economy trading among each other, what will happen is that you deal only with a small circle of trust because you know the law will not be followed. Therefore, you always rely on your blood relatives; [you] always rely on your connections. Therefore, the ability of the economy to get the best from everyone, to get scale economies, is severely limited. Clearly, the reason self-interest is good for society is that there are rules, beliefs, and values that prevent self-interest from going haywire. In other words, no society can progress without those institutions. Indeed, Nobel Prize winner Douglass North won his Nobel Prize on that claim: that institutions explain why some countries are poor and some are rich. When he was lecturing, he was asked, “How do societies get ‘there’ [a desired stage in development]?” His answer was, “I do not know. What I know is that some societies are ‘there.’” They are based on rules. Contracts are, by and large, enforced. Therefore, investments take a longer time horizon, precisely because of the belief that no matter how long the period of time of the contract is, it is [will remain] effective. In other words, free markets without the necessary support of institutions that reconcile self-interest with the overall social good are really inadequate for development. That is why, I think, the speech of the Chief Justice should be required reading for every BSP employee. The evolution of banking laws and central banking powers: banking as public trust and equipping regulators with the necessary tools Having been a member of the Monetary Board for nearly 11 years—to be exact, 11 years and a little bit less than 11 months—I have seen firsthand rapid changes [in banking law]. I am moving to the fact that a very special part of the law is banking laws. In fact, I tell my banking friends, “This means being more loyal to depositors than the owner. After all, the [bank] owner, for every peso that you [the bank] will invest or lend, the owner owns no more than 20 percent. The depositors own 80 percent of what is being lent out.” In a sense, a banker is almost like a public servant. He is taking care of the people’s money. That is why it is very important that banking laws give regulators great cover when they do their jobs. For instance, we [the central bank] received new powers to borrow from the public. I will explain why that is important. There were times when there was so much money flowing into the country that the only way to prevent excessive appreciation of the peso was for the central bank to borrow money when it buys foreign exchange (forex). Because if we were to print money to buy forex, the very act of buying forex will create too much money in the system. In the past, we were not allowed to borrow from the public. The most recent revision of the law now allows us to do that. This has allowed us to refine our monetary tools. We also strengthened regulations for the institutions that we regulate. And as was already pointed out by the Chief Justice, we saw rapid changes in digital payments. But any progress has a dark side. With digital payments, [there is a risk that] the bank runs are more efficient as shown by the Silicon Valley Bank. It was gone; the money [deposits] was gone in less than 24 hours because people were withdrawing their money digitally. Bad rumors also spread faster. Capturing rapid changes: lessons from the past, compass to the future It is important to chronicle these changes [in banking laws] to see where we have come from and to chart where we will go. Our book, Banking Laws of the Philippines – Annotated, is the fourth legal book published by the BSP. This publication consolidates and updates the inaugural series to incorporate recently enacted laws, such as the National Payment Systems Act, the Financial Institutions Strategic Transfer Act, and the Financial Products and Services Consumer Protection Act. Just keeping up with these is not easy. That is why it is very important that a good book covers all of these. This latest publication builds on the annotation of three essential books on the country’s banking laws between 2010 and 2012, namely the New Central Bank Act, the General Banking Law, and other special laws, collectively known as the “First Series Books.” Thank you very much, Chief Justice [Gesmundo], for coming and for lecturing us—giving us an even picture not just of banking laws but of laws in general—that rules and beliefs are extremely important for a progressive and inclusive society. We are also pleased to announce our collaboration with Rex Bookstore, which is marketing and distributing this book. The book is also available online so you do not have to buy it physically. But I am old enough; I need a physical book so I can fold and mark the pages that I like. Clarity, compliance, and comprehension: book objectives and aims As the BSP engages in legal and regulatory activities, the many benefits of developing and consolidating such references cannot be understated. First, they aid in attaining regulatory clarity because regulations are hard to predict. That, again, is a source of friction. Consequently, with clarity comes better compliance. Legal literature helps stakeholders correctly interpret laws, strengthening adherence and accountability. Another advantage is providing a historical record of legislative changes, facilitating the study of policy shifts and regulatory trends in the country’s banking system. Closing message In closing, let me thank everyone involved in this project from developing the content to getting it out to print, especially Senior Assistant Governor and General Counsel Elmore Capule who oversaw this project from conceptualization to completion. Congratulations to the authors and members of the book team for helping the public gain a better understanding and appreciation of central banking and the work that we do. Maraming salamat po, at magandang gabi!

Title: The Central Bank’s Inflation-Fighting Playbook
Occasion: Bank of America Small Talks Symposium at the sidelines of the 2023 International Monetary Fund-World Bank Spring Meetings in Washington
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ We [at the central bank] have three pillars: price stability, financial stability—on financial stability, we have to work with two other regulatory bodies [the Securities and Exchange Commission and the Insurance Commission]—and then, of course, the payments and settlements system. I will not talk much about the last two [pillars] because the problems are not there. Payments digitalization on the rise All I can say about the payment system is, it used to be boring; now, it is one of the most exciting due to digital payments. The principal contribution is [because of] the two systems: [The first is] PESONet, which does batch processing and allows large transfers. Then, we have the fast payment system that we call InstaPay, where it is almost real-time. Both are doing very well. Financial stability: a pillar of strength On the other hand, what I can say about [financial] stability is that banks in the Philippines are strong. They are well capitalized. Because of that [strength], it is not a constraint on how high we can raise the policy rate. The usual concern [following the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank] is that if you [the central bank] raise the policy rate too much, the banks themselves that hold a lot of government securities would take a loss, and, therefore, the higher policy rate becomes a threat to financial stability. In the Philippines, that is not the case. Effectively, we have what is called the “Tinbergen rule”: tools for price stability, tools for financial stability, and, of course, tools for payments system. We Filipinos call it “lifting your own bench” when you are praising yourself. I am doing a little bench lifting [when I say that] because of that, we [at the central bank] are able to do quite well in responding to the challenges. Narrowing interest rate differential weighed on the peso, which, in turn, added to inflationary pressures Now, the challenges. Of course, loose monetary policy itself became a challenge. In the past, we cared very little about the effect of US [United States] policy rate changes because the gap between our policy rate and their policy rate was high. But then, as the two policy rates became close, Philippine markets think that the peso should depreciate if the policy rate differential is too small. They think that there should be a premium for the dollar. So, as the US policy rate rose, the peso weakened. Normally, we [at the central bank] did not care too much about it because the exchange rate changes were not large. Under normal times, changes in the exchange rate do not translate much to inflation for two reasons: [First,] the changes are not large, and [second,] the changes can go both ways. Of course, the problem is, as the US policy rate rose, the peso fell. It also happened when the current account is large because of the high price of imports. From January last year to October last year, the peso depreciated. It was depreciating every week, every month. So, we were afraid that if we let that happen continuously, nobody would believe our inflation targets anymore. Inflationary expectations will be disanchored. Now, all of these were happening with the Ukraine-Russia war, which raised transportation costs. By the way, our statistical office estimates how much gasoline a person with a motorcycle uses per day and how much gasoline a person who drives to work [uses] every day. They generate a big part of the inflation number from that. Then, of course, the cost of fertilizer and flour [rose]. So, the result is very high inflation that is accompanied by a rapidly deteriorating exchange rate. Taking action appropriate for an unprecedented time So, we had to act. By the way, I am an accidental Governor. The reason I became the Governor is that the President wanted the [former] Governor [Ben Diokno] to be the secretary of finance. I was on the Monetary Board for 11 years, and I was the logical person to continue what he was doing. The first central bank governor [whose term I am serving] died. There are actually three governors serving the same term because the [Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas] Governor gets a six-year term. I got the last year of the term, and I ended up raising policy rates more than the first two governors. This was from 200 basis points (bps) to 625 bps. But in spite of that, there are still pressures on the currency to depreciate. We sold 9.0–10.0 percent of our forex [foreign exchange] reserves. It did work. The peso, starting in October, recovered some of its losses. Year-to-date [from 1 January to 4 April 2023], it has appreciated [by] 2.0 percent. Supply-side pressures add to the inflation problem To complicate things, there were shortages that arose from supply shocks to agriculture. In an economy that can freely import agricultural goods, this would not have been a serious problem. The problem is, because of politics, policies are designed to heavily protect the farmers [and restrict imports] so shortages have [caused] very large spikes in [the prices of] sugar, vegetables, fruits, [and] meat. These came in a sequence. Month-on-month inflation on the rise You can see this in the chart. The green bars below are month-on-month (m-o-m) inflation. What you will see there is, if you look at 12 months that ended in March this year, all the m-o-m changes in the price index—every number, except for two [months]—were much higher than what is consistent with our target. The first bad month was March [2022]. It [seasonally adjusted m-o-m inflation] was 0.9 percent in just one month, followed by another 0.9 percent [in April 2022], and this was caused by the Ukraine war. As that was fading, we got another 0.9 percent [m-o-m inflation] in October [2022]. These are [caused by] agriculture [shortages, such as] sugar, the price of which increased by 50 percent in the last six months of last year. Later on, [we had] bad weather [that affected key agricultural items]. The worst one was January 2023, when it [m-o-m inflation] was 1.7 percent. But if we adjust it for seasonality, we end up with 1.0 percent. Bank of America (BofA) Global Research Managing Director and Co-Head for Asia Foreign Exchange and Rates Strategy Claudio Piron: But I see you got a zero? Yes. BofA: So, is it mission accomplished? It seems. But as they say, two birds do not make a spring. By the way, we thought the first 0.3 percent [m-o-m inflation in December 2022] was it [the peak] already because 0.3 times 12 [is within our target]. We thought that the supply shocks—because of our aggressive policy [rate] changes, because we were able to see the peso start to appreciate—we thought we have killed it [elevated inflation] already, and then, another big shock [in January 2023]. By the way, it became a joke because what happened was, the thing that became most expensive were onions. It became almost like jewelry now. But then, there was a swing in public opinion. “While we support the farmers, we think, it is about time we start importing again.” That is why we are now beginning to see negative m-o-m [inflation]. On the other hand, the pure momentum—what we call “knock-on effects”—from the first three major groups of shocks is still there. You have negative food [inflation] now, and, in addition, there are other things that are idiosyncratic, like we periodically raise the tax on cigarettes, what we call “sin taxes.” So, at the beginning of the year, there is a spike in tobacco and alcoholic products. Mission accomplished? Inflation expected to settle within target by end-2023 But at the same time, our forecast is with what we have done. Of course, we probably slowed down the economy a bit. The fact that the peso is now much stronger, we cannot see inflation having its own momentum without new shocks. Then, our forecast is in the absence of shocks. We expect inflation to fall below 4.0 percent by November, December [2023]. Indeed, we have a little anomaly, which is when we hit January next year, because of the very high base, inflation will actually be below 2.0 percent. BofA: So, there is some suggestion that you are considering a pause? Yes. I myself said that if the next month, April, is another below 0.3 percent [m-o-m inflation month], then we have a little more confidence that the trend, the decline in inflation [is more apparent], and the supply shocks have finally completely waned. By the way, the way we report it, the inflation number for the year is the average of the 12 headline [inflation numbers]. So, if you have 8.0 percent inflation in January and 4.0 percent at the end of the year, the [full-year] average is, more or less, 6.0 percent. By the way, this pattern is observed many times in the past. The longest streak of above 4.0 percent inflation that we have is about 15 months. But these [current] shocks are particularly long and enlarged so we may have 19 or 20 months of above-target inflation. The good news is that people think that it is not the fault of the central bank because everybody knows that these are supply shocks. By the way, this is one importance of our communication. We do not guarantee. We tell the people that monetary policy cannot stop supply shocks. All it can do is prevent second-order effects, making supply shocks turn into a much longer period. BofA: And you can substantiate this? What about inflation expectations? Yes. That is the other chart. Despite the high inflation in 2022 and 2023, inflation expectations for 2024 and 2025 are now within target. What we found is, they [the private sector] follow ours [central bank inflation forecasts], with a lag. So, if we are wrong, then we are wrong. And, of course, we will be wrong if there are supply shocks. Private [sector inflation] forecasts are 3.7 percent [for 2024], but as you can see, ours is 2.9 percent. But we are not too worried about this. This happens every time we have high inflation. It takes time, a couple of months, for private-sector forecasts to converge with ours. BofA: Is there a better

Title: Beyond Just a Bellwether: The Capital Markets as Catalyst for Dynamic and Inclusive Economic Growth
Occasion: Inauguration of the Philippine Stock Exchange’s new events hall
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Magandang umaga sa inyong lahat, especially to [House] Speaker [Martin Romualdez]. Given my background as a former professor of economics, I will give you a long lecture. Just kidding! This is indeed a great day. Indeed, a former trading floor [that] creates space for an events hall is, in itself, symbolic of technological change. We need less space now for a lot of things that used to require a lot of space. Ensuring the growth of the capital markets alongside the banking system and the broader economy I am often asked, “What is the role of stock markets in the economy?” The first thing I did was go to Google. What I found out was that the market cap [of the stock exchange]1 is small in comparison to the total assets of the banking system,2 which, in turn, is about the same as [nominal] GDP [gross domestic product].3 What does that mean? What it means is that, over time, if you wait long enough, the market cap will probably be growing faster than the economy. What that says is that, over time, initially, economies are driven by big personalities and families. Of course, it is only appropriate that we are talking about the Ayalas or the San Miguels. But, over time, as the economy tries to grow and get more and more financing from everyone, we need capital markets. Of course, banks usually grow ahead of capital markets but, hopefully, soon to follow are other ways of raising capital—the biggest two of which are the bond market and the stock exchange. Looking to long-run averages over short-term movements as more accurate barometers of growth As someone famously said in the US [United States], “The stock market predicted eight out of four recessions.” This means it overreacts. In a way, the stock market is like your pulse rate. You get excited, it goes up. You hear Professor Medalla, it goes down because he lulls you to sleep. But what matters is the long-run average. You need people to watch the economy 24/7. You need people who watch companies 24/7. By the way, this means great value for the government itself. Because as companies make accounting [disclosures] to their stakeholders, the BIR [Bureau of Internal Revenue] gets a lot of data. [So,] it gets harder and harder to have multiple books. As the government, in turn, gets more money, it can have more money for financing infrastructure and social development. Strengthening data exchange within the Capital Markets Development Council This, indeed, is a great day. It is a great day because it says [that] the Philippines, its capital markets, are developing. From the point of view of the central bank, one area where we interact very closely [with the PSE] is the Capital Markets Development Council. We talk about all the issues, including tax reforms that we might want to propose to Congress. The other thing we get, of course, is data. For instance, one data [set that] we watch at the BSP [Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas] is what kind of money is leaving [the Philippines] and what kind of money is entering. For instance, money is entering the Philippine government bond market. Those are strong signals of what people think about the interest rate and exchange rate. On the other hand, the money that comes in and comes out of the PSE reacts to a greater range of news, including politics, calamities, and so on and so forth. Nurturing a virtuous cycle: capital markets as another catalyst for inclusive growth I am very optimistic [that] this hall represents what could be in the future: an economy where capital markets are more developed; an economy where funding big projects and activities could be funded by thousands of, hopefully, ordinary people—a more developed capital market; a more developed economy, which, in turn, gives you a more developed capital market. Hopefully, that virtuous cycle together with the support of our national leaders, like the Speaker and, of course, the President, will give us an economy that is dynamic and, hopefully, one of the fastest-growing and very inclusive economies in the region. Maraming salamat, at magandang umaga sa inyong lahat. ### ________________________________________________________ 1. The market cap of the stock exchange is ₱13.46 billion as of end-May 2023, based on Philippine Stock Exchange (PSE) data. 2. The total assets of the Philippine banking system amount to ₱23.1 trillion, according to preliminary data as of end-March 2023. 3. Annualized nominal GDP is ₱22.7 trillion as of end-March 2023.​

Title: The Current Philippine Digital Landscape: What’s Next Beyond the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap
Occasion: FinTech Alliance.ph General Membership Meeting
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ It is a fantastic day. Good time to meet old friends that I have not seen for a long time. [FinTech Alliance President] Lito [Villanueva], members of FinTech Alliance.ph, colleagues from government, friends from the banking industry, digital champions, magandang umaga. I have just come from the Philippine Stock Exchange (PSE) where they inaugurated their new trading floor. Even the trading floor is a consequence of digitalization. PSE does not need it anymore because trades have shifted online. The point is, it [digitalization] will affect so many sectors, including the stock market, and it will affect the economy all over the place. The twin goals of the Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap The industry is well aware of the targets under our Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap (DPTR) by this year. But what does our vision look like beyond 2023? Of course, we have our twin goals: [First,] more and more retail payments will be digital; our target is 50 percent of retail transactions will be digital. Second, 70 percent of Filipinos have or use a transaction account. By the way, there is a survey that we conducted on why people do not have [formal] accounts. Surprise, surprise, the biggest answer is: “I have no money.” But there are other things other than that, which we [at the central bank] can address. Indeed, even that other one [concern] can be addressed by very, very targeted programs of the government. A good example is the Pantawid Pamilya[ng Pilipino Program (4Ps)], where poor families who keep their children in school to break the cycle of poverty get direct assistance from the government. Even in that particular case where people have no money, we really have good programs, like the 4Ps.
