Walking the talk on sustainability In sustainability, green is the color of recovery. We are a step ahead as we have already made a deliberate choice to include sustainability as one of our strategic focus areas. Even within the IMF [International Monetary Fund], there is a debate [on] whether a financial institution that does not have fiscal power—the power to punish, the power to give incentives—is taking on a task that it cannot deliver. What we hope for is that fiscal authorities are also doing their part so that we are not really being set up for an impossible mission. The 11-point Sustainable Central Banking Strategy will help enable the BSP to become an enabler, doer, and mobilizer of ESG [environmental, social, and governance] principles. In our commitment to lead by example, we are actively embedding sustainability in our for

Title: Thoughts on Mentoring, Education, and True Learning
Occasion: Appreciation program for the mentors and mentees of the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas’ MILEs program
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ To our guest speaker, Mr. Francis Kong, fellow Monetary Board Members (MBMs), fellow BSPers, mentors, and mentees, magandang hapon po. Let me start by saying that I am the typical skeptic of anything. By [virtue of my] training [as an economist], I have to find out empirically whether something is a coincidence or not. A good example is a story by two very famous psychologists, [Daniel] Kahneman and [Amos] Tversky, who won the Nobel Prize in economics. They were describing how the Israeli army decided who would be the best officers. They had a beautiful concept: They had a heavy object to move through a difficult course, and a team of soldiers was assigned without anyone being told what his role will be. Somewhere along the line, somebody will rise up and do the coordinating and the leadership. They [Kahneman and Tversky] could observe this because they feel that they [the subjects] did not know. Therefore, the choices for [who would make “good soldiers” from] those special training were largely drawn from this beautiful thematic exercise. But Kahneman and Tversky, being scientists, looked at the records and found out that it had nothing to do with success. The battlefield is very different. Different people react [differently] when they find out that their lives are at stake. Beautiful as this experiment was, there was no statistical significance between those who were chosen this way and those who were chosen some other way. In other words, we always have to ask ourselves: Is something really working and what is our basis for concluding? The promise and limits of education: signaling, skills-building, and values formation Another related literature [explores] what is education. To economists, some of them, it is signaling. You do not learn anything from school. Maybe, you get your networks and language skills, but unless you are taking very specific sciences, [or] highly technical fields, much of what you learn in school is not very useful. The communication, reasoning, and writing skills and what you go through and survive are what makes you, eventually, a leader, not the schooling itself. My favorite mentor was a Belgian priest who was also an economist. He said, “When you look at all the leaders of Great Britain, they studied the classics in the original Latin and Greek.” What does that have to do with education? Why do you have to study it in the original language? The answer was: If you are dedicated enough to learn something useless, you can learn anything. What it explains is perseverance and discipline. The other view is that education is just signaling then. It signals what you are. If you do well during education, maybe you will do well after education. It is a screening device; it screens out the less disciplined. That could actually mean it also screens out the entrepreneurs. There is this famous quote in Yale, “Take care of your A students; they are your future professors. Take care of the B students, they are the future managers of the corporation. Take care of the C students, they will be your donors.” The answer is in the attempt: mentoring as a productive exercise in two-way learning Whatever it is, the important thing, of course, is we try. What is the worst thing that can happen if it turns out that mentoring is not effective? You have a happy mentor and a happy mentee. Even if that is all it achieves, then, clearly, something has been achieved. And who knows? Maybe, the byproduct of them [was something] that we never expected. For instance, if you look at my mentors, some of them are role models. Some of them are what to do and what not to do. One example is [former President] Erap [Estrada]. I learned a lot of things about what not to do. I do not have to give you a list, but I did learn a lot of things from Erap. When Erap is talking to an audience, he connects. When you look at him interacting with the voters, he knows how to inspire. When you look at how he deals with political leaders, the same thing. In that sense, the other lesson is to pick up only the good things from your mentor. The theme “Celebrating MILEstones and Beyond” is fitting for today’s occasion. What lies beyond? As I said, if all you did achieve is an MBM meets a head of a regional branch, at least the MBM now understands the branches a little bit better. If the MBM mentor meets somebody from currency forecasting, then he knows currency forecasting from the point of view of the people who produce the first sets of forecasts. I have learned so much about the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP). I am doubtful if anybody has learned anything from me because the [mentoring] process is really too short and too formal. “Oh, he is waiting for me to sign his form.” Sometimes, we are too busy to get a common schedule. But whatever happens, I think that we tried. Sometimes, there are things that we may expect from it that actually do not come from it. But even if that is the case, there are some things that we never expected from it anyway. The point here [is that] it looks like a success. [If] we are happy with it, then we should continue. The point is, the bigger question is learning by doing in the BSP. Clearly, this would help. Many times, you actually learn from somebody who never really taught you, by just watching. Hopefully, the boss would be fair. Sometimes, your boss not being fair is good for you. It forces you to look for another department where you can find a fairer boss, or it teaches you resilience. This is something we should be happy about. We cannot be like the two psychologists I mentioned who are always looking for measurements. At the very least, I had a great time doing it, and I hope those who were under me felt the same way. To our graduates, once again, congratulations and thank you for your willingness and commitment to lifelong learning. Precisely, that is the goal: lifelong learning. And to the new batch of mentors and mentees, I offer you a warm welcome. Again, remember, mentorship is a two-way street. Both mentors and mentees have much to gain from one another’s insights. At worst, you meet a wonderful human being who may teach you something that has nothing to do with the BSP itself but may make you a better person in the long run. With the culture shaped by the previous program participants, I have full confidence that you will continue to be a champion of excellence within the Bank. To [Managing Director] Jayzle [Ravelo] and company, keep mentoring! Maraming salamat, at mabuhay ang BSP MILEs at ang buong BSP! ### ​

Title: The Halftime Report: The BSP’s Pursuit of Price Stability
Occasion: The Manila Times Mid-Year Economic Forum
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Thank you for inviting me to speak at your Mid-Year Economic Forum. I think it is quite fitting that my first off-site speaking engagement after I was appointed [as Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) Governor] was for the Manila Times. Nearly 12 months since [we last spoke], the economic environment has changed dramatically—improved somewhat, significantly improved [in some respects]—but, of course, risks remain. Allow me to share developments in the first half of 2023. From the point of view of the central bank, we look at three things: price stability—operationally, that is inflation being within 2.0–4.0 percent; financial stability—largely because we are a bank-centric economy, [means] strong banks; and a safe, secure, and efficient payments and settlements system. The question is: How we are performing if you look at these three pillars? Marking 18 consecutive months of above-target inflation from April 2022 The next chart shows you what a terrible year the year was. I look at inflation largely on a month-on-month (m-o-m) basis rather than on a year-on-year basis. It is very simple. If your [inflation] target is 3.0 percent, m-o-m inflation should be 3.0 percent divided by 12 [months]. If you look at this chart, there is only one month [December 2022 when m-o-m inflation was 0.3 percent] that passed the test. It is like a student taking 13 exams and failing 12. [M-o-m inflation for] January 2023 was the worst one. Without adjusting for seasonal effects, m-o-m was 1.7 percent. Adjusted for seasonal effects, it is 1.0 percent. This means [that] the January numbers were consistent with more than 12 percent [annualized] inflation. Monetary and non-monetary actions helped nip inflation in the bud Thankfully, I think, this extreme period of bad luck where every month, [there is] a new [supply] shock or one negative shock after another—the Ukraine war, fertilizer shortage, pork shortage due to Swine flu, even egg shortage affecting the chickens, and, of course, the famous onions. In addition, because we protect the farmers, these [agricultural] shortages resulted in very high food prices. The President and his cabinet acted quickly and said, “Well, we have to make a choice: protecting the farmers or controlling inflation.” And they made a very good decision [to relax import restrictions]. In addition, of course, we have a very aggressive central bank. We raised the policy rate by [a cumulative] 425 basis points. We sold dollars when we thought the exchange rate was overshooting. By the way, at its peak, the [year-to-date] depreciation of the peso by October 2022 was almost 13 percent. Of course, the peso has regained strength since then. In addition, [with] our policy rate increases and actions taken by the national government, if you look only at the last part of the chart, average m-o-m inflation for February, March, and April 2023 is a little bit more than 1.0 percent. Of course, that is too low because we were starting from a very bad January. Path of inflation already target-consistent Now, our forecast is that we [inflation] will actually be below 2.0 percent by next January [2024]. The reason for that, of course, is January [inflation] last year was so high. When you have normal inflation at 0.2 or 0.3 percent every month, then you will actually be below 2.0 percent or very close to 2.0 percent by January [2024] and will be below 4.0 percent by October or November 2023. For this reason, you can see that we have revised our forecast of average inflation down to 5.4 percent [at our June policy meeting]. This used to be 6.0 percent. We are now quite confident that this year and the next, unless there are new shocks, we will be very close to the midpoint of the [inflation] target. In other words, our message is, unless there are new shocks, new terrible developments, you have an entire set of people at the central bank whose entire job is to think about all those things. Historically, the biggest [supply] shocks [driving inflation] are oil and, in the past, rice. Thanks to the previous administration, they depoliticized rice importation. The government said, “If you want to protect the farmers, impose a tariff. Make it high if necessary, but let the private sector decide when and how much to import.” I was scolded as saying the worst possible system you could have are bureaucrats deciding how much and when to import. All of the terrible things could happen: favoritism, uneven licenses, and wrong forecasts. That is one of the reasons why rice is not a problem [for inflation] this year and last year. Unfortunately, that is a problem for many parts of agriculture because of phytosanitary and all other permits that you have to get in order to permit. The President and his team have solved that problem very quickly with NEDA [National Economic and Development Authority] Secretary [Arsenio] Balisacan and [Finance] Secretary [Benjamin] Diokno leading the way. Central bank forecasts influence private-sector expectations, reflecting inflation-targeting credibility What is the result [of our combined actions]? The result is, the private sector is now saying, “We agree with the [inflation] forecasts of the BSP.” They are slightly behind us. We [at the central bank] are saying [that] we are moving to the middle of the [inflation] target; they [the private-sector economists] are saying, “You are halfway to the middle of the midpoint and the upper band.” Historically, this is always true: They [the private sector] are a bit more skeptical. They say, “Give me a few good months [of within-target inflation] before I believe you.” If you follow this, the expectations [of the private sector] are actually moving close and closer to our own forecasts [at the central bank]. That is how we decide or rate our effectiveness as an inflation-targeting central bank. Do our forecasts influence private-sector inflation forecasts? The statistical finding is [that] our forecasts lead private-sector forecasts. Maximizing use of expanded toolkit The next chart lists all of the things the government did. The battle against inflation was [by] the central bank with the rest of the government. That is why we are going to beat inflation. But who knows? Another shock could hit [and add to inflationary pressures]—a major new political problem, like China tries to take back Taiwan, the war in Ukraine escalates, [or] Russia uses nuclear weapons. Easing foreign exchange pressures The other point is, if you look at the exchange rate, you will also see that the worst is over. The exchange rate has healthily adjusted to the fact that our current account deficit is high and, therefore, you need a weaker peso to encourage exports and protect domestic industries from competition. When you look at this year rather than last year, the peso is actually doing quite well. What accounts for this? The usual answer is remittances, BPO [business process outsourcing]. In addition, we are getting significant net foreign direct investments. In other words, our current account is being financed partly by the central bank’s sales of foreign exchange (forex) but largely by foreign inflows. Our gross international reserves have actually stabilized. This is the summary of what we did: We allowed the exchange rate to adjust. We raised the policy rate. We sold forex. As a result, we were able to stabilize the macroeconomy. Growth prospects remain bright, but moderation in the horizon I think [NEDA Undersecretary] Rose [Edillion] will talk about this so I will not stay very long here. We are seeing a very strong economy, especially last year. Our own forecast is that GDP [gross domestic product] growth will be above 6.0 percent this year. Of course, it is hard to grow when your base is larger. For example, [spending on] restaurants and accommodation rose to 24.7 percent [in Q4 2022]. It is hard to repeat that [because] we have a large base. There is less revenge spending so we will actually see a normalization in forecasts. [The economy might grow] between 6.0 and 6.5 percent, I think. [The growth outlook for] next, next year [2025] is a little bit problematic because of what is going on in the rest of the world. Second and third pillars: no less important Let me now go over to the other things: banking. Our banks are strong. They are well capitalized. Liquidity remains ample. Loan quality remains good. Assets are growing. Deposits are growing. By and large, the picture for banking is quite good. The other thing we are looking at is digitalization. We are seeing great progress there: PESONET will soon overtake cheque writing. Our dream is [that] no one has to write a cheque anymore, but it is very hard. If you want a paper trail, you write a cheque. InstaPay has already overtaken ATM [automated teller machine] withdrawals. More and more people, we hope, will be using QR [quick response code] payments. Laser focused on the three pillars during and beyond 2023 In sum, current circumstances have allowed the BSP to prioritize price stability because the banks are strong. We were able to raise interest rates as much as we have to because we were confident that the banks will remain stable and the borrowers will remain safe even with the relatively high-interest rates. The BSP can maintain its focus on price stability since its key policy rate decisions have not been constrained, unlike the US [United States], by fears of financial instability and implications of higher rates. Finally, I thank Manila Times for inviting the BSP to this forum. It is through these events that we are able to communicate why we do what we do. And the more people understand the BSP, the more effective its policies will be. Thank you very much. ​ ###

Title: So Long, BSP
Occasion: Bank-wide send-off of Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ I think, “speechless” is not an adjective that describes me, but this time, it is. I am really at a loss of words to say—when both your emotions and your brain are just trying to process this. What a way to end my 12 years in the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP). If this is not going out with a bang, what is? The personal touch that you gave to your performances, you somehow knew a little bit of me in each of the sets: from the songs that my mother loved—How [did] you find them out?—to the songs I loved when I was a young man. [With] the praises that I heard, I really had to have both feet on the ground because if I believe only half of them, baka mawala ‘yung isang adjective na dinescribe niyo sa akin, “modest and humble.” Talagang, I am really beyond words. Talagang, I truly thank you all for these. If you say that “Mahal niyo ako dahil mahal ko kayo,” there is a very simple answer [to the question] why: “You are easy to love.” If the incoming Governor were to ask me for advice, and I am allowed only one sentence, [I would say] “Just remember the most important resource of the BSP is its people.” Sabi niya, one sentence lang, but let us change the scenario. Suppose he asks, “Why?” If he asks why, [I would say,] “Well, where can you find more than 5,000 people who are dedicated to very clear objectives? You know, they know how important those things are to the nation.” By the way, ‘yung nagturo sa akin ng sayaw, sabi ko, “Ang hirap! Pwede bang three pillars na lang? Mas madali yata!” But a group of people who had the training, dedication, love of country, integrity, mental agility, and, I would say, the courage to speak what they believe are the right things for the country. Of course, [Senior Assistant Governor (SAG)] Elmore [Capule] was saying [that] I was very courageous or brave. Actually, I was just following what I thought were the right things to do. If you are almost 100 percent sure—I say “almost” because the very nature of the course of things is seldom [that] you are really sure of anything—but if you believe strongly that you are on the right side of history, then it is so easy to say “Forget everything else, and do what has to be done.” What you must realize is part of the reason [that] it is so clear is that I have spent 12 years with BSPers. I started out as a Batangueño[ng] probinsyano; then naging Lasalista; then naging taga-UP [University of the Philippines]; then, briefly, naging tiga-NEDA [National Economic and Development Authority]; then naging UP ulit. Little did I know that I would end my career in government at this beautiful institution called the BSP. What else can I say? God has been very kind to me. Ang aming conversation lagi ni [SAG] EV [Edna Villa], and EV, of course, can recite verses from the Bible without looking at quotes. Ano ba ito? Is this luck, random events, or is this a greater being that is making us do things that we have done? Well, one explanation is that this is just written in your stars, or there is a greater being that brings us to a place where we have to make choices. Somehow, I think, we made the right ones. A good example is when and how much to raise interest rates, how to react to a depreciating peso, what to do with a problem bank, and so on and so forth. I keep saying that the reason Monetary Board meetings are relatively easy is [because of] the materials in front of you, which, of course, come from the various sectors. By the way, ‘yung BSP parang Star Trek—may sector 1, sector 2, sub-sector! Hinihintay ko na lang si Captain Kirk lumabas eh! By the way, I am such a bad father. I should ask my kids to please stand up: Louie and Iya. Dalawa pa lang iyan. ‘Yung dalawa nagtatrabaho. ‘Yung nanay nila, hindi makita. No, no, she is in the US [United States]. From the bottom of my heart and from the very core of my brain, ‘di ko makakalimutan ito. Assuming I get into a situation very similar to what is happening last year, I know that I will be okay, and if I am not okay, I will be calling all of you for your help. Again, maraming, maraming salamat. I will never forget this, except for medical reasons, which we hope will never happen. Talaga, I cannot think of a better way to end any man’s career in government. Maraming, maraming salamat sa inyong lahat. ###

Title: Navigating the Road Ahead: Looking Back to Look Forward
Occasion: Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas Turnover Ceremony
Speaker: BSP Governor Felipe M. Medalla
Content: ​ Magandang, magandang umaga sa inyong lahat! Gov. Eli [Remolona], members of the Monetary Board (MB), and fellow BSPers, it is the 30th anniversary of our beloved Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP). Let us celebrate! Ladies and gentlemen of the BSP, we, together, survived and, not only that, we thrived in a year full of extreme challenges, which [Deputy Governor] Chuchi [Fonacier] had already described. As COVID-19 lingered and work-from-home arrangements continued, we managed to ensure the uninterrupted supply of money across our country, kept the banking system strong and stable, made our financial system even more inclusive, battled record-high inflation—by the way, at the rate it is going, 18 straight months of above-target inflation, three months longer than the previous record—and helped provide stability to our economy. We did these together! Congratulations, BSPers! Now, for the specifics. Do not forget, I used to be a professor so this will take an hour. I promise, Gov. Eli, you will [have to] wait just a little bit more. Not an hour—maybe half an hour [or] shorter than that. Preserving price stability and building on solid foundations On price stability, it was a year like no other. We encountered unprecedented challenges over which the BSP has little control. Of course, we call them supply shocks. Supply chain disruptions, here and overseas, triggered price hikes in basic commodities, transport, and other services. We had to act decisively. And we did. During my term as Governor, the BSP raised the policy rates by a total of 350 basis points (bps), on top of the 75 bps already taken before I became Governor. By the way, I have to thank my predecessor, Gov. Ben [Diokno]. The last thing I thought a month before I became Governor was that am I really Governor? Of course, a series of events [happened]. Gov. Ben cannot say no to the call of duty to be Finance secretary in what I consider to be the toughest time to be Finance secretary. Here I am now, serving the term that actually started with [then Gov.] Nesting Espenilla. This is really, again, another unusual thing: 12 years, one governor—Say Tetangco; six years, three governors. I thank all the three governors before me. They laid the foundation for facing what turned out to be a very difficult year. As I said, we acted decisively. We also sold foreign exchange (forex) as necessary. By the way, this has now become standard language in the IMF [International Monetary Fund]. They used not to like it. It even has an acronym, “FXI,” [which stands for] foreign exchange intervention. In the old days, the IMF frowned on all these things. In addition, we communicated our policy actions and explained why we were doing them through forward guidance. Combined with non-monetary measures, our actions helped reduce second-round effects and re-anchor inflationary expectations. We have already brought inflation to a target-consistent path, operationally defined as within 2.0–4.0 percent. Unless there are new shocks, we should see inflation below 4.0 percent before the end of this year. This is something to look forward to and, of course, to pray for—that there will be no more large shocks. Of course, as they say, anything that can happen can happen, but anything you pray for, you hope to happen. On the exchange rate front: After depreciating year-to-date from January to October 2022 to a record low of ₱59.0 to US$1, the peso has since appreciated against the dollar, closing on 30 June 2023 at ₱55.2 to US$1. Our more stable exchange rate helps us avoid excessive depreciation that may dislodge inflation expectations and add to inflationary pressures. For those of you who have taken monetary economics, [in] a standard [economics] textbook on inflation targeting, there is hardly any mention of the exchange rate, aside from saying that to a good inflation-targeting central bank, the exchange rate does not matter until it does. Unfortunately, that time came. It did matter quite a bit. I am also pleased to share that Fitch acknowledged the credibility of the BSP’s inflation-targeting framework and flexible exchange rate regime. Keeping banks healthy and strong Now, to our second pillar. On maintaining price stability, I am pleased to report that on account of our continuing reforms, our banks are strong, stable, well-capitalized, highly liquid, and well-governed. Thus, our banks have remained a source of strength and support to our local economy and [facilitated] a strong recovery right after the restrictions on mobility were lifted. While financial stability is a responsibility that we share with other financial regulators, like the Securities and Exchange Commission, the Insurance Commission, the Philippine Deposit Insurance Corporation, and, of course, the Department of Finance, bank regulation remains central in a bank-centric economy [like the Philippines] where the balance sheet of the banking system was ₱23.1 trillion, about the same as our country’s [nominal] GDP [gross domestic product as of Q1 2023]. Today, the BSP has the flexibility and policy space to preserve its two pillars of price and financial stability without needing to sacrifice one for the other. In addition, we take an active role in promoting sustainable finance through our 11-point Sustainable Central Banking Strategy. In leading by example, we have also made it a point to invest some of our forex reserves in green assets. Making the payments system more digital and inclusive Now, to our third pillar. We continue to make good progress on our third pillar of providing a safe, secure, and efficient payments and settlements system. Among others, 42.1 percent of retail payments in 2022 were already digital. This puts us on track to achieve our target of digitalizing half of domestic retail payments by the end of this year. The volume of digital payments continues to grow significantly. For instance, InstaPay transaction volumes have already exceeded ATM [automated teller machine] transactions. Soon, PESONet transactions will overtake checks in our paperless shift. Now, on financial inclusion. We actively rolled out the Paleng-QR Ph Plus program, which promotes the use of digital transactions and QR [quick response] payments among market vendors, public transportation drivers, and microentrepreneurs. On top of that, we now have been given the power to strengthen consumer protection alongside widespread digitalization. We have produced circulars in line with the implementation of the Financial Products and Services Consumer Protection Act. By the way, for those who are not familiar, we actually have a little court here in the BSP that settles [consumer] disputes. Awards and recognition I am also happy to report that the BSP continues to receive awards: • the Presidential Lingkod Bayan Award for the BSP’s Cash Service Alliance core team and • the Cyber Resilience Initiative Award from Central Banking’s FinTech & RegTech Global Awards. Other citations received were for our polymer banknotes, investor relations engagement, museum website, and even a National Book Award. Expression of gratitude Ladies and gentlemen, the BSP hurdled the challenges that came our way in the past year because we were focused and committed to pursuing our mandates. I can say that all BSPers—in Luzon, Visayas, and Mindanao—contributed to this amazing year. Everybody should be thanked in general, but some people are worth mentioning by name. Allow me to give my own citations. To the members of the Monetary Board and their valuable guidance and counsel—as I keep saying, we disagree but we are never disagreeable: • Finance Secretary Benjamin Diokno, • Peter Favila, and • Tony Abacan. By the way, Tony and Peter, just like me, are moving to other endeavors. I suppose they will help the BSP in every way they can. Now, the board members whose terms expire three years from now: • Bruce Tolentino, • Annie Aquino, and, • of course, Gov. Eli, who finished my term as a board member and is now Governor. For their excellent work, I would like to thank the following—as I call you, please stand up and get recognized: • Deputy Governor Francis Dakila and the Monetary and Economics Sector—I love Francis. He knows how to get out of his comfort zone. During our programs, he even dances; • Deputy Governor Chuchi Fonacier and the Financial Supervision Sector—when there is a job to be done, send the woman. The banks understand that; • Deputy Governor Mamerto Tangonan and the Payments and Currency Management Sector; • I think, Ed is feeling bad so he could not be here this morning, but Deputy Governor Ed Bobier and the Corporate Services Sector; and • of course, how could I forget, my former student, Deputy Governor Berna Romulo-Puyat and the Regional Operations and Advocacy Sector. My dear BSPers, I have served the BSP for 12 years—11 years as a member of the MB and one year as head of our institution. And while my one-year stint as Governor of the BSP was full of challenges, ultimately, it was successful and memorable because we worked together for our economy and our people. Fellow BSPers, President Ferdinand R. Marcos, Jr. has chosen Governor Eli M. Remolona to head our central bank for the next six years. We are very, very fortunate to have him here. Finally, although they are not with us now—this is the secret of my happy marriage; my wife is often out of the country—I thank my family, especially my wife Pinky and our children, although they are too old to be called “children.” I am blessed to have this loving family that has kept me inspired, grounded, and strong, no matter what I do or fail to do. And for that, I will always be grateful. Muli, maraming, maraming salamat sa inyong lahat! Mabuhay ang BSP! Mabuhay ang ating mahal na bansang Pilipinas! ###​

Title: Message during the BSP's 30th Anniversary and Turnover Ceremony
Occasion: BSP's 30th Anniversary and Turnover Ceremony
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli. M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ Governor Felipe Medalla, Secretary Ben Diokno, Secretary Arsenio Balisacan, Secretary Amenah Pangandaman, members of the Monetary Board, our whole BSP family, our friends—magandang umaga po! First of all, happy 30th anniversary! What a distinct honor it is to stand with Gov. Philip at this great moment in our history as an institution! His resolute leadership during trying times allowed the BSP to find solid ground for our country’s post-pandemic recovery. And on this ground we can continue to build! Today we honor Gov. Philip for his expert stewardship of this institution and the economy it serves. I myself owe him a personal debt of gratitude. He has been a great mentor and a lifelong friend. And what an experience it has been to work with someone with his brilliance and his sense of humor! Si Gov. Philip po ay hindi lang magaling na gobernador, masaya rin siyang kasama! Maraming maraming salamat, Gov. Philip! Sa unang araw ko naman bilang BSP Governor, may dalawang puntos akong nais ibahagi. Ang unang puntos po ay PAGPAPATULOY. Together we must build on the progress we have already made. Inflation has finally started to come down as Gov. Philip explained. And if our models are right, we should be back in our target range even by the end of this year. Our banking system has remained strong. Capital and liquidity have been more than adequate. That’s why, in recovering from the pandemic, our banks have been a source of strength, unlike in previous crises, when they were a source of weakness. The plumbing of our system -- our payments and settlement system -- has increasingly become digitalized and efficient. We are delivering greater and greater access to financial services for our people. Ang pangalawang puntos po ay PAGPAPABUTI. I know we’re all eager to work even harder. I know we have what it takes to do even better -- especially if we work together. Naalala niyo ba yung finals ng NBA -- noong Hunyo? Ang pakirandam ko para tayong Denver Nuggets. Diba ang galing nila, lalo na si Nikola Jokic? Pero sa game 2 parang natsambahan sila ng Miami Heat. Kaya sabi siguro ni Coach Michael Malone sa Nuggets, sana galingan pa natin para hindi tayo matsambahan! Sana sa BSP rin, galingan pa natin para hindi tayo matsambahan! Allow me to end by quoting from the BSP Hymn, and you’ll thank me for not singing it: Ang layunin mo ay sadyang wagas, Antas ng buhay ay iyong itaas, Sa kaunlarang di magwawakas. Maraming salamat po at mabuhay tayong lahat!

Title: Message during the Post-SONA Philippine Economic Briefing
Occasion: Post-SONA Philippine Economic Briefing
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli. M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ Colleagues in government, friends from the banking community, partners from the private sector, ladies and gentlemen, magandang araw po. It is my pleasure to welcome you all to the second post-State of the Nation Address and Economic Briefing of President Ferdinand Marcos, Jr. themed "Agenda for Prosperity: Economic Transformation Toward Inclusivity and Sustainability." Our economy recovered from one of the world’s longest and most stringent lockdowns to stage a remarkable recovery–one of the fastest in the region. Part of the reason behind this economic resurgence–I would say–is the strength of our banking system. Unlike in previous crises, our banks formed part of the solution rather than part of the problem. This is, in part, due to our work in ensuring financial stability. Our challenge now, is inflation. Fortunately, the BSP’s inflation-targeting framework has served us well in the face of unusual supply shocks. We continue to focus on our mandate of price stability and have dedicated our resources and attention in pursuit of this goal. Our models tell us that inflation will be within the target range of 2 to 4 percent by the 4th quarter of 2023. This is the range that we think is ideal for an economy like the Philippines when it is growing at full capacity. We also face increased digitalization in both our banking and payments systems. This presents opportunities for efficiency, competitiveness, and inclusion. We are mindful and we will manage the attendant risks to ensure continued trust and confidence in this increasingly digital economy. We want to bring more Filipinos into this financial fold, so that as many of us as possible can share in the fruits of economic progress. In all this, collaboration between the public and private sectors is crucial. We need to work together toward an economy that is strong, stable, sustainable, and financially inclusive. I am sorry that I cannot join you in person but our Deputy Governor, Francis Dakila, will represent the BSP in the panel discussion. Thank you very much at mabuhay po tayong lahat. ###

Title: Building on the past, nurturing a bright future
Occasion: BSP 30th Anniversary Reception for the Banking Community
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli. M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ Former central bank governors, members of the Monetary Board, friends from the banking sector, the diplomatic corps and fellow workers in government: Magandang gabi po; good evening! Tonight, I am honored to be standing here before all of you. Thank you for joining us tonight. Let me begin by expressing my gratitude to my predecessor and good friend, Governor Philip Medalla. While he’s not able to join us tonight, I want to express my thanks to him for his dedication to upholding the integrity of our monetary system and for resolutely keeping the economy to its course towards price stability. Let me now tell you about an interview I had two weeks ago with Kathleen Hays, Bloomberg’s dean of economic reporting. She asked me, “Governor, when you’re not thinking about monetary policy, what do you think about?” The right answer, of course, was, “When I’m not thinking about monetary policy, I think about my lovely wife!” But being the central banking nerd that I am, I said instead, “When I’m not thinking about monetary policy, I think about banking supervision and the payments system!” So you’ll forgive me for saying a few words about each of those three things: monetary policy, banking supervision, and the payments system. Let me start with how our economy recovered from the crisis of the pandemic. As we all know, it was a surprisingly strong recovery. Coming out of a crisis, it was a recovery like no other. What was the difference this time? I’d say it was the banking system. Our banks maintained more than adequate levels of capital and remained flush with liquidity. This time, there was no need to repair balance sheets. I have said this before, and I’ll say it again. Unlike in previous crises, this time our banks were part of the solution rather than part of the problem. Let me pause at this point to thank our friends in the banking sector. And let’s not forget to also thank Deputy Governor Chuchi Fonacier for her persistence and that of her team in banking supervision. Let me now turn to our immediate challenge, that of inflation. While still in the process of recovering from the pandemic, we were hit by an unusual confluence of supply shocks. We were hit, for example, by a spike in global prices of fertilizer – so not just food prices and not just oil prices.. This was due, of course, to the sanctions on [shipping from] Belarus and Russia. In response, we tightened monetary policy and we tightened aggressively, the way an inflation-targetting central bank would. We worried about expectations, and we worried about second-round effects. Today, we’re beginning to see tantalizing fruits of our efforts. Headline inflation seems to have peaked and looks to be on its way to our target range of 2 to 4 percent. Our monetary board, our deputy governor Francis Dakila and his team are to be thanked for this. Nonetheless, it’s too soon to declare victory. Core inflation remains high. There are still upside risks to inflation – for example, risks in the form of El Niño and further supply shocks. We will wait and see. We will analyze the data as they arrive, and that analysis will decide monetary policy down the road. The next challenge is the payments system. For the sake of efficiency, we seek the magic of digitalization. We want this magic to also help with financial inclusion. We’re making some progress. At last count, 42 percent of retail payments were in digital form. This is up from just 1 percent ten years ago. That proportion should hit our target of 50 percent this year. At this stage, we’ve given licenses to 258 payments system operators1. Over time, we expect competition and network effects to result in a system where the most innovative, efficient and responsible providers truly respond to the needs of customers. This digitalization has been a pathway to financial inclusion. More Filipinos are now part of the formal financial system. In our financial inclusion survey in 2021, 56 percent of [adults] in the country had a bank account, a significant increase from just 23 percent in 2017. We’re confident we will reach our target of 70 percent by this year. We’re not stopping here. These accounts should provide the opportunity for people to build savings buffers, invest in their future and more actively participate in the digital economy. Programs like Paleng-QR help digitalize crucial value chains of merchants and SMEs. We’re so excited about all this! For this excitement, we can thank Deputy Governors Mert Tangonan and Berna Romulo Puyat and their teams. We will continue to work hard in pursuing our mandate of ensuring price stability, financial stability and a safe and efficient payment system. We will do this through greater investment in our research and operational capacities to become a more responsive, efficient, agile and future-ready institution. We lean on Deputy Governor Ed Bobier and his team. Thank you, DG Ed. We’re going to work actively with our colleagues in government and stakeholders on reforms to deepen our capital market as well as strengthen our sustainability agenda. In all these, we need your help. Let us continue to work together. At this point, let me call on my fellow Monetary Board members to join me onstage for a toast: • Secretary Diokno • MBM Annie • MBM Bruce ### ______________________________________________________________________ 1. Corrected

Title: Rising to the Challenge: The Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas
Occasion: Philippine Economic Briefing in Laoag, Ilocos Norte
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ Congressman Angelo Marcos Barba, Vice Governor Cecilia Araneta Marcos, Batac City Mayor Albert Chua, Laoag City Mayor Michael Marcos Keon, guests from various sectors, partners in nation-building, naimbag a bigat! I am happy to be in Laoag for the first post-State of the Nation Address Economic Briefing. I would like to talk about just three things: inflation, exchange rate pressures, [and] our banking system, including its role in sustainability. Let me start by discussing the recent trend in inflation. The blue line that you can see is the inflation rate for headline inflation. This is the part of inflation that is driven more by supply-side factors, and this is the part that the central bank has less control over. The red line is inflation based on the core CPI [consumer price index], which excludes the volatile components of prices, namely food and energy. This is the one that is more easily subject to monetary policy. From the peak of 8.7 percent in January, headline inflation slowed to 4.7 percent in July. This is due to improving domestic food supply conditions and lower global oil prices. But core inflation has remained high at 6.7 percent, although it has started to decline. That decline is due mainly to monetary policy. So, how did we bring about this inflation downtrend? The Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) responded to inflation by aggressively raising its policy rate. As of today, we have raised the policy rate by [a cumulative] 425 basis points (bps). In the last two meetings, we have paused. We are reassessing the situation. That is where we stand. We will go where the data leads us, but sometimes, the data does not seem to know where to go. The good news is that inflation expectations are still well-anchored. The green line in the picture shows inflation expectations for this year have evolved. The blue line is more important. It is about [inflation] expectations for 2024. The red line is about 2025. They are very well-anchored. In other words, the markets continue to believe that we will hit our target range by 2024 and stay there for 2025. There are good reasons for these expectations. The blue graph here reflects what our models say. If you just focus on the darkest part of the blue graph, you will see that by the end of this year, it [inflation] will have fallen to our target range of 2.0–4.0 percent. In fact, it may overshoot at the end of Q1 next year. The bad news is that there are upside risks. These include transport fare hikes, higher minimum wage constraints, and the El Niño phenomenon, which is already causing drought in our neighbors, including Vietnam where we get most of our rice imports. If you look at the Ilocos Region, the downward trend [in inflation] is also evident. That is on the next slide. Inflation in the Ilocos Region declined from a peak of 9.3 percent in January to 3.6 percent in July. We see the same trend in all four provinces of the region. We have also to thank non-monetary measures [for the decline in inflation]. We have had short-term strategies, including ensuring food reserves, enhancing agricultural resilience, and boosting economic support. We also have medium to long-term strategies, including strengthening the local food systems, upgrading infrastructure, and streamlining regulations for investments. Let me now turn to the exchange rate. As you know, the exchange rate also affects inflation. The central bank has been using three tools. In its use of monetary policy tools, the BSP keeps an eye on the exchange rate. Monetary policy is about adjusting the key policy interest rate. As I have said before, we have raised that by [a cumulative] 425 bps. That has helped strengthen and stabilize the peso. We have occasionally intervened in the foreign exchange (forex) market. This is to avoid destabilizing swings in the exchange rate, which tend to upset the markets. We have also been offering forward guidance. These are hints about what we might do in the next few [policy] meetings. This forward guidance reduces uncertainty in the market. As a consequence of that, forex pressures have eased. Since 2023, you can see that it has been relatively stable. The peso appreciated by 4.9 percent as of [8] August 2023, relative to its year-high depreciation level of ₱59 to the dollar in October 2022. As of 8 August, the peso closed at ₱56.24 to a dollar. This is partly due to the US Fed [United States Federal Reserve]’s decision to raise its own policy rate by 25 bps as well as the rating downgrade by Fitch of the US’ long-term rating from AAA to AA+. Compared to our neighbors, the peso has weakened only slightly. The peso is somewhere in the middle. It is the red bar. That red bar shows that since the beginning of the year, the peso has depreciated by less than 1.0 percent. This depreciation is smaller than those of our neighbors. As you can see in the red bars, that includes the Thai baht, Malaysian ringgit, South Korean won, and Japanese yen. In relation to the exchange rate, external inflows have helped us stabilize the exchange rate as well as helped us maintain our international reserves. On the left, you see the [overseas] Filipinos’ cash remittances. That is the light blue bar. Then, you can see the dark blue bar. The light blue bar is BPO [business process outsourcing] revenues. Those have been growing. Remittances and BPO revenues together amount to more than six times net foreign direct investments—which is shown in the right picture—which has somewhat declined but, thankfully, is a smaller part of our [forex] inflows. As a consequence of these inflows, we have been able to maintain ample [forex] reserves. These reserves provide a cushion against negative global spillovers. Our foreign reserves have hovered around US$100 billion, and we expect it to remain at roughly the same level in the coming years. We need these reserves because, we think, the world will slow down next year and in the following years, especially [because] of what the Fed has done. As you know, the Fed has tightened so aggressively that it is bound to slow down not just the US economy but also the rest of the world. This poses risks to us and other emerging markets because financial accidents could happen. For example, [the collapse of] SVB [Silicon Valley Bank] and Credit Suisse, which, thankfully, so far, have left us unscathed. It is a good thing we have ample reserves because this is part of our defense against negative global spillovers. In looking at our external accounts, it is important to go beyond just reserves and quarter-to-quarter inflows. It is [also] important to look at the current account. The current account, which is how much we borrow abroad and send abroad, is basically the difference between our investments as a country and our savings as a country. In the graph, the red line is savings and the blue line is investments. Until recently, we have been saving more than we have been investing, and it has led to a current account surplus. But there is, as you know, an acute shortage of investments. We have started to ramp up our investments, especially in infrastructure. Our savings have not yet caught up with that. That is why, in 2022, we saw current account deficit [increase] to 4.4 percent of GDP [gross domestic product]. That is not really a bad thing. With productive investments, what will eventually happen is that GDP growth and savings will outpace investments. This will narrow the gap between savings and investments. Eventually, savings will exceed investments, and we will have a current account surplus. Whatever we borrowed when we had a deficit, we can repay now that we have a surplus. That is a direction we anticipate for the current account. Let me now say something about our banking system. As you can see from the picture, despite the tightening [in monetary policy], growth has been robust. We have recovered strongly from one of the world’s longest and most stringent lockdowns. We have done so faster than most Asian countries. For this, I think, we can thank our banking sector as well as the BSP’s emphasis on financial stability. The picture on the left shows you the international standard for capital. That is the blue bar. The banks’ actual capital, as shown in the yellow bar on the right, far exceeded what international standards say about the capital that banks need to hold. The bars on the right show the liquidity position of the banks. The blue bar shows the international standard for the liquidity of banks, and the yellow bar shows the actual liquidity that banks hold. This gives us confidence that the policy rate adjustments were done without risking financial stability. Let me now turn to digitalization. I think, our digitalization efforts are on track. Account ownership has almost doubled in two years [from 2019 to 2021], as shown by the picture on the left. That means 22 million Filipinos opened an account in 2021. This is the highest growth to date for the country. We would like it to go to 70 percent by the end of this year. On the right, you will see the percentage of Filipino grown-ups with e-money accounts. That has breached 36 percent in 2021. This trend is a big jump in the percentage of Filipinos with e-money accounts. The payment that we use in shops and stores is increasingly digital. In 2022, the share of digital payment transactions to the total volume of retail payments rose to 42 percent, and we are trying to hit our target of 50 percent by the end of this year. Let me say something about our strategies for sustainability. We want to strengthen our programs for sustainability, but we want sustainability with a heart. As a nation, we signed the Paris Agreement in 2015. This means we are committed to the global initiative of net zero carbon emissions by 2025, and nationally, our determined contributions are to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by 75 percent by 2030. For its part, the BSP champions sustainability in the financial system. We

Title: 2023 Alliance for Financial Inclusion Global Policy Forum “Stability, Sustainability, and Inclusivity for Shared Prosperity”
Occasion: 2023 Alliance for Financial Inclusion Global Policy Forum
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ Ladies and gentlemen, good morning … welcome to the 2023 Global Policy Forum of the Alliance for Financial Inclusion! The Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas is honored to host this year’s gathering of financial inclusion champions and advocates from the Philippines and other parts of the globe. We are joined this morning by: The Honorable Secretary of the Philippines Department of Social Welfare and Development Rex Gatchalian The Honorable Secretary of the Philippine Department of Finance Benjamin Diokno Board Chair of the Alliance for Financial Inclusion Governor Ariff Ali of the Reserve Bank of Fiji AFI Executive Director Dr. Alfred Hannig Founding Chair of the Asia School of Business and former Governor of Bank Negara Malaysia Dr. Zeti Aziz Colleagues from central banks and financial regulators Partners and stakeholders from government, business, and NGOs Resource persons, the media, and special guests We have about 700 delegates from over 70 countries signed up for this year’s Global Policy Forum. We thank you all for joining us here. We can look forward to diverse, rich, and multi-faceted discussions on how we can more effectively uplift the lives of the marginalized and the excluded… through financial inclusion. Gains in financial inclusion are being made, in varying degrees, from country to country. But there are also setbacks that we must reckon with. Climate change is one. In the Philippines, for instance, stronger and more frequent storms destroy agricultural crops and other livelihoods. These result in income losses that drive the underprivileged deeper into poverty. Our sustainability agenda, therefore, is one that will be inherently inclusive. On the other hand, there are challenges that present both risks and opportunities. Among others, the pandemic-induced lockdowns that kept people indoors caused business closures and job loss. But the same lockdowns triggered a boom in online selling and digital payments for goods and services from businesses that were quick to adapt to mobility constraints. This raised the share of digital transactions to retail payments in our country from 14% in 2019 to 42% in 2022. Many consumers opened digital accounts or e-wallets. Government also used digital accounts to distribute cash assistance during the pandemic. As a result, mobile wallet ownership surged from 8% in 2019 to 36% in 2021. This helped raise the overall number of Filipinos with transaction accounts from 29% in 2019 to 56% in 2021… equivalent to 22 million Filipinos who became financially included in the span of two years. This milestone is important to us. Transaction accounts are gateways to financial inclusion. We have seen depositors improve their lives from gaining access to other financial services, such as loans, insurance, and investments. For instance, insurance helps farmers recover faster from losses inflicted by natural calamities, while small business loans help microentrepreneurs expand or pivot their businesses. Indeed, while we have a long way to go in our financial inclusion journey, millions of Filipinos are already benefitting from our multi-pronged financial inclusion initiatives. Ladies and gentlemen, I stand on the shoulders of giants. The BSP’s financial inclusion journey started during the term of then Governor Rafael Buenaventura. The General Banking Law, which was enacted in 2000, mandated the BSP to recognize microfinance as a legitimate banking activity. As a response, he declared microfinance as the BSP’s flagship program to help alleviate poverty in our country. His vision was to bring microfinance to every province in the Philippines. He called on banks to develop microfinance programs, provided incentives to banks that set up dedicated microfinance units, created a microfinance team within the central bank, and frequently requested journalists to write about microfinance. His successor was Governor Amando Tetangco Jr. He saw how microcredits could be so empowering for the entrepreneurial poor and, sometimes, even uplift the communities around them. For 12 straight years, Governor Tetangco chaired the national annual search for the best microentrepreneurs … and witnessed how thousands were liberated from poverty by economic activities financed by microcredit. He said: “We learned that financial inclusion is complementary to our primary objective of price and financial stability. Also, individuals who are adequately informed and included are effective economic agents.” Under Governor Tetangco’s term, the BSP expanded its focus from microfinance to financial inclusion. The goal was to give millions of Filipinos access to a broader range of financial services. The BSP also developed multisectoral partnerships to promote financial inclusion. This was the foundation for the inter-agency committee to implement the National Strategy for Financial Inclusion. It was Governor Nestor Espenilla Jr. who led the BSP’s adoption of digital technology for financial inclusion. To him, digital technology brings scale and affordability to financial transactions. And it opens new possibilities for delivering financial services … even to difficult-to-serve markets within our archipelago of 7,641 islands. Governor Espenilla was ready to give innovation a chance if it can move financial inclusion forward. He used the regulatory sandbox framework of “test and learn” to allow BSP supervised institutions, third-party service providers, and new players to offer financial products and services using new technology to a limited number of customers, in a controlled environment. This then informs the appropriate regulatory and supervisory approach. Last year, AFI started giving out the “Nestor Espenilla, Jr. Financial Inclusion Award,” which recognizes institutions that demonstrate outstanding commitment toward innovation and the use of technology to further financial inclusion. It was AFI’s way of honoring Governor Espenilla’s contributions to the advancement of financial inclusion through innovation. On behalf of the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas and Governor Espenilla’s family, represented here today by his wife Tess, we thank you AFI. To Governor Benjamin Diokno, BSP’s inclusion story is one of evolution, and laying the solid foundation that would carry us through ordinary and extraordinary times. It was under his term as chair of the NSFI Steering Committee that a Presidential Memorandum Circular (No. 97 dated June 23, 2022) was issued, institutionalizing the whole-of-government support for the implementation of the National Strategy for Financial Inclusion 2022-2028. The Circular also declared the adoption of financial inclusion as a development agenda at both the national and subnational levels. Governor Felipe Medalla, for his part, walked his talk on financial inclusion. With a team from the BSP, he went to public markets to engage market vendors, public transport drivers, and small businesses to shift to the safer and more convenient digital payments platform through Paleng-QR Ph Plus. To Governor Medalla, it is imperative that the BSP remains focused on doing its best to fulfill its mandates of maintaining price stability, financial stability, and a secure and efficient payment system. By doing so, we will have a strong foundation on which to expand our national financial inclusion program. And now, it is my turn. What an honor to join these champions, these giants of financial inclusion. Like my predecessors, I am fully committed to broaden and deepen the reach of financial inclusion, toward financial health, resilience, and a better life for millions of Filipinos. My previous posts at the Bank for International Settlements exposed me to how financial inclusion has been evolving. In fact, I was the BIS Chief Representative for Asia and the Pacific when the BIS co-hosted with the BSP a Research Conference on Financial Inclusion. This was in 2016 in Cebu City. And now, the BSP is hosting the 2023 AFI Global Policy Forum. All these should serve me and the BSP in good stead. With the continuing support of our colleagues, partners, and stakeholders, here and overseas, we at the Team BSP are confident that we will continue to help improve the lives of our unserved, underserved, and unreached. Together, let us address AFI’s call for “Stability, Sustainability, and Inclusivity for Shared Prosperity.” Thank you everyone! Marami pong salamat at Mabuhay tayong lahat! ###

Title: Closing Remarks during the 2023 Alliance for Financial Inclusion Global Policy Forum
Occasion: Closing Ceremonies, AFI 2023 Global Policy Forum
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ Fellow members of the Alliance for Financial Inclusion (AFI), esteemed colleagues, special guests, ladies and gentlemen. Good afternoon. We have come to the conclusion of the 2023 AFI Global Policy Forum. The active participation of the delegates and resource persons in this event has made our Forum a roaring success. On behalf of the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas, I thank you all for your contributions to our common goal of financial inclusion toward shared prosperity. Over the last two days, we had the opportunity to exchange knowledge, insights, and experiences. We examined how the twin goals of financial inclusion and sustainable development are vital to stability. We recognized how the continued introduction of new technology, such as artificial intelligence, presents both opportunities and risks. And how we must prioritize responsible innovation. We have identified the hurdles to driving financial inclusion in a dynamic environment. We have also taken a closer look at the unique challenges in serving various sectors. This entails a nuanced approach to adequately serve displaced persons, women, the youth, and small enterprises. The insights we have gained from fellow regulators and partners in the academe, government, and industry will inform us on how to address these challenges. The initiatives and commitments shared in the last few days will help us sharpen our tools and strengthen our resolve in building a global financial ecosystem that is stable, sustainable, and inclusive. The AFI research initiative that was presented this morning reminds us of the importance of evidence-based policymaking. The Manila Manifesto urges us to apply global regulatory standards without hindering financial inclusion. May the collective commitment serve as a powerful impetus for regulations that are responsive and relevant to our country contexts and shared goals. All these will build on our Maya Declaration commitments. Ladies and gentlemen. We have come so far, and farther, we will go. Let us work together to focus on building a more inclusive world where no one is left behind. Once again, the BSP thanks AFI for this opportunity to host this Forum. Thank you, AFI. And once again, thank you everyone for participating in this forum. Let’s also give ourselves a round of applause. Maraming salamat po! See you all in 2024.​

Title: Welcome Message for the Second Digital Financial Inclusion Awards (DFIA) – Awarding Ceremony
Occasion: 2nd Digital Financial Inclusion Awards
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli. M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ Citi Philippines CEO Paul Favila; Chairperson of the Microfinance Council of the Philippines Gilbert Maramba; founder of CARD MRI Aries Alip; fellow members of the National Selection Committee; advocates for digital financial inclusion; our outstanding digital microentrepreneurs and microfinance institutions, special guests, good afternoon and welcome to the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas. Today, we honor five microfinance institutions and 20 microentrepreneurs under the second Digital Financial Inclusion Awards. They have embraced the transformative power of digital technologies to grow their operations and serve their clients. The digital solutions they adopted ranged from e-commerce, social media marketing, and use of e-wallets. I can tell you, the National Selection Committee had a really tough time choosing the finalists. So rich was the pool of nominees. We applaud all the nominees for investing time and resources in digitalization. The BSP values this Digital Financial Inclusion Awards. It supports and promotes both microfinance institutions and microentrepreneurs who successfully adopted digital​ization to boost efficiencies and scale up customer services. Their initiatives are aligned with the BSP’s Digital Payments Transformation Roadmap and our National Strategy for Financial Inclusion. Under this roadmap, our goal is to convert 50 percent of the total volume of retail payments into digital form and to onboard 70 percent of Filipino adults to the formal financial system. So how are we doing? Well, the share of digital payment transactions reached 42 percent in 2022; account ownership was 56 percent in 2021; and 65 percent in 2022. We are gaining ground, but there is still so much more to do. Ladies and gentlemen. Microenterprises and microfinance improve the lives of so many Filipinos, more so when they are digitalized. Today’s awardees prove this. They are good role models that inspire and inform. I therefore thank our partners from Citi, from MCPI and the members of our national selection committee for supporting digital financial inclusion. To our awardees, we thank and congratulate all of you for your well-deserved recognition. Sana ay patuloy na mas dumami pa ang katulad ninyo na matagumpay sa larangan ng digital financial inclusion! Maraming salamat at magandang hapon po sa ating lahat! ​

Title: An Environment of Stability that Fosters Inclusive Growth
Occasion: Philippine Economic Briefing
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ Maayong umaga sa tanan. Nalipay ako, naari ako, subong sa Iloilo. Namit ang pagkaon; matahum ang esplanade. Cabinet secretaries and Mr. Speaker, it is my pleasure to give the opening remarks. I will talk about the role of the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) in fostering economic growth, and that role is largely to provide an environment of stability—stability in prices and stability in the banking system. In describing that role, I would also like to present the three challenges that we face as we try not just to provide stability, but also to strengthen sustainable growth and to strengthen inclusive growth. So, these challenges are three things. We are doing monetary policy in what is essentially a supply-side economy. We are being hit by supply shocks all the time. This is something new to the globe and to our economy. And then, to make the banking system even stronger, we want to digitalize the banking system. Finally, to make growth more inclusive, we are trying to fashion a payment system that serves the unbanked. Monetary policy So, let me start with the supply shocks. We have been hit by supply shocks, not just us but the whole world. The kinds of shocks, the likes of which we have never really seen before. We had shocks in oil, and we had shocks in food—those are not unusual. But we had shocks in semiconductors, and we had shocks in fertilizer—those supply shocks pushed inflation rates up. If you look at the graph, the blue line is headline inflation. That is our whole basket of food, energy, and services. That blue line went up to as high as 8.7 percent in January this year. What was pushing it up mainly was supply shocks. The difference between the blue line and the orange line, the orange line is core inflation—that is when we take out food and energy. But the difference is supply shocks. You can see the orange line was pushing up the blue line. This is true for the whole world, not just us. But we were harder hit than most. Finally, with the right kind of monetary policy, we have been bringing down inflation. As of November, the inflation rate was 4.1 percent, and I will explain what we have been doing to do that. Our dilemma is that, in some cases, when there are supply shocks, you just look through supply shocks; you ignore the supply shocks. Like what my mother used to say, “'Wag mo nang kamutin ang sugat mo, gagaling din 'yan." The thing with supply shocks is if they are large enough, if they are frequent enough, they affect expectations. And then, you get second-round effects—you get increases in wages; you get increases in services. The interesting thing about this other price is they go up, but they do not come down. The goods prices go up, and they come down. These other things, they go up, and they stay up. And so, that is the kind of inflation we have to manage somehow. And, I think, we have the advantage that in 2002, we instituted this thing called inflation targeting framework. In other words, we announce our target inflation rate. The target is now 2.0 [percent] to 4.0 percent. We announced that many years ago, and we are trying to stick to that target. If we are credible, the target serves to anchor inflation expectations. It tends to minimize the second-round effects. So, that is what we have been doing with monetary policy. We have been watching expectations closely. We have been making forward guidance. We have been telling the market that this is what we are going to do in the next few meetings, and that seems to help anchor expectations and reduce second-round effects. So, that is monetary policy. Robust banking system Now, thankfully, in our banking system, our banks have remained strong even through the pandemic. In the past when we had a crisis, the banks became part of the problem rather than part of the solution; they had to repair their balance sheets. This time, they remained very strong and very supportive of growth. The international standard for capital is 10 percent. So, we have been following that. But our banks are at 16 percent. They are more capitalized than the international standard. In terms of liquidity, the international standard is 100 percent. Our banks are at 183 percent—a lot of liquidity. So, liquidity, even with the monetary policy tightening, is very available, and the banks are strong enough to continue to lend. I say they are strong enough, but we want them to be even stronger, which is why we go to our program for digitalizing banks. Digitalizing banks There are four approaches. We have an Open Finance Framework—this is the thing that connects banks together. They share information of customers and so on. But we want to make sure that in this framework, the customers' data is only used with the consent of the customer. Of course, we also worry about cyber risk and all that. But the basic principle for the Open Finance Framework is when banks use all these new digital platforms, they use consumer data, and they make sure they first ask permission when they want to use that data. And then, we have this thing called “regulatory sandbox." This is where if you have a new innovation, you want to play around, you want to test it, you want to prove that it works, but we assign a regulator to you. So, the regulator helps you to minimize regulatory risk. This is not about predicting whether you will be successful or not, it is predicting whether you can obey important regulations. Digital banking is one of those things that are in a sandbox. We issued six digital licenses. These guys are able, actually very successful, to collect deposits just online. They do not have offices; it is all online. They are still struggling with figuring out how to make loans. That is the hard part, that is why they are still in a sandbox. And then, of course, we have generative AI [artificial intelligence]. This is a new thing for us, but, as you know, it is inevitable. So, even now, we have to examine the risks. So, when they become more widely used, we know how to regulate generative AI. Finally, we want a digital payment system that serves the unbanked. This is our strategy for helping the government with inclusive growth. We have two strategies actually. We have the local strategy in which we have e-wallets [electronic wallets], or we have the basic deposit account. That leads to bank credit for the unbanked and to other financial services so that the unbanked become part of the whole financial ecosystem. That is on the domestic side. On the international side, we have an exciting collaboration with our neighbors in the ASEAN [Association of Southeast Asian Nations]. For the neighbors that have digital fast payment [systems]—in our case, InstaPay is our digital fast payment system—we are collaborating on a platform. So, this will avoid going into dollars; this will avoid corresponding banks. We go to a platform where the peso can be easily exchanged for baht, ringgit, or rupiah. And then, it is going to be efficient, and the cost will come down. We hope—this is our goal in two years—to [reduce cost up to] 1.0 percent of the transfer. The G20 goal is 3.0 percent; our goal is 1.0 percent for moving money. And this thing is very scalable. It is going to be easy to include other countries. The United States, of course, they are not ready yet; they do not have the kind of fast payment [system] that we have. The countries that seem to be ready to be part of this are Brazil and India. This is very scalable, and the network effects are very powerful. Let me say a little bit more of what is happening domestically. We feel good that when you look at the share of digital payments to total transactions, we are seeing a shift from cash to digital payments. As of 2022, 42 percent of transactions have been digital. We are hoping that by this year, we do not have the numbers yet for 2023, it will reach 50 percent. Now, what has this done? It has brought people into the formal financial system. As of 2021, 56 percent of Filipino grownups have formal financial accounts. And we hope that by this year, it will be 70 percent so we are reassured that we are able to make progress. Conclusion So, to summarize, we are doing monetary policy in a supply-shock economy, and this is about understanding inflation expectations and anchoring those inflation expectations. We are also trying to digitalize the banking system. And here, we offer the Open Finance Framework, digital banking, a regulatory sandbox, and regulated generative AI. When it comes to inclusion, we are trying to fashion a payment system that offers a gateway into the banking system for the unbanked and an efficient cross-border payment system. Madamo gid nga salamat! ###

Title: Message for the First Ateneo Economics and Management Alumni Homecoming
Occasion: First Ateneo Economics and Management Alumni Homecoming
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli. M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ Magandang gabi sa inyong lahat! How about we talk about monetary policy in a supply-shock economy? In 2022, the world was hit by supply shocks, the likes of which we had not seen before. We saw these shocks in food, in oil, in semiconductors, and in fertilizer. As a consequence, global inflation rates went through the roof. In our case, in the Philippines, headline inflation rose to 8.7 percent last January, the highest in 14 years. In these circumstances, what is monetary policy to do? In the old-school Keynesian world, inflation is inflation regardless of the source. In the face of inflationary supply shocks, the central bank should tighten, as it would in the face of inflationary demand shocks. Of course, monetary policy works with a lag, while the impact of supply shocks tends to be immediate. You can do the math. On the other hand, if you lived in the make-believe world of so-called rational expectations, what you would do is you would look through the supply shocks. No need to do anything. Anyway, the shocks will dissipate. “'Wag mong kamutin ang sugat mo," my mother would say. “Gagaling din 'yan." Second-round effects Unfortunately, the real world is more complicated. In the real world, if supply shocks are large enough and frequent enough, they give rise to the kind of inflation expectations that lead to second-round inflationary effects. These second-round effects tend to be sticky downwards. Once wages go up because of supply shocks, they do not come down after the shocks dissipate. In general, with supply shocks, goods prices go up and then come down. In their wake, however, the prices of services go up and stay up. In other words, second-round effects are asymmetric. In this world of asymmetric second-round effects, inflation expectations become crucial. In this world of expectations, it helps enormously that in 2002, the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas adopted an inflation targeting framework. We now have a target range for inflation of between 2.0 [percent] and 4.0 percent. When monetary policy is credible, that target serves to anchor inflation expectations. In November, the inflation rate was 4.1 percent. That is within striking distance of our target. Yes, it is too soon to declare victory. But much of the credit for the decline in inflation is the fact that our inflation expectations have remained well-anchored and second-round effects have remained manageable. I guess that is enough economics for an alumni homecoming. Conclusion So, let me close with a few words of wisdom: Basta ang inflation rate ay bumaba ng todo, hindi na baleng si Gov. Eli ay sintunado! Noong Marso, pumutok ang SVB [Silicon Valley Bank] at Credit Suisse, buti na lang naiwasan natin ang global crisis! Ang masaklap lang, si KathNiel hindi na raw nagki-kiss! Maraming, maraming salamat po! ###

Title: Paradigm Shifts, Headwinds, and Opportunities
Occasion: Bloomberg Foreign Exchange Forum Manila
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ I am grateful that you have decided to listen to me. I think you expect me to talk about monetary policy, so I will talk about something else. I am going to talk about Greenspan's “spare tire." It is really about deepening the capital markets, and I am hoping, since you are in the markets, you can somehow help us in the central bank. As you know, the capital markets, if they are deep and liquid, they enhance the transmission of monetary policy. But I am going to focus on some other aspect of capital markets, and this is the aspect of the market as Greenspan's “spare tire." Let me just read to you what Alan Greenspan said in 1999, after the Asian [financial] crisis, about capital markets. “This leads one to wonder how severe East Asia's problems would have been had those economies not relied so heavily on banks as their means of financial intermediation. This is very Greenspan. Had a functioning capital market existed, the outcome might well have been far more benign." And then, he ends the paragraph by saying that East Asia had no spare tire. He never really defined what he meant by the “spare tire." But in Asia, especially in the countries hit hard by the crisis, we interpreted that to mean, essentially, the stock market and the corporate bond market. So, what Greenspan was suggesting was that when you manage your financial system, it is a matter of risk management that you have a banking system, and you also have an alternative source of financing, which would be the capital markets. And in my book, that means two things: capital markets and the stock market.
