If you just look at the size of those markets in Asia relative to GDP [gross domestic product], you get these two pictures. With regard to the stock market to GDP, this is 2022 data. Thailand has a stock market that is about 106 percent of its GDP; South Korea, 100 percent; Malaysia, 90 [percent]; Philippines, 72 [percent]; and Indonesia, 48.72 percent. That is kind of pathetic, I think. We used to have a vibrant stock market in the Philippines; it is not so vibrant anymore. If we look at the corporate bond markets—South Korea, 87 percent; Malaysia, 54 [percent]; Thailand, 26 [percent]; Philippines, 7.0 percent; and Indonesia, 2.0 percent—Indonesia makes us look good. But, I think, 7.0 percent is also pathetic. So, what are the issues? I think that for these markets, we have more questions than answers. For the corporate bond market, we call it the “Lake Wobegon question." This is all about the access of borrowers to the bond market, and I will talk about that. Then, there is an issue of the stock market. The issue is that when an event is not an event. I am talking here [of] insider trading. And then, finally, I think there is a big shift globally away from active investing to passive investing. These days, that has to do with exchange-traded funds, or ETFs, and we seem to not have been invited to this party. The rest of the world, they are having fun, and we are not invited. So, we need to do something about that. Those of you who are old enough would know about this guy, Garrison Keillor. There was a dude named Garrison Keillor, [an] announcer in Minneapolis, I think. He would refer to Lake Wobegon, a fictional town in Minnesota. He said, “In Lake Wobegon, all the women are strong; all the men are good-looking; and all the children are above-average. I think that is the corporate bond market in the Philippines. If you look at the pie chart, the pie chart on the left is the composition by credit ratings of the corporate bond market. Blue is triple-A, and the grey is double-A. Local bond market So, in the Philippines, the corporate bond market is like Lake Wobegon because the bonds are all either triple-A or double-B. If you look at Thailand, 60 percent of the corporate bond market is actually single-A. Single-A—and then, an additional 13 percent is triple-B. So, this is a market where there is much more access by borrowers to the bond market. In the Philippines, BPI [Bank of the Philippine Islands] just issued a corporate bond. BPI, of course, is triple-A. And they only wanted to—the plan was to issue only ₱5.0 billion. Does anyone remember this? I think it was a week ago. They ended up issuing ₱36 billion. There is such a demand for corporate bonds. And you would think that with that kind of demand, it should be easy to issue single-A. But, somehow, no single-As. People say that investors are not going to be interested in single-A corporate bonds. That is what they used to say in Thailand, too. But now, the median bond issued in Thailand is single-A. There is a significant number of triple-B issues. There are even double-B issues in Thailand, non-investment-grade issues. So, why cannot we do what Thailand has done? One possible solution, maybe, is to bring in someone like Michael Milken. I do not know if you guys remember Michael Milken. He was known as a “junk bond king" of the 1980s. He worked for Drexel Burnham Lambert, and he created a whole network of investors in high-yield bonds. High-yield bonds [or] “junk bonds" are below investment grade. And then, he was able to raise vast amounts of money through high-yield bonds. A lot of this money went into leveraged buyouts—very successful. And today, the median corporate bond issue in the United States is double-B. It is not investment-grade; it is junk. The median issue is junk. So, maybe, we can do the same thing? Of course, to be honest, Michael Milken, eventually went to jail for corporate fraud. He went to jail for two years. But, I think, maybe, he just overdid it and went too far. But the beginning of his career before he went to jail, maybe, would be something to emulate. Insider trading How about the stock market? In the stock market, there is an issue of when is an event not an event. As you know, sometimes listed firms' stock prices show no reaction at all to the public release of, say, earnings report. The earnings report comes out, but the stock price does not move. Something is funny about that. In the States, of course, if the earnings report is better than expected, the stock prices go up on the day of the release of the report. And, of course, if the report is not as good as expected, the stock price goes down. There are markets in which the stock price does not move upon the release of the earnings report. And we know why. It suggests that insiders have already seen the report before it was released. So, when it is released, there is no more reaction from the stock market. There is an academic named Utpal Bhattacharya. He looked up 87 stock markets in the world, and these are the markets that had insider trading laws. Now, among those 87 countries, only in 38 of them were insider trading laws enforced. And that makes a difference. The laws in the books are not enough. You have to enforce the laws. But, interestingly, in the places where insider trading laws are enforced, if you estimate the cost of equity financing for firms, it is much lower. This suggests that if you can somehow fix insider trading, then there will be more IPOs [initial public offerings]. And then, you will have a bigger market—and, maybe, a deeper and more liquid market. In the Philippines, the enforcement of insider trading laws is an issue. Let me quote from corporate lawyer Francis Lim. For those of you who know law firms, he is part of ACCRA [Angara, Abello, Concepcion, Regala, and Cruz Law Offices] today, but he was also president of the Philippine Stock Exchange. And he admits that insider trading still exists. He said, “Insider trading is an evil that our laws have long wanted to eradicate to shore up public confidence in our stock market." However, he lamented that the courts have yet to find one single individual guilty of insider trading. So, that is where we are at the moment. This is from the Philippine Star in January 2019. That is the second issue. Exchange-traded funds issue Let me get to the third one, which is the ETF [exchange-traded funds] issue. As you know, William Sharpe, a professor at Stanford University, received the Nobel Prize in 1990. It was essentially about market efficiency, how you price stocks. But one of his conclusions was you might as well just diversify because markets are so efficient that it is very hard to actually make money by trading actively. So, leave that to the big boys, to the guys who do Bloomberg. You, yourself, the mortals among us, just diversify. Just buy the market portfolio. And the market portfolio is the most diversified of all portfolios. He did that work in the 1980s. And after 30 years, investors began to listen, and there was a massive shift from active investing to passive investing. So, what you do is you just buy an index, try to replicate the index as best you can, and invest in that index. Now, the big asset management firms have done that for you. They have put together index funds. The big three are, of course, BlackRock, Vanguard, and State Street. So, they offer index funds that replicate big indices—these are very diversified portfolios that you can invest in. The thing about ETFs, these are basically indices that are very liquid. You can trade them. So, if you want a portfolio, you can buy an ETF. If you do not like the portfolio and want to move to a fixed income, then you can sell the ETF. I do not know if you know how ETFs are created, but they used to be closed-end funds. But closed-end funds had the issue that there was always some kind of a discount or a premium over net asset value. And so, the likes of BlackRock, Vanguard, and State Street, they invented an arbitrage strategy so that there would be no discount or no premium over asset value and you have a very liquid—essentially, a market portfolio that is very liquid that you would trade very actively. Now, that is in equity. The same thing has been happening in fixed income. For our purposes, the relevant fixed-income ETFs are managed by the big ones, JP Morgan, Barclays, and Inv

Title: Message for the 34th National Statistics Month Closing Ceremony and Public Information Campaign
Occasion: 34th National Statistics Month Closing Ceremony and Public Information Campaign
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ The Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) joins the Philippine statistical community in celebrating the 34th National Statistics Month. The BSP is a member of the Philippine statistical system and a producer of economic and financial data. We underscore the importance of accurate, timely, and relevant statistics. These are essential in fostering our economy's growth and resilience against shocks so that we can promote a high quality of life for all Filipinos. Our plans are only as good as the information upon which they are based. As such, the BSP continues to improve our statistics, in part, by adhering to international standards, including best statistical practices. We are leveraging advancements in technology and analytics to develop better and better economic indicators. The BSP will continue to support the Philippine statistical community in its goal of a stronger and more inclusive economy. Maraming salamat po, at mabuhay tayong lahat. ###​

Title: Message for the Outstanding BSP Stakeholders Appreciation Ceremonies
Occasion: Outstanding BSP Stakeholders Appreciation Ceremonies
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ Magandang hapon po sa inyong lahat! On behalf of my colleagues at the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP), I am pleased to welcome all of our partners from the public and the private sectors to this 2023 Outstanding BSP Stakeholders Appreciation Ceremony. Today, we recognize 29 of our outstanding partners from Metro Manila, nearby provinces, and South Luzon. All of you helped the central bank fulfill its mandates; all of you have helped us with this work. You have contributed to supporting our economy and making lives better for the Filipino people. You have helped us build our three pillars of central banking. These are price stability; financial stability; and a safe, secure, and efficient payments and settlements system. We also have advocacies of financial inclusion and of proper handling of banknotes and coins. The stakeholders we work with belong to four broad categories. The first category covers the financial institutions supervised by the BSP. These financial institutions work with the BSP in the pursuit of all our three pillars. They serve as conduits for monetary policy transmission, they are our partners in ensuring financial stability, and they are champions of digital payments. The second category covers our partners from the national and local governments that collaborate with the BSP for specific advocacies. The third category covers the associations that represent different areas of the financial services industry. Their participation in monetary and regulatory discourse provides valuable insights into policymaking at the BSP. The fourth and last category recognizes the institutions that support the BSP through innovative services and solutions. Ladies and gentlemen, the BSP is fortunate to have partners and stakeholders like you who are committed to helping us pursue our mandates and advocacies in the service of our country. For this, we at the BSP thank all of you. Together, let us congratulate all of our awardees today! I hope that the partnerships we forge continue to develop and deepen so that we may generate more gains and more benefits for the people we serve. Marami pong salamat, at mabuhay tayong lahat! ###​

Title: Message for the 2023 Philippine Economic Briefing - USA
Place: The Ritz Carlton, San Francisco, United States
Occasion: 2023 Philippine Economic Briefing
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ Good morning. It is an honor to be here. As you know, the job of a central bank is to provide an environment of stability that will foster growth, that will foster investments. So today, let me talk very quickly about the three challenges we face. These three are the ones that tend to keep us awake at night. But we do have other challenges, and I am happy to talk about those. So those three, I would say, are: First, how do you conduct monetary policy in an economy that keeps getting hit by supply shocks? It is because of our geography. We tend to be prone to supply shocks. There is a kind of monetary policy that deals with that, and I want to say a little bit about that. Second, just like the national government, we want to digitalize. And here, we want to help the banking system digitalize—make the banking system more effective in the sense of making it more efficient, more inclusive, more reliable, and safe. So, that is for the banking system. And then, I want to say a little bit about the payments system. In this role, the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) is trying to fashion a new payment system that works especially for financial inclusion. Navigating monetary policy amid supply shocks So, let me go to monetary policy. If you look at the chart, the red line is oil prices, spot oil prices, and the blue line is core inflation. You know, the old school for monetary policy says, “If you [see] supply shocks, you just look through them because they are going to dissipate anyway." But that is really not how it works because when supply shocks are large and they come in quick succession, then they really begin to affect expectations, and then they cause second-round effects. So, that is what we are dealing with. To deal with that, what you need is a way to anchor expectations. And in the BSP, we anchor expectations by announcing that we will keep inflation rates. We will target the inflation rate so that they will stay within the range of 2.0 [percent] to 4.0 percent in the next two years. We have what is called “flexible inflation targeting." The horizon can change a little bit, but the target remains the same. And this is kind of what central banks that deal with supply shocks tend to do. So, it is very important for us to maintain this inflation targeting framework. And then, monetary policy in terms of policy rates, in terms of forward guidance, is really about making sure that the framework remains credible. So, that is monetary policy in the face of supply shocks. Resilient banks When it comes to the banking system, as you know, the banking system in the Philippines remains strong and resilient. It emerged from the pandemic with very high levels of capital. The banks are flushed with liquidity. This time, the banks have been a source of stability for the whole economy. They have been part of the solution, whereas in the past when we had a crisis, the banks were part of the problem. Not this time. And we are trying to do three things in the banking system. Digital solution We have this Open Finance Framework, which is essentially about APIs [application programming interfaces]. These are platforms that allow banks more access to consumer data—not just their own consumer data, their own deposit data, but other data. This allows banks to have more analytical platforms so that they can expand into other financial services. But, at the same time, we want this Open Finance Framework to be sure that the cyber risks are minimized. And then, of course, we have digital banking. We have six licenses now for digital banks. So far, the digital banks seem to be doing very well when it comes to deposits. Online deposits seem easy enough. The hard part is the assets side, [particularly on] how they make loans online. So, that is a struggle. I think, for now, we have six licenses. We might give them a few more years to prove themselves, then we may open it up. And that is digital banking. We have also a regulatory sandbox. This is for fintech [financial technology] innovators. As you know, the more significant the innovation, the higher the uncertainty of regulation. So, the thing with the regulatory sandbox is you have the partnership between the fintech, or the innovator, and the regulator so that at the end of it, you minimize the uncertainty of regulation. Finally, let me talk about the payments system. We see the payments system as the gateway to financial inclusion. So, we start with something like an e-money [electronic money] account or a basic deposit account. This should lead, eventually, to people who used not to have bank accounts, who used not to deal with banks, to be able to access other services that banks offer—credit for example—and, from then on, access the rest of the financial system, [including] investment, insurance, and so on. So, that is one way. The other way is by going regional. [So, that is] going digital, going regional, and then going international. We are now working with our ASEAN [Association of Southeast Asian Nations] partners to develop what is called the Nexus payment system, which should be digital and interoperable. From then on, because this [was] designed to be interoperable across banks around the world, we hope to go global and, in the process, reduce the fees for, say, remittance. It costs about 5.0 percent now to send money to the Philippines when it comes to retail payments. We want to bring it down to 3.0 percent and, eventually, bring it down to 1.0 percent. So, that is the plan, and I am happy to take questions and discuss other challenges. Thank you very much. ###​

Title: Remarks for the 22nd Weekly Membership Meeting of the Rotary Club of Manila and Makati
Occasion: 22nd Weekly Membership Meeting of the Rotary Club of Manila and Makati
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​I am very happy to be here. I just realized from the beginning of the program that it will be very hard for me to become a member of the Rotary Club because, as it is well known in the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP), the Governor is sintunado. Three challenges I will talk about three challenges that we face in the Bangko Sentral. I do not know if you can show the slides, but we have three things over which we do things in the Bangko Sentral. The first thing is how to conduct monetary policy in what we call a “supply-shock economy." As you know, the world was hit by an unusual combination of supply shocks, especially in 2022. We, ourselves, we are hit by those things. In fact, we are the harder-hit economies when it comes to supply shocks. So, I will talk about that—how you do monetary policy in the face of those kinds of shocks. And then, I want to talk about digitalizing the banking system. One of our mandates is to maintain financial stability, but I feel—and the BSP feels—that part of maintaining stability is also to keep up with technological trends, especially within the banking system. And then, I want to talk about the payments system. The challenge in the payments system for us now is how to use [it] as a gateway for financial inclusion so that we get more and more kababayans to become part of the formal financial system. So, those are the three things I would like to talk about, and I would welcome any questions on these things. Supply shocks As I said, we were hit by supply shocks, the likes of which we have never seen before. There are shocks in oil; they were in food; they were in fertilizer; and they were not just large shocks, but they came with a high degree of frequency. This part of it is unusual. Globally, inflation rates rose to levels that have not been seen in decades. In our case, the inflation rates that we face reached their highest peak in 14 years in January of 2023. So, the inflation rate at that time was 8.7 percent. Since then, we have brought it down to 4.1 percent. So, my discussion is how we did that. Now, among observers, we are told that the supply shocks, you can just ignore it. You can just look through it and pretend it was not there because anyway, those shocks will dissipate. If the price goes up, it will come down. So, why bother tightening the monetary policy? That is actually a debate in academic circles, but among central banks, we have settled the debate. We know what to do in the face of those supply shocks. I will show you the graph of inflation. Blue is the headline inflation; this includes everything. And then, orange is core inflation; this is when you take food and energy out of the CPI [consumer price index]. The difference between the two is the consequence of supply shocks. I do not know if you notice, but when supply shocks go up, even the core goes up. And then, when supply shocks dissipate, the core stays up for a while. That is very important to know because that means you cannot just look through supply shocks. What happens in our experience from our statistical work is that supply shocks lead to a kind of inflation expectation that leads to second-round effects. So, other things go up; wages go up; transportation fares go up; and then, some of the things that go up with supply shocks do not come down. So, the supply shocks come down; the price of oil comes down; and the price of food comes down; but then, the second-round effects do not come down. Monetary policy has to do something about that, and that is the reason we have to tighten the monetary policy in the face of supply shocks. This is challenging because this is a new phenomenon. We have models. We have statistics that are not calibrated to this phenomenon. So, we use the models, but we use a lot of judgment. And, I think, somehow, things have worked. So, supply shocks lead to inflation expectations that lead to second-round effects. What we do in the face of that phenomenon is rely on our inflation target. It is fortunate that we are a central bank that is an inflation-targeting central bank. That means we say, we announce to everybody that we target inflation rates so that they stay within 2.0 percent and 4.0 percent. That is a big help. Because of those targets, we are able to anchor expectations. Otherwise, expectations may get out of control, and you get very large second-round effects. By being able to anchor inflation expectations, we are able to manage the second-round effects. So, that is our strategy. That is monetary policy strategy in what we call a “supply-shock economy." So, that seems to be working. On Friday, the next inflation number will come out, and we are crossing our fingers that it will be a good number. But that is where we are. By the way, people say that we are tightening too much. That is a very difficult challenge because we want to make sure that we do not tighten unnecessarily. We do not want to cause a loss of output that is not necessary. Sometimes, we make a mistake, and we tighten too much. But even when we make a mistake and we tighten too much, the loss of output tends to be temporary. So far, I think, we have been okay. We have been doing just the right amount of tightening. If you look at our GDP [gross domestic product] growth in 2023, [it is] 5.5 percent. That does not look so high compared to 2021 for example. But 5.5 percent is the highest growth rate within the fastest-growing region, Southeast Asia. China is doing 5.2 [percent], and we are doing 5.5 [percent]. So, that is not so bad. There are more jobs with that kind of growth rate. The unemployment rate is down to 4.2 percent. The underemployment rate is down to 11 percent; it used to be 14 percent. So, in terms of growth, in terms of employment, we seem to be managing reasonably well in the face of supply shock. Strong banking system The banking system, I think, is in good shape unlike in previous crises. Many of us remember in the 1980s, our banking system got into trouble in the 1980s along with Latin American countries. In fact, we call that the Latin American debt crisis. We were part of it. And so, when we tried to recover around 1989 from the Latin American debt crisis, the banks were part of the problem; they were not part of the solution. The banks had to repair their balance sheets. The same thing happened in 1997–1998. The banks got into trouble. They had to repair their balance sheets. And so, they were part of the problem. This time around, they were part of the solution. They went through the pandemic with very healthy balance sheets. If you look at, for example, their capital adequacy ratios (CARs)—we call them “capital buffers"—banks, on average, have a 16-percent CAR. That is capital divided by risk-weighted assets. The international standard is 10 percent. They are well above the standard for capital adequacy. When it comes to liquidity, we are at 183 percent. The technical word for this is the liquidity coverage ratio, [which we are at] 183 percent. The international standard is 100 percent. So, our banks are very liquid. Banks have more than adequate capital, which means they can still support our growth. In fact, loan growth has been 8.3 percent—that is quite healthy. And then, our non-performing loan (NPL) ratio, the ratio of NPLs to loans, is at 3.4 percent. That is pretty good. Coming out of the pandemic, it was nearly 5 percent. During the Asian [financial] crisis, it had reached 12 percent. 3.4 percent is not so bad. We want to bring it down to 2.0 percent. 1.5 percent—that is what you get during good times. So, the banks, as I have said, have been part of the solution rather than part of the problem. Digital efforts But we want to do better for the banks. And so, we are encouraging them to digitalize. I think that is inevitable. They have to digitalize, and there are four approaches to doing that. We have something called the Open Finance Framework. This is what they are doing in Europe for example. Banks these days, they are connected to something called APIs, or application programming interfaces. They are connected to each other. They are connected to other institutions through these APIs that allow them to do business that involves consolidating customer balance sheets, doing investments for customers, and doing data analytics for customers. In fact, for compliance purposes, APIs are going to be used to help banks satisfy their compliance requirement with the central bank. This is the jargon in the field; this is called “reg tech" [regulatory technology]. The BSP itself is using technology to oversee regulations and compliance of banks; that is called “sup tech" [supervisory technology]. Apart from the Open Finance Framework, we have what we call “regulatory sandboxes." What this means is that if you are a fintech [financial technology] and you have a very promising innovation, then you can come to the BSP, and we will give you a regulator. So, you can work with that regulator—you can test your idea, you can discuss the proof of concept, you can pilot your idea—and your regulator will be next to you to get your side, to tell you what might be the regulatory consequences if you bring your idea to the market. So, it is not about judging whether you really have a good idea and whether you will succeed. It is about minimizing regulatory uncertainty so when you actually put your project into place, you will have a very good idea of what the regulations will be. A very special case of that is our digital banking framework. We have now given licenses to six digital banks. They are doing well on the deposits side. They are able to get deposits online. A digital bank is not supposed to have any physical, no brick-and-mortar presence. So, everything has to be done online. And our digital banks have been very successful in raising deposits online. They are, to be honest, still struggling to make loans online. And it seems to be very hard to collect on loans online. In the Ph

Title: Message for the Annual Reception for the Banking Community
Occasion: Annual Reception for the Banking Community
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​Introduction Magandang gabi. I am so glad the weather cooperated. Former central bank governors, members of the Monetary Board, colleagues and friends from the banking and diplomatic community, fellow government officers, ladies and gentlemen, a pleasant good evening to all of you. On behalf of the Monetary Board, I'd like to thank you all for gracing the 2024 annual reception for the banking community within these walls of Fort San Antonio Abad. I am especially delighted to join you in this yearly tradition because tonight's celebration is a very special one. This year marks 75 years of central banking in the Philippines. It is fitting that we gather here at Fort Antonio Abad. This fort, together with Intramuros, was designated a national cultural treasure by the National Museum of the Philippines. It played a significant part of the history of Manila and similar to this fort, the BSP [Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas] has come a long way as a central bank from its inauguration on January 3, 1949. Due to the leadership of visionaries who recognized that the BSP must change with the times and the painstaking efforts of the men and women of the BSP who made this happen, we are all here today. The BSP started as a central monetary authority in 1949. It later assumed the responsibility for regulating the financial system. And then in 2019, we were able to inscribe in our charter our three fundamental mandates, maintaining price stability; financial stability; and a safe and efficient payments and settlement system. Through all these years, the BSP has managed to be a source of stability for the economy. We are not resting on our accomplishments. We deeply feel that we still have work to do. Indeed, we have outlined a reform agenda. It is an ambitious agenda, and we will need your help for most of it. Four goals Let me just mention four goals in this agenda. First, we want to further enhance our monetary policy framework by sharpening our research, our model, and non-monetary tools. We want these to be more responsive to inflation pressures, particularly during periods of unusual and large supply shocks. So far, the BSP managed to anchor inflation expectations and thus, effectively control second-round effects. Through our inflation-targeting framework, headline inflation has gone down to 3.9 percent as of December 2023, from a high of 8.7 percent in January 2023. Second, we will strengthen the way we conduct systemic risk oversight. You saw what happened in March 2023 with Silicon Valley Bank and Credit Suisse. We don't want this to happen to us. And for now, the banking system remains healthy, characterized by strong balance sheets, profitable operations, and sound performance indicators. The banking system likewise stood firm and proved its true resilience through the pandemic and through the international disruptions of March 2023. Third, the BSP financial stability mandates also carry with it the responsibility to deepen our capital markets. This is meant to diversify the sources of funds in our financial system so that our businesses and our investments do not have to rely entirely on the banking system in case there is a credit crunch. Fourth, we will strive to make the BSP sustainability initiative more meaningful by infusing it with an inclusion perspective. We want to ensure that the entire financial system supports an inclusive adaptation program so that the burden of transition does not fall on the most vulnerable segments of our society. Parallel to this, the BSP is harnessing digital technology to empower sectors which have been traditionally underbanked. We think our open financial framework will unlock opportunities for consumers. The BSP has been relentless in bringing our Paleng-QR Ph Plus Program to various parts of the country, giving market vendors, public transport drivers, and other merchants a more convenient way of accepting e-payments for their transactions. Conclusion So, the BSP has accomplished a lot in the last 75 years. None of these would have happened without the help of various stakeholders present here today. To the past members of the BSP Monetary Board and BSP officers, we salute you for helping shape the BSP into the world-class organization that it is today. Meanwhile, I hope that through the initiatives which we will put into play in collaboration with the banking industry, the BSP will be able to contribute to a brighter and better future for our countrymen. To officially cap this evening, let me now call on my fellow Monetary Board Members (MBM) to please join me on stage for the ceremonial toast, MBM Bruce Tolentino, MBM Anita Aquino, MBM. Rosalia de Leon. MBM. Romeo Bernardo, and MBM Benjamin Diokno. Mabuhay po tayong lahat and enjoy the evening. ###​

Title: Message during the Release of the 2023 Financial Stability Report
Occasion: Release of the 2023 Financial Stability Report
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​Introduction Magandang umaga sa inyong lahat! I thank everyone for making time to join us here in the BSP [Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas] on a Tuesday morning. Tomorrow is Valentine’s Day, and we will speak about love and roses! But today, let me talk about the danger of rosy scenarios. Today, we present to you our 2023 Financial Stability Report (FSR)—an FSCC [Financial Stability Coordination Council] communication tool for managing systemic risk. What is our FSR? It is a report published at least annually, pieced together from the collective wisdom of the FSCC, from suggestions by the BSP’s Monetary Board and the FSCC agency-offices, and from inputs from other participants of our financial system. The reason for the FSR is that we want to communicate to participants of the financial system to help them make better-informed decisions, and maybe tell us what we may have missed. The challenge is that systemic risk is inherently difficult to comprehend. Markets are fluid, and things can change very quickly. Some risks arise from our own markets and some risks in foreign markets can spill over onto our shores. The risks that are of the biggest concern are those that are brewing in out-of-sight vulnerabilities, risks that turn out to be contagious and to lead to broader dislocations. You have heard of "black swans" indicating highly unlikely surprises or "the butterfly effect" to describe how small things can lead to far-reaching consequences. These are the things we worry about. Indeed, financial market participants often make risky investments based on rosy scenarios. The more widely shared the scenario, the more dangerous it is. When something goes wrong in these scenarios, it sometimes leads to mass panic. There is a rush for the exits, causing massive investments to collapse. This is no idle theory. In 1993, the World Bank published a volume called The East Asian Miracle. It told a rosy scenario about our neighbors, including Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and South Korea. International investors bought the story. They bought it lock, stock and barrel. The World Bank was nice enough not to include the Philippines in its East Asian Miracle. Yet, somehow, we did not escape the rosy scenario, Nadamay pa rin tayo. In 1995, the Metro Pacific Group, along with a consortium of international investors, submitted the winning bid for Fort Bonifacio. The bid was US$1.6 billion. It was called "the property deal of the decade." We all know what happened next. In July 1997, we saw the sudden onset of the Asian crisis. All around the region, currencies and stock markets plunged. Massive loans went unpaid, property prices collapsed, and tall buildings languished unfinished. The whole region went into recession. We remember the crisis, but we often forget the rosy scenario that led to it. This FSR is about reminding us about the dangers of the rosy scenario and identifying the possible channels of contagion. We purposely wrote our FSR so that it is not a technical report crowded with fancy equations. Instead, we wrote it as a thematic narrative that tries to unearth the risks beneath the rosy scenarios. We provide a view of the macroeconomy and the financial markets from the perspective of systemic risk. We present the headline numbers and how they might interact with one another. This particular FSR takes a very institutional-level focus and makes an assessment of how capital markets may play a greater role in the resilience of the Philippine economy. So, I invite everyone to download a copy and parse through the pages of the 2023 Financial Stability Report. Read about the risks we see and our planned courses of action. Let us know what you think, and we are more than happy to engage. Join us in navigating the financial stability journey together. Marami pong salamat at mabuhay tayong lahat! ###​

Title: Capital Market Development
Place: Manila Golf and Country Club, Makati City
Occasion: Fellows Meeting of the Foundation for Economic Freedom
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ Magandang gabi sa inyong lahat. Thank you, Vaughn, for that generous introduction. The secrets that he revealed, I deny them all. It is wonderful to be here. I think I am still a member of FEF [Foundation for Economic Freedom]. I follow your discussions on Viber. So, I decided maybe I will talk about something I have not seen on Viber, and maybe I can get some help from you guys. A deeper capital market I want to talk about deepening capital markets. As you know, over the years, there have been roadmap after roadmap, on how to deepen our capital markets. In the 1990s, the IMF would borrow me from the New York Fed [Federal Reserve of New York]. I would come here to see how to deepen capital markets. But so far, there is still much to be done. So, maybe we could try harder. From the point of view of the central bank—there are two reasons why we would care about capital markets. First, we need the capital markets to strengthen the transmission mechanism of monetary policy. I will explain a little bit about that. And then, there is also the Greenspan reason, which is to diversify the sources of financing for our economy, for our investment, and for our businesses. So, let me start with the transmission mechanism. As you know, the policy rate that we have, the overnight RRP (reverse repurchase rate), is an overnight rate. An overnight rate does not affect too many economic decisions. Maybe a one-year rate that would affect credit and consumer decisions. A five-year rate might affect mortgages and other business investments. The way it works now is we have a policy rate, and then that vaguely affects expectations of what the policy rate might be in the future. These vague expectations would lead to maybe changes in asset prices, but we do not know the mechanism exactly because we do not see it. What has happened in the last few years — especially since 2008 and when we had a global fund crisis - the emphasis has become forward guidance instead of just the policy rate itself. The nice thing about forward guidance is it directly affects expectations. You can actually see it happen. You give the forward guidance today—2:00, [at] 2:15, you will see the yield curve move. So, it is a very strong mechanism. In fact, it is a stronger mechanism than just the policy rate. The forward guidance itself is a very powerful tool. We would like to have the same tool in the Philippines. And what we want to affect especially is the yield curve, but it would affect other asset prices. Spare tire When it comes to the risk management of the financial system as a whole, let me quote from Alan Greenspan. In the wake of the Asian Crisis he said, “This leads one to wonder how severe East Asia's problems would have been…had those economies not relied so heavily on banks as their means of financial intermediation…Had a functioning capital market existed, the outcome might well have been far more benign." In academic circles, this is called the Greenspan's spare tire. You need a spare tire for the banking system, and that spare tire would be the capital market. Regional picture Our neighbors have taken this to heart, and they have developed their capital markets. And we are still here with the same capital market that we had 30 years ago. Let me divide this up. If you look at stock markets as a percent of GDP [gross domestic product], you can see Thailand, South Korea, and Malaysia have bigger markets than us in terms of ratio to GDP. Corporate bond markets are the same thing. South Korea, Malaysia, [and] Thailand have bigger bond markets than we do. So here are the questions. I would like to talk a little bit about our choppy yield curve and then talk about what I call our Lake Wobegon corporate bond market, and then finally talk about our missing portfolio flows. Let me start with the yield curve and how we would build a benchmark yield curve. The yield curve that we use now is called BVAL [Bloomberg Valuation Service]; it is constructed for us by Bloomberg, doing the best it can with the data that is available. The orange line is what the BVAL curve looks like as of [January] 26th. As you notice, the curve kinks. If it were a liquid market, there would be no kicks because it is so easy, I think, [for] most of you guys, especially the bankers among you, to take positions in two neighboring maturities, and then you will make money just by selling the one with the maturity with the spike and then buy the neighboring bonds. So, I have to think that the bankers among you can do something similar. You would use the smoother curve that you get when you do the right kind of arbitrage. But then, if that is what you do, you do not know how well our transmission mechanism is working. Interest rate swaps But I think we can think about… using the swaps curve. This is the experience in the Euro area, around 2000-2010. Because of a fragmented market, they turned to the swaps market. And interest rate swaps are so easy. This contract is by far the largest over-the-counter derivative contract in the world. When we last looked at it, it was US$440 trillion. It is so easy to use that one wonders why it is not well used in the Philippines. Some people say it is the contracts—the master agreements that we have. You know the GMRA [Global Master Repurchase Agreement] contract, or the short end of the curve, the repo [repurchase] contract was designed to be so simple and so easy to use. The swaps contract itself is the master agreement. Again, it is very simple, designed to be widely accepted, but somehow, not in the Philippines. What I propose—please let me know what you think—is we use the 5-year swaps contract. It is the sweet spot for interest rates: one benchmark, one maturity using the swaps contract. Japan, for example, has only one benchmark, the 10-year JGB [Japanese Government Bond]. So, I think, especially for a market like ours, one maturity should be enough. So, I would like comments on that. The next issue for me is what I call our Lake Wobegon corporate bond market. Those of you who are old enough might remember Garrison Keillor. He used to talk about Lake Wobegon—a fictional town in Minnesota, and he said, “All the women are strong, all the men are good-looking, and all the children are above average." I think that applies to our corporate bond market. In our corporate bond market, virtually all of the issues are AAA. A few issues are AA but no A, no BBB. Now, if you look at the graph, the pie chart on the left is the Philippines, the blue slice is the triple A, and the gray slice is AA. So, very highly rated bonds. If you look at the Thai corporate bond market, the brown slice is A, and then the yellow slice is BBB. So, their market gives access to lower-rated issuers, which we seek not to be able to manage. Maybe, PhilGuarantee can help with this. As you know, in the 1980s, there was a dude named Michael Milken who was known as the “junk bond king." In the 1980s, he worked with Drexel Burnham Lambert to put together a large network of investors in high-yield bonds. This allowed him to raise vast amounts of money for leveraged buyout firms. And today, because of Michael Milken, the median bond issue in the United States corporate bond market is non-investment grade, it is BB. So that is quite an achievement I think, for Michael Milken. Eventually, he went to jail for fraud, but that is not part of his life we can learn from. Hot money The third issue I think I would like to bring to you is portfolio inflows. We are scared of portfolio inflows because it is hot money. This is from a newspaper just last Monday, US$247 million leaving the Philippines, mainly portfolio outflows. It looks like a scary headline, but US$247 million is nothing. It is negligible relative to things like remittances, things like BPO [business process outsourcing] revenues. If you look at the data, here I will show you the data for our neighbors including ourselves. This is relative to GDP. Blue is the Philippines, yellow is Malaysia, gray is Thailand, orange is South Korea, and green is Indonesia. As you can see, we have some of the smaller portfolio inflows. There is still something like hot money; they come in and out, but it is very small for the Philippines. The good news is that the investing game has changed. It is now all about passive investing. A long time ago in the 1960s, two other dudes, Harry Markowitz and William Sharpe, wrote papers that said, what you must do is diversify. Buy the whole market, buy the market portfolio. Of course, these days, you want your core portfolio to be the market portfolio, and then you can play around, if you are smart enough, to play around with the stock-taking. But your core portfolio must be the diversified market portfolio. Around 2008, investors finally saw the insights of William Sharpe and Harry Markowitz, 20 years after they won the Nobel prize for this. So, passive investing became the mode for investing in capital markets. These days, the way you do passive investing is to buy an index. An index of the market, or you buy an index of a sector of the market. Bonds, equities, you do the same thing. You do passive investing. Exchange-traded fund Now, the world has introduced index funds that are traded as ETF [exchange traded fund], global ETF. When an ETF invests in the Philippines, it is not hot money because it is the whole index. To get out of the Philippines, you have to sell the whole index. So, it is not hot money. The big three are Vanguard, BlackRock, and State Street. As you know, they manage the biggest exchange-traded funds. In fact, between the three of them, they owned 90 percent of the S&P 500. We are not included in these ETFs. We are not part of the index. We are excluded from the index. But other countries are in the index: Colombia, India, you name it. Bakit wala tayo? Bakit hindi tayo makasali? What is wrong with us? Why do they not like us? The top three emerging market bond funds for ETFs are managed by

Title: Opening Remarks for the Workshop on Nature, Macroeconomy, and Finance
Occasion: Workshop on Nature, Macroeconomy, and Finance
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ Introduction Good morning, everyone. ​ In a larger sense, this morning has been too nice. I took my usual walk this morning, and I said to myself, ang sarap naman, hindi mainit. There was a nice cool breeze. Just perfect for a morning walk. And it was misleadingly perfect because I think it allows us to be complacent about climate change. In 2015, when Mark Carney was still governor of the Bank of England, he warned about the tragedy over the horizon. He said, once climate change becomes a defining issue for financial stability, it may already be too late. Climate change At some point, maybe in the 2030s, we might reach tipping points. There are thresholds where the effects of climate change, accelerate and become irreversible. At those points, there might be very little we can do. These tipping points include the melting of the ice sheets near the North Pole, the melting of the ice sheets near the South Pole, the melting of the permafrost—the frozen layers near the surface of the earth, the destruction of coral reefs in the warmer parts of the ocean, and the collapse of ocean currents in the North Atlantic. So, workshops like this are a matter of urgency. I welcome this two-day international workshop on nature, macroeconomy, and finance. Thank you to our co-organizers, the London School of Economics and Political Science (LSE), the Grantham Research Institute on Climate Change and the Environment, [and the] International Network for Sustainable International Policy Insights, Research, and Exchange (INSPIRE). We are pleased to host this event in the BSP [Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas], following similar workshops in Singapore, Indonesia, and Malaysia. The objective of this workshop is to understand the impact and dependencies of nature and ecosystem services on the macroeconomy and financial sector. We will discuss the opportunities, financial risks, and policy challenges from the perspective of emerging market economies. Today, the global economy faces urgent risks due to the degradation of nature, particularly through economic activities such as deforestation and land use change. These will have significant macroeconomic implications. In 2009, we saw the destruction brought by Ondoy. In climate change, we might expect more and more typhoons like Ondoy. For our part, our mandate is to contribute to the stability of the financial system. We have to monitor the buildup of environmental risks through our supervised financial institutions. We are working with other member agencies, regional counterparts, and international networks to help us create a network for the financial system to manage environment-related opportunities. We are constructing a taxonomy that will apply to the activities and assets of financial institutions. ​ This taxonomy will be the framework for classification and can be the basis for initially disclosure, and potentially for incentives like the assignment of risk weights and, eventually, a basis for a carbon trading. So, we are working on that. Preliminary research on environment-related risks shows that big banks face international risks, primarily for extreme weather events, through their exposure to the agricultural sector and residential mortgages. As a result, we have been building our capacity to conduct analysis and research, introduce nature-related risks, and how they will affect the structure of the economy and their implications for financial stability. Conclusion So, let me thank the speakers who took the time to share their expertise in this field, participants from different departments of the BSP, our partner and government agencies, and supervised banks. Thank you again to the LSE group, Dr. Simon Dikau, Elena Almeida, and Thessa Vasudhevan, the World Wildlife Fund, and the INSPIRE group for bringing this workshop to the BSP and contributing to the current discussions on nature and biodiversity laws. As we delve deeper into this topic, it is useful to keep in mind the words of John Muir, a Scottish-born nationalist. He said, “When you try to pick out anything by itself, we find it hitched to everything else in the universe." This means working together to understand better the dynamics and solutions that benefit our economies and planet. ​ Thank you, maraming salamat po, mabuhay tayong lahat. ###​

Title: Message for the Ceremonial Signing of Revised Memorandum of Agreement on Information Exchange
Occasion: Ceremonial Signing of Revised Memorandum of Agreement on Information Exchange
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ Introduction​ Magandang hapon po. This is a very important occasion, and I am pleased to be part of it. We are here to celebrate the conclusion of the revised Memorandum of Agreement or MOA on Information Exchange between the BSP [Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas] and PDIC [Philippine Deposit Insurance Corporation]. This is important because the deposit contract is a very strange contract. If you are a depositor, you either get your money back or you do not. This means the biggest risk for banks is the deposit run. And our biggest tool against that risk is deposit insurance. Now, over the years, things have changed. The environment has changed for deposits. The last amendment to this agreement took place in 2004. I think we are still recovering from the Asian [financial] crisis. This was followed by the global [financial] crisis in 2008. And these crises are reasons for change. So, it is timely that we revise our [agreement on] information exchange. When I say things have changed, one thing that has changed was in 2014 [when] we adopted Basel III. Now we have capital adequacy ratios [and] liquidity coverage ratios. ​ But more than that, I think the culture has changed in terms of regulation, both by the PDIC and the BSP. We have moved from a very prescriptive examination and supervision style to a more risk-based and more principles-based approach. We do not tell banks specifically what to do. We tell banks to take risks seriously, and that is the reason for all these regulatory standards. Because of these changes, we need a new kind of agreement for information exchange. I extend my gratitude to all those who worked hard in the development of this agreement. I appreciate the thoughtful deliberation made by the team to ensure that this agreement is comprehensive and that all the information that needs to be shared falls within the revised MOA. The MOA also covers information that supports other functional departments of the BSP such as legal services, economic statistics, as well as loans and credit.​​ As we move forward, let us remember that the success of this collaboration will depend on the effective information [and] implementation of this agreement. I enjoin everyone as stewards of this revised MOA who have joined us today to follow through on your commitments to keeping the revised MOA updated, relevant, and operational in pursuit of our primary objective of promoting financial system stability. Mabuhay po tayong lahat! ###

Title: The challenges we face at Bangko Sentral
Occasion: General Membership Meeting of the Financial Executives Institute of the Philippines
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ I want to talk today about the challenges we face at the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) and begin with what is going on in our economy. Economic prospects remain firm We saw in 2023, a 5.6-percent GDP [gross domestic product] growth. That is pretty good. We are still the fastest-growing economy in the fastest-growing region of the world. As you know, Europe basically had zero growth, the US [United States] maybe 3.0 percent, which is remarkably strong, stronger than expected. China, our next biggest trading partner, perhaps maybe at 5.0 percent. It looks strong, but that is very low compared to what used to be 9.7 percent over 15 years. So, China is a very different economy now from what it used to be. Unemployment is down to 3.1 percent, which is also pretty good. So, thanks to our economic managers, I think we are doing well. On the monetary side, we have raised the monetary policy rate by 450 basis points in the face of a very high inflation rate. And that seems to have worked. Our inflation rate peaked at 8.7 percent in January 2023 and is now down to 3.4 percent, which is within our target range for inflation. I think those are good, reassuring numbers. But I would say it is still too soon to declare victory. If you look at our forecast for inflation for this year, the average is hovering near the edge of our forecast range, around 3.9 percent. We hope it will drop to 3.5 percent next year. So, 3.9 percent is just within our target range. But there are upside risks. If you look at what we consider to be the average probability of our inflation rate exceeding our target range for 2024, the probability is over 50 percent. That is not good enough. The upside risks are mainly electricity rates, transport charges, and higher oil prices. There are some downside risks, but the government is making strenuous efforts to mitigate the impact of El Niño mainly through drought-resistant seeds. So, we are hoping that will work. That is what is going on in the inflation front. Our number one mandate is to stabilize prices, keeping inflation within the target range of two to four percent. The good news is that our banks are doing well. Suppose you look at the regulatory numbers. Our what we call the capital adequacy ratio is at 16.4 percent. The regulatory standard or the regulatory floor is 10 percent. So, we are well above the regulatory floor regarding capital. When it comes to liquidity, 183 percent. The liquidity we are measuring here is the LCR or the liquidity coverage ratio. The regulatory standard is 100 percent. Loans have been growing briskly, 9.8 percent, and the NPL [non-performing loans] is manageable at 3.2 percent. So, the banks are okay. So, the banks should be able to finance growth. So, that is where we stand. Let me now go on to talk about what challenges we face. Conducting monetary policy in a supply-shock economy We are facing the challenge of monetary policy in what has become a supply-shock economy. Our economy has been subject to more supply shocks than in the past, which is very unusual. We also aspire to help digitalize the banking and payment system. We will talk about that in a little bit. We also aspire to help deepen the capital markets with your help. So, I hope to discuss some issues related to deepening the capital market. Let me say about how we are doing monetary policy these days. As you know, we got hit by oil price shocks, food price shocks, and fertilizer price shocks. Globally, inflation peaked in 2022. Our inflation rate peaked in January 2023 at 8.7 percent—the highest in 14 years. As I have said, we are down to 3.4 percent, partly because of the monetary policy. The difference between the economy we now have and the economy we used to have is the dominance of supply shocks hitting us. What happens, I think, in our analysis of the supply shocks is that supply shocks lead to inflation expectations. The oil price goes up, people think other prices will also go up because oil, of course, is an input to the production of many things, and so prices go up for those other things. We call those things second-round effects. So, the main effect is oil price shock, which leads to second-round effects. The thing about second-round effects is that they are asymmetric. So, the oil price will come down, but the second-round effects do not. They are sticky downwards, so it is asymmetric. And that is what we worry about.​ This means we cannot just look through supply shocks. We have to worry about supply shocks because of the asymmetry of second-round effects. What helps us is our framework for inflation targeting. We decided to target inflation so it stays within two to four percent. We made ourselves an inflation-targeting central bank. That helps because it anchors expectations. So, when you have supply shocks, inflation expectations do not go crazy. They are kind of sticky as well. That anchoring has been a big help to our monetary policy. Once you have a well-anchored inflation, you can more easily manage second-round effects. We worry about whether inflation will remain well-anchored. So, we have estimates of the risks of the anchoring and worry about them de-anchoring every time we meet as a Monetary Board. So, that is the new monetary policy strategy or the new monetary policy framework that we follow.​ Digitalizing the banking and payments system We also aspire to digitalize the financial system. We have four initiatives or four pathways to that end. One is called the Open Finance Framework. If you are a well-established bank, you would be participating in this. If you are BPI [Bank of the Philippine Islands], BDO, or Metrobank, you will be part of the Open Finance Framework. Open finance means working with application programming interfaces (APIs). These platforms connect you to other financial services and connect your customers to other financial services. Then, you have to figure out how to integrate that platform into your system. But banks now are working with 50 [to] 100 APIs doing many different things. Our role here is to make sure that when you use your customers' data, you use it with their permission. They still own the data; you do not own that data. Your customers own their data and want to ensure that when you use that data with APIs or other platforms, you get their permission first, and then you can do whatever you like. You could also do digital banking. This means you raise deposits online and make loans online. The first part is easy; the second part is not so easy. In our culture, apparently, you need a human being to collect on loans, but nonetheless, we are hoping digital banks can figure this out. And a couple of them are figuring it out. They are six now. We are limiting the number of digital banks to six licenses, but we are looking very closely at the industry and are going to decide if we are going to open it up again. Maybe we can attract different business models and make the industry more exciting. We also have a regulatory sandbox. The regulatory sandbox means you try your innovation, do a proof of concept, and do a pilot, but when you do that, you work with a regulator. The regulator is there to help you and tell you what the regulatory implications might be if you succeed. We are not there to judge whether you will succeed or not. We are just there to help you. If you grow, you know what you are getting into regarding regulations. It is about minimizing regulatory uncertainty. If you have a new idea, enter a sandbox. We will assign you a regulator. We will scold you, oh bawal 'yan, but that is for your own good. Then, of course, there is generative AI [artificial intelligence]. That is inevitable. If you have used ChatGPT, you know how irresistible it is. So, we do not even have to encourage it. You guys will do it without any encouragement, but of course, we have to think of guard rails for generative AI. As you know, generative AI leads to what we call hallucinations; it imagines things that never happened. It leads to herding; it gives the same answer to different questions. So, we think that, for now, at least, when using generative AI, a human being should work with it and look at the answers before you decide. Apparently, it is not so hard to tell whether the answers are wrong. If you teach at Ateneo or SMU and take a take-home exam, you are almost sure the student will use AI. A student who does not use AI is not a good student. But apparently, it is easy to tell. When the homework comes in, you look at it, and you will know whether AI is used. The point is to use AI so that you will have reasonable answers, good answers, not just ones generated by the computer. So, that is what we are trying to do with digitalizing the economy and the financial system. On top of these things, we are promoting a digital payment system that serves the unbanked. So, here, you can start with an e-wallet or a basic deposit account. A simplified deposit account is supposed to be easy to open for anyone. We hope this will lead to people joining the formal financial system. They will have access to bank credit, other financial services, and eventually the whole financial ecosystem—access to investment instruments, access to the right kind of insurance, and so on. That, in general, is what we are doing, and the guy in charge of that is [BSP] Deputy Governor Mert Tangonan. So, he is the guy to ask questions about digital payments. One thing we are doing regarding cross-border retail digital payments is the Project Nexus. It is a project by the ASEAN [Association of Southeast Asian Nations] central banks. If you have a fast payment system like InstaPay, you can connect to Nexus, which is the case for ASEAN. Singapore has it, Indonesia has it, and Malaysia has it. ASEAN central banks or economies will all be connected to Nexus. We think Nexus will be operational in 2026, and this will allow remittances to come to the Philippines at no more than 3.0 percent. The nice

Title: Digital bridges for financial inclusion
Occasion: Media Launch of the 3rd Digital Financial Inclusion Awards
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ Magandang hapon sa inyong lahat. We at the Bangko Sentral [ng Pilipinas] (BSP) are pleased to launch the third Digital Financial Inclusion Awards (DFIA) in partnership with the Microfinance Council of the Philippines, Inc. (MCPI). The MCPI is led by Gilbert Maramba and Paul Favila [for] Citi Philippines. This event shows the strength of the partnership among MCPI, Citi, and the BSP in promoting financial inclusion through the digitalization of microfinance. I think digitalization is the secret sauce. Parang bagoong sa kare-kare. So, we thank the members of the national selection committee of our awards program. They have chosen inspiring leaders from a broad cross-section of our economy. ​ All these leaders shared their time and expertise to support financial inclusion through the digitalization of microfinance institutions (MFI). To us at the BSP, financial inclusion is an undertaking that requires a whole-of-nation collaboration. Every form of support matters, whether from the private sector, NGOs [non-government organizations], multilateral institutions, local governments, microentrepreneurs, microfinance institutions, or the media. The National Strategy for Financial Inclusion (NSFI) of 2022-2028 defines financial inclusion as a state where everyone, especially the vulnerable, has access to a wide range of financial services. ​ To us, microfinance remains integral to the initiatives and programs in the NSFI. Here is where we stand. As of the third quarter of 2023, 138 banks engaged in microfinance served approximately 1.9 million borrowers. Non-bank microfinance institutions, such as cooperatives, supported 9.7 million members by the end of 2021. Microfinance NGOs catered to 6.6 million clients as of 2022. If microfinance institutions adopt innovative financial services, especially through digitalization, we expect additional benefits that would generate better reach and better quality of services. This is where the DFIA program comes in. The DFIA focuses on recognizing outstanding MFIs and microentrepreneurs who have successfully adopted digitalization in their operations. By encouraging innovation in microfinance, we hope to inspire more organizations to do the same. As we promote increased access to credit through digitalization, let us remember that microfinance is not only about borrowing. Microfinance also provides access to savings, investments, and insurance. These tools will enable the vulnerable to better manage their resources and protect themselves against certain risks. This resilience is about the overall financial health of microentrepreneurs. So, digitalization is a bridge that connects financially underserved segments of the population to providers of formal financial services. So, let us build more digital bridges so that everyone may enjoy the benefits of financial inclusion. ​ Thank you all for your continuing support. Magandang hapon sa inyong lahat! ###

Title: Forging a Sustainable Future for the Philippines
Place: Senate Hall, National Museum, Manila City
Occasion: World Economic Forum: Country Roundtable on the Philippines-Welcome Reception
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ Introduction President of the World Economic Forum Børge Brende, Special Assistant to the President for Investment and Economic Affairs Secretary Frederick Go, Finance Secretary Ralph Recto, and the rest of the Cabinet secretaries who are here with us tonight. To the members of the Monetary Board, distinguished guests, ladies and gentlemen, a pleasant evening to you all. Thank you for joining us tonight for the Welcome Reception for the Delegates and Guests of the World Economic Forum's Country Roundtable on the Philippines. The magnificent 10-panel work of art you see around you is by National Artist Botong Francisco. It depicts the Filipino struggle through history. Today, we face another struggle. That is the struggle to forge a sustainable future. Toward sustainability With the specter of climate change, the concept of sustainability naturally evokes a vision for a livable and thriving planet. For the BSP [Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas], building a resilient and inclusive financial sector is a strategic thrust toward a sustainable future for the country. Thus, we have embarked on an agenda with the goal of building a climate-resilient financial system that still supports the financing needs of a growing economy. To this end, we have issued regulations to promote the banks’ effective management of their ESG [environmental, social, and governance]-related risks and to nudge them to fulfill their catalytic role in sustainability. Just recently, we issued a circular adopting the first Philippine Sustainable Finance Taxonomy Guidelines. This circular provides a common benchmark for classifying financed activities based on their alignment with environment and social objectives. A common benchmark allows for transparent reporting which builds institutional credibility and investor confidence. It also guides data collection to inform initiatives to further mobilize sustainable financing. The other agenda we pursue for a resilient and inclusive financial sector is digitalization. Apart from promoting efficiency and adapting to the rapidly changing financial landscape, we see digitalization as a pathway to financial inclusion. Today, more and more Filipinos are part of the formal financial system.1 We look forward to seeing this inclusion pave the way for access to more financial products and economic opportunities for all. Conclusion Allow me to extend my gratitude to the World Economic Forum for providing a platform and opportunities for emerging markets, such as the Philippines, to showcase our potential. Let me end by wishing all the Forum delegates a productive and enjoyable two days in Manila. We look forward to the great exchanges on clean energy and digital transformation as well as other topics, touching on the drivers of infrastructure development, competitiveness, and inclusive growth. May these dialogue and discussions lead to meaningful partnerships. We are confident that your investments in the country—both existing and forthcoming—will be worthwhile and will yield mutually beneficial outcomes. Thank you once again for gracing this occasion. Please enjoy the rest of the dinner. ###​ ____________________________________ 1 65 percent of Filipino adults have transaction/formal accounts in 2022, a significant increase from 56 percent in 2021 and just 23 percent in 2017.​

Title: Message during the “Reuniting the Surigao Treasure” Exhibition
Occasion: Opening Reception of “Reuniting the Surigao Treasure” Exhibition
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ Ayala Foundation Chairman Fernando Zobel de Ayala, Foundation President Tony Lambino, Ayala Museum Director Mariles Gustilo, Dr. Nina Baker, officials and representatives of the Ayala Foundation, BSP colleagues, guests, our dear friends from the media, ladies and gentlemen, magandang gabi po. It is a pleasure to be with you to mark this important and timely between the Ayala Museum and the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas. [It is] timely because, as Mariles said, our museums are both 50 years old this year. As you know, the BSP is the safekeeper of the country's gold reserves. You will be relieved to know that what you see here today is not part of those reserves. The gold collection represents our history, our talent, and our potential. Some people say that one of the things that sets man apart from other creatures is our capacity to be awed. When we see something this amazing, we realize how big the world really is. How rich is the history of a race—or the whole of our country, the whole of our humanity—beyond the people who live around us and in our lives. These inspire us to strive harder, be more creative, and do more good in the world. To see the Surigao Treasure is to be awed by our ancestors' achievements in technology, art, commerce, and society long before Magellan arrived. In some ways, these ancestors lived better than we do! To see the two collections is to be awed by the vision and yearslong efforts of Cecilia and Leandro Locsin, and Jaime Laya to assemble them, piece by piece, after they were dug up and sold by treasure hunters in 1981. I join you [the audience] in saluting the Locsin family and [former] Governor Laya. To see these collections is also to be grateful to institutions like Ayala Museum and the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas, not only trying to protect this legacy but also to work together to share them with Filipinos and the world. The exhibit brings together the two biggest collections from the historic find of 1981. Clothes, jewelry, tools, and other articles that families used in their homes and villages hundreds of years ago [were] brought together again here. With two so-called twins, we have brought together two parts of a sash and two parts of a crown-like ornament. The BSP may have billions of dollars in gold reserves, but this exhibit is more than its weight in gold. Thank you to the museum, the foundation, [and] the Zobel de Ayala family for giving the Filipino people a beautiful venue to be awed by Filipino gold, Filipino art, and our Filipino heritage. Maraming salamat po. ### ​

Title: Mitigating Climate Change through Collaboration
Occasion: 2nd Philippine Climate Forum
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​ I am grateful to join this 2nd Philippine Climate Forum. The theme, “Gearing Towards Low-Carbon and Climate-Resilient 2030,” echoes the urgency of necessary actions as we move closer to the end of the decade. While the country’s greenhouse gas emissions seem negligible as compared with those of the advanced economies, we disproportionately bear the brunt of the impact of climate change. The investments needed to reach our country’s climate goals is estimated at US$17 billion a year up to 2030. The national budget appropriation for this only stood at around US$8 billion for 2024. This means that a significant amount of private sector financing is needed to augment the national government’s budget. It is in this light that the financial sector regulators issued guidelines aimed at promoting effective management of risks and accelerating the growth of the sustainable finance market in the country. One such issuance is the Sustainable Finance Taxonomy Guidelines. The taxonomy is a tool to classify activities on the extent to which they meet the identified objectives. This initially covers climate change mitigation and adaptation, and in the future, circular economy, and biodiversity. We are in the very early days of a taxonomy framework. The BSP (Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas) will observe how the banks are applying the taxonomy in their credit and investment portfolios. This can then inform supplementary regulatory guidance and reporting. Over time, as we establish trends on the banking sector’s taxonomy-disaggregated exposures, we expect to have more meaningful industry discussion on setting targets. We want to see the percentage of the banks’ green-labeled exposures increasing, with adaptation activities getting equal if not higher share than mitigation. We also need to see green-labeled exposures stand to benefit small businesses and vulnerable communities where adaptation finance is most needed. This is in line with the BSP’s inclusive sustainability agenda. An inclusive approach entails two things. One, we avoid unintended exclusionary effects of imposed sustainability standards, and two, that we are deliberate in supporting green finance mobilization not only for the economy’s just transition to low carbon but also for climate adaptation on which our country’s climate strategy is anchored. We also envision the taxonomy to become a tool for green finance market development. We will be studying possible taxonomy-based market incentives, whether via conventional regulatory levers or something akin to a market pricing mechanism. We want to see the market recognizing the price premium on green investments, which can then enable trading. This idea is quite analogous to what we see in carbon and transition credits. These are potential innovative use cases of the taxonomy to develop and deepen the country’s green finance market. We are keen to explore these possibilities and I would be happy to hear your thoughts on this. Meanwhile, we recognize that the taxonomy should be complemented by other equally important measures to ensure that financial institutions effectively manage climate-change related risks. We have published initial studies quantifying the impact of climate change and other environmental risks to the financial sector. The BSP will continue to improve our data and methodologies to measure the impact of climate change. We will also continue our work on enhancing sustainability-related disclosures and reporting. The BSP can only do so much. All actors—the financial sector regulators, finance providers, businesses, and relevant government agencies—must work together. We hope the Climate Forum can catalyze such collaboration. Maraming salamat at mabuhay tayong lahat!

Title: Going the Extra Mile for Financial Inclusion
Occasion: 2024 Cerise+SPTF Annual Meeting
Speaker: BSP Governor Eli M. Remolona, Jr.
Content: ​​​Introduction​ SEC (Securities and Exchange Commission) Commissioner Javey Paul Francisco, Social Performance Task Force Executive Director Laura Foose, Cerise Executive Director Cecile Lapenu, Microfinance Council of the Philippines, Inc. Chairman Gilbert Maramba, Kabalikat para sa Maunlad na Buhay, Inc. President and BSP (Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas) Adviser Ed[uardo] Jimenez, CARD Bank Vice Chair and Senior Management Adviser Dr. Dolores Torres, guests, ladies and gentlemen, good morning. Welcome to the social performance task force annual meeting. We are set for four days. Four busy days of training, breakout sessions, and field visits here in the Philippines. Thank you especially to those who have travelled from afar to join us here today. Our theme today is all about going the extra mile to build effective financial inclusion--a principle we at the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas hold dear. Let me give you a quick over​​​​view of our progress. Gateway to financial inclusion In 2015, only 22 percent of Filipinos had bank accounts. By 2021, that figure had risen to 56 percent. We see digital payments as a gateway to financial inclusion. These payments have increased dramatically from just 1 percent in 2013 to 42 percent in 2022. This progress was achieved with the help of so many partners. It started with a strategic roadmap—the National Strategy for Financial Inclusion launched in 2015. It was followed by an updated strategy in 2022. The Financial Inclusion Steering Committee, which I chair, includes 21 government agencies working together to develop inclusive policies. As you know, for our efforts to work, it is critical to build trust in formal financial services. Hence, the BSP has been actively involved in consumer protection.
